formulationsCanada: Capsule (100 MG, 75 MG, 50 MG, 25 MG, 10 MG, 150 MG, 5 %, 6 MG, 3 MG)
moa: Tertiary-amine tricyclic; blocks NE and 5-HT reuptake and is one of the most potent H1 antihistamines available. M1/α1 antagonist.
receptorProfile: Extremely potent H1 blockade → strong sedation, exploited at very low dose as a selective H1 hypnotic. Anticholinergic; Na-channel block in overdose at antidepressant doses.
onsetOfEffect: Sleep onset benefit same night at low dose; antidepressant effect 2–4 wk.
metabolism: Hepatic; CYP2D6 (also 1A2/2C19/3A4) — demethylated to nordoxepin (active).
startingDose: Depression 25–75 mg qhs; insomnia 3–6 mg qhs (low-dose H1 hypnotic).
titration: For depression, increase gradually; the insomnia dose is not titrated upward.
maxDose: 150 mg/day outpatient (up to 300 mg/day inpatient); insomnia 6 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Bedtime (sedating).
startStopCrossTaper: Taper at antidepressant doses. 14-day MAOI washout each way.
boxedWarning: WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Antidepressants increase the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in pediatric and young adult patients in short-term studies. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening and emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1)] . Doxepin hydrochloride capsules are not approved for use in pediatric patients [see Use in Specific Populations (8.4)]. WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. • Increased risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adults taking antidepressants. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening and emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors (5.1) • Doxepin hydrochloride capsules are not approved for use in pediatric patients (8.4)
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Doxepin hydrochloride capsules are contraindicated in patients: With hypersensitivity to doxepin (hypersensitivity reactions have included tongue edema and urticaria). The possibility of cross sensitivity with other dibenzoxepines should be kept in mind. With glaucoma [see Warnings and Precautions (5.3)]. With current or past urinary retention [see Adverse Reactions (6.1)]. Taking MAOIs, or within 14 days of stopping MAOIs (including the MAOIs linezolid or intravenous methylene blue) because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome [see Warnings and Precautions (5.2) and Drug Interactions (7)]. Hypersensitivity to doxepin (4) Glaucoma (4) Current or past urinary retention (4) Taking MAOIs, or within 14 days of stopping MAOIs (4)
seriousAE: Cardiac conduction delay / arrhythmia (wide QRS, QT) at antidepressant doses
overdoseLethality: HIGH at antidepressant doses — cardiotoxicity (wide QRS, arrhythmia), seizures and coma; the low-dose (3–6 mg) hypnotic formulation carries far less TCA cardiotoxicity but a full antidepressant supply is lethal. IV sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms.
discontinuationSyndrome: Cholinergic rebound at antidepressant doses — taper.
baselineWorkup: ECG (baseline at antidepressant doses, especially age >40 or cardiac disease)
ongoingMonitoring: Sedation/next-day impairment; mood/suicidality; ECG if cardiac risk at higher doses.
majorDDI: CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, bupropion) raise levels
Other sedatives / CNS depressants / alcohol (additive sedation)
contraindicatedCombos: Untreated narrow-angle glaucoma / urinary retention (strong anticholinergic)
pregnancy: Weigh untreated illness. Tie to CANMAT perinatal 2024.
lactation: A case of infant sedation/respiratory depression reported — generally avoided in breastfeeding.
geriatric: Anticholinergic/sedating at antidepressant doses; the low-dose hypnotic (3–6 mg) is comparatively better studied for insomnia in older adults.
cardiac: Na-channel block at antidepressant doses; ECG and caution.
pearls: One of the most potent H1 antihistamines — at low dose (3–6 mg) it is a selective histamine-blocking hypnotic for sleep maintenance, with minimal TCA toxicity.
At full antidepressant doses it behaves like any tertiary-amine TCA: sedating, anticholinergic and cardiotoxic in overdose.
mnemonics: "Low-dose Doxepin = Dozing (H1 hypnotic)".
confusables: Doxepin (TCA / H1 hypnotic) vs doxazosin (α1-blocker for BPH/HTN) — sound-alikes.
classDifferentiators: The most antihistaminic TCA; uniquely repurposed at very low dose as a pure H1 hypnotic.
examHooks: Which tricyclic, at 3–6 mg, is used as a selective H1-antihistamine hypnotic for sleep maintenance? (doxepin)
counsellingPoints: Very sedating — take at bedtime and beware next-day drowsiness.
Do not combine with alcohol/other sedatives.
Full antidepressant doses are dangerous in overdose.

vignettes.stem: A 70-year-old with sleep-maintenance insomnia but no depression is prescribed a 3 mg tricyclic at bedtime. Which agent is being used, and why is this low dose favoured?
options: Doxepin — selective H1 blockade with minimal anticholinergic/cardiac effect
vignettes.answer: Doxepin — selective H1 blockade with minimal anticholinergic/cardiac effect
vignettes.explanation: At 3–6 mg, doxepin acts as a nearly pure H1 antihistamine hypnotic for sleep maintenance, avoiding the anticholinergic and cardiotoxic effects seen at antidepressant doses.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies. These studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior with antidepressant use in patients over age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressant use in patients aged 65 and older [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ] . In patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy, monitor closely for worsening, and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Advise families and caregivers of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ] . WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. Increased risk of suicidal thinking and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults taking antidepressants ( 5.1 ) Monitor for worsening and emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors ( 5.1 )

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS The use of MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with Duloxetine delayed-release capsules or within 5 days of stopping treatment with Duloxetine delayed-release capsules is contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome. The use of Duloxetine delayed-release capsules within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders is contraindicated [see Dosage and Administration (2.8) and Warnings and Precautions (5.4) ]. Starting Duloxetine delayed-release capsules in a patient who is being treated with MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue is also contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome [see Dosage and Administration (2.9) and Warnings and Precautions (5.4) ]. Concomitant use of an MAOI antidepressant with Duloxetine delayed-release capsules is contraindicated Use of Duloxetine delayed-release capsules within 14 days of stopping an MAOI antidepressant is contraindicated In linezolid- or intravenous methylene blue-treated patients, initiation of Duloxetine delayed-release capsules is contraindicated ( 4 )

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE 10.1 Signs and Symptoms In postmarketing experience, fatal outcomes have been reported for acute duloxetine overdoses, primarily with mixed overdoses, but also with duloxetine only, including 1000 mg of duloxetine (approximately 8.3 times the maximum recommended dosage). Signs and symptoms of overdose (duloxetine alone or with mixed drugs) included somnolence, coma, serotonin syndrome, seizures, syncope, tachycardia, hypotension, hypertension, and vomiting. 10.2 Management of Overdose There is no specific antidote to a Duloxetine delayed-release capsules overdosage, but if serotonin syndrome ensues, specific treatment (such as with cyproheptadine and/or temperature control) …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Both CYP1A2 and CYP2D6 are responsible for duloxetine metabolism. Potent inhibitors of CYP1A2 should be avoided ( 7.1 ) Potent inhibitors of CYP2D6 may increase Duloxetine delayed-release capsules concentrations ( 7.2 ) Duloxetine delayed-release capsules is a moderate inhibitor of CYP2D6 ( 7.9 ) 7.1 Inhibitors of CYP1A2 When duloxetine 60 mg was co-administered with fluvoxamine 100 mg, a potent CYP1A2 inhibitor, to male subjects (n=14) duloxetine AUC was increased approximately 6-fold, the C max was increased about 2.5-fold, and duloxetine t 1/2 was increased approximately 3-fold. Other drugs that inhibit CYP1A2 metabolism include cimetidine and quinolone antimicrobials …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary Data from a postmarketing retrospective cohort study indicate that use of duloxetine in the month before delivery may be associated with an increased risk of postpartum hemorrhage. Data from published literature and from a postmarketing retrospective cohort study have not identified a clear drug-associated risk of major birth defects or other adverse developmental outcomes (see Data ) . There are risks associated with untreated depression and fibromyalgia in pregnancy, and with exposure to SNRIs and SSRIs, including Duloxetine delayed-release capsules, during pregnancy (see Clinical Considerations ). moa: Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (blocks SERT and NET) with a relatively balanced 5-HT/NE effect across the usual dose range; the noradrenergic action also mediates its analgesic effect.
receptorProfile: SERT and NET affinity (more balanced than venlafaxine at usual doses); negligible off-target receptor binding.
onsetOfEffect: Mood: 4–6 wk. Neuropathic/fibromyalgia pain: often within 1–4 wk (independent of mood).
metabolism: Hepatic CYP1A2 (major) and CYP2D6; moderate CYP2D6 inhibitor. Smoking (a CYP1A2 inducer) lowers duloxetine levels.
elimination: Renal (metabolites); avoid in severe renal impairment (CrCl <30).
foodEffect: May be taken with or without food (food slightly delays absorption).
startingDose: 30–60 mg/day (start 30 mg to improve tolerability, then 60 mg).
titration: Increase to 60 mg/day; 60 mg is usually optimal for depression — higher doses add side effects more than efficacy.
maxDose: 120 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once daily (may divide BID); delayed-release capsule swallowed whole.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Avoid in significant hepatic impairment and in severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min).
startStopCrossTaper: Taper to limit discontinuation.
administrationNotes: Do not crush/chew the enteric-coated capsule.

In patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy, monitor closely for worsening, and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Advise families and caregivers of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ] . WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. Increased risk of suicidal thinking and behavior in children, …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS The use of MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with Duloxetine delayed-release capsules or within 5 days of stopping treatment with Duloxetine delayed-release capsules is contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome. The use of Duloxetine delayed-release capsules within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders is contraindicated [see Dosage and Administration (2.8) and Warnings and Precautions (5.4) ]. Starting Duloxetine delayed-release capsules in a patient who is being treated with MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue is also contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome [see Dosage and Administration (2.9) and Warnings and Precautions (5.4) ]. Concomitant use of an MAOI antidepressant with Duloxetine delayed-release capsules is contraindicated Use of Duloxetine …
seriousAE: Hepatotoxicity (avoid in hepatic disease / heavy alcohol use)
overdoseLethality: SNRI overdose — seizures, serotonin toxicity, and cardiac effects possible; more concerning than SSRIs.
discontinuationSyndrome: Moderate — dizziness, nausea, irritability, paresthesias; taper.
baselineWorkup: Consider LFTs / alcohol history given hepatotoxicity risk
ongoingMonitoring: Blood pressure; watch for hepatic signs (jaundice, RUQ pain); mood/suicidality; sodium if symptomatic.
majorDDI: Potent CYP1A2 inhibitors (fluvoxamine, ciprofloxacin) markedly raise levels
contraindicatedCombos: Potent CYP1A2 inhibitors (e.g., fluvoxamine — avoid combination)
pregnancy: Limited data; possible neonatal adaptation late in pregnancy. Weigh untreated illness — tie to CANMAT perinatal guidance.
lactation: Low relative infant dose; monitor infant.
geriatric: Watch hyponatremia, BP, falls; renal/hepatic caution.
renalHepatic: Avoid in hepatic impairment and significant alcohol use (hepatotoxicity); avoid in severe renal impairment (CrCl <30).
pearls: The SNRI with strong pain indications — diabetic neuropathy, fibromyalgia, chronic musculoskeletal pain (analgesia is partly noradrenergic and can precede mood benefit).
Hepatotoxicity — avoid in liver disease or substantial alcohol use.
CYP1A2 substrate: smoking lowers levels; fluvoxamine (potent 1A2 inhibitor) dangerously raises them.
mnemonics: "Duloxetine does DOLOR (pain)" — dual reuptake for neuropathy/fibromyalgia; "Don't Use in Liver."
confusables: Duloxetine vs venlafaxine — duloxetine has the pain indications and a hepatotoxicity/CYP1A2 profile; venlafaxine is BP/discontinuation-forward.
classDifferentiators: Balanced SNRI with formal chronic-pain indications; hepatotoxicity caution; CYP1A2 + 2D6 involvement.
examHooks: SNRI of choice when depression coexists with diabetic neuropathy or fibromyalgia? (duloxetine)
Which SNRI is avoided in hepatic impairment / heavy alcohol use? (duloxetine)
counsellingPoints: Swallow the capsule whole.
Avoid heavy alcohol; report yellowing of skin/eyes.
Pain relief can appear before mood improves.

vignettes.stem: A 58-year-old with major depression also has painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Which single antidepressant best addresses both?
vignettes.explanation: Duloxetine is an SNRI approved for both depression and diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain (and fibromyalgia); its noradrenergic action underlies the analgesic effect.
vignettes.testedFact: Duloxetine dual depression + neuropathic pain indication

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adult patients in short-term studies. Escitalopram is not approved for use in pediatric patients less than 7 years of age [ see Use in Specific Populations (8.4) ] . contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Escitalopram is contraindicated in patients: taking MAOIs with escitalopram or within 14 days of stopping treatment with escitalopram because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome. The use of escitalopram within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders is also contraindicated [ see Dosage and Administration (2.7) and Warnings and Precautions (5.2) ]. Starting escitalopram in a patient who is being treated with MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue is also contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome [ see Dosage and Administration (2.6) and Warnings and Precautions (5.2) ]. taking pimozide [ see Drug Interactions (7) ] with a hypersensitivity to escitalopram or citalopram or any of the inactive ingredients in escitalopram. Do not use MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with escitalopram or within 14 days of stopping treatment with escitalopram. Do not use escitalopram within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders. In addition, do not start escitalopram in a patient who is being treated with linezolid or intravenous methylene blue ( 4 ) Concomitant use of pimozide ( 4 ) Known hypersensitivity to escitalopram or citalopram or any of the inactive ingredients ( 4 )

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE The following have been reported with escitalopram tablet overdosage: Seizures, which may be delayed, and altered mental status including coma. Cardiovascular toxicity, which may be delayed, including QRS and QTc interval prolongation, wide complex tachyarrhythmias, and torsade de pointes. Hypertension most commonly seen, but rarely can see hypotension alone or with co-ingestants including alcohol. Serotonin syndrome (patients with a multiple drug overdosage with other proserotonergic drugs may have a higher risk). Prolonged cardiac monitoring is recommended in escitalopram overdosage ingestions due to the arrhythmia risk. Gastrointestinal decontamination with activated …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (5 MG, 20 MG, 10 MG, 15 MG, 2 MG)
Tablet (Orally-Disintegrating) (5 MG, 20 MG, 10 MG, 15 MG, 2 MG)
moa: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor; the active S-enantiomer of citalopram (allosteric SERT binding).
receptorProfile: High, selective SERT affinity; cleanest off-target profile of the SSRIs.
onsetOfEffect: 1–2 wk early; 4–6 wk full.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP2C19 (and 3A4/2D6); minimal CYP inhibition — few interactions.
foodEffect: With or without food.
approvedPopulations: Adults; adolescents for MDD in some labels.
startingDose: 10 mg PO once daily.
titration: Increase to 20 mg/day after ≥1 week if needed.
maxDose: 20 mg/day (10 mg/day in elderly/hepatic impairment).
frequencyTiming: Once daily.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper to limit discontinuation.
administrationNotes: Often first-line for its tolerability and clean interactions.
boxedWarning: WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adult patients in short-term studies. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening, and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors [ see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ]. Escitalopram is not approved for use in pediatric patients less than 7 years of age [ see Use in Specific Populations (8.4) ] . WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning Increased risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in pediatric and young adult patients taking antidepressants. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening and emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors ( 5.1 ). Escitalopram is not approved for use in pediatric patients less than 7 years of age ( …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Escitalopram is contraindicated in patients: taking MAOIs with escitalopram or within 14 days of stopping treatment with escitalopram because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome. The use of escitalopram within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders is also contraindicated [ see Dosage and Administration (2.7) and Warnings and Precautions (5.2) ]. Starting escitalopram in a patient who is being treated with MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue is also contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome [ see Dosage and Administration (2.6) and Warnings and Precautions (5.2) ]. taking pimozide [ see Drug Interactions (7) ] with a hypersensitivity to escitalopram or citalopram or any of the inactive ingredients in escitalopram. Do not use MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with escitalopram …
overdoseLethality: Relatively safe in overdose; QT less of a concern than citalopram.
discontinuationSyndrome: Mild–moderate; taper.
ongoingMonitoring: Mood/suicidality; sodium if symptomatic; ECG only if QT risk.
majorDDI: Other QT drugs (additive, less than citalopram)
pregnancy: No consistent major teratogenic signal; a reasonable choice — weigh untreated illness. Tie to CANMAT perinatal 2024.
lactation: Acceptable; monitor infant.
geriatric: Max 10 mg/day in elderly/hepatic; well tolerated.
cardiac: Some QT effect but less than citalopram.
pearls: Often first-line: best-tolerated, cleanest interactions.
S-enantiomer of citalopram; 20 mg escitalopram ≈ 40 mg citalopram.
CANMAT first-line for MDD and approved for GAD.
mnemonics: "Escitalopram = the clean one" — few interactions, first-line.
confusables: Escitalopram vs citalopram — half the max dose (20 vs 40 mg).
classDifferentiators: Tolerability/interaction sweet spot; GAD approval.
examHooks: First-line, best-tolerated SSRI with GAD approval? (escitalopram)
counsellingPoints: Once daily.
Effect over 4–6 weeks.
Report palpitations.

vignettes.stem: A patient on high-dose omeprazole needs an SSRI with minimal CYP-mediated interactions and both MDD and GAD approval. Which is the best fit?
vignettes.explanation: Escitalopram has minimal CYP inhibition and clean interactions and is approved for both MDD and GAD; fluvoxamine/paroxetine/fluoxetine are stronger CYP inhibitors.

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Management of Overdosage There is no specific antidote for esketamine overdose. In the case of overdose, the possibility of multiple drug involvement should be considered. Contact a Certified Poison Control Center for the most up to date information on the management of overdosage (1-800-222-1222 or www.poison.org).

Closely monitor for sedation with concomitant use of SPRAVATO with CNS depressants. 7.2 Psychostimulants Concomitant use with psychostimulants (e.g., amphetamines, methylphenidate, modafinil, armodafinil) may increase blood pressure [see Warnings and Precautions (5.7) ] . pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Pregnancy Exposure Registry There is a pregnancy exposure registry that monitors pregnancy outcomes in women exposed to antidepressants, including SPRAVATO, during pregnancy. Healthcare providers are encouraged to register patients by contacting the National Pregnancy Registry for Antidepressants at 1-844-405-6185 or online at https://womensmentalhealth.org/clinical-and-research-programs/pregnancyregistry/antidepressants/ . Risk Summary SPRAVATO is not recommended during pregnancy. There are insufficient data on SPRAVATO use in pregnant women to draw conclusions about any drug-associated risk of major birth defects, miscarriage, or adverse maternal or fetal outcomes. moa: Non-competitive NMDA-glutamate receptor antagonist (S-enantiomer of ketamine). NMDA blockade on GABAergic interneurons disinhibits a glutamate surge → AMPA-receptor activation and rapid synaptogenesis (BDNF/mTOR signalling), producing a fast antidepressant effect.
receptorProfile: NMDA-receptor antagonist (higher NMDA affinity than R-ketamine); downstream AMPA/BDNF/mTOR signalling.
onsetOfEffect: Rapid — antidepressant and anti-suicidal effect within hours to ~24 h, versus 2–6 wk for classic monoaminergic antidepressants. The defining selling point.
halfLife: Terminal ~7–12 h (biphasic; rapid initial decline over 2–4 h). Active metabolite noresketamine terminal ~8 h.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP2B6 and CYP3A4 (minor CYP2C9/2C19) → noresketamine (active). Not a clinically important CYP inhibitor.
elimination: Metabolites excreted predominantly in urine; <1% unchanged.
foodEffect: Intranasal; avoid food for ~2 h and liquids for ~30 min before dosing to limit nausea/vomiting.
approvedCanada: Treatment-resistant major depressive disorder (with an oral antidepressant)
MDD with acute suicidal ideation or behaviour (with an oral antidepressant)
approvedPopulations: Adults; always used in conjunction with a newly initiated or ongoing oral antidepressant.
startingDose: Intranasal. Day 1: 56 mg. Induction (wk 1–4): 56–84 mg twice weekly (MDSI: 84 mg twice weekly). Each device = 28 mg; wait 5 min between devices.
maxDose: 84 mg per treatment session.
frequencyTiming: Twice weekly (induction) → weekly → every 1–2 weeks (maintenance). Self-administered under direct clinician supervision.
administrationNotes: Dispensed and given only in a certified healthcare setting under a REMS-type program — never for home use. Monitor ≥2 h after each dose (sedation, dissociation, BP). No driving until the next day after restful sleep.

Consider the risks and benefits of prescribing SPRAVATO prior to using in patients at higher risk of abuse. Monitor patients for signs and symptoms of abuse and misuse. ( 5.4 ) SPRAVATO is only available through a restricted program called the SPRAVATO REMS. ( 5.5 ) Increased risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adult patients taking antidepressants. Closely monitor all …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS SPRAVATO is contraindicated in patients with: Aneurysmal vascular disease (including thoracic and abdominal aorta, intracranial, and peripheral arterial vessels) or arteriovenous malformation [see Warnings and Precautions (5.7) ] History of intracerebral hemorrhage [see Warnings and Precautions (5.7) ] Hypersensitivity to esketamine, ketamine, or any of the excipients. Aneurysmal vascular disease (including thoracic and abdominal aorta, intracranial and peripheral arterial vessels) or arteriovenous malformation. ( 4 ) Intracerebral hemorrhage. ( 4 ) Hypersensitivity to esketamine, ketamine, or any of the excipients. ( 4 )
overdoseLethality: Limited nasal-route data; principal acute risks are excessive sedation/airway compromise and marked BP rise, amplified by other CNS depressants. Supervised administration mitigates risk.
baselineWorkup: Baseline blood pressure (defer dosing if uncontrolled hypertension; caution with aneurysmal vascular disease / AVM / prior intracerebral haemorrhage)
ongoingMonitoring: Assess BP before dosing, ~40 min after, then as clinically indicated; observe ≥2 h for sedation/dissociation; monitor mood and suicidality.
majorDDI: CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, opioids, alcohol) — additive sedation / respiratory depression
Psychostimulants (amphetamines, methylphenidate, modafinil) — additive BP increase
pregnancy: Not recommended; may cause fetal harm (ketamine class — neurodevelopmental / animal-toxicity concerns). Consider the pregnancy exposure registry.
lactation: Ketamine is present in human milk; breastfeeding not recommended during treatment.
cardiac: Transient BP surge peaks ~40 min post-dose; caution with cardiovascular / cerebrovascular disease.
pearls: Rapid onset (hours–1 day) is the whole point — a sharp contrast with the 2–6 wk lag of SSRIs/SNRIs; used for treatment-resistant depression and MDD with acute suicidality.
Must be given in-clinic under a REMS-type program with ≥2 h monitoring for sedation, dissociation and BP rise — never dispensed for home use.
Intranasal S-enantiomer of ketamine; a controlled substance with abuse potential — always paired with an oral antidepressant.
mnemonics: "Spravato = the four S": Supervised, Sedation, dISSociation, Systolic BP↑ — the monitoring themes.
confusables: Esketamine (intranasal, S-enantiomer, NMDA antagonist) vs racemic ketamine IV (off-label, anaesthetic origin).
Esketamine vs escitalopram — similar "es-" prefix, entirely different drug and class.
classDifferentiators: The only glutamatergic (NMDA-antagonist) antidepressant here — rapid-acting, dissociative, controlled, requiring supervised in-clinic dosing, unlike oral monoaminergic agents.
examHooks: Which antidepressant works within hours and requires ≥2 h in-clinic monitoring for dissociation/sedation/BP? (esketamine)
Mechanism of esketamine? (NMDA-receptor antagonist → glutamate/AMPA/BDNF surge)
counsellingPoints: You will stay in the clinic for at least 2 hours after each dose.
Do not eat for ~2 hours or drink for ~30 minutes before dosing (reduces nausea).
You cannot drive until the next day, after a full night’s sleep.
Keep taking your oral antidepressant — this is added on top of it.

vignettes.stem: A 43-year-old with treatment-resistant MDD (three adequate antidepressant trials failed) receives his first intranasal esketamine dose augmenting his oral antidepressant. Forty minutes later he feels "detached from his body" and his BP is 168/98. What does this represent?
options: Expected transient dissociation and BP rise — continue in-clinic monitoring
vignettes.answer: Expected transient dissociation and BP rise — continue in-clinic monitoring
vignettes.explanation: Dissociation and a transient BP rise peaking ~40 min are known, self-limited esketamine effects; they are managed by observation (≥2 h) rather than emergency treatment unless severe or sustained. This supervised monitoring is exactly why esketamine is REMS-restricted.
vignettes.testedFact: Esketamine dissociation + transient BP rise are expected, monitored effects

source.name: openFDA/DailyMed Spravato label — warnings (dissociation, blood pressure)

vignettes.stem: A patient with MDD and acute suicidal ideation needs an antidepressant that reduces symptoms within 24 hours rather than weeks. Which agent’s mechanism explains this rapid effect?
vignettes.explanation: Esketamine antagonises NMDA receptors, triggering a rapid glutamate/AMPA/BDNF/mTOR cascade and synaptogenesis, giving benefit within hours to a day — unlike monoaminergic antidepressants, which take 2–6 weeks.
vignettes.testedFact: Rapid onset of esketamine vs weeks for monoaminergic antidepressants

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies. These studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior with antidepressant use in patients over age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressant use in patients aged 65 and older [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ] . In patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy, monitor closely for worsening and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Advise families and caregivers of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ] . Fluoxetine is not approved for use in children less than 7 years of age [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) and Use in Specific Populations (8.4) ] . When using fluoxetine and olanzapine in combination, also refer to Boxed Warning section of the package insert for Symbyax. WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS When using fluoxetine capsules and olanzapine in combination, also refer to the Contraindications section of the package insert for Symbyax. Serotonin Syndrome and MAOIs: Do not use MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with fluoxetine or within 5 weeks of stopping treatment with fluoxetine. Do not use fluoxetine within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders. In addition, do not start fluoxetine in a patient who is being treated with linezolid or intravenous methylene blue ( 4.1 ) Pimozide: Do not use. Risk of QT prolongation and drug interaction ( 4.2 , 5.11 , 7.7 , 7.8 ) Thioridazine: Do not use. Risk of QT interval prolongation and elevated thioridazine plasma levels. Do not use thioridazine within 5 weeks of discontinuing fluoxetine. Do not use thioridazine within 5 weeks of discontinuing fluoxetine ( 4.2 , 5.11 , 7.7 , 7.8 ) When using fluoxetine and olanzapine in combination, also refer to the Contraindications section of the package insert for Symbyax ( 4 ) 4.1 Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors (MAOIs) The use of MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with fluoxetine or within 5 weeks of stopping treatment with fluoxetine is contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome. overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE 10.1 Human Experience Worldwide exposure to fluoxetine hydrochloride is estimated to be over 38 million patients (circa 1999). Of the 1578 cases of overdose involving fluoxetine hydrochloride, alone or with other drugs, reported from this population, there were 195 deaths. Among 633 adult patients who overdosed on fluoxetine hydrochloride alone, 34 resulted in a fatal outcome, 378 completely recovered, and 15 patients experienced sequelae after overdosage, including abnormal accommodation, abnormal gait, confusion, unresponsiveness, nervousness, pulmonary dysfunction, vertigo, tremor, elevated blood pressure, impotence, movement disorder, and hypomania. The remaining 206 …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Pregnancy Category C — Fluoxetine should be used during pregnancy only if the potential benefit justifies the potential risk to the fetus. All pregnancies have a background risk of birth defects, loss, or other adverse outcome regardless of drug exposure. Treatment of Pregnant Women during the First Trimester — There are no adequate and well-controlled clinical studies on the use of fluoxetine in pregnant women. Results of a number of published epidemiological studies assessing the risk of fluoxetine exposure during the first trimester of pregnancy have demonstrated inconsistent results. More than 10 cohort studies and case-control studies failed to demonstrate an increased …

formulationsCanada: Capsule (20 MG, 10 MG, 40 MG, 8 MG, 16 MG, 32 MG, 64 MG, 60 MG)
Liquid (20 MG, 10 MG, 40 MG, 8 MG, 16 MG, 32 MG, 64 MG, 60 MG)
moa: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (blocks SERT), increasing synaptic 5-HT.
receptorProfile: High SERT affinity; some 5-HT2C antagonism (mildly activating). Minimal H1/M1/α1.
onsetOfEffect: Emotional/somatic symptoms 1–2 wk; full antidepressant effect 4–6 wk.
halfLife: Fluoxetine 1–4 days; active metabolite norfluoxetine 7–15 days (longest of the SSRIs).
metabolism: Hepatic CYP2D6 (also 2C9/2C19/3A4). Potent CYP2D6 inhibitor; moderate 2C19/3A4 inhibitor.
foodEffect: May be taken with or without food.
approvedPopulations: Adults; pediatric use for MDD/OCD in some jurisdictions.
startingDose: 20 mg PO once daily (usually AM due to activation).
titration: May increase after several weeks; usual 20–60 mg/day.
maxDose: 80 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once daily, morning (activating).
startStopCrossTaper: Self-tapering due to long half-life — discontinuation syndrome rare. Long washout needed before starting an MAOI (5 weeks).
administrationNotes: Long half-life is an advantage for non-adherent patients.
boxedWarning: WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies. These studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior with antidepressant use in patients over age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressant use in patients aged 65 and older [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ] . In patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy, monitor closely for worsening and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Advise families and caregivers of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ] . Fluoxetine is not approved for use in children less than 7 years of age [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) and Use in Specific Populations (8.4) ] . When using fluoxetine …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS When using fluoxetine capsules and olanzapine in combination, also refer to the Contraindications section of the package insert for Symbyax. Serotonin Syndrome and MAOIs: Do not use MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with fluoxetine or within 5 weeks of stopping treatment with fluoxetine. Do not use fluoxetine within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders. In addition, do not start fluoxetine in a patient who is being treated with linezolid or intravenous methylene blue ( 4.1 ) Pimozide: Do not use. Risk of QT prolongation and drug interaction ( 4.2 , 5.11 , 7.7 , 7.8 ) Thioridazine: Do not use. Risk of QT interval prolongation and elevated thioridazine plasma levels. Do not use thioridazine within 5 weeks of discontinuing fluoxetine. Do not use thioridazine within 5 weeks of discontinuing fluoxetine ( 4.2 , 5.11 , 7.7 , 7.8 ) …
overdoseLethality: Relatively safe in overdose compared with TCAs/MAOIs; seizures and serotonin toxicity possible in large ingestions or combinations.
discontinuationSyndrome: Uncommon because of the long half-life — effectively self-tapering.
ongoingMonitoring: Monitor for clinical worsening/suicidality (esp. first weeks and after dose changes); serum sodium if symptoms of hyponatremia.
majorDDI: MAOIs (contraindicated — serotonin syndrome; 5-wk washout after fluoxetine)
CYP2D6 substrates (e.g., TCAs, metoprolol, atomoxetine, risperidone) — levels rise
contraindicatedCombos: MAOIs (incl. within 14 days of stopping an MAOI; 5 weeks after stopping fluoxetine)
maoiWashout: Stop MAOI ≥14 days before fluoxetine; stop fluoxetine ≥5 weeks before an MAOI (long half-life).
pregnancy: Generally considered compatible; weigh untreated depression risk. Long half-life and secretion into milk are considerations. Tie to CANMAT perinatal 2024.
lactation: Excreted in breast milk more than sertraline; sertraline/citalopram often preferred if starting in lactation.
geriatric: Long half-life and CYP2D6 inhibition raise interaction/accumulation risk; watch hyponatremia.
pearls: Longest half-life SSRI → best for non-adherent patients and least discontinuation syndrome.
Most activating SSRI — dose in the morning; can worsen initial anxiety/insomnia.
Potent CYP2D6 inhibitor — a classic source of interactions.
mnemonics: "Fluoxetine = the long one" — 5-week MAOI washout because norfluoxetine lingers.
confusables: Fluoxetine vs fluvoxamine vs fluphenazine — all "flu-" but different classes.
classDifferentiators: The activating, longest-half-life SSRI; strong 2D6 inhibitor.
examHooks: Which SSRI needs a 5-week washout before an MAOI? (fluoxetine)
counsellingPoints: Take in the morning.
Full effect takes 4–6 weeks.
Do not combine with MAOIs.

vignettes.stem: A 24-year-old started on an SSRI 3 months ago must switch to phenelzine (an MAOI). Compared with other SSRIs, which requires the LONGEST washout before starting the MAOI?
vignettes.explanation: Fluoxetine and its active metabolite norfluoxetine have very long half-lives, requiring ~5 weeks off fluoxetine before starting an MAOI (vs ~2 weeks for other SSRIs).

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: SUICIDALITY AND ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS Antidepressants increased the risk compared to placebo of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies of major depressive disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Anyone considering the use of fluvoxamine maleate extended-release capsules or any other antidepressant in a child, adolescent, or young adult must balance this risk with the clinical need. Short-term studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidality with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults beyond age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults aged 65 and older. Depression and certain other psychiatric disorders are themselves associated with increases in the risk of suicide. Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored appropriately and observed closely for clinical worsening, suicidality, or unusual changes in behavior. Families and caregivers should be advised of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber. ( See WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS-Clinical Worsening and Suicide Risk [ 5.1 ] and USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS-Pediatric Use [ 8.4 ].) overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE 10.1 Human Experience Exposure to immediate-release fluvoxamine maleate tablets includes over 45,000 patients treated in clinical trials and an estimated exposure of 50,000,000 patients treated during worldwide marketing experience (end of 2005). Of the 539 cases of deliberate or accidental overdose involving fluvoxamine reported from this population, there were 55 deaths. Of these, 9 were in patients thought to be taking immediate-release fluvoxamine tablets alone and the remaining 46 were in patients taking fluvoxamine along with other drugs. Among non-fatal overdose cases, 404 patients recovered completely. Five patients experienced adverse sequelae of overdosage, to …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Teratogenic Effects: When pregnant rats were given daily doses of fluvoxamine (60 mg/kg, 120 mg/kg, or 240 mg/kg) orally throughout the period of organogenesis, developmental toxicity in the form of increased embryofetal death and increased incidences of fetal eye abnormalities (folded retinas) was observed at doses of 120 mg/kg or greater. Decreased fetal body weight was seen at the high dose. The no effect dose for developmental toxicity in this study was 60 mg/kg (approximately 2 time the maximum recommended human dose [MRHD] on a mg/m 2 basis). moa: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor; also a sigma-1 receptor agonist.
receptorProfile: High SERT affinity; sigma-1 agonism; minimal H1/M1/α1.

metabolism: Hepatic; POTENT CYP1A2 and CYP2C19 inhibitor (also 3A4/2D6) — the most interaction-prone SSRI.

startingDose: 50 mg PO at bedtime (sedating).
titration: Increase by 50 mg every 4–7 days; doses >100 mg often split BID.

startStopCrossTaper: Taper — short half-life gives notable discontinuation symptoms.
administrationNotes: Strong choice for OCD; interaction profile is the main drawback.

overdoseLethality: Relatively safe alone; danger is largely via interactions (e.g., elevating clozapine/theophylline levels).
discontinuationSyndrome: Moderate — taper.
ongoingMonitoring: Mood/suicidality; if combined with CYP1A2 substrates (clozapine, theophylline), monitor those levels closely; sodium if symptomatic.
majorDDI: CYP1A2 substrates: clozapine, olanzapine, theophylline, caffeine, tizanidine (levels rise sharply)
pregnancy: Data more limited than sertraline/citalopram; weigh untreated illness. Tie to CANMAT perinatal 2024.
lactation: Used; monitor infant.
geriatric: Interaction burden and sedation — use care.
pearls: Most drug interactions of the SSRIs — potent CYP1A2 & 2C19 inhibitor (raises clozapine, theophylline, caffeine, tizanidine).
A go-to for OCD.
Sigma-1 agonism is a distinctive feature.
mnemonics: "FluvoxaMINE mines the CYPs" — biggest interaction footprint; think clozapine/theophylline levels up.
confusables: Fluvoxamine vs fluoxetine — fluvoxamine = 1A2/2C19 (OCD), fluoxetine = 2D6, long half-life.
classDifferentiators: Highest interaction burden; OCD-oriented; sedating; short half-life.
examHooks: Which SSRI dangerously raises clozapine and theophylline levels? (fluvoxamine — CYP1A2)
counsellingPoints: Take at bedtime.
Tell prescribers about all medications (interactions).
Taper when stopping.

vignettes.stem: A patient on clozapine is started on an SSRI for OCD and develops sedation, hypersalivation and a rising clozapine level. Which SSRI most likely caused this?
vignettes.explanation: Fluvoxamine is a potent CYP1A2 inhibitor; clozapine is a CYP1A2 substrate, so fluvoxamine markedly raises clozapine levels — a classic dangerous interaction.

boxedWarning.sourceText: Suicidality and Antidepressant Drugs Antidepressants increased the risk compared to placebo of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies of major depressive disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Anyone considering the use of imipramine hydrochloride or any other antidepressant in a child, adolescent, or young adult must balance this risk with the clinical need. Short-term studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidality with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults beyond age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults aged 65 and older. Depression and certain other psychiatric disorders are themselves associated with increases in the risk of suicide. Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored appropriately and observed closely for clinical worsening, suicidality, or unusual changes in behavior. Families and caregivers should be advised of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber. Imipramine hydrochloride is not approved for use in pediatric patients. (See WARNINGS: Clinical Worsening and Suicide Risk , PRECAUTIONS: Information for Patients , and PRECAUTIONS: Pediatric Use )

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS The concomitant use of monoamine oxidase inhibiting compounds is contraindicated. Hyperpyretic crises or severe convulsive seizures may occur in patients receiving such combinations. The potentiation of adverse effects can be serious, or even fatal. When it is desired to substitute imipramine hydrochloride in patients receiving a monoamine oxidase inhibitor, as long an interval should elapse as the clinical situation will allow, with a minimum of 14 days. Initial dosage should be low and increases should be gradual and cautiously prescribed. The drug is contraindicated during the acute recovery period after a myocardial infarction. Patients with a known hypersensitivity to this compound should not be given the drug. The possibility of cross-sensitivity to other dibenzazepine compounds should be kept in mind.

Lower dosages are also recommended for outpatients as compared to hospitalized patients who will be under close supervision. Dosage should be initiated at a low level and increased gradually, noting carefully the clinical response and any evidence of intolerance. Following remission, maintenance medication may be required for a longer period of time, at the lowest dose that will maintain remission. seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Clinical Worsening and Suicide Risk Patients with major depressive disorder (MDD), both adult and pediatric, may experience worsening of their depression and/or the emergence of suicidal ideation and behavior (suicidality) or unusual changes in behavior, whether or not they are taking antidepressant medications, and this risk may persist until significant remission occurs. Suicide is a known risk of depression and certain other psychiatric disorders, and these disorders themselves are the strongest predictors of suicide. There has been a long-standing concern, however, that antidepressants may have a role in inducing worsening of depression and the emergence of suicidality in …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Deaths may occur from overdosage with this class of drugs. Multiple drug ingestion (including alcohol) is common in deliberate tricyclic overdose. As the management is complex and changing, it is recommended that the physician contact a poison control center for current information on treatment. Signs and symptoms of toxicity develop rapidly after tricyclic overdose. Therefore, hospital monitoring is required as soon as possible. Children have been reported to be more sensitive than adults to an acute overdosage of imipramine hydrochloride. An acute overdose of any amount in infants or young children, especially, must be considered serious and potentially fatal. Manifestations …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions Drugs Metabolized by P450 2D6 : The biochemical activity of the drug metabolizing isozyme cytochrome P450 2D6 (debrisoquin hydroxylase) is reduced in a subset of the Caucasian population (about 7% to 10% of Caucasians are so-called "poor metabolizers"); reliable estimates of the prevalence of reduced P450 2D6 isozyme activity among Asian, African, and other populations are not yet available. Poor metabolizers have higher than expected plasma concentrations of tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) when given usual doses. Depending on the fraction of drug metabolized by P450 2D6, the increase in plasma concentration may be small, or quite large (8-fold increase in plasma AUC of …

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy Animal reproduction studies have yielded inconclusive results (see also ANIMAL PHARMACOLOGY & TOXICOLOGY .) There have been no well-controlled studies conducted with pregnant women to determine the effect of imipramine hydrochloride on the fetus. However, there have been clinical reports of congenital malformations associated with the use of the drug. Although a causal relationship between these effects and the drug could not be established, the possibility of fetal risk from the maternal ingestion of imipramine hydrochloride cannot be excluded. Therefore, imipramine hydrochloride should be used in women who are or might become pregnant only if the clinical condition clearly …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (25 MG, 50 MG, 10 MG, 100 MG, 75 MG, 12.5 MG)
moa: Tertiary-amine tricyclic (the prototype TCA); blocks NE and 5-HT reuptake. H1/M1/α1 antagonist.
receptorProfile: Balanced NE/5-HT reuptake block with significant anticholinergic/antihistaminic/α1 effects; Na-channel block in overdose.
onsetOfEffect: Mood 2–4 wk; antienuretic effect within days.
metabolism: Hepatic — demethylated to desipramine (active). CYP2D6 and CYP2C19/1A2/3A4 substrate.
startingDose: 25–75 mg PO daily for depression; enuresis 25 mg qhs (age-adjusted).
titration: Increase gradually; give larger portion at bedtime.
maxDose: 200 mg/day outpatient (up to 300 mg/day inpatient); enuresis usually ≤50–75 mg qhs.
frequencyTiming: Once daily (bedtime) or divided.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper to avoid cholinergic rebound. 14-day MAOI washout each way.

seriousAE: Cardiac conduction delay / arrhythmia (wide QRS, QT)
overdoseLethality: HIGH — lethal in overdose (3 C's: cardiotoxicity, convulsions, coma) plus anticholinergic toxicity; particular caution because it is prescribed to children for enuresis (accidental pediatric ingestion). IV sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms.
discontinuationSyndrome: Cholinergic rebound — taper.
baselineWorkup: ECG (baseline, especially age >40, cardiac disease, or before pediatric enuresis dosing)
ongoingMonitoring: Mood/suicidality; anticholinergic/orthostatic effects; ECG with cardiac risk or higher doses.
majorDDI: CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, bupropion) raise levels
contraindicatedCombos: Recent acute MI / significant conduction disease
pregnancy: Long experience; weigh untreated illness. Tie to CANMAT perinatal 2024.
lactation: Excreted in small amounts; monitor infant.
geriatric: Anticholinergic/orthostatic — prefer a secondary amine (desipramine/nortriptyline) if a TCA is required.
cardiac: Na-channel block; ECG and caution in conduction disease; classic caution with pediatric enuresis dosing.
pearls: Prototype TCA; classic agents for childhood nocturnal enuresis and for panic disorder.
Demethylated to desipramine (active) — a tertiary→secondary amine pair mirroring amitriptyline→nortriptyline.
Overdose lethality is especially concerning when tablets are in a household with children (enuresis indication).
mnemonics: "imIPramine → Incontinence (Pediatric)": the enuresis TCA.
confusables: Imipramine (tertiary) vs desipramine (its secondary-amine, most noradrenergic metabolite).
classDifferentiators: Prototype tertiary-amine TCA with enuresis and panic indications; parent of desipramine.
examHooks: Which TCA is classically used for childhood nocturnal enuresis? (imipramine)
counsellingPoints: For enuresis, dose at bedtime and secure tablets from children.
Rise slowly (dizziness).
Effect on mood takes weeks.
Overdose is dangerous.

vignettes.stem: A 9-year-old with refractory nocturnal enuresis (after alarms and desmopressin) may be offered a tricyclic. Which TCA is the classic choice, and what must be counselled about supply safety?
vignettes.explanation: Imipramine is the classic TCA for nocturnal enuresis. Because TCA overdose is lethal (cardiotoxicity, seizures, coma) and the drug is used in a household with a child, families must secure the tablets.

The use of FETZIMA within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders is also contraindicated [see Dosage and Administration ( 2.5 ) and Warnings and Precautions ( 5.2 )] . Starting FETZIMA in a patient who is being treated with MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue is also contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome [see Dosage and Administration ( 2.6 ) and Warnings and Precautions ( 5.2 )] . Hypersensitivity to levomilnacipran, milnacipran HCl, or any excipient in the FETZIMA formulation ( 4 ). Do not use MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with FETZIMA or within 7 days of stopping treatment with FETZIMA. Do not use FETZIMA within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders. overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE 10.1 Human Experience There is limited clinical experience with FETZIMA overdose in humans. In clinical studies, cases of ingestions up to 360 mg daily were reported with none being fatal. 10.2 Management of Overdose No specific antidotes for FETZIMA are known. In managing overdose, provide supportive care, including close medical supervision and monitoring, and consider the possibility of multiple drug involvement. In case of an overdose, consult a Certified Poison Control Center (1-800-222-1222) for up-to-date guidance and advice. The high volume of distribution of levomilnacipran suggests that dialysis will not be effective in reducing levomilnacipran plasma concentrations.

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Pregnancy Exposure Registry There is a pregnancy exposure registry that monitors pregnancy outcomes in women exposed to antidepressants during pregnancy. Healthcare providers are encouraged to advise patients to register by calling the National Pregnancy Registry for Antidepressants at 1-844-405-6185 or visiting online at https://womensmentalhealth.org/research/pregnancyregistry/antidepressants . Risk Summary Based on data from published observational studies, exposure to SNRIs, particularly in the month before delivery, has been associated with a less than 2-fold increase in the risk of postpartum hemorrhage [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.5 ) and Clinical Considerations] . …

moa: Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor; the active enantiomer of milnacipran. Relatively MORE noradrenergic — greater potency at NET than SERT (the reverse of most SNRIs).
receptorProfile: NET > SERT selectivity; negligible off-target receptor activity.
onsetOfEffect: 1–2 wk early; 4–6 wk full effect.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 (major); not a significant CYP inhibitor.
elimination: Renal (~58% excreted unchanged); dose-cap in renal impairment.
foodEffect: May be taken with or without food; swallow ER capsule whole.
startingDose: 20 mg PO once daily for 2 days, then 40 mg once daily (ER).
titration: Increase by 40 mg at ≥2-day intervals as tolerated; usual range 40–120 mg/day.
maxDose: 120 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once daily; ER capsule swallowed whole.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Renal dose caps — max 80 mg/day (moderate) and 40 mg/day (severe renal impairment). No adjustment for hepatic impairment.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper to limit discontinuation.
administrationNotes: Assess for urinary hesitancy (noradrenergic) before/during use.

Hypersensitivity to levomilnacipran, milnacipran HCl, or any excipient in the …
overdoseLethality: Limited data; expect SNRI-type toxicity (serotonin/noradrenergic effects, seizures).
discontinuationSyndrome: Taper — SNRI-class discontinuation.
ongoingMonitoring: Blood pressure and heart rate; urinary symptoms; mood/suicidality.
majorDDI: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g., ketoconazole) raise levels — cap dose
pregnancy: Limited data; weigh untreated illness. Possible neonatal adaptation late in pregnancy.
lactation: Limited data; monitor infant.
geriatric: Watch BP/HR, urinary retention; renal dose caps.
cardiac: More noradrenergic — expect the greatest heart-rate/BP effect of the SNRIs; monitor.
pearls: The MOST noradrenergic SNRI (NET > SERT) — so expect more tachycardia, BP rise and urinary hesitancy than serotonergic-leaning SNRIs.
Active enantiomer of milnacipran; CYP3A4-metabolized (cap dose with strong 3A4 inhibitors and in renal impairment).
Twice-daily 20 mg for the first 2 days is a deliberate tolerability step before once-daily 40 mg.
mnemonics: "Levomilnacipran = Levo/NE-heavy" — the norepinephrine-leaning SNRI (racing heart, urinary hesitancy).
confusables: Levomilnacipran (MDD) vs milnacipran (marketed for fibromyalgia) — enantiomer vs racemate.
classDifferentiators: The noradrenergic-predominant SNRI; CYP3A4 substrate with renal dose caps.
examHooks: Which SNRI is the most noradrenergic (NET > SERT)? (levomilnacipran)
counsellingPoints: Swallow the capsule whole.
Report a racing heart, trouble urinating, or high blood-pressure readings.
Do not stop abruptly.

vignettes.stem: A patient started on an SNRI develops notable tachycardia and urinary hesitancy, effects reflecting predominant norepinephrine reuptake inhibition. Which agent is most consistent with this?
vignettes.explanation: Levomilnacipran is the most noradrenergic SNRI (greater NET than SERT potency), so noradrenergic effects such as tachycardia, raised BP and urinary hesitancy are more prominent.

moa: Tetracyclic antidepressant; selective noradrenergic (NET) reuptake inhibitor with strong H1 and moderate M1/α1 blockade — pharmacologically TCA-like.
receptorProfile: Selective NE reuptake block; sedating/anticholinergic; notably lowers the seizure threshold. Na-channel block in overdose.
onsetOfEffect: Mood 2–4 wk.
halfLife: Long — ~27–58 hours (~43 h); accumulation contributes to seizure/toxicity risk.
metabolism: Hepatic; CYP2D6 substrate — demethylmaprotiline metabolite.
startingDose: 25–75 mg PO daily.
titration: Increase slowly (long half-life → accumulation); keep at the low end of the range to limit seizure risk.
maxDose: 150 mg/day outpatient (up to 225 mg/day short-term specialist use) — seizure-risk limited.
frequencyTiming: Once daily (bedtime) or divided.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper. 14-day MAOI washout each way.
seriousAE: Cardiac conduction delay / arrhythmia (wide QRS, QT)
overdoseLethality: HIGH — lethal in overdose with cardiotoxicity (wide QRS, arrhythmia), coma and prominent seizures owing to its long half-life and strong seizure-lowering effect. IV sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms; benzodiazepines for seizures.
discontinuationSyndrome: Taper (long half-life softens but does not eliminate rebound).
baselineWorkup: ECG (baseline, especially age >40 or cardiac disease)
ongoingMonitoring: Seizure risk (avoid rapid titration/high doses); mood/suicidality; ECG if cardiac risk; skin rash.
majorDDI: CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, bupropion) raise levels
contraindicatedCombos: Recent acute MI / significant conduction disease
pregnancy: Weigh untreated illness. Tie to CANMAT perinatal 2024.
lactation: Excreted in milk; monitor infant.
geriatric: Long half-life → accumulation; sedating/anticholinergic and seizure-lowering — use cautiously.
cardiac: Na-channel block; ECG and caution in conduction disease.
pearls: A tetracyclic that is functionally a selective-noradrenergic TCA; its long half-life and strong seizure-lowering effect give it a notably HIGH seizure risk — titrate slowly and cap the dose.
Skin rash is a recognized, relatively common adverse effect.
mnemonics: "Maprotiline = More seizures" — tetracyclic with high seizure risk and a long half-life.
confusables: Maprotiline (tetracyclic, high seizure risk) vs mirtazapine (a NaSSA tetracyclic — very different pharmacology).
Maprotiline vs protriptyline — different classes, both activating/noradrenergic-flavoured.
classDifferentiators: Tetracyclic, selective-NE, long half-life, high seizure risk; TCA-like toxicity.
examHooks: Which tetracyclic antidepressant carries a notably high seizure risk (long half-life, NE-selective)? (maprotiline)
counsellingPoints: Report any seizure-like episodes.
Avoid rapid dose increases.
Watch for skin rash.
Overdose is dangerous — keep secured.

vignettes.stem: A tetracyclic antidepressant with a long half-life is being titrated for depression. Compared with other antidepressants, which adverse effect is it particularly known for?
vignettes.explanation: Maprotiline is a tetracyclic with a long half-life that markedly lowers the seizure threshold — seizures are a signature risk, especially with rapid titration, high doses, or overdose.

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Mirtazapine tablets are contraindicated in patients: • Taking, or within 14 days of stopping, MAOIs (including the MAOIs linezolid and intravenous methylene blue) because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome [see Warnings and Precautions (5.3) , Drug Interactions (7) ]. • With a known hypersensitivity to mirtazapine or to any of the excipients in mirtazapine tablets. Severe skin reactions, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome, bullous dermatitis, erythema multiforme and toxic epidermal necrolysis have been reported following the use of mirtazapine tablets [see Adverse Reactions 6.2 ]. • Concomitant use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) or use within 14 days of stopping MAOIs. ( 2.4 , 4 , 7 ) • Known hypersensitivity to mirtazapine or any of the excipients in mirtazapine tablets. ( 4 )

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Human Experience In premarketing clinical studies, there were reports of mirtazapine overdose alone or in combination with other pharmacological agents. Signs and symptoms reported in association with overdose included disorientation, drowsiness, impaired memory, and tachycardia. Based on postmarketing reports, serious outcomes (including fatalities) may occur at dosages higher than the recommended doses, especially with mixed overdoses. In these cases, QT prolongation and Torsades de Pointes have also been reported [see Warnings and Precautions (5.5) , Adverse Reactions (6.2) , and Drug Interactions (7) ]. Overdose Management No specific antidotes for mirtazapine are known. …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Reproduction studies in pregnant rats and rabbits at doses up to 100 mg/kg and 40 mg/kg, respectively [20 and 17 times the maximum recommended human dose (MRHD) on an mg/m 2 basis, respectively], have revealed no evidence of teratogenic effects. However, in rats, there was an increase in post-implantation losses in dams treated with mirtazapine. There was an increase in pup deaths during the first 3 days of lactation and a decrease in pup birth weights. The cause of these deaths is not known. The effects occurred at doses that were 20 times the MRHD, but not at 3 times the MRHD, on an mg/m 2 basis. There are no adequate and well-controlled studies in pregnant women. Because …

moa: Noradrenergic and specific serotonergic antidepressant (NaSSA): central presynaptic α2-adrenergic antagonist → increases NE and 5-HT release; additionally blocks postsynaptic 5-HT2A, 5-HT2C and 5-HT3 receptors and is a potent H1 antihistamine.
receptorProfile: α2 antagonist; 5-HT2A/2C and 5-HT3 antagonist; POTENT H1 antagonist (sedation/appetite). 5-HT3 block → antiemetic and less nausea/sexual dysfunction than SSRIs.
onsetOfEffect: Sleep/appetite benefit within days; 2–4 wk for mood; 4–6 wk full effect.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP2D6, CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 (substrate); not a significant CYP inhibitor.
foodEffect: May be taken with or without food; an orally disintegrating tablet is available.
offLabel: Antidepressant augmentation ("California rocket fuel" with venlafaxine)
startingDose: 15 mg PO at bedtime.
titration: Increase to 30–45 mg at ≥1–2-week intervals as needed.
maxDose: 45 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once daily at bedtime (sedation). Paradoxically, LOW doses can be MORE sedating (H1 effect dominates; higher doses add offsetting noradrenergic tone).
startStopCrossTaper: Taper to limit discontinuation; can be combined with an SSRI/SNRI for augmentation.
administrationNotes: Bedtime dosing exploits sedation for insomnia and appetite for weight loss/cachexia.
boxedWarning: WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adult patients in short-term studies. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening, and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ] . Mirtazapine tablets are not approved for use in pediatric patients [see Use in Specific Populations (8.4) ]. WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. Increased risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in pediatric and young adult patients taking antidepressants. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening and emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Mirtazapine tablets are not approved for use in pediatric patients. ( 5.1 , 8.4 )
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Mirtazapine tablets are contraindicated in patients: • Taking, or within 14 days of stopping, MAOIs (including the MAOIs linezolid and intravenous methylene blue) because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome [see Warnings and Precautions (5.3) , Drug Interactions (7) ]. • With a known hypersensitivity to mirtazapine or to any of the excipients in mirtazapine tablets. Severe skin reactions, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome, bullous dermatitis, erythema multiforme and toxic epidermal necrolysis have been reported following the use of mirtazapine tablets [see Adverse Reactions 6.2 ]. • Concomitant use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) or use within 14 days of stopping MAOIs. ( 2.4 , 4 , 7 ) • Known hypersensitivity to mirtazapine or any of the excipients in mirtazapine tablets. ( 4 )
overdoseLethality: Relatively safe in overdose alone (sedation the main effect); more dangerous with co-ingestants.
discontinuationSyndrome: Generally mild; taper.
baselineWorkup: Consider baseline lipids given metabolic effect
ongoingMonitoring: Weight and lipids; CBC if signs of infection (fever, sore throat — possible agranulocytosis); mood/suicidality.
pregnancy: Limited data; no strong teratogenic signal. Weigh untreated illness — sometimes used for hyperemesis/poor intake.
lactation: Low relative infant dose; monitor sedation/weight gain in infant.
geriatric: Useful for depression with insomnia/poor appetite/weight loss; watch sedation, falls and hyponatremia.
pearls: Sedation and appetite/weight gain are WORSE at LOW doses — the potent H1 antihistamine effect dominates until higher doses add offsetting noradrenergic drive.
Best for depression with insomnia, poor appetite or weight loss (cachexia); 5-HT3 blockade gives antiemetic effect and less nausea/sexual dysfunction than SSRIs.
Rare but serious agranulocytosis — tell patients to report fever/sore throat.
mnemonics: "Mirtazapine = More at night, More munchies" — sedation + appetite, strongest at LOW dose; the "M" is for Munchies and Metabolic gain.
confusables: Mirtazapine vs mianserin (related tetracyclic) and vs trazodone — all sedating atypicals but different receptor targets.
classDifferentiators: Presynaptic α2 antagonist (not a reuptake inhibitor): sedation + appetite (LOW-dose predominant), antiemetic, low sexual dysfunction, rare agranulocytosis.
examHooks: Antidepressant that helps insomnia, poor appetite and nausea, and is MORE sedating at LOW dose? (mirtazapine)
Which antidepressant causes rare agranulocytosis? (mirtazapine)
counsellingPoints: Take at bedtime — it helps sleep and appetite.
Expect possible weight gain; we will track weight and lipids.
Report fever or sore throat promptly (rare blood-count effect).

vignettes.stem: A depressed 70-year-old with insomnia, 6 kg unintentional weight loss and poor appetite needs an antidepressant. Which best targets all three problems?
vignettes.explanation: Mirtazapine's potent H1 antihistamine and 5-HT2C/5-HT3 effects promote sleep and appetite/weight gain, making it well suited to depression with insomnia and weight loss — often chosen in older adults with these features.
vignettes.testedFact: Mirtazapine for depression with insomnia/anorexia/weight loss

moa: Reversible Inhibitor of MAO-A (RIMA) — selectively and reversibly inhibits MAO-A, raising 5-HT, NE and dopamine. Canada-available (not marketed in the US).
receptorProfile: No significant direct receptor binding; reversibility means dietary tyramine can competitively DISPLACE it from MAO-A, greatly limiting the "cheese reaction".
onsetOfEffect: Antidepressant effect over 1–3 wk.
halfLife: Short (~1–3 h) — a key reason its washout is much shorter than irreversible MAOIs.
metabolism: Hepatic, largely via CYP2C19 (some 2D6). Moderate inhibitor of CYP2D6/2C19 — levels rise with 2C19 inhibitors (e.g., cimetidine → halve the dose).
foodEffect: Take AFTER meals — food (and taking it post-prandially) further reduces any tyramine pressor effect.
startingDose: 300 mg/day PO in divided doses (e.g., 150 mg BID) after meals.
titration: Increase after ~1 week as needed to a maximum of 600 mg/day.
maxDose: 600 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: BID–TID with/after meals.
startStopCrossTaper: Short washout: because it is reversible with a short half-life, moclobemide clears within ~24 h — but still allow adequate washout when switching from an irreversible MAOI (~2 wk) or from an SSRI/SNRI (~1 wk; 5 wk after fluoxetine) to avoid serotonin syndrome.
administrationNotes: The Canadian "safer MAOI" — reversibility markedly lowers hypertensive-crisis risk versus phenelzine/tranylcypromine.
commonSE: Less orthostatic hypotension / weight gain than irreversible MAOIs
seriousAE: Hypertensive reaction (much lower risk; possible only with very large tyramine loads or overdose)
overdoseLethality: Generally safer in isolated overdose than irreversible MAOIs, but combined overdose with a serotonergic (esp. SSRI/citalopram) can cause fatal serotonin toxicity.
discontinuationSyndrome: Minimal; taper is generally straightforward.
ongoingMonitoring: Mood/suicidality; blood pressure if high tyramine intake or overdose suspected; CYP2C19-inhibitor co-prescription (dose reduce).
majorDDI: Serotonergics — SSRIs/SNRIs/TCAs, tramadol, meperidine, linezolid, dextromethorphan, triptans (serotonin syndrome — the main danger)
Sympathomimetics (attenuated but still avoid large doses)
Cimetidine and other CYP2C19 inhibitors (raise levels — reduce moclobemide dose)
maoiWashout: Moclobemide itself clears in ~24 h (reversible, short half-life). Still leave ≥14 days after an irreversible MAOI, and the usual ~1 week (5 weeks for fluoxetine) after an SSRI/SNRI, before/after moclobemide to avoid serotonin syndrome.
foodInteractions: No strict tyramine diet — reversibility lets tyramine displace the drug from MAO-A, so ordinary meals are safe. Advise only to AVOID LARGE quantities of tyramine-rich food (a lot of aged cheese) at once, and to take moclobemide after meals.
pregnancy: Limited data; use only if benefit outweighs risk (CANMAT perinatal 2024).
lactation: Small amounts in milk; limited data — caution.
geriatric: Better tolerated than irreversible MAOIs (less orthostasis); still watch drug interactions.
pearls: RIMA = Reversible Inhibitor of MAO-A → tyramine can outcompete it, so NO strict low-tyramine diet and a much SHORTER washout than irreversible MAOIs.
Canada-specific (Manerix) — not sold in the US; a favourite Royal-College distinguishing point.
Serotonin syndrome risk PERSISTS — still contraindicated with SSRIs/SNRIs, tramadol, meperidine, dextromethorphan.
Take after meals and halve the dose if given with cimetidine (CYP2C19).
mnemonics: "moclobeMIDE = MILDer MAOI": Reversible (RIMA), Minimal diet, Rapid (short) washout — but serotonin syndrome still applies.
confusables: Moclobemide (RIMA, reversible, minimal diet) vs phenelzine/tranylcypromine (irreversible, strict tyramine diet).
classDifferentiators: The reversible MAO-A–selective agent (RIMA): dramatically lower tyramine/hypertensive risk, no strict diet, short washout — but the serotonin-syndrome contraindications are unchanged.
examHooks: Which MAOI is reversible, MAO-A selective, Canadian, and needs no strict tyramine diet? (moclobemide)
Why does moclobemide avoid the cheese reaction? (reversible — tyramine displaces it from MAO-A).
counsellingPoints: Take after meals; no strict special diet, but avoid eating large amounts of aged cheese or other very tyramine-rich foods at one sitting.
Still do NOT combine with other antidepressants, tramadol, meperidine or dextromethorphan — serious serotonin reactions can occur.
Tell any prescriber you take moclobemide before starting new medicines.

vignettes.stem: A Canadian patient with social anxiety disorder is intolerant of the strict tyramine diet required by classic MAOIs. Which agent offers MAOI efficacy with a much lower hypertensive-crisis risk and no strict dietary restriction, because its enzyme inhibition is reversible?
vignettes.explanation: Moclobemide is a RIMA (reversible inhibitor of MAO-A). Because tyramine can competitively displace it from MAO-A, the hypertensive "cheese reaction" risk is low and no strict diet is required. It is available in Canada but not the US. Serotonin-syndrome contraindications still apply.
vignettes.testedFact: Moclobemide = RIMA, reversible, minimal tyramine diet (Canada-specific)

boxedWarning.sourceText: Suicidality and Antidepressant Drugs Antidepressants increased the risk compared to placebo of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies of major depressive disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Anyone considering the use of nortriptyline hydrochloride or any other antidepressant in a child, adolescent, or young adult must balance this risk with the clinical need. Short-term studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidality with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults beyond age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults aged 65 and older. Depression and certain other psychiatric disorders are themselves associated with increases in the risk of suicide. Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored appropriately and observed closely for clinical worsening, suicidality, or unusual changes in behavior. Families and caregivers should be advised of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber. Nortriptyline hydrochloride is not approved for use in pediatric patients ( see WARNINGS, Clinical Worsening and Suicide Risk ; PRECAUTIONS, Information for Patients , and PRECAUTIONS, Pediatric Use ).

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors (MAOIs) The use of MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with nortriptyline hydrochloride or within 14 days of stopping treatment with nortriptyline hydrochloride is contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome. The use of nortriptyline hydrochloride within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders is also contraindicated ( see WARNINGS and DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION ). Starting nortriptyline hydrochloride in a patient who is being treated with MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue is also contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome ( see WARNINGS and DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION ). Hypersensitivity to Tricyclic Antidepressants Cross-sensitivity between nortriptyline hydrochloride and other dibenzazepines is a possibility. Myocardial Infarction Nortriptyline hydrochloride is contraindicated during the acute recovery period after myocardial infarction.

therapeuticLevel.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Nortriptyline hydrochloride is not recommended for children. Nortriptyline hydrochloride is administered orally in the form of capsules. Lower than usual dosages are recommended for elderly patients and adolescents. Lower dosages are also recommended for outpatients than for hospitalized patients who will be under close supervision. The physician should initiate dosage at a low level and increase it gradually, noting carefully the clinical response and any evidence of intolerance. Following remission, maintenance medication may be required for a longer period of time at the lowest dose that will maintain remission. If a patient develops minor side effects, the …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Clinical Worsening and Suicide Risk Patients with major depressive disorder (MDD), both adult and pediatric, may experience worsening of their depression and/or the emergence of suicidal ideation and behavior (suicidality) or unusual changes in behavior, whether or not they are taking antidepressant medications, and this risk may persist until significant remission occurs. Suicide is a known risk of depression and certain other psychiatric disorders, and these disorders themselves are the strongest predictors of suicide. There has been a long-standing concern, however, that antidepressants may have a role in inducing worsening of depression and the emergence of suicidality in …

Suicide is a known risk of depression and certain other psychiatric disorders, and these disorders themselves are the strongest predictors of suicide. overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Deaths may occur from overdosage with this class of drugs. Multiple drug ingestion (including alcohol) is common in deliberate tricyclic antidepressant overdose. As the management is complex and changing, it is recommended that the physician contact a poison control center for current information on treatment. Signs and symptoms of toxicity develop rapidly after tricyclic antidepressant overdose, therefore, hospital monitoring is required as soon as possible. Manifestations Critical manifestations of overdose include: cardiac dysrhythmias, severe hypotension, shock, congestive heart failure, pulmonary edema, convulsions, and CNS depression, including coma. Changes in the …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions Administration of reserpine during therapy with a tricyclic antidepressant has been shown to produce a "stimulating" effect in some depressed patients. Close supervision and careful adjustment of the dosage are required when nortriptyline hydrochloride is used with other anticholinergic drugs and sympathomimetic drugs. Concurrent administration of cimetidine and tricyclic antidepressants can produce clinically significant increases in the plasma concentrations of the tricyclic antidepressant. The patient should be informed that the response to alcohol may be exaggerated. A case of significant hypoglycemia has been reported in a type II diabetic patient maintained on …

Concurrent administration of cimetidine and tricyclic antidepressants can produce clinically significant increases in the plasma concentrations of the tricyclic antidepressant. The patient should be informed that the response to alcohol may be exaggerated. A case of significant hypoglycemia has been reported in a type II diabetic patient maintained on …

pregnancy.sourceText: Use in Pregnancy Safe use of nortriptyline hydrochloride during pregnancy and lactation has not been established; therefore, when the drug is administered to pregnant patients, nursing mothers, or women of childbearing potential, the potential benefits must be weighed against the possible hazards. Animal reproduction studies have yielded inconclusive results.
