moa: Secondary-amine tricyclic (active metabolite of amitriptyline); blocks NE > 5-HT reuptake. Less H1/M1/α1 blockade than tertiary amines.
receptorProfile: More noradrenergic and cleaner than amitriptyline: least orthostatic hypotension of the TCAs, less sedation/anticholinergic load. Retains Na-channel block in overdose.
onsetOfEffect: Mood 2–4 wk (full 4–6 wk).
metabolism: Hepatic; hydroxylated primarily by CYP2D6 (2D6 polymorphism affects levels).
therapeuticLevel: Therapeutic window ~50–150 ng/mL (curvilinear — efficacy falls off above ~150 ng/mL); one of the few antidepressants with genuine TDM.
approvedPopulations: Adults; preferred TCA in the elderly when a TCA is required.
startingDose: 25 mg PO at bedtime (10–25 mg in elderly).
titration: Increase by 25 mg every few days toward 75–100 mg/day; check level to target window.
maxDose: 150 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once daily at bedtime or divided.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper to limit cholinergic rebound. 14-day MAOI washout each way.
boxedWarning: Suicidality and Antidepressant Drugs Antidepressants increased the risk compared to placebo of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies of major depressive disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Anyone considering the use of nortriptyline hydrochloride or any other antidepressant in a child, adolescent, or young adult must balance this risk with the clinical need. Short-term studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidality with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults beyond age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults aged 65 and older. Depression and certain other psychiatric disorders are themselves associated with increases in the risk of suicide. Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored appropriately and …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors (MAOIs) The use of MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with nortriptyline hydrochloride or within 14 days of stopping treatment with nortriptyline hydrochloride is contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome. The use of nortriptyline hydrochloride within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders is also contraindicated ( see WARNINGS and DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION ). Starting nortriptyline hydrochloride in a patient who is being treated with MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue is also contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome ( see WARNINGS and DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION ). Hypersensitivity to Tricyclic Antidepressants Cross-sensitivity between nortriptyline hydrochloride and other dibenzazepines is a possibility. Myocardial Infarction …
seriousAE: Cardiac conduction delay / arrhythmia (wide QRS, QT)
overdoseLethality: HIGH — like all TCAs, lethal in overdose via Na-channel-blockade cardiotoxicity (wide QRS, arrhythmia), seizures and coma. Narrow therapeutic index despite better routine tolerability. IV sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms.
discontinuationSyndrome: Cholinergic rebound — taper.
baselineWorkup: ECG (baseline, especially age >40 or cardiac disease)
Plasma level to target therapeutic window (~50–150 ng/mL)
ongoingMonitoring: Serum level (TDM) toward 50–150 ng/mL; mood/suicidality; ECG if cardiac risk.
majorDDI: CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, bupropion) raise levels
contraindicatedCombos: Recent acute MI / significant conduction disease
pregnancy: Long experience; often the preferred TCA in pregnancy when one is needed — weigh untreated illness. Tie to CANMAT perinatal 2024.
lactation: Low relative infant dose; generally compatible with monitoring.
geriatric: Preferred TCA in older adults — least orthostatic hypotension and less anticholinergic than amitriptyline; still monitor and use TDM.
cardiac: Na-channel effects persist — ECG and caution in conduction disease; avoid post-MI.
pearls: Therapeutic WINDOW (~50–150 ng/mL): a rare antidepressant with true therapeutic drug monitoring — levels above the window can reduce efficacy.
Secondary-amine metabolite of amitriptyline: more noradrenergic, less sedating/anticholinergic, and the least-orthostatic TCA → preferred in the elderly.
mnemonics: "NORtriptyline has a NARrow window" — target 50–150 ng/mL (check a level).
confusables: Nortriptyline (secondary amine, TDM window, better tolerated) vs amitriptyline (its sedating tertiary-amine parent).
Nortriptyline vs protriptyline — both secondary amines, but protriptyline is activating, not sedating.
classDifferentiators: The better-tolerated secondary-amine TCA with a genuine therapeutic window; TDM-guided; elderly-preferred.
examHooks: Which TCA has a therapeutic WINDOW (~50–150 ng/mL) rather than a floor? (nortriptyline)
counsellingPoints: We will check a blood level to get the dose right.
Take at bedtime.
Rise slowly.
Overdose is dangerous — keep secured.

vignettes.stem: A 68-year-old on a TCA for depression has a partial response; the level returns at 210 ng/mL. What is the best next step and why?
options: Increase the dose — higher levels are more effective
Reduce the dose — the level is above the therapeutic window
Add lithium and keep the dose
No change — TCAs have no useful level
vignettes.answer: Reduce the dose — the level is above the therapeutic window
vignettes.explanation: Nortriptyline shows a curvilinear therapeutic window (~50–150 ng/mL); efficacy can fall at higher levels, so lowering the dose into the window is appropriate. It is one of the few antidepressants with genuine TDM.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adult patients in short-term studies. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening, and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 )] . Paroxetine tablets are not approved for use in pediatric patients [see Use in Specific Populations ( 8.4 )] . WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. Increased risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in pediatric and young adult patients taking antidepressants. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening and emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Paroxetine tablets are not approved for use in pediatric patients. ( 5.1 , 8.4 )

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Paroxetine tablets are contraindicated in patients: Taking, or within 14 days of stopping, MAOIs (including the MAOIs linezolid and intravenous methylene blue) because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.2 ), Drug Interactions ( 7 )]. Taking thioridazine because of risk of QT prolongation [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.3 ), Drug Interactions ( 7 )] Taking pimozide because of risk of QT prolongation [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.3 ), Drug Interactions ( 7 )]. With known hypersensitivity (e.g., anaphylaxis, angioedema, Stevens-Johnson syndrome) to paroxetine or any of the inactive ingredients in paroxetine tablets [see Adverse Reactions ( 6.1 ), ( 6.2 )] . Concomitant use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) or use within 14 days of discontinuing a MAOI. ( 4 , 5.3 , 7 ) Concomitant use of pimozide or thioridazine. ( 4 , 5.3 , 7 ) Known hypersensitivity to paroxetine or to any of the inactive ingredients in paroxetine tablets. ( 4 )

seriousAE.sourceText: 5 WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS Serotonin Syndrome: Increased risk when co-administered with other serotonergic agents, but also when taken alone. If occurs, discontinue paroxetine tablets and serotonergic agents and initiate supportive measures. ( 5.2 ) Embryofetal Toxicity: May cause fetal harm. Meta-analyses of epidemiological studies have shown increased risk (less than 2-fold) of cardiovascular malformations with exposure during the first trimester. overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE The following have been reported with paroxetine tablet overdosage: Seizures, which may be delayed, and altered mental status including coma. Cardiovascular toxicity, which may be delayed, including QRS and QTc interval prolongation. Hypertension most commonly seen, but rarely can see hypotension alone or with co-ingestants including alcohol. Serotonin syndrome (patients with a multiple drug overdosage with other proserotonergic drugs may have a higher risk). Gastrointestinal decontamination with activated charcoal should be considered in patients who present early after a paroxetine overdose. Consider contacting a poison center (1-800-222-1222) or a medical toxicologist for …

Paroxetine tablets are associated with a less than 2-fold increase in cardiovascular malformations when administered to a pregnant woman during the first trimester. While individual epidemiological studies on the association between paroxetine use and cardiac malformations have reported inconsistent findings, some meta-analyses of epidemiological studies have identified an increased risk of …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (30 MG, 20 MG, 10 MG, 37.5 MG, 40 MG, 25 MG, 12.5 MG)
moa: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (blocks SERT); mild anticholinergic and weak NET effects.
receptorProfile: High SERT affinity; notable muscarinic (M1) antagonism (most anticholinergic SSRI).
onsetOfEffect: 1–2 wk early; 4–6 wk full.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP2D6 — and a POTENT CYP2D6 inhibitor (inhibits its own metabolism → nonlinear kinetics).
foodEffect: With or without food.
startingDose: 20 mg PO once daily.
titration: Increase by 10 mg/day at ≥1-week intervals.
maxDose: 50 mg/day (60 mg for OCD in some labels).
frequencyTiming: Once daily, often morning; more sedating than other SSRIs.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper slowly — worst discontinuation syndrome of the SSRIs (short half-life, no active metabolite).
administrationNotes: CR formulation available.

discontinuationSyndrome: Worst-in-class — dizziness, paresthesia, "brain zaps", flu-like symptoms; taper very slowly.
ongoingMonitoring: Mood/suicidality; sodium if symptomatic; caution for anticholinergic load in elderly.
majorDDI: CYP2D6 substrates (TCAs, tamoxifen — reduces active endoxifen, metoprolol)
pregnancy: Associated with cardiac malformations (e.g., septal defects) in some studies — generally AVOIDED in pregnancy; formerly higher-risk category. Tie to CANMAT perinatal 2024.
lactation: Low milk levels but generally not first-choice if initiating.
geriatric: Most anticholinergic SSRI — caution (falls, confusion, constipation); on Beers-type caution lists.
pearls: Worst discontinuation syndrome + most anticholinergic + most weight gain + most sexual dysfunction of the SSRIs.
Potent CYP2D6 inhibitor; reduces tamoxifen activation.
Generally avoided in pregnancy (cardiac malformation signal).
mnemonics: "Paroxetine = the problem child": Pregnancy risk, weight gain, anticholinergic, discontinuation.
confusables: Paroxetine vs fluoxetine — opposite ends: short vs long half-life, worst vs least discontinuation.
classDifferentiators: Short half-life, potent 2D6 inhibitor, anticholinergic, sedating, pregnancy-avoid.
examHooks: Which SSRI is most associated with discontinuation syndrome and with pregnancy cardiac risk? (paroxetine)
counsellingPoints: Do not stop abruptly — taper.
Avoid in pregnancy/if planning pregnancy — discuss.
May cause weight gain and sedation.

vignettes.stem: A patient abruptly stops their SSRI and 2 days later has dizziness, nausea, paresthesias and "electric shock" sensations. Which SSRI is MOST likely responsible?
vignettes.explanation: Paroxetine has the shortest half-life and no active metabolite, giving it the most prominent discontinuation syndrome; fluoxetine (long half-life) has the least.

boxedWarning.sourceText: Suicidality and Antidepressant Drugs Antidepressants increased the risk compared to placebo of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies of major depressive disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Anyone considering the use of Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets or any other antidepressant in a child, adolescent, or young adult must balance this risk with the clinical need. Short-term studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidality with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults beyond age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults aged 65 and older. Depression and certain other psychiatric disorders are themselves associated with increases in the risk of suicide. Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored appropriately and observed closely for clinical worsening, suicidality, or unusual changes in behavior. Families and caregivers should be advised of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber. Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets is not approved for use in pediatric patients. (See Warnings: Clinical Worsening and Suicide Risk , Precautions: Information for Patients , and Precautions: Pediatric Use )

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets should not be used in patients who are hypersensitive to the drug or its ingredients, with pheochromocytoma, congestive heart failure, severe renal impairment or renal disease, a history of liver disease, or abnormal liver function tests. The potentiation of sympathomimetic substances and related compounds by MAO inhibitors may result in hypertensive crises (see WARNINGS ). Therefore, patients being treated with Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets should not take sympathomimetic drugs (including amphetamines, cocaine, methylphenidate, dopamine, epinephrine, and norepinephrine) or related compounds (including methyldopa, L-dopa, L-tryptophan, L-tyrosine, and phenylalanine). Hypertensive crises during Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets therapy may also be caused by the ingestion of foods with a high concentration of tyramine or dopamine. Therefore, patients being treated with Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets should avoid high protein food that has undergone protein breakdown by aging, fermentation, pickling, smoking, or bacterial contamination. Patients should also avoid cheeses (especially aged varieties), pickled herring, beer, wine, liver, yeast extract (including brewer's yeast in large quantities), dry sausage (including Genoa salami, hard salami, pepperoni, and Lebanon bologna), pods of broad beans (fava beans), and yogurt. Excessive amounts of …

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Initial dose The usual starting dose of Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets is one tablet (15 mg) three times a day. Early phase treatment Dosage should be increased to at least 60 mg per day at a fairly rapid pace consistent with patient tolerance. It may be necessary to increase dosage up to 90 mg per day to obtain sufficient MAO inhibition. Many patients do not show a clinical response until treatment at 60 mg has been continued for at least 4 weeks. Maintenance dose After maximum benefit from Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets is achieved, dosage should be reduced slowly over several weeks. Maintenance dose may be as low as one tablet, 15 mg, a day or every other day, and …

Suicide is a known risk of depression and certain other psychiatric disorders, and these disorders themselves are the strongest predictors of suicide. There has been a long-standing concern, however, that antidepressants may have a role in inducing worsening of depression and the emergence of suicidality in …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Note —For management of hypertensive crises see WARNINGS section. Accidental or intentional overdosage may be more common in patients who are depressed. It should be remembered that multiple drugs and/or alcohol may have been ingested. Depending on the amount of overdosage with Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets, a varying and mixed clinical picture may develop, including signs and symptoms of central nervous system and cardiovascular stimulation and/or depression. Signs and symptoms may be absent or minimal during the initial 12-hour period following ingestion and may develop slowly thereafter, reaching a maximum in 24–48 hours. Death has been reported following overdosage. Therefore, …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions In patients receiving nonselective monoamine oxidase (MAO) inhibitors in combination with serotoninergic agents (e.g., dexfenfluramine, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, paroxetine, sertraline, citalopram, venlafaxine) there have been reports of serious, sometimes fatal, reactions. Because Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets is a monoamine oxidase (MAO) inhibitor, Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets should not be used concomitantly with a serotoninergic agent (See CONTRAINDICATIONS ). Administration of guanethidine to patients receiving an MAO inhibitor can produce moderate to severe hypertension due to release of catecholamines. At least two weeks should elapse between withdrawal of the MAO …

moa: Irreversible, non-selective monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAO-A and MAO-B) → raises NE, 5-HT, dopamine. Hydrazine structure.
receptorProfile: No direct receptor affinity; effect is enzymatic (blocks monoamine breakdown). Depletes pyridoxine (vitamin B6) as a hydrazine.
onsetOfEffect: Antidepressant effect over 2–4 wk; full enzyme inhibition builds over days.
halfLife: Parent drug short (~1.5–4 h), but effect lasts ~2 weeks because inhibition is IRREVERSIBLE — activity returns only as new MAO is synthesized.
metabolism: Hepatic; acetylation and oxidation (hydrazine metabolites). Not a major CYP inhibitor, but PD interactions dominate.
foodEffect: Take with food if GI upset; the critical issue is tyramine content, not absorption.
startingDose: 15 mg PO three times daily.
titration: Increase to 60–90 mg/day over weeks based on response and tolerability.
maxDose: 90 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Divided doses (BID–TID); can be sedating.
startStopCrossTaper: Requires a 14-day MAOI washout each way when switching to/from serotonergics (5 weeks after fluoxetine). Allow ~2 weeks for MAO regeneration after stopping before dietary/drug restrictions lift.
administrationNotes: More sedating and anxiolytic than tranylcypromine; historically favoured for atypical depression with anxiety.
boxedWarning: Suicidality and Antidepressant Drugs Antidepressants increased the risk compared to placebo of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies of major depressive disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Anyone considering the use of Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets or any other antidepressant in a child, adolescent, or young adult must balance this risk with the clinical need. Short-term studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidality with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults beyond age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults aged 65 and older. Depression and certain other psychiatric disorders are themselves associated with increases in the risk of suicide. Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored appropriately and …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets should not be used in patients who are hypersensitive to the drug or its ingredients, with pheochromocytoma, congestive heart failure, severe renal impairment or renal disease, a history of liver disease, or abnormal liver function tests. The potentiation of sympathomimetic substances and related compounds by MAO inhibitors may result in hypertensive crises (see WARNINGS ). Therefore, patients being treated with Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets should not take sympathomimetic drugs (including amphetamines, cocaine, methylphenidate, dopamine, epinephrine, and norepinephrine) or related compounds (including methyldopa, L-dopa, L-tryptophan, L-tyrosine, and phenylalanine). Hypertensive crises during Phenelzine Sulfate Tablets therapy may also be caused by the ingestion of foods with a high concentration of tyramine or dopamine. Therefore, patients …
overdoseLethality: Dangerous in overdose — delayed (up to 12–24 h) onset of agitation, hyperthermia, autonomic instability and cardiovascular collapse; narrow margin. High lethality relative to SSRIs.
discontinuationSyndrome: Abrupt withdrawal can cause agitation, vivid dreams and (rarely) psychosis; taper.
ongoingMonitoring: Blood pressure (supine and standing); watch for occipital headache signalling hypertensive crisis; mood/suicidality. Consider pyridoxine if neuropathic symptoms.
majorDDI: Serotonergics — SSRIs/SNRIs/TCAs, tramadol, meperidine, linezolid, dextromethorphan, triptans, St John's wort (serotonin syndrome)
Sympathomimetics — pseudoephedrine, phenylephrine, amphetamines, cocaine (hypertensive crisis)
maoiWashout: Stop other MAOI/serotonergic ≥14 days before starting phenelzine; stop phenelzine ≥14 days before starting a serotonergic (5 weeks after fluoxetine).
foodInteractions: STRICT low-tyramine diet required. Avoid aged/matured cheese, cured or smoked/fermented meats, fermented soy (soy sauce, miso, tofu left out), sauerkraut, tap/draught beer, broad (fava) bean pods, overripe fruit, and yeast/meat extracts (Marmite, Vegemite, Oxo). Tyramine + MAOI → hypertensive crisis.
pregnancy: Generally avoided; limited data and risk of hypertensive reactions. Weigh against untreated illness (CANMAT perinatal 2024).
lactation: Limited data; generally avoided.
geriatric: Orthostatic hypotension and falls risk; dietary adherence harder — use caution.
pearls: Hydrazine MAOI → can deplete pyridoxine (B6) causing peripheral neuropathy, and carries hepatotoxicity risk.
More sedating/anxiolytic than tranylcypromine — historically the MAOI for atypical/anxious depression.
Irreversible: dietary and drug restrictions persist ~2 weeks after stopping while new MAO is made.
mnemonics: Tyramine crisis foods = "the 3 C's + beer": Cheese (aged), Cured meats, Chianti/fermented, plus tap beer & Marmite.
confusables: Phenelzine (MAOI antidepressant) vs phenytoin (anticonvulsant) vs phentermine (sympathomimetic — dangerous WITH an MAOI).
classDifferentiators: The sedating, anxiolytic hydrazine MAOI; unique risks of B6 depletion/neuropathy and hepatotoxicity.
examHooks: Which MAOI is a hydrazine linked to pyridoxine (B6) deficiency and hepatotoxicity? (phenelzine)
Occipital headache + hypertension on an MAOI → tyramine crisis.
counsellingPoints: Follow the low-tyramine diet strictly — no aged cheese, cured/fermented meats, fermented soy, tap beer, Marmite; continue for 2 weeks after stopping.
A severe throbbing (usually occipital) headache, palpitations or chest pain means possible hypertensive crisis — seek emergency care.
Do not take any new medication (including OTC cold/decongestant remedies, dextromethorphan, tramadol) without pharmacist/physician check.
Rise slowly to avoid dizziness (orthostatic hypotension).

vignettes.stem: A 47-year-old on phenelzine for atypical depression eats an aged-cheddar and salami platter with a draught beer at a party. Two hours later he presents with a severe throbbing occipital headache, BP 220/125, diaphoresis and palpitations. What is the mechanism?
options: Serotonin syndrome from excess synaptic serotonin
Tyramine-induced release of stored noradrenaline causing a hypertensive crisis
vignettes.answer: Tyramine-induced release of stored noradrenaline causing a hypertensive crisis
vignettes.explanation: Aged cheese, cured meats and tap beer are high in tyramine. With gut/hepatic MAO-A irreversibly blocked, ingested tyramine is not degraded, floods the circulation and displaces noradrenaline from nerve terminals → abrupt severe hypertension ("cheese reaction").
vignettes.testedFact: Tyramine hypertensive crisis with irreversible MAOIs

boxedWarning.sourceText: Boxed Warning Suicidality and Antidepressant Drugs Antidepressants increased the risk compared to placebo of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies of major depressive disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Anyone considering the use of protriptyline hydrochloride or any other antidepressant in a child, adolescent, or young adult must balance this risk with the clinical need. Short-term studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidality with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults beyond age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults aged 65 and older. Depression and certain other psychiatric disorders are themselves associated with increases in the risk of suicide. Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored appropriately and observed closely for clinical worsening, suicidality, or unusual changes in behavior. Families and caregivers should be advised of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber. Protriptyline hydrochloride is not approved for use in pediatric patients. (See WARNINGS: Clinical Worsening and Suicide Risk , PRECAUTIONS: Information for Patients , and PRECAUTIONS: Pediatric Use .)

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Protriptyline hydrochloride tablets are contraindicated in patients who have shown prior hypersensitivity to it. It should not be given concomitantly with a monoamine oxidase inhibiting compound. Hyperpyretic crises, severe convulsions, and deaths have occurred in patients receiving tricyclic antidepressant and monoamine oxidase inhibiting drugs simultaneously. When it is desired to substitute protriptyline for a monoamine oxidase inhibitor, a minimum of 14 days should be allowed to elapse after the latter is discontinued. Protriptyline should then be initiated cautiously with gradual increase in dosage until optimum response is achieved. Protriptyline is contraindicated in patients taking cisapride because of the possibility of adverse cardiac interactions including prolongation of the QT interval, cardiac arrhythmias and conduction system disturbances. This drug should not be used during the acute recovery phase following myocardial infarction.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Dosage should be initiated at a low level and increased gradually, noting carefully the clinical response and any evidence of intolerance. Usual Adult Dosage Fifteen to 40 mg a day divided into 3 or 4 doses. If necessary, dosage may be increased to 60 mg a day. Dosages above this amount are not recommended. Increases should be made in the morning dose. Adolescent and Elderly Patients In general, lower dosages are recommended for these patients. Five mg 3 times a day may be given initially, and increased gradually if necessary. In elderly patients, the cardiovascular system must be monitored closely if the daily dose exceeds 20 mg. When satisfactory improvement has …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Deaths may occur from overdosage with this class of drugs. Multiple drug ingestion (including alcohol) is common in deliberate tricyclic antidepressant overdose. As management of overdose is complex and changing, it is recommended that the physician contact a poison control center for current information on treatment. Signs and symptoms of toxicity develop rapidly after tricyclic antidepressant overdose, therefore, hospital monitoring is required as soon as possible.

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions When protriptyline is given with anticholinergic agents or sympathomimetic drugs, including epinephrine combined with local anesthetics, close supervision and careful adjustment of dosages are required. Hyperpyrexia has been reported when tricyclic antidepressants are administered with anticholinergic agents or with neuroleptic drugs, particularly during hot weather. Cimetidine is reported to reduce hepatic metabolism of certain tricyclic antidepressants, thereby delaying elimination and increasing steady-state concentrations of these drugs. Clinically significant effects have been reported with the tricyclic antidepressants when used concomitantly with cimetidine. …

moa: Secondary-amine tricyclic; potent noradrenergic (NET) reuptake inhibitor. Activating rather than sedating; anticholinergic; Na-channel block in overdose.
receptorProfile: Strong NE reuptake block with an activating/stimulating profile (least sedating TCA); still meaningfully anticholinergic. Very long half-life.
onsetOfEffect: Mood 2–4 wk; activation/energy may appear earlier.
halfLife: Long — ~54–92 hours (~74 h), the longest of the TCAs.
metabolism: Hepatic; CYP2D6 substrate.
startingDose: 5–10 mg PO three times daily (avoid late-evening doses — activating).
titration: Increase gradually; the most potent TCA per mg, so doses are low.
maxDose: 60 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Divided doses earlier in the day (activating — avoid bedtime).
startStopCrossTaper: Taper. 14-day MAOI washout each way.
boxedWarning: Boxed Warning Suicidality and Antidepressant Drugs Antidepressants increased the risk compared to placebo of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies of major depressive disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Anyone considering the use of protriptyline hydrochloride or any other antidepressant in a child, adolescent, or young adult must balance this risk with the clinical need. Short-term studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidality with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults beyond age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults aged 65 and older. Depression and certain other psychiatric disorders are themselves associated with increases in the risk of suicide. Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Protriptyline hydrochloride tablets are contraindicated in patients who have shown prior hypersensitivity to it. It should not be given concomitantly with a monoamine oxidase inhibiting compound. Hyperpyretic crises, severe convulsions, and deaths have occurred in patients receiving tricyclic antidepressant and monoamine oxidase inhibiting drugs simultaneously. When it is desired to substitute protriptyline for a monoamine oxidase inhibitor, a minimum of 14 days should be allowed to elapse after the latter is discontinued. Protriptyline should then be initiated cautiously with gradual increase in dosage until optimum response is achieved. Protriptyline is contraindicated in patients taking cisapride because of the possibility of adverse cardiac interactions including prolongation of the QT interval, cardiac arrhythmias and conduction system disturbances. This drug …
seriousAE: Cardiac conduction delay / arrhythmia (wide QRS, QT); tachycardia prominent
overdoseLethality: HIGH — lethal in overdose via Na-channel-blockade cardiotoxicity (wide QRS, arrhythmia), seizures and coma; very long half-life prolongs toxicity. Narrow therapeutic index; IV sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms.
discontinuationSyndrome: Cholinergic rebound — taper (very long half-life softens it).
baselineWorkup: ECG (baseline, especially age >40 or cardiac disease)
ongoingMonitoring: Activation/insomnia and tachycardia; mood/suicidality; ECG if cardiac risk.
majorDDI: CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, bupropion) raise levels
contraindicatedCombos: Recent acute MI / significant conduction disease
pregnancy: Weigh untreated illness; nortriptyline usually preferred if a TCA is needed. Tie to CANMAT perinatal 2024.
lactation: Small amounts in milk; monitor infant.
geriatric: Activating with prominent tachycardia and anticholinergic effects — use cautiously; the very long half-life aids accumulation.
cardiac: Na-channel block plus tachycardia; ECG and caution in conduction disease.
pearls: The activating, least-sedating TCA — a secondary amine used for depression with psychomotor retardation or hypersomnia; dose early in the day to avoid insomnia.
Longest half-life of the TCAs (~74 h) and the most potent per milligram, so doses are low (≤60 mg/day).
mnemonics: "PROtriptyline = PROceed/GO" — the activating (stimulating), non-sedating TCA.
confusables: Protriptyline (activating secondary amine) vs nortriptyline (sedating-leaning secondary amine with a TDM window).
Protriptyline vs amitriptyline — activating vs very sedating.
classDifferentiators: The activating, non-sedating secondary-amine TCA; longest half-life, lowest mg doses.
examHooks: Which TCA is activating (not sedating) and used for depression with psychomotor retardation/hypersomnia? (protriptyline)
counsellingPoints: Take earlier in the day — it can be stimulating/cause insomnia.
Report palpitations.
Rise slowly.
Overdose is dangerous — keep secured.

vignettes.stem: A patient with depression marked by fatigue and psychomotor retardation is intolerant of sedation. Which secondary-amine TCA is uniquely activating and is dosed low and early in the day?
vignettes.explanation: Protriptyline is the activating, least-sedating TCA — a potent noradrenergic secondary amine dosed low (≤60 mg/day) and earlier in the day to avoid insomnia; the other options are sedating tertiary/tetracyclic agents.

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Selegiline hydrochloride is contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to this drug. Selegiline is contraindicated for use with meperidine. This contraindication is often extended to other opioids. (See Drug Interactions .)

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Selegiline hydrochloride tablets USP are intended for administration to Parkinsonian patients receiving levodopa/carbidopa therapy who demonstrate a deteriorating response to this treatment. The recommended regimen for the administration of Selegiline Hydrochloride Tablets USP is 10 mg per day administered as divided doses of 5 mg each taken at breakfast and lunch. There is no evidence that additional benefit will be obtained from the administration of higher doses. Moreover, higher doses should ordinarily be avoided because of the increased risk of side effects. After two to three days of selegiline treatment, an attempt may be made to reduce the dose of …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Selegiline should not be used at daily doses exceeding those recommended (10 mg/day) because of the risks associated with non-selective inhibition of MAO. (See CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY .) The selectivity of selegiline for MAO B may not be absolute even at the recommended daily dose of 10 mg a day. Rare cases of hypertensive reactions associated with ingestion of tyramine-containing foods have been reported in patients taking the recommended daily dose of selegiline. The selectivity is further diminished with increasing daily doses. The precise dose at which selegiline becomes a non-selective inhibitor of all MAO is unknown, but may be in the range of 30 to 40 mg a day. Severe CNS …

(See CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY .) The selectivity of selegiline for MAO B may not be absolute even at the recommended daily dose of 10 mg a day. The selectivity is further diminished with increasing daily doses. The precise dose at which selegiline becomes a non-selective inhibitor of all MAO is unknown, but may be in the range of 30 to 40 mg a day. Severe CNS …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Selegiline No specific information is available about clinically significant overdoses with selegiline hydrochloride. However, experience gained during selegiline's development reveals that some individuals exposed to doses of 600 mg of d,l-selegiline suffered severe hypotension and psychomotor agitation. Since the selective inhibition of MAO B by selegiline hydrochloride is achieved only at doses in the range recommended for the treatment of Parkinson's disease (e.g., 10 mg/day), overdoses are likely to cause significant inhibition of both MAO A and MAO B. Consequently, the signs and symptoms of overdose may resemble those observed with marketed non-selective MAO inhibitors …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions The occurrence of stupor, muscular rigidity, severe agitation, and elevated temperature has been reported in some patients receiving the combination of selegiline and meperidine. Symptoms usually resolve over days when the combination is discontinued. This is typical of the interaction of meperidine and MAOIs. Other serious reactions (including severe agitation, hallucinations, and death) have been reported in patients receiving this combination (see CONTRAINDICATIONS ). Severe toxicity has also been reported in patients receiving the combination of tricyclic antidepressants and selegiline and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and selegiline. (See WARNINGS for …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (5 MG, 15 MG, 30 MG, 2 MG, 10 MG)
moa: Irreversible MAO-B–selective inhibitor at LOW dose (raises dopamine); loses selectivity and also inhibits MAO-A at HIGH or oral antidepressant doses (then serotonergic/tyramine risks apply).
receptorProfile: MAO-B selective (dopamine) at low dose. Transdermal patch bypasses first-pass gut/hepatic MAO-A, so low patch doses spare intestinal MAO-A → no tyramine diet at 6 mg/24h.
onsetOfEffect: Antidepressant effect (patch) over 2–4 wk; dopaminergic effect in Parkinson's is faster.
halfLife: Parent short (~1.5–2 h); active metabolites (l-amphetamine, l-methamphetamine) longer — can cause insomnia/activation.
metabolism: Hepatic (CYP2B6 and others) → l-amphetamine and l-methamphetamine metabolites. Inhibition of MAO-B is irreversible.
foodEffect: Oral capsule/tablet is affected by first-pass; the transdermal patch specifically AVOIDS first-pass, which is why low-dose patches skip the tyramine diet.
offLabel: Major depressive disorder (transdermal patch — US-approved as Emsam; for awareness)
startingDose: Transdermal (depression): 6 mg/24 h patch — no dietary restriction at this dose. Oral (Parkinson's): 5 mg with breakfast and 5 mg with lunch (max 10 mg/day keeps MAO-B selectivity).
titration: Patch may be increased to 9 or 12 mg/24 h (dietary tyramine restriction then required); Parkinson's oral kept ≤10 mg/day.
maxDose: Transdermal 12 mg/24 h; oral 10 mg/day (higher oral doses lose MAO-B selectivity).
frequencyTiming: Patch once daily; oral doses earlier in the day (activating metabolites cause insomnia).
startStopCrossTaper: 14-day washout each way with serotonergics (5 weeks after fluoxetine), as for other MAOIs — the serotonin-syndrome contraindication applies regardless of dose/route.
administrationNotes: Transdermal route is the trick: skipping first-pass spares intestinal MAO-A so the 6 mg/24h patch needs no diet; 9–12 mg/24h reinstate the tyramine restriction.
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Selegiline hydrochloride is contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to this drug. Selegiline is contraindicated for use with meperidine. This contraindication is often extended to other opioids. (See Drug Interactions .)
seriousAE: Serotonin syndrome (with serotonergics — at ANY dose/route)
Hypertensive crisis (with tyramine or sympathomimetics at higher patch/oral doses that inhibit MAO-A)
overdoseLethality: Overdose can lose MAO-B selectivity → hypertensive/hyperthermic crisis; dangerous, especially combined with serotonergics.
discontinuationSyndrome: Generally mild.
ongoingMonitoring: Blood pressure; sleep/activation; mood/suicidality; reinforce diet at 9–12 mg/24h patch or higher oral doses.
majorDDI: Serotonergics — SSRIs/SNRIs/TCAs, tramadol, meperidine, linezolid, dextromethorphan (serotonin syndrome, at any dose)
maoiWashout: Stop serotonergic ≥14 days before selegiline and stop selegiline ≥14 days before a serotonergic (5 weeks after fluoxetine) — the serotonin-syndrome rule holds at every dose/route.
foodInteractions: Dose/route dependent: the 6 mg/24 h transdermal patch needs NO tyramine restriction (first-pass sparing preserves gut MAO-A). At 9–12 mg/24 h patch, or high oral doses that inhibit MAO-A, the STRICT low-tyramine diet (aged cheese, cured meats, tap beer, fermented soy, Marmite) applies.
pregnancy: Limited data; generally avoided in psychiatric use. Weigh untreated illness (CANMAT perinatal 2024).
lactation: Limited data; caution.
geriatric: Orthostatic hypotension and insomnia; use lower doses.
pearls: MAO-B selective at LOW dose (dopamine, used in Parkinson's); loses selectivity and inhibits MAO-A at high/oral antidepressant doses → then tyramine/serotonin risks return.
The transdermal patch bypasses first-pass gut MAO-A, so the 6 mg/24 h dose needs no dietary restriction — a classic exam point; 9–12 mg/24 h reinstates the diet.
Metabolised to l-amphetamine and l-methamphetamine → can cause insomnia and activation.
Serotonin-syndrome contraindications apply at ANY dose/route (unlike the dose-dependent tyramine risk).
mnemonics: Selegiline "B for Brain-dopamine": MAO-B selective, used in Parkinson's; PATCH at low dose = no diet (skips the gut).
confusables: Selegiline (MAO-B selective, Parkinson's + patch for depression) vs rasagiline (also MAO-B, Parkinson's, no amphetamine metabolites) vs phenelzine (non-selective MAO-A/B, antidepressant, strict diet).
classDifferentiators: The MAO-B–selective, first-pass-sparing MAOI: low-dose oral/6 mg patch spares MAO-A (no diet), but selectivity is lost at higher doses; amphetamine metabolites distinguish it.
examHooks: Which MAOI at a low transdermal dose needs no tyramine diet, and why? (selegiline 6 mg/24h patch — bypasses first-pass gut MAO-A).
Which MAOI is MAO-B selective and yields amphetamine metabolites? (selegiline)
counsellingPoints: At the 6 mg/24 h patch no special diet is required, but at 9 or 12 mg/24 h you must follow the low-tyramine diet.
Apply the patch to clean, dry, intact skin; rotate sites; do not cut the patch.
Never combine with other antidepressants, tramadol, meperidine or dextromethorphan (serotonin syndrome) at any dose.
Report severe headache or high blood pressure (hypertensive reaction) at higher doses.

vignettes.stem: A patient is started on the 6 mg/24 h selegiline transdermal patch for depression and asks whether she must avoid aged cheese and cured meats. What is the correct advice and rationale?
options: Strict tyramine avoidance is required, as with all MAOIs
No dietary restriction is needed at 6 mg/24 h because the patch bypasses first-pass intestinal MAO-A
Tyramine is irrelevant to any MAOI
She must avoid only serotonergic foods
vignettes.answer: No dietary restriction is needed at 6 mg/24 h because the patch bypasses first-pass intestinal MAO-A
vignettes.explanation: Transdermal selegiline avoids first-pass metabolism, sparing intestinal MAO-A that normally degrades dietary tyramine; at 6 mg/24 h no tyramine diet is required. At 9 and 12 mg/24 h, MAO-A inhibition increases and the low-tyramine diet is reinstated. Serotonin-syndrome drug contraindications still apply at every dose.
vignettes.testedFact: Low-dose transdermal selegiline spares MAO-A → no tyramine diet

boxedWarning.sourceText: BOXED WARNING Suicidality and Antidepressant Drugs Antidepressants increased the risk compared to placebo of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies of major depressive disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Anyone considering the use of Sertraline hydrochloride or any other antidepressant in a child, adolescent, or young adult must balance this risk with the clinical need. Short-term studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidality with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults beyond age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults aged 65 and older. Depression and certain other psychiatric disorders are themselves associated with increases in the risk of suicide. Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored appropriately and observed closely for clinical worsening, suicidality, or unusual changes in behavior. Families and caregivers should be advised of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber. Sertraline hydrochloride tablets are not approved for the treatment of major depressive disorder in pediatric patients. (See WARNINGS:Clinical Worsening and Suicide Risk , PRECAUTIONS:Information for Patients , and PRECAUTIONS:Pediatric Use ).

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS All Dosage Forms of Sertraline: The use of MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with Sertraline hydrochloride or within 14 days of stopping treatment with Sertraline hydrochloride is contraindicated because of an because of an increased risk if serotonin syndrome. The use of Sertraline hydrochloride within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders is also contraindicated (see WARNINGS and DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION ). Starting Sertraline hydrochloride in a patient who is being treated with MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methyelene blue is also contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome (see WARNINGS and DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION). Concomitant use in patients taking pimozide is contraindicated (see PRECAUTIONS ). Sertraline is contraindicated in patients with a hypersensitivity to sertraline or any of the inactive ingredients in sertraline hydrochloride tablets.

Suicide is a known risk of depression and certain other psychiatric disorders, and these disorders themselves are the strongest predictors of suicide. There has been a long-standing concern, however, that antidepressants may have a role in inducing worsening of depression and the emergence of suicidality in …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Human Experience – Of 1,027 cases of overdose involving sertraline hydrochloride worldwide, alone or with other drugs, there were 72 deaths (circa 1999). Among 634 overdoses in which sertraline hydrochloride was the only drug ingested, 8 resulted in fatal outcome, 75 completely recovered, and 27 patients experienced sequelae after overdosage to include alopecia, decreased libido, diarrhea, ejaculation disorder, fatigue, insomnia, somnolence and serotonin syndrome. The remaining 524 cases had an unknown outcome. The most common signs and symptoms associated with non-fatal sertraline hydrochloride overdosage were somnolence, vomiting, tachycardia, nausea, dizziness, agitation and …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions Potential Effects of Coadministration of Drugs Highly Bound to Plasma Proteins –Because sertraline is tightly bound to plasma protein, the administration of sertraline hydrochloride to a patient taking another drug which is tightly bound to protein (e.g., warfarin, digitoxin) may cause a shift in plasma concentrations potentially resulting in an adverse effect. Conversely, adverse effects may result from displacement of protein bound sertraline hydrochloride by other tightly bound drugs. moa: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (blocks SERT). Weak dopamine reuptake inhibition and sigma-1 binding at higher doses.
receptorProfile: High SERT affinity; minor sigma-1; minimal H1/M1/α1 (well tolerated).
onsetOfEffect: 1–2 wk early effects; 4–6 wk full effect.
metabolism: Hepatic (CYP2B6/2C19/2C9/3A4); mild CYP2D6 inhibitor (dose-dependent). Fewer interactions than fluoxetine/paroxetine.
foodEffect: Absorption increased modestly with food; take consistently.
startingDose: 50 mg PO once daily (25 mg in panic disorder to limit activation).
titration: Increase by 50 mg at ≥1-week intervals as needed.
maxDose: 200 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once daily, morning or evening; take with food to reduce GI upset.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper on discontinuation to limit withdrawal.
administrationNotes: Broadest anxiety-spectrum approvals; a common first choice.

overdoseLethality: Relatively safe in overdose; among the safer SSRIs. Serotonin toxicity risk with co-ingestants.
discontinuationSyndrome: Moderate — taper to avoid flu-like symptoms, dizziness, "brain zaps".
ongoingMonitoring: Monitor mood/suicidality; sodium if hyponatremia symptoms; generally no routine bloodwork.
pregnancy: One of the preferred SSRIs in pregnancy/lactation given data and low milk transfer; per CANMAT perinatal 2024, treat maternal illness.
lactation: Low relative infant dose — often first-choice SSRI in breastfeeding.
geriatric: Favourable interaction profile; watch hyponatremia.
cardiac: Preferred SSRI post-MI (SADHART evidence); minimal QT effect.
pearls: Preferred SSRI in cardiac disease (SADHART) and in pregnancy/lactation.
Diarrhea is the signature GI effect.
Broadest anxiety-disorder approvals of the SSRIs.
mnemonics: "Sertraline for the heart" — safest SSRI post-MI.
confusables: Sertraline (SSRI) vs sertindole (antipsychotic).
classDifferentiators: Clean interaction profile; cardiac- and lactation-preferred; GI (diarrhea) prominent.
examHooks: Best antidepressant shortly after MI? (sertraline)
counsellingPoints: Take with food to ease nausea/diarrhea.
Effect builds over 4–6 weeks.
Taper when stopping.

vignettes.stem: A 61-year-old develops major depression 4 weeks after an acute MI. Which antidepressant has the best cardiac safety evidence to start?
vignettes.explanation: Sertraline has the strongest post-MI safety data (SADHART) with minimal cardiac/QT effect; TCAs are avoided post-MI.

In the U.S. general population, the background risk of major birth defects and miscarriage in clinically recognized pregnancies is 2-4% and 15-20%, respectively. moa: Irreversible, non-selective monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAO-A and MAO-B). Amphetamine-like structure → activating, mildly stimulant.
receptorProfile: Structurally related to amphetamine; some direct catecholamine-releasing/reuptake effect adds to the enzymatic (MAO) mechanism.
onsetOfEffect: Often faster/more activating than phenelzine; antidepressant effect over 1–3 wk.
halfLife: Parent drug very short (~1.5–3 h), but effect lasts ~1–2 weeks because inhibition is IRREVERSIBLE.
metabolism: Hepatic. PD (not CYP) interactions dominate the risk profile.
foodEffect: Take earlier in the day to limit insomnia; tyramine content is the critical dietary issue.
startingDose: 10 mg PO twice daily.
titration: Increase gradually; usual 30 mg/day, up to 60 mg/day if needed.
maxDose: 60 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Divided doses, earlier in the day (activating — dosing late causes insomnia).
startStopCrossTaper: 14-day MAOI washout each way vs serotonergics (5 weeks after fluoxetine); ~2 weeks for MAO regeneration after stopping.
administrationNotes: Activating/stimulant profile; amphetamine-like structure means some dependence/abuse potential has been reported.

overdoseLethality: Dangerous in overdose — delayed hyperthermia, agitation, seizures, autonomic instability and cardiovascular collapse; high lethality relative to SSRIs.
discontinuationSyndrome: Rebound agitation/insomnia possible on abrupt stop; taper.
ongoingMonitoring: Blood pressure (supine/standing); occipital headache as a crisis warning; sleep and activation; mood/suicidality.
majorDDI: Serotonergics — SSRIs/SNRIs/TCAs, tramadol, meperidine, linezolid, dextromethorphan, triptans, St John's wort (serotonin syndrome)
Sympathomimetics — pseudoephedrine, phenylephrine, amphetamines, cocaine (hypertensive crisis)
maoiWashout: Stop other MAOI/serotonergic ≥14 days before starting tranylcypromine; stop tranylcypromine ≥14 days before a serotonergic (5 weeks after fluoxetine).
foodInteractions: STRICT low-tyramine diet required (identical to phenelzine): avoid aged cheese, cured/smoked/fermented meats, fermented soy (soy sauce, miso), sauerkraut, tap/draught beer, broad (fava) beans, and yeast/meat extracts (Marmite, Vegemite). Tyramine + MAOI → hypertensive crisis.
pregnancy: Generally avoided; limited data and hypertensive-reaction risk. Weigh untreated illness (CANMAT perinatal 2024).
lactation: Limited data; generally avoided.
geriatric: Activation, insomnia and orthostatic hypotension; caution.
pearls: Amphetamine-like structure → the ACTIVATING MAOI (insomnia, dose early); some abuse potential.
Contrast with phenelzine: tranylcypromine = activating/less weight gain; phenelzine = sedating/more weight gain.
Same strict tyramine diet and 14-day washout rules as all irreversible MAOIs.
mnemonics: "tranylcyproMINE = amphetaMINE-like" → activating, causes insomnia, has abuse potential.
confusables: Tranylcypromine vs trimipramine (a TCA) — similar-looking names, different classes.
classDifferentiators: The activating, amphetamine-like irreversible MAOI; less weight gain than phenelzine but more insomnia and some dependence risk.
examHooks: Which MAOI is amphetamine-like and most activating (causes insomnia, dose in the morning)? (tranylcypromine)
counsellingPoints: Take earlier in the day to avoid insomnia.
Same strict low-tyramine diet as other MAOIs; continue ~2 weeks after stopping.
Avoid all decongestants, stimulants, dextromethorphan, tramadol and other antidepressants unless cleared.
A sudden severe headache with high blood pressure is an emergency (hypertensive crisis).

vignettes.stem: A 34-year-old with treatment-resistant depression on tranylcypromine is given meperidine (pethidine) in the ED for renal colic. Within an hour she develops agitation, hyperthermia (40°C), diaphoresis, tremor, hyperreflexia and inducible clonus. What is the diagnosis and the offending mechanism?
options: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome from dopamine blockade
Serotonin syndrome from an MAOI + meperidine (serotonergic) combination
vignettes.answer: Serotonin syndrome from an MAOI + meperidine (serotonergic) combination
vignettes.explanation: Meperidine has serotonergic activity and is contraindicated with MAOIs. Clonus/hyperreflexia, hyperthermia and autonomic instability point to serotonin syndrome, not NMS (which is bradykinesic/rigid). Use a non-serotonergic analgesic (e.g., morphine) in MAOI patients.

Maximum dose: 400 mg per day in divided doses ( 2 ). Trazodone hydrochloride tablets should be taken shortly after a meal or light snack ( 2 ). Tablets should be swallowed whole or broken in half along the score line, and should not be chewed or crushed ( 2 ). The dosage should be initiated at a low-dose and increased gradually, noting the clinical response and any evidence of intolerance. Occurrence of drowsiness may …

If it occurs, discontinue trazodone and initiate supportive treatment ( 5.2 ). Cardiac Arrhythmias: Increases the QT interval. Avoid use with drugs that also increase the QT interval and in patients with risk factors for prolonged QT interval ( 5.3 ) Orthostatic Hypotension and Syncope: Warn patients of risk and symptoms of hypotension ( 5.4 ). overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Death from overdose has occurred in patients ingesting trazodone and other CNS depressant drugs concurrently (alcohol; alcohol and chloral hydrate and diazepam; amobarbital; chlordiazepoxide; or meprobamate). The most severe reactions reported to have occurred with overdose of trazodone alone have been priapism, respiratory arrest, seizures, and ECG changes, including QT prolongation and syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH). The reactions reported most frequently have been drowsiness and vomiting. Overdosage may cause an increase in incidence or severity of any of the reported adverse reactions. There is no specific antidote for trazodone …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS CNS Depressants: Trazodone may enhance effects of alcohol, barbiturates, or other CNS depressants ( 7 ). CYP3A4 Inhibitors: Consider trazodone dose reduction based on tolerability ( 2.5 , 7 ). CYP3A4 Inducers: Increase in trazodone dosage may be necessary ( 2.5 , 7 ). Digoxin or Phenytoin: Monitor for increased digoxin or phenytoin serum levels ( 7 ). Healthcare providers are encouraged to register patients by calling the National Pregnancy Registry for Antidepressants at 1-844-405-6185 or visiting online at https://womensmentalhealth.org/clinical-and-research-programs/ pregnancyregistry/ antidepressants/ Risk Summary Published prospective cohort studies, case series, and case reports over several decades with trazodone hydrochloride tablets use in pregnant women have not identified any drug-associated risks of major birth defects, miscarriage, or adverse maternal or fetal …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (100 MG, 150 MG, 50 MG, 300 MG, 75 MG, 68.25 MG)
moa: Serotonin antagonist and reuptake inhibitor (SARI): postsynaptic 5-HT2A antagonism plus (dose-dependent) SERT inhibition. Also α1-adrenergic and H1 antagonism — the basis for its sedative/hypnotic and orthostatic effects at low doses.
receptorProfile: 5-HT2A antagonist; α1-adrenergic antagonist (orthostasis, priapism); H1 antagonist (sedation); SERT inhibition mainly at higher (antidepressant) doses.
onsetOfEffect: Hypnotic effect same night; antidepressant effect 2–4 wk (needs higher doses many cannot tolerate).
metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 (substrate) → active metabolite mCPP (via CYP2D6). CYP3A4 inhibitors raise levels (sedation/QT).
activeMetabolites: m-Chlorophenylpiperazine (mCPP) — a 5-HT2C agonist that can cause anxiety/migraine-like effects.
foodEffect: Take with food to slow absorption and reduce dizziness/light-headedness.
startingDose: Insomnia (off-label): 25–50 mg at bedtime. Depression: 150 mg/day in divided doses, titrate up.
titration: For depression, increase by ~50 mg every few days as tolerated; sedation often limits dosing.
maxDose: 400 mg/day (outpatient); up to 600 mg/day (inpatient).
frequencyTiming: Bedtime (sedating); divided dosing with food for higher antidepressant doses.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper if on antidepressant doses; low hypnotic doses usually stopped without serotonergic withdrawal.
administrationNotes: Widely used as a non-habit-forming hypnotic at low dose — but antidepressant efficacy needs doses that are often too sedating.
boxedWarning: WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS and BEHAVIORS Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adult patients in short-term studies. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening, and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 )] . Trazodone is not approved for use in pediatric patients [see Use in Specific Populations ( 8.4 )] . WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS and BEHAVIORS See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adult patients ( 5.1 ) Closely monitor for clinical worsening and emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors ( 5.1 ) Trazodone is not approved for use in pediatric patients ( 8.4 )
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Concomitant use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), or use within 14 days of stopping MAOIs ( 4 ). Trazodone hydrochloride tablets are contraindicated in: Patients taking, or within 14 days of stopping, monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), including MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue, because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.2 ), Drug Interactions ( 7.1 )].
overdoseLethality: Relatively less lethal than TCAs but CNS depression, hypotension and QT/arrhythmia can occur; danger increases with co-ingestants.
discontinuationSyndrome: Mild at hypnotic doses; taper antidepressant doses.
ongoingMonitoring: Orthostatic BP; mood/suicidality; ECG if cardiac/QT risk.
majorDDI: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ritonavir, ketoconazole) raise levels
pregnancy: Limited data; no strong teratogenic signal. Weigh untreated illness.
lactation: Limited data; monitor infant sedation.
geriatric: Common low-dose hypnotic, but orthostatic hypotension → falls risk; use cautiously.
cardiac: Can prolong QT and cause orthostatic hypotension; caution with cardiac disease/other QT drugs.
pearls: PRIAPISM is the classic board fact — α1 blockade; warn men to seek emergency care for an erection lasting >4 hours.
Used mainly as a low-dose hypnotic; antidepressant doses are often too sedating and cause orthostatic hypotension.
α1-adrenergic blockade also explains the orthostatic hypotension/dizziness.
mnemonics: "traZODone → ZZZ (sleep) but watch the erection that won't quit (priapism) and the drop on standing (orthostasis)."
confusables: Trazodone vs nefazodone — both SARIs; nefazodone carries a hepatotoxicity black-box and is rarely used.
classDifferentiators: The sedating SARI hypnotic — 5-HT2A/α1/H1 block; signature priapism and orthostatic hypotension; sub-therapeutic-dose sleep use.
examHooks: Antidepressant/hypnotic associated with priapism? (trazodone)
Which sedating antidepressant is limited by orthostatic hypotension? (trazodone)
counsellingPoints: Take at bedtime with a snack; rise slowly to avoid dizziness.
Men: an erection lasting over 4 hours is an emergency — go to the ER.
Expect drowsiness, especially early on.

vignettes.stem: A 45-year-old man on an antidepressant for insomnia presents with a painful erection lasting 5 hours. Which drug is the most likely cause?
vignettes.explanation: Trazodone's α1-adrenergic antagonism can cause priapism, a urologic emergency; patients should be warned to seek immediate care for a prolonged erection.

boxedWarning.sourceText: Suicidality and Antidepressant Drugs Antidepressants increased the risk compared to placebo of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies of major depressive disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Anyone considering the use of trimipramine maleate or any other antidepressant in a child, adolescent, or young adult must balance this risk with the clinical need. Short-term studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidality with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults beyond age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults aged 65 and older. Depression and certain other psychiatric disorders are themselves associated with increases in the risk of suicide. Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored appropriately and observed closely for clinical worsening, suicidality, or unusual changes in behavior. Families and caregivers should be advised of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber. Trimipramine maleate is not approved for use in pediatric patients. (See WARNINGS - Clinical Worsening and Suicide Risk , PRECAUTIONS: Information for Patients , and PRECAUTIONS: Pediatric Use .)

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors (MAOIs) The use of MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with trimipramine maleate or within 14 days of stopping treatment with trimipramine maleate is contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome. The use of trimipramine maleate within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders is also contraindicated (see WARNINGS and DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION ). Starting trimipramine maleate in a patient who is being treated with MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue is also contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome (see WARNINGS and DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRTION ). Hypersensitivity to Tricyclic Antidepressants Cross-sensitivity between trimipramine maleate and other dibenzazepines is a possibility. Myocardial Infarction The drug is contraindicated during the acute recovery period after a myocardial infarction.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Dosage should be initiated at a low level and increased gradually, noting carefully the clinical response and any evidence of intolerance. Lower dosages are recommended for elderly patients and adolescents. Lower dosages are also recommended for outpatients as compared to hospitalized patients who will be under close supervision. It is not possible to prescribe a single dosage schedule of trimipramine maleate that will be therapeutically effective in all patients. The physical psychodynamic factors contributing to depressive symptomatology are very complex; spontaneous remissions or exacerbations of depressive symptoms may occur with or without drug therapy. …

Suicide is a known risk of depression and certain other psychiatric disorders, and these disorders themselves are the strongest predictors of suicide. There has been a long-standing concern, however, that antidepressants may have a role in inducing worsening of depression and the emergence of suicidality in …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions Cimetidine There is evidence that cimetidine inhibits the elimination of tricyclic antidepressants. Downward adjustment of trimipramine maleate dosage may be required if cimetidine therapy is initiated; upward adjustment if cimetidine therapy is discontinued. Alcohol Patients should be warned that the concomitant use of alcoholic beverages may be associated with exaggerated effects. Catecholamines/Anticholinergics It has been reported that tricyclic antidepressants can potentiate the effects of catecholamines. Similarly, atropine-like effects may be more pronounced in patients receiving anticholinergic therapy. Therefore, particular care should be exercised when it is …

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy Teratogenic Effects Trimipramine maleate has shown evidence of embryotoxicity and/or increase incidence of major anomalies in rats or rabbits at doses 20 times the human dose. There are no adequate and well-controlled studies in pregnant women. Trimipramine maleate should be used during pregnancy only if the potential benefit justifies the potential risk to the fetus.

formulationsCanada: Tablet (50 MG, 25 MG, 100 MG, 75 MG, 12.5 MG)
Capsule (50 MG, 25 MG, 100 MG, 75 MG, 12.5 MG)
moa: Tertiary-amine tricyclic; weak monoamine reuptake inhibition relative to its strong H1/M1/α1 antagonism — behaves mainly as a sedating antidepressant.
receptorProfile: Marked H1 (sedation) and anticholinergic blockade with comparatively weak reuptake inhibition; little REM suppression. Na-channel block in overdose.
onsetOfEffect: Sleep benefit early; mood 2–4 wk.
metabolism: Hepatic; CYP2D6 (also 2C19) substrate.
startingDose: 25–50 mg PO at bedtime.
titration: Increase gradually toward 75–150 mg/day.
maxDose: 150 mg/day outpatient (up to 300 mg/day inpatient).
frequencyTiming: Bedtime (sedating).
startStopCrossTaper: Taper. 14-day MAOI washout each way.

Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored appropriately and …

Hypersensitivity to Tricyclic Antidepressants Cross-sensitivity between trimipramine maleate and other dibenzazepines is a possibility. Myocardial Infarction The drug is contraindicated during the acute …
seriousAE: Cardiac conduction delay / arrhythmia (wide QRS, QT)
overdoseLethality: HIGH — like other tertiary-amine TCAs, lethal in overdose (cardiotoxicity, seizures, coma) plus anticholinergic toxicity. Narrow therapeutic index; IV sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms.
discontinuationSyndrome: Cholinergic rebound — taper.
baselineWorkup: ECG (baseline, especially age >40 or cardiac disease)
ongoingMonitoring: Sedation; mood/suicidality; ECG if cardiac risk.
majorDDI: CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, bupropion) raise levels
contraindicatedCombos: Recent acute MI / significant conduction disease
pregnancy: Weigh untreated illness. Tie to CANMAT perinatal 2024.
lactation: Small amounts in milk; monitor infant.
geriatric: Sedating/anticholinergic — use cautiously; prefer a secondary amine if a TCA is needed.
cardiac: Na-channel block; ECG and caution in conduction disease.
pearls: A markedly sedating tertiary-amine TCA with relatively weak reuptake inhibition — chosen mainly for depression with prominent insomnia.
Notable for causing little REM-sleep suppression compared with other TCAs, but still fully cardiotoxic in overdose.
mnemonics: "triMIPramine = time for sleep" — sedating TCA for depression with insomnia.
confusables: Trimipramine (sedating TCA) vs trifluoperazine (an FGA antipsychotic) — similar prefix, different class.
classDifferentiators: A sedating tertiary-amine TCA with weak reuptake inhibition and minimal REM suppression.
examHooks: Which sedating TCA has relatively weak reuptake inhibition and little REM suppression? (trimipramine)
counsellingPoints: Very sedating — take at bedtime.
Rise slowly.
Avoid alcohol/other sedatives.
Overdose is dangerous.

vignettes.stem: A patient with depression and severe insomnia is started on a sedating tertiary-amine TCA noted for weak reuptake inhibition and minimal REM suppression. Which agent fits best?
vignettes.explanation: Trimipramine is a strongly sedating tertiary-amine TCA with comparatively weak monoamine reuptake inhibition and little REM suppression, favouring depression with prominent insomnia. The listed secondary amines are more activating.

boxedWarning.sourceText: Suicidality and Antidepressant Drugs Antidepressants increased the risk compared to placebo of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies of Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders. Anyone considering the use of venlafaxine tablets or any other antidepressant in a child, adolescent, or young adult must balance this risk with the clinical need. Short-term studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidality with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults beyond age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressants compared to placebo in adults aged 65 and older. Depression and certain other psychiatric disorders are themselves associated with increases in the risk of suicide. Patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy should be monitored appropriately and observed closely for clinical worsening, suicidality, or unusual changes in behavior. Families and caregivers should be advised of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber. Venlafaxine tablets are not approved for use in pediatric patients (see WARNINGS: Clinical Worsening and Suicide Risk , PRECAUTIONS: Information for Patients , and PRECAUTIONS: Pediatric Use ).

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Hypersensitivity to venlafaxine hydrochloride or to any excipients in the formulation. The use of MAOIs intended to treat psychiatric disorders with venlafaxine tablets or within 7 days of stopping treatment with venlafaxine tablets is contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome. The use of venlafaxine tablets within 14 days of stopping an MAOI intended to treat psychiatric disorders is also contraindicated (see WARNINGS and DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION ). Starting venlafaxine tablets in a patient who is being treated with MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue is also contraindicated because of an increased risk of serotonin syndrome (see WARNINGS and DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION ).

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Initial Treatment The recommended starting dose for venlafaxine tablets is 75 mg/day, administered in two or three divided doses, taken with food. Depending on tolerability and the need for further clinical effect, the dose may be increased to 150 mg/day. If needed, the dose should be further increased up to 225 mg/day. When increasing the dose, increments of up to 75 mg/day should be made at intervals of no less than 4 days. In outpatient settings there was no evidence of usefulness of doses greater than 225 mg/day for moderately depressed patients, but more severely depressed inpatients responded to a mean dose of 350 mg/day. Certain patients, including more …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Human Experience There were 14 reports of acute overdose with venlafaxine tablets, either alone or in combination with other drugs and/or alcohol, among the patients included in the premarketing evaluation. The majority of the reports involved ingestions in which the total dose of venlafaxine tablets taken was estimated to be no more than several-fold higher than the usual therapeutic dose. The 3 patients who took the highest doses were estimated to have ingested approximately 6.75 g, 2.75 g, and 2.5 g. The resultant peak plasma levels of venlafaxine for the latter 2 patients were 6.24 and 2.35 mcg/mL, respectively, and the peak plasma levels of O-desmethylvenlafaxine were 3.37 …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions As with all drugs, the potential for interaction by a variety of mechanisms is a possibility. Alcohol A single dose of ethanol (0.5 g/kg) had no effect on the pharmacokinetics of venlafaxine or ODV when venlafaxine was administered at 150 mg/day in 15 healthy male subjects. Additionally, administration of venlafaxine in a stable regimen did not exaggerate the psychomotor and psychometric effects induced by ethanol in these same subjects when they were not receiving venlafaxine. Cimetidine Concomitant administration of cimetidine and venlafaxine in a steady-state study for both drugs resulted in inhibition of first-pass metabolism of venlafaxine in 18 healthy subjects. The …

pregnancy.sourceText: Clinical Worsening and Suicide Risk Patients, their families, and their caregivers should be encouraged to be alert to the emergence of anxiety, agitation, panic attacks, insomnia, irritability, hostility, aggressiveness, impulsivity, akathisia (psychomotor restlessness), hypomania, mania, other unusual changes in behavior, worsening of depression, and suicidal ideation, especially early during antidepressant treatment and when the dose is adjusted up or down. Families and caregivers of patients should be advised to look for the emergence of such symptoms on a day-to-day basis, since changes may be abrupt. Such symptoms should be reported to the patient's prescriber or health professional, …
