moa: High-potency butyrophenone; potent post-synaptic D2 receptor antagonist (mesolimbic → antipsychotic; nigrostriatal → EPS; tuberoinfundibular → ↑prolactin).
receptorProfile: Very high D2 affinity; minimal H1/M1 (little sedation or anticholinergic effect); modest α1. This "clean" off-target profile is why EPS dominate.
onsetOfEffect: Behavioural calming within hours (IM); antipsychotic effect over days–weeks.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 (major) and CYP2D6; glucuronidation. Reduced to (less active) reduced-haloperidol.
startingDose: 0.5–5 mg PO; 2–5 mg IM for acute agitation.
titration: Adjust to response; lower and slower in elderly (start ~0.25–0.5 mg).
maxDose: Up to ~20 mg/day orally in schizophrenia (higher rarely used); minimize dose to limit EPS.
frequencyTiming: Oral once daily to BID–TID. Decanoate LAI given deep IM every 4 weeks (≈10–20× the oral daily dose).
startStopCrossTaper: Overlap oral coverage when initiating decanoate (delayed steady state). No serotonergic washout issues.
administrationNotes: IV haloperidol carries the highest torsades risk — if used, continuous ECG/telemetry; IM/PO preferred. Decanoate is a mainstay LAI for non-adherence.

Although the causes of death were varied, most of the deaths appeared to be either cardiovascular (e.g., heart failure, sudden death) or infectious (e.g., pneumonia) in nature. Observational studies suggest that, similar to atypical antipsychotic drugs, …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Haloperidol tablets are contraindicated in severe toxic central nervous system depression or comatose states from any cause and in individuals who are hypersensitive to this drug or have Parkinson's disease.
seriousAE: Increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis
overdoseLethality: Less lethal than TCAs/MAOIs, but severe EPS, sedation, hypotension and QT prolongation/arrhythmia can occur; monitor ECG.
baselineWorkup: ECG (QT), especially with cardiac risk or IV use
ongoingMonitoring: AIMS for tardive dyskinesia; watch for EPS/akathisia; ECG if IV or QT risk; prolactin if symptomatic.
pregnancy: Third-trimester exposure risks neonatal EPS/withdrawal; weigh untreated psychosis. Tie to monograph/CANMAT.
lactation: Excreted in milk; monitor infant for sedation/EPS.
geriatric: Increased mortality in elderly with dementia-related psychosis; start very low; falls, EPS, QT.
cardiac: Dose-dependent QT prolongation; IV route highest risk — telemetry.
pearls: The prototype HIGH-potency FGA: maximal EPS, minimal sedation/anticholinergic/orthostatic effect.
Decanoate LAI every 4 weeks is a workhorse for non-adherence — overlap oral until steady state.
IV haloperidol → torsades risk; QT monitoring is mandatory (many hospitals restrict IV use).
mnemonics: "HALO on top" — high-potency: high EPS, low sedation. Acute dystonia = the halo slips (fix with benztropine/diphenhydramine).
confusables: Haloperidol (butyrophenone antipsychotic) vs droperidol (its short-acting cousin) — droperidol is the antiemetic/agitation agent with the QT warning.
classDifferentiators: High-potency benchmark; most EPS, least sedation/anticholinergic; available as decanoate LAI; IV form is the QT worry.
examHooks: Young patient develops torticollis/oculogyric crisis hours after IM haloperidol — diagnosis and treatment? (acute dystonia → IM benztropine or diphenhydramine).
counsellingPoints: Report muscle stiffness, restlessness, or abnormal movements early.
Do not stop abruptly.
Depot injection means fewer daily pills but steady blood levels build slowly.

vignettes.stem: Four hours after receiving IM haloperidol for agitation, a 22-year-old man develops a sustained upward gaze and painful neck spasm. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
vignettes.explanation: This is acute dystonia (oculogyric crisis + torticollis), an EPS most common with high-potency agents in young men early in treatment. First-line is an anticholinergic (benztropine) or diphenhydramine; dantrolene is for NMS, not dystonia.

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Loxapine is contraindicated in comatose or severe drug-induced depressed states (alcohol, barbiturates, narcotics, etc.). Loxapine is contraindicated in individuals with known hypersensitivity to dibenzoxazepines.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Loxapine Capsules, USP are administered, usually in divided doses, two to four times a day. Daily dosage (in terms of base equivalents) should be adjusted to the individual patient's needs as assessed by the severity of symptoms and previous history of response to antipsychotic drugs. Oral Administration Initial dosage of 10 mg twice daily is recommended, although in severely disturbed patients initial dosage up to a total of 50 mg daily may be desirable. Dosage should then be increased fairly rapidly over the first seven to ten days until there is effective control of symptoms of schizophrenia. The usual therapeutic and maintenance range is 60 mg to 100 mg daily. …

Daily dosage (in terms of base equivalents) should be adjusted to the individual patient's needs as assessed by the severity of symptoms and previous history of response to antipsychotic drugs. Oral Administration Initial dosage of 10 mg twice daily is recommended, although in severely disturbed patients initial dosage up to a total of 50 mg daily may be desirable. Dosage should then be increased fairly rapidly over the first seven to ten days until there is effective control of symptoms of schizophrenia. The usual therapeutic and maintenance range is 60 mg to 100 mg daily. …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Signs and symptoms of overdosage will depend on the amount ingested and individual patient tolerance. As would be expected from the pharmacologic actions of the drug, the clinical findings may range from mild depression of the CNS and cardiovascular systems to profound hypotension, respiratory depression, and unconsciousness. The possibility of occurrence of extrapyramidal symptoms and/or convulsive seizures should be kept in mind. Renal failure following loxapine overdosage has also been reported. The treatment of overdosage is essentially symptomatic and supportive. Early gastric lavage and extended dialysis might be expected to be beneficial. Centrally-acting emetics may have …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions There have been rare reports of significant respiratory depression, stupor and/or hypotension with the concomitant use of loxapine and lorazepam. The risk of using loxapine in combination with CNS-active drugs has not been systematically evaluated. Therefore, caution is advised if the concomitant administration of loxapine and CNS-active drugs is required.

contraindicatedCombos.sourceText: Drug Interactions There have been rare reports of significant respiratory depression, stupor and/or hypotension with the concomitant use of loxapine and lorazepam. The risk of using loxapine in combination with CNS-active drugs has not been systematically evaluated. Therefore, caution is advised if the concomitant administration of loxapine and CNS-active drugs is required.

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy Non-teratogenic Effects Neonates exposed to antipsychotic drugs, during the third trimester of pregnancy are at risk for extrapyramidal and/or withdrawal symptoms following delivery. There have been reports of agitation, hypertonia, hypotonia, tremor, somnolence, respiratory distress and feeding disorder in these neonates. These complications have varied in severity; while in some cases symptoms have been self-limited, in other cases neonates have required intensive care unit support and prolonged hospitalization. Loxapine Succinate should be used during pregnancy only if the potential benefit justifies the potential risk to the fetus. Safe use of loxapine during pregnancy or …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (6.8 MG, 50 MG, 13.6 MG, 34 MG, 68 MG, 25 MG, 5 MG, 10 MG, 2.5 MG)
Liquid (6.8 MG, 50 MG, 13.6 MG, 34 MG, 68 MG, 25 MG, 5 MG, 10 MG, 2.5 MG)
Solution (6.8 MG, 50 MG, 13.6 MG, 34 MG, 68 MG, 25 MG, 5 MG, 10 MG, 2.5 MG)
moa: Mid-potency dibenzoxazepine (structurally related to clozapine); D2 antagonist with 5-HT2A antagonism.
receptorProfile: Moderate D2 affinity; some 5-HT2A, H1 and M1 blockade — intermediate sedation/anticholinergic burden between low- and high-potency agents.
onsetOfEffect: Oral over days–weeks; inhaled formulation calms agitation within ~10 minutes.
halfLife: Oral ~4 h (biphasic; active metabolites longer, e.g., 8-hydroxyloxapine).
metabolism: Hepatic; metabolites include amoxapine and 7/8-hydroxyloxapine.
startingDose: 10 mg PO BID (oral); inhaled 10 mg as a single dose for acute agitation.
titration: Increase oral dose over days to response.
maxDose: Up to ~250 mg/day orally; inhaled limited to a single 10 mg dose per 24 h.
frequencyTiming: Oral BID–QID. Inhaled: one dose only, in a monitored healthcare setting.
administrationNotes: Inhaled loxapine (Adasuve) delivers rapid control of agitation but can cause bronchospasm — administer only where a short-acting bronchodilator and airway support are available; contraindicated in asthma/COPD/active bronchospasm.
boxedWarning: WARNING Increased Mortality in Elderly Patients with Dementia-Related Psychosis Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Analyses of seventeen placebo-controlled trials (modal duration of 10 weeks), largely in patients taking atypical antipsychotic drugs, revealed a risk of death in drug-treated patients of between 1.6 to 1.7 times the risk of death in placebo-treated patients. Over the course of a typical 10-week controlled trial, the rate of death in drug-treated patients was about 4.5%, compared to a rate of about 2.6% in the placebo group. Although the causes of death were varied, most of the deaths appeared to be either cardiovascular (e.g., heart failure, sudden death) or infectious (e.g., pneumonia) in nature. Observational studies suggest that, similar to atypical antipsychotic drugs, treatment with …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Loxapine is contraindicated in comatose or severe drug-induced depressed states (alcohol, barbiturates, narcotics, etc.). Loxapine is contraindicated in individuals with known hypersensitivity to dibenzoxazepines.
seriousAE: Increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis
overdoseLethality: Sedation, EPS, hypotension and seizures possible; supportive care.
ongoingMonitoring: For inhaled use: assess for respiratory symptoms/bronchospasm after dose; AIMS for TD; EPS.
contraindicatedCombos: Inhaled form with asthma/COPD or acute bronchospasm
pregnancy: Neonatal EPS/withdrawal risk with late exposure; weigh maternal illness.
lactation: Limited data; monitor infant.
geriatric: Increased mortality in dementia; sedation and EPS; caution.
cardiac: QT prolongation possible.
pearls: Mid-potency FGA — but the exam angle is the INHALED formulation for acute agitation (fast, single-dose, in-clinic only).
Inhaled loxapine can cause bronchospasm — contraindicated in asthma/COPD; a rescue bronchodilator must be on hand.
A dibenzoxazepine chemically related to clozapine; its metabolite amoxapine is an antidepressant.
mnemonics: "LOX you breathe in" — inhaled loxapine for agitation, but watch the LUNGS (bronchospasm).
confusables: Loxapine (FGA, dibenzoxazepine) vs clozapine (SGA dibenzodiazepine, agranulocytosis) — similar skeleton, very different class/risk.
classDifferentiators: Intermediate potency; unique inhaled route for rapid agitation control with a bronchospasm caveat.
examHooks: Which antipsychotic has an inhaled formulation for acute agitation, and what is its key contraindication? (loxapine; asthma/COPD — bronchospasm).
counsellingPoints: Report wheeze/chest tightness after the inhaled dose immediately.
Inhaled dose is a one-time, in-clinic treatment.
Report abnormal movements or restlessness.

vignettes.stem: An emergency department wants to use inhaled loxapine to rapidly settle an agitated patient. Which pre-existing condition makes this route contraindicated?
vignettes.explanation: Inhaled loxapine can precipitate bronchospasm; it is contraindicated in patients with asthma, COPD, or other conditions with bronchospasm, and must be given only where a bronchodilator and airway support are available.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS; and SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Increased Mortality in Elderly Patients with Dementia-Related Psychosis Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. CAPLYTA is not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ]. Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adults in short-term studies. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening, and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors [see Warnings and Precautions (5.2) ]. The safety and effectiveness of CAPLYTA have not been established in pediatric patients [see Use in Specific Populations (8.4) ]. overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE No specific antidotes for CAPLYTA are known. In managing a CAPLYTA overdose, provide supportive care, including close medical supervision and monitoring and consider the possibility of multiple drug involvement. Consider contacting the Poison Help line (1-800-222-1222) or a medical toxicologist for additional overdose management recommendations.

moa: Novel-mechanism antipsychotic with a dose-dependent, multi-target profile: potent post-synaptic 5-HT2A antagonism, LOW/partial striatal D2 occupancy (pre-synaptic partial agonist + post-synaptic antagonist), and SERT inhibition. The high 5-HT2A:D2 ratio and low absolute D2 occupancy (~40% at therapeutic dose) explain its low EPS/prolactin burden versus classic D2 blockers.
receptorProfile: Very high 5-HT2A affinity ≫ D2; also modulates glutamate (indirect NMDA/AMPA via D1) and inhibits SERT. Minimal H1/M1/α1 activity at therapeutic dose → low sedation/metabolic signal.
onsetOfEffect: Antipsychotic effect over 1–2 weeks; full response weeks (as with other antipsychotics).
timeToSteadyState: A few days with once-daily dosing.
metabolism: Extensive hepatic metabolism, predominantly CYP3A4 (also UGT, AKR, CYP2C8/1A2). A sensitive CYP3A4 substrate — strong 3A4 inhibitors/inducers markedly change exposure.
foodEffect: Give with food — food lowers peak concentration and improves tolerability.
startingDose: 42 mg PO once daily (no titration needed — start at the therapeutic dose).
titration: None — 42 mg is both the starting and target dose.
maxDose: 42 mg/day (single fixed therapeutic dose; higher doses have not shown added benefit).
frequencyTiming: Once daily, with food; timing flexible (often given in the evening).
renalHepaticAdjustment: Not recommended in moderate–severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C) — exposure rises.
administrationNotes: Fixed-dose simplicity is a practical selling point; swallow capsule whole with food.
boxedWarning: WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS; and SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Increased Mortality in Elderly Patients with Dementia-Related Psychosis Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. CAPLYTA is not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ]. Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adults in short-term studies. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening, and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors [see Warnings and Precautions (5.2) ]. The safety and effectiveness of CAPLYTA have not been established in pediatric patients [see Use in Specific Populations (8.4) ]. WARNING: …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS CAPLYTA is contraindicated in patients with history of hypersensitivity reaction to lumateperone or any components of CAPLYTA. Reactions have included pruritus, rash (e.g. allergic dermatitis, papular rash, and generalized rash), and urticaria. CAPLYTA is contraindicated in patients with history of hypersensitivity reaction to lumateperone or any components of CAPLYTA. ( 4 )
seriousAE: Increased mortality in elderly with dementia-related psychosis (class effect)
overdoseLethality: Limited overdose data; supportive care. Sedation and cardiovascular monitoring; no specific antidote.
discontinuationSyndrome: No specific withdrawal syndrome described; watch for relapse of psychosis.
ongoingMonitoring: Standard antipsychotic monitoring: weight/metabolic panel, mental status; lower expected metabolic/EPS burden than most agents but still monitor.
majorDDI: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g., ketoconazole, clarithromycin) — avoid/markedly raise levels
Strong/moderate CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampin, carbamazepine) — reduce efficacy, avoid
contraindicatedCombos: Co-administration with strong CYP3A4 inducers (loss of efficacy)
Concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (per label caution/avoid)
pregnancy: Third-trimester antipsychotic exposure risks EPS/withdrawal in the neonate (class effect); limited human data. Weigh maternal illness. Enrol in pregnancy registry where available.
lactation: Limited data; monitor infant for sedation/feeding.
geriatric: Boxed-warning population (dementia-related psychosis) — increased mortality; use general antipsychotic caution.
renalHepatic: Avoid in moderate–severe hepatic impairment; no adjustment needed in renal impairment.
pearls: US-only — for awareness (Caplyta): approved for schizophrenia and bipolar I/II depression (mono or adjunct with lithium/valproate); not marketed in Canada.

The mechanism is notable: a high 5-HT2A:D2 ratio with LOW absolute D2 occupancy (~40%) plus SERT inhibition gives an antipsychotic with unusually low EPS, low prolactin, and low metabolic/weight signal — distinct from classic potent D2 blockers.
Fixed single dose (42 mg, with food, no titration) — practically simple.
mnemonics: "LUMA lights up serotonin, barely touches dopamine" — 5-HT2A ≫ D2, so few EPS and little prolactin.
confusables: Lumateperone vs pimavanserin — both spare D2, but pimavanserin has NO D2 activity at all (pure 5-HT2A inverse agonist for PD psychosis), whereas lumateperone still has low/partial D2 activity and treats schizophrenia + bipolar depression.
classDifferentiators: Among "atypicals," the lowest-metabolic/lowest-EPS profile by design; unique SERT inhibition + high 5-HT2A:D2 ratio; the only one approved across schizophrenia AND both bipolar I and II depression.
examHooks: Which novel antipsychotic combines potent 5-HT2A antagonism, low D2 occupancy, and SERT inhibition, with a low metabolic/EPS burden and approval for bipolar depression? (lumateperone)
counsellingPoints: Take once daily with food.
Less likely to cause weight gain or movement side effects than many antipsychotics, but keep monitoring appointments.
Do not stop abruptly — discuss any changes.
Tell your prescriber about medications that affect the liver enzyme CYP3A4.

vignettes.stem: A 34-year-old with schizophrenia has gained 12 kg and developed akathisia on prior antipsychotics. You consider lumateperone. Which pharmacological feature best explains its comparatively low weight-gain and EPS risk?
options: High 5-HT2A affinity with low absolute striatal D2 occupancy
vignettes.answer: High 5-HT2A affinity with low absolute striatal D2 occupancy
vignettes.explanation: Lumateperone occupies D2 only ~40% at the therapeutic dose while potently antagonising 5-HT2A, and has minimal H1/5-HT2C activity, giving low EPS/prolactin and a low metabolic signal.
vignettes.testedFact: Lumateperone 5-HT2A:D2 ratio and low D2 occupancy → low EPS/metabolic burden

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS • Known hypersensitivity to lurasidone HCl or any components in the formulation. Angioedema has been observed with lurasidone [see Adverse Reactions ( 6.1 )]. • Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g., ketoconazole, clarithromycin, ritonavir, voriconazole, mibefradil, etc.) [see Drug Interactions ( 7.1 )]. • Strong CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampin, avasimibe, St. John’s wort, phenytoin, carbamazepine, etc.) [see Drug Interactions ( 7.1 )]. • Known hypersensitivity to lurasidone hydrochloride tablets or any components in the formulation ( 4 ). • Concomitant use with a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor (e.g., ketoconazole) ( 2.6 , 4 , 7.1 ). • Concomitant use with a strong CYP3A4 inducer (e.g., rifampin) ( 2.6 , 4 , 7.1 ).

This patient recovered without sequelae. This patient resumed lurasidone hydrochloride treatment for an additional two months. 10.2 Management of Overdosage No specific antidotes for lurasidone hydrochloride are known. In managing overdose, provide supportive care, including close medical supervision and monitoring, and consider the possibility of multiple drug involvement. pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary Neonates exposed to antipsychotic drugs during the third trimester of pregnancy are at risk for extrapyramidal and/or withdrawal symptoms following delivery [see Clinical Considerations]. There are no studies of lurasidone hydrochloride use in pregnant women. The limited available data are not sufficient to inform a drug-associated risk of birth defects or miscarriage. In animal reproduction studies, no teratogenic effects were seen in pregnant rats and rabbits given lurasidone during the period of organogenesis at doses approximately 1.5- and 6-times, the maximum recommended human dose (MRHD) of 160 mg/day, respectively based on mg/m 2 body surface area [see …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (120 MG, 60 MG, 40 MG, 80 MG, 20 MG)
moa: Combined 5-HT2A/D2 antagonist with potent 5-HT7 antagonism and 5-HT1A partial agonism and α2C antagonism; minimal H1/muscarinic (metabolically favourable).
receptorProfile: D2/5-HT2A/5-HT7 antagonist, 5-HT1A partial agonist; very low H1/muscarinic → weight-, lipid- and glucose-favourable; low prolactin.
onsetOfEffect: Days–weeks.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 (major) — strong 3A4 inhibitors and inducers are contraindicated (large exposure changes).
foodEffect: CRITICAL: take WITH FOOD of ≥350 kcal — food roughly doubles absorption (empty-stomach dosing gives erratic, low levels).
approvedCanada: Bipolar I depression (monotherapy and adjunct to lithium or valproate)
approvedPopulations: Adults; adolescents for schizophrenia/bipolar depression in some labels.
startingDose: 20–40 mg once daily WITH food.
titration: Adjust at ≥1-week intervals; usual 40–80 mg/day.
maxDose: 120 mg/day (per Canadian labelling).
frequencyTiming: Once daily with a meal (≥350 kcal), often in the evening.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce dose in moderate–severe renal or hepatic impairment (per label).
administrationNotes: Food requirement (≥350 kcal) is the key counselling point; less than ziprasidone's 500 kcal.
boxedWarning: WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS; and SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Increased Mortality in Elderly Patients with Dementia-Related Psychosis Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Lurasidone hydrochloride is not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 )]. Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in pediatric and young adults in short-term studies. Closely monitor all antidepressant-treated patients for clinical worsening, and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.2 )]. WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS; and SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS See full …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS • Known hypersensitivity to lurasidone HCl or any components in the formulation. Angioedema has been observed with lurasidone [see Adverse Reactions ( 6.1 )]. • Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g., ketoconazole, clarithromycin, ritonavir, voriconazole, mibefradil, etc.) [see Drug Interactions ( 7.1 )]. • Strong CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampin, avasimibe, St. John’s wort, phenytoin, carbamazepine, etc.) [see Drug Interactions ( 7.1 )]. • Known hypersensitivity to lurasidone hydrochloride tablets or any components in the formulation ( 4 ). • Concomitant use with a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor (e.g., ketoconazole) ( 2.6 , 4 , 7.1 ). • Concomitant use with a strong CYP3A4 inducer (e.g., rifampin) ( 2.6 , 4 , 7.1 ).
overdoseLethality: Generally low; sedation and EPS — supportive care.
ongoingMonitoring: Metabolic panel per class practice (though favourable); assess akathisia/EPS; AIMS for tardive dyskinesia.
majorDDI: Strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine) — contraindicated (loss of efficacy)
pregnancy: Limited data; neonatal EPS/withdrawal risk third trimester — weigh untreated illness. Tie to CANMAT perinatal guidance.
lactation: Limited data; monitor infant.
geriatric: Increased mortality/stroke in dementia; adjust for renal/hepatic decline.
cardiac: Low QT risk.
pearls: Take WITH FOOD (≥350 kcal) — less than ziprasidone's 500 kcal, but still essential for absorption.
Strong first-line for BIPOLAR I DEPRESSION and metabolically favourable (weight/lipid/glucose-neutral, low prolactin, low QT).
CYP3A4 substrate — strong inhibitors and inducers are contraindicated (and avoid grapefruit).
mnemonics: "LURasidone: Lunch (≥350 kcal) for Low-metabolic bipolar depression."
confusables: Lurasidone vs ziprasidone — both require food, but lurasidone needs ≥350 kcal, is once daily, is low-QT, and treats bipolar depression; ziprasidone needs ≥500 kcal, is BID, and prolongs QT.
classDifferentiators: Metabolically favourable, low-QT SGA for bipolar depression, but with an absolute ≥350 kcal food requirement and CYP3A4-driven contraindicated interactions.
examHooks: Which metabolically-favourable SGA is first-line for bipolar I depression and must be taken with ≥350 kcal of food? (lurasidone)
counsellingPoints: Always take with a meal of at least 350 kcal.
Avoid grapefruit and tell us about antibiotics/antifungals or anti-seizure drugs (interactions).
It is weight- and cholesterol-friendly.

vignettes.stem: A 32-year-old with bipolar I disorder in a depressive episode has a strong family history of diabetes and is very concerned about weight gain. Which agent is a good evidence-based, metabolically favourable choice (taken with food)?
vignettes.explanation: Lurasidone is approved for bipolar I depression and is weight/lipid/glucose-favourable; it must be taken with ≥350 kcal of food for adequate absorption. Olanzapine and quetiapine carry more metabolic risk.
vignettes.testedFact: Lurasidone for bipolar depression, metabolically favourable, food requirement

formulationsCanada: Tablet (2 MG, 25 MG, 5 MG, 50 MG, 40 MG)
Solution (2 MG, 25 MG, 5 MG, 50 MG, 40 MG)
Liquid (2 MG, 25 MG, 5 MG, 50 MG, 40 MG)
Drops (2 MG, 25 MG, 5 MG, 50 MG, 40 MG)
moa: Low-potency phenothiazine (a.k.a. levomepromazine); D2 antagonist with broad H1/M1/α1 and 5-HT2 blockade; intrinsic analgesic and antiemetic activity.
receptorProfile: Strong H1 (very sedating), α1 (marked orthostasis) and M1 (anticholinergic); potent antiemetic and analgesic — used well beyond psychosis.
onsetOfEffect: Sedation/analgesia/antiemesis within hours.
metabolism: Hepatic (demethylation, sulfoxidation, glucuronidation).
startingDose: Palliative: low doses (e.g., 6.25–12.5 mg) for nausea/agitation; analgesic/sedative dosing individualized.
titration: Titrate to symptom control; watch orthostasis and sedation.
maxDose: Wide range; higher antipsychotic doses limited by sedation/hypotension.
frequencyTiming: Oral or SC/IM; often at bedtime or by continuous SC infusion in palliative care.
administrationNotes: A palliative-care staple — combines sedation, analgesia and antiemesis; frequently given subcutaneously for terminal restlessness, nausea and pain.
seriousAE: Increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis
overdoseLethality: Profound sedation and hypotension, anticholinergic toxicity, QT prolongation; supportive care.
ongoingMonitoring: BP (orthostasis); sedation; AIMS for TD; in palliative use, symptom control vs oversedation.
majorDDI: CNS depressants/opioids (additive sedation — often intended in palliative care)
pregnancy: Weigh maternal illness; neonatal EPS/sedation with late exposure.
lactation: Excreted in milk; sedation risk in infant.
geriatric: Very sedating and hypotensive — high fall/delirium risk; increased mortality in dementia.
cardiac: Marked orthostatic hypotension and QT prolongation.
pearls: Canada-specific low-potency phenothiazine (levomepromazine) — VERY sedating, with genuine analgesic and antiemetic properties.
A palliative-care workhorse: given (often subcutaneously) for terminal agitation, intractable nausea and pain.
Marked orthostatic hypotension and sedation are the dose-limiting effects; EPS are comparatively mild (low-potency).
mnemonics: "MethoTRIMEPRAZINE = the palliative TRIPLE" — Tranquillizes, Treats pain, and Tames nausea.
confusables: Methotrimeprazine (levomepromazine, phenothiazine) vs methotrexate — sound-alike, entirely different drugs.
classDifferentiators: Low-potency phenothiazine unique for combined sedative + analgesic + antiemetic use in palliative care; heavy orthostasis/sedation, mild EPS.
examHooks: Which Canadian low-potency phenothiazine is prized in palliative care for combined sedation, analgesia and antiemesis? (methotrimeprazine/levomepromazine).
counsellingPoints: Expect significant drowsiness.
Rise slowly — risk of fainting.
Report muscle stiffness or abnormal movements.

vignettes.stem: A palliative-care patient has terminal restlessness, intractable nausea and pain. The team selects a low-potency phenothiazine with sedative, antiemetic AND analgesic properties, often given subcutaneously. Which drug is it?
vignettes.explanation: Methotrimeprazine is a low-potency phenothiazine with marked sedation plus intrinsic analgesic and antiemetic activity, making it a palliative-care staple (frequently SC). High-potency agents (haloperidol, fluphenazine, pimozide) lack this analgesic profile and cause more EPS.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Analyses of seventeen placebo-controlled trials (modal duration of 10 weeks), largely in patients taking atypical antipsychotic drugs, revealed a risk of death in drug-treated patients of between 1.6 to 1.7 times the risk of death in placebo-treated patients. Over the course of a typical 10-week controlled trial, the rate of death in drug-treated patients was about 4.5%, compared to a rate of about 2.6% in the placebo group. Although the causes of death were varied, most of the deaths appeared to be either cardiovascular (e.g., heart failure, sudden death) or infectious (e.g., pneumonia) in nature. Observational studies suggest that, similar to atypical antipsychotic drugs, treatment with conventional antipsychotic drugs may increase mortality. The extent to which the findings of increased mortality in observational studies may be attributed to the antipsychotic drug as opposed to some characteristic(s) of the patients is not clear. ZYPREXA (olanzapine) is not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 ), Use in Specific Populations ( 8.5 ), and Patient Counseling Information ( 17 )] . When using olanzapine and …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE 10.1 Human Experience In premarketing trials involving more than 3100 patients and/or normal subjects, accidental or intentional acute overdosage of olanzapine was identified in 67 patients. In the patient taking the largest identified amount, 300 mg, the only symptoms reported were drowsiness and slurred speech. In the limited number of patients who were evaluated in hospitals, including the patient taking 300 mg, there were no observations indicating an adverse change in laboratory analytes or ECG. Vital signs were usually within normal limits following overdoses. In postmarketing reports of overdose with olanzapine alone, symptoms have been reported in the majority of …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (5 MG, 10 MG, 7.5 MG, 2.5 MG, 20 MG, 15 MG, 15.0 MG, 5.0 MG, 10.0 MG, 20.0 MG)
Tablet (Orally-Disintegrating) (5 MG, 10 MG, 7.5 MG, 2.5 MG, 20 MG, 15 MG, 15.0 MG, 5.0 MG, 10.0 MG, 20.0 MG)
moa: Combined 5-HT2A/D2 antagonist with broad additional H1, muscarinic, 5-HT2C and α1 blockade.
receptorProfile: Broad ("dirty") receptor profile: potent H1 (sedation, weight gain), muscarinic (anticholinergic), 5-HT2C (weight/appetite), D2, α1. Low EPS/prolactin at usual doses.
onsetOfEffect: Days–weeks; calming/sedation earlier.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP1A2 (major, plus direct glucuronidation and minor 2D6). Smoking induces 1A2 → lowers levels; fluvoxamine (1A2 inhibitor) raises them.
foodEffect: No clinically significant food effect.
approvedPopulations: Adults; orally-disintegrating (Zydis) and short-acting IM available.
startingDose: 5–10 mg PO once daily.
titration: Adjust by 5 mg at ≥1-week intervals; usual 10–20 mg/day.
maxDose: 20 mg/day (higher doses used off-label with limited evidence).
frequencyTiming: Once daily, usually at bedtime (sedating).
administrationNotes: Short-acting IM for agitation and a long-acting IM (olanzapine pamoate) that carries a post-injection delirium/sedation syndrome risk requiring monitored administration.

Over the course of a typical 10-week controlled trial, the rate of death in drug-treated patients was about 4.5%, compared to a rate of about 2.6% in the placebo group. Although the causes of death were varied, most of the deaths appeared to be either cardiovascular (e.g., heart failure, sudden death) or infectious (e.g., pneumonia) in nature. Observational studies suggest that, similar to atypical antipsychotic drugs, treatment with …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS None with olanzapine monotherapy. When using olanzapine and fluoxetine in combination, also refer to the Contraindications section of the package insert for Symbyax. For specific information about the contraindications of lithium or valproate, refer to the Contraindications section of the package inserts for these other products. None with olanzapine monotherapy.( 4 ) When using olanzapine and fluoxetine in combination, also refer to the Contraindications section of the package insert for Symbyax ® . ( 4 ) When using olanzapine in combination with lithium or valproate, refer to the Contraindications section of the package inserts for those products. ( 4 )
commonSE: Marked weight gain and increased appetite
seriousAE: Severe metabolic syndrome (among the worst SGAs, with clozapine)
overdoseLethality: Sedation, tachycardia, hypotension, anticholinergic effects; deaths mostly with co-ingestants. Combining IM olanzapine with parenteral benzodiazepines risks excessive sedation and cardiorespiratory depression.
ongoingMonitoring: Weight frequently early on; fasting glucose and lipids at 12 weeks then annually (sooner if gaining weight) given high metabolic risk; AIMS for tardive dyskinesia; LFTs if indicated.
majorDDI: Smoking (1A2 induction) lowers levels — cessation raises them
Additive CNS depression; IM olanzapine + parenteral benzodiazepine (cardiorespiratory depression)
contraindicatedCombos: Co-administration of IM olanzapine with parenteral benzodiazepines is not recommended (excessive sedation/cardiorespiratory depression)
pregnancy: No clear major teratogen signal; substantial gestational weight gain / glucose effects; neonatal EPS/withdrawal in third trimester. Tie to CANMAT perinatal guidance.
lactation: Excreted in milk; monitor infant for sedation.
geriatric: Increased mortality/stroke in dementia; anticholinergic and orthostatic burden — use with caution.
pearls: One of the two highest metabolic-risk SGAs (with clozapine) — big weight gain, dyslipidemia, hyperglycemia/DKA.
Very sedating (H1) with low EPS and low prolactin.
Do not combine IM olanzapine with a parenteral benzodiazepine (cardiorespiratory depression); and remember smoking lowers levels via CYP1A2.
mnemonics: "OLANZapine = ObesitY" — highest weight gain of the mainstream SGAs (clozapine aside).
confusables: Olanzapine vs clozapine — both thienobenzodiazepines with the worst metabolic burden, but clozapine is reserved (agranulocytosis) and uniquely effective in resistant illness.
Olanzapine vs quetiapine — both sedating and metabolic; olanzapine causes more weight gain and has greater efficacy.
classDifferentiators: High efficacy, very sedating, low EPS/prolactin, but among the worst metabolically.
examHooks: Which mainstream SGA (excluding clozapine) causes the most weight gain and metabolic derangement? (olanzapine)
counsellingPoints: Expect increased appetite/weight — diet, exercise, and monitoring matter.
We will check your glucose and cholesterol regularly.
Very sedating — usually taken at bedtime.

vignettes.stem: A 30-year-old with schizophrenia gains 9 kg over 3 months on an antipsychotic, with new fasting hyperglycemia and elevated triglycerides. Which agent is the most likely cause among these?
vignettes.explanation: Olanzapine (with clozapine) carries the highest risk of weight gain, hyperglycemia and dyslipidemia; ziprasidone, aripiprazole and lurasidone are weight/metabolically favourable.

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Paliperidone extended-release tablets are contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to either paliperidone or risperidone, or to any of the excipients in the paliperidone extended-release tablet formulation. Hypersensitivity reactions, including anaphylactic reactions and angioedema, have been reported in patients treated with risperidone and in patients treated with paliperidone. Paliperidone is a metabolite of risperidone. Known hypersensitivity to paliperidone, risperidone, or to any excipients in paliperidone. (4)

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE 10.1 Human Experience While experience with paliperidone overdose is limited, among the few cases of overdose reported in pre-marketing trials, the highest estimated ingestion of paliperidone was 405 mg. Observed signs and symptoms included extrapyramidal symptoms and gait unsteadiness. Other potential signs and symptoms include those resulting from an exaggeration of paliperidone’s known pharmacological effects, i.e., drowsiness and somnolence, tachycardia and hypotension, and QT prolongation. Torsade de pointes and ventricular fibrillation have been reported in a patient in the setting of overdose. Paliperidone is the major active metabolite of risperidone. Overdose …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Centrally-acting drugs: Due to CNS effects, use caution in combination. Avoid alcohol. (7.1) Drugs that may cause orthostatic hypotension: An additive effect may be observed when co-administered with paliperidone. (7.1) Strong CYP3A4/P-glycoprotein (P-gp) inducers: It may be necessary to increase the dose of paliperidone when a strong inducer of both CYP3A4 and P-gp (e.g., carbamazepine) is co-administered. Conversely, on discontinuation of the strong inducer, it may be necessary to decrease the dose of paliperidone. formulationsCanada: Suspension (Extended-Release) (150 MG, 6 MG, 75 MG, 50 MG, 25 MG, 100 MG, 350 MG, 263 MG, 12 MG, 3 MG, 9 MG, 525 MG, 175 MG)
Tablet (Extended-Release) (150 MG, 6 MG, 75 MG, 50 MG, 25 MG, 100 MG, 350 MG, 263 MG, 12 MG, 3 MG, 9 MG, 525 MG, 175 MG)
moa: Combined 5-HT2A/D2 antagonist; it is the active 9-hydroxy metabolite of risperidone.
receptorProfile: D2 and 5-HT2A antagonism with α and H1 blockade — profile similar to risperidone, including dose-dependent EPS and hyperprolactinemia.
onsetOfEffect: Days–weeks; full effect ~2–4 wk.
halfLife: Oral extended-release ~23 h; LAI forms far longer (Sustenna ~weeks, Trinza ~months).
metabolism: Undergoes LIMITED hepatic metabolism; ~59% excreted UNCHANGED in urine — fewer CYP interactions than risperidone.
elimination: Predominantly renal (unchanged drug) — dose must be reduced in renal impairment.
foodEffect: OROS extended-release: food increases absorption; administer consistently (e.g., with breakfast).
offLabel: Maintenance via LAI to support adherence
approvedPopulations: Adults; adolescents for schizophrenia in some labels.
startingDose: 6 mg PO once daily in the morning (extended-release; no initial titration required).
titration: Adjust in 3 mg increments at intervals ≥5 days if needed; usual range 3–12 mg/day.
maxDose: 12 mg/day (oral ER).
frequencyTiming: Once daily in the morning (OROS delivery).
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce dose in renal impairment (CrCl 50–79: max 6 mg; CrCl <50: max 3 mg); not recommended in severe renal impairment. No adjustment for mild–moderate hepatic impairment.
administrationNotes: Swallow OROS tablet whole (non-absorbable ghost shell passed in stool). LAIs: Sustenna monthly, Trinza every 3 months.
boxedWarning: WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Paliperidone extended-release tablets are not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ]. WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Paliperidone is not approved for use in patients with dementia-related psychosis. (5.1)
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Paliperidone extended-release tablets are contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to either paliperidone or risperidone, or to any of the excipients in the paliperidone extended-release tablet formulation. Hypersensitivity reactions, including anaphylactic reactions and angioedema, have been reported in patients treated with risperidone and in patients treated with paliperidone. Paliperidone is a metabolite of risperidone. Known hypersensitivity to paliperidone, risperidone, or to any excipients in paliperidone. (4)
overdoseLethality: Relatively low alone; sedation, EPS, hypotension, QT — supportive care.
ongoingMonitoring: Renal function (dosing); metabolic panel at ~12 weeks then annually; AIMS for tardive dyskinesia; prolactin if symptomatic; BP.
majorDDI: Fewer CYP interactions than risperidone (mostly renal clearance)
pregnancy: As with risperidone, third-trimester neonatal EPS/withdrawal risk; weigh untreated illness. Tie to CANMAT perinatal guidance.
lactation: Excreted in milk; monitor infant.
geriatric: Increased mortality/stroke in dementia psychosis; reduce for age-related renal decline.
cardiac: Modest QT prolongation.
pearls: It is 9-hydroxyrisperidone — the active metabolite of risperidone, so effects mirror risperidone (EPS, prolactin).
Renally excreted largely unchanged → few CYP interactions (useful with hepatic impairment or polypharmacy) BUT must be dose-reduced for renal function.
LAI options (Sustenna monthly, Trinza every 3 months) are major adherence tools.
mnemonics: "PALIperidone → PEE it out" — predominantly renal (unchanged) elimination; risperidone → liver (CYP2D6).
confusables: Paliperidone vs risperidone — same pharmacology; paliperidone is renally cleared (few CYP interactions) and needs renal dose adjustment.
classDifferentiators: Renally-cleared risperidone metabolite: minimal CYP interactions but renal dose adjustment required; extensive LAI options.
examHooks: Which antipsychotic is eliminated largely unchanged by the kidneys and needs renal dose adjustment? (paliperidone)
counsellingPoints: Take in the morning, consistently with respect to food.
You may see the empty tablet shell in your stool — the drug has been absorbed.
Kidney function affects your dose.

vignettes.stem: A patient with schizophrenia has significant hepatic impairment and is on several CYP-inhibiting medications. Which oral antipsychotic is least dependent on hepatic CYP metabolism, being largely excreted unchanged renally?
vignettes.explanation: Paliperidone (9-hydroxyrisperidone) undergoes limited hepatic metabolism and is excreted ~59% unchanged in urine, minimizing CYP interactions — but it requires renal dose adjustment.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING Increased Mortality in Elderly Patients with Dementia-Related Psychosis Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Analyses of seventeen placebo-controlled trials (modal duration of 10 weeks), largely in patients taking atypical antipsychotic drugs, revealed a risk of death in drug-treated patients of between 1.6 to 1.7 times the risk of death in placebo-treated patients. Over the course of a typical 10-week controlled trial, the rate of death in drug-treated patients was about 4.5%, compared to a rate of about 2.6% in the placebo group. Although the causes of death were varied, most of the deaths appeared to be either cardiovascular (e.g., heart failure, sudden death) or infectious (e.g., pneumonia) in nature. Observational studies suggest that, similar to atypical antipsychotic drugs, treatment with conventional antipsychotic drugs may increase mortality. The extent to which the findings of increased mortality in observational studies may be attributed to the antipsychotic drug as opposed to some characteristic(s) of the patients is not clear. Perphenazine tablets, USP are not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis ( see WARNINGS ).

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Perphenazine products are contraindicated in comatose or greatly obtunded patients and in patients receiving large doses of central nervous system depressants (barbiturates, alcohol, narcotics, analgesics, or antihistamines); in the presence of existing blood dyscrasias, bone marrow depression, or liver damage; and in patients who have shown hypersensitivity to perphenazine products, their components, or related compounds. Perphenazine products are also contraindicated in patients with suspected or established subcortical brain damage, with or without hypothalamic damage, since a hyperthermic reaction with temperatures in excess of 104°F may occur in such patients, sometimes not until 14 to 16 hours after drug administration. Total body ice-packing is recommended for such a reaction; antipyretics may also be useful.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Dosage must be individualized and adjusted according to the severity of the condition and the response obtained. As with all potent drugs, the best dose is the lowest dose that will produce the desired clinical effect. Since extrapyramidal symptoms increase in frequency and severity with increased dosage, it is important to employ the lowest effective dose. These symptoms have disappeared upon reduction of dosage, withdrawal of the drug, or administration of an antiparkinsonian agent. Prolonged administration of doses exceeding 24 mg daily should be reserved for hospitalized patients or patients under continued observation for early detection and management of …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE In the event of overdosage, emergency treatment should be started immediately. Consultation with a poison center should be considered. All patients suspected of having taken an overdose should be hospitalized as soon as possible.

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions Metabolism of a number of medications, including antipsychotics, antidepressants, ß- blockers, and antiarrhythmics, occurs through the cytochrome P450 2D6 isoenzyme (debrisoquine hydroxylase). Approximately 10% of the Caucasian population has reduced activity of this enzyme, so-called "poor" metabolizers. Among other populations the prevalence is not known. Poor metabolizers demonstrate higher plasma concentrations of antipsychotic drugs at usual doses, which may correlate with emergence of side effects. In one study of 45 elderly patients suffering from dementia treated with perphenazine, the 5 patients who were prospectively identified as poor P450 2D6 metabolizers had …

moa: Mid-potency piperazine phenothiazine; D2 receptor antagonist.
receptorProfile: Moderate–high D2 affinity; intermediate H1/M1/α1 blockade — fewer autonomic effects than chlorpromazine, more EPS.
onsetOfEffect: Antipsychotic effect over days–weeks.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP2D6 (major); poor metabolizers/2D6 inhibitors raise levels.

titration: Increase to response; usual 12–24 mg/day.
maxDose: Up to ~64 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: BID–TID dosing.
administrationNotes: The FGA comparator in the CATIE effectiveness trial — performed comparably to several SGAs at much lower cost.

majorDDI: CYP2D6 inhibitors (paroxetine, fluoxetine, bupropion) raise levels
pregnancy: Neonatal EPS/withdrawal with late exposure; weigh maternal illness.
lactation: Excreted in milk; monitor infant.
geriatric: Increased mortality in dementia; EPS and orthostasis; caution.
pearls: The CATIE trial FGA — mid-potency perphenazine was roughly as effective as several SGAs and highly cost-effective, challenging assumptions that newer = better.
Sits between chlorpromazine and haloperidol: moderate EPS, moderate sedation/autonomic effects.
CYP2D6-metabolized — interactions with 2D6 inhibitors matter.
mnemonics: "PERphenazine = the PERformer in CATIE" — the mid-potency FGA that held its own against SGAs.
confusables: Perphenazine (antipsychotic) is combined with amitriptyline in older fixed-dose products — don't confuse the combo with monotherapy.
classDifferentiators: Mid-potency piperazine phenothiazine; the CATIE FGA benchmark; balanced side-effect profile.
examHooks: Which FGA served as the first-generation comparator in CATIE and performed comparably to SGAs? (perphenazine).
counsellingPoints: Report abnormal movements or restlessness.
Effect builds over weeks.
Rise slowly if lightheaded.

vignettes.stem: In the CATIE schizophrenia effectiveness trial, one first-generation antipsychotic was compared with several second-generation agents and proved similarly effective and more cost-effective. Which drug was it?
vignettes.explanation: CATIE used mid-potency perphenazine as its FGA comparator; outcomes were broadly comparable to several SGAs (except olanzapine on some measures) at lower cost, an important health-economics teaching point.
vignettes.testedFact: Perphenazine as the CATIE FGA comparator

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE 10.1 Human Experience The pre-marketing clinical trials involving NUPLAZID in approximately 1200 subjects and patients do not provide information regarding symptoms with overdose. In healthy subject studies, dose-limiting nausea and vomiting were observed. 10.2 Management of Overdose There are no known specific antidotes for NUPLAZID. In managing overdose, cardiovascular monitoring should commence immediately and should include continuous ECG monitoring to detect possible arrhythmias [see Warnings and Precautions (5.2) ] . If antiarrhythmic therapy is administered, disopyramide, procainamide, and quinidine should not be used, as they have the potential for QT-prolonging …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary There are no data on NUPLAZID use in pregnant women that would allow assessment of the drug-associated risk of major congenital malformations or miscarriage. In animal reproduction studies, no adverse developmental effects were seen when pimavanserin was administered orally to rats or rabbits during the period of organogenesis at doses up to 10- or 12-times the maximum recommended human dose (MRHD) of 34 mg/day, respectively. Administration of pimavanserin to pregnant rats during pregnancy and lactation resulted in maternal toxicity and lower pup survival and body weight at doses which are 2-times the MRHD of 34 mg/day [see Data ] . moa: Selective serotonin 5-HT2A receptor INVERSE AGONIST/antagonist (with some 5-HT2C activity) and NO dopamine (D2) receptor activity. By dampening 5-HT2A-mediated signalling rather than blocking dopamine, it treats hallucinations/delusions of Parkinson disease psychosis without worsening motor function — the key advantage over D2-blocking antipsychotics.
receptorProfile: High-affinity 5-HT2A inverse agonist; lower 5-HT2C activity; essentially no affinity for D2, muscarinic, histaminergic, or adrenergic receptors — hence no dopaminergic motor worsening and no anticholinergic/α1 burden.
onsetOfEffect: Improvement in psychotic symptoms over ~2–6 weeks.
halfLife: Long — parent ~57 hours and active metabolite (AC-279) ~200 hours; once-daily dosing, slow accumulation.
timeToSteadyState: Several weeks due to the long half-life.
metabolism: Hepatic, predominantly CYP3A4 (and 3A5) to an active metabolite; a CYP3A4 substrate — strong 3A4 inhibitors/inducers change exposure significantly.
foodEffect: May be taken with or without food.
offLabel: Dementia-related psychosis (studied; not approved and carries the class mortality warning)
approvedPopulations: Adults with Parkinson disease psychosis (hallucinations and delusions).
startingDose: 34 mg PO once daily (no titration required).
titration: None — start and maintain at 34 mg once daily.
maxDose: 34 mg/day (reduce to 10 mg/day when used with a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor).
frequencyTiming: Once daily, with or without food.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Not recommended in severe hepatic impairment; no adjustment for mild–moderate renal impairment.
administrationNotes: Fixed dose; the main dose adjustment is halving (to 10 mg) with strong 3A4 inhibitors.
boxedWarning: WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. NUPLAZID is not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia who experience psychosis unless their hallucinations and delusions are related to Parkinson's disease [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ] . WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning . Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. NUPLAZID is not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia who experience psychosis unless their hallucinations and delusions are related to Parkinson's disease. ( 5.1 )
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS NUPLAZID is contraindicated in patients with a history of a hypersensitivity reaction to pimavanserin or any of its components. Rash, urticaria, and reactions consistent with angioedema (e.g., tongue swelling, circumoral edema, throat tightness, and dyspnea) have been reported [see Adverse Reactions (6.2) ] . Known hypersensitivity to NUPLAZID or any of its components. ( 4 )
seriousAE: Increased mortality in elderly with dementia-related psychosis (boxed class warning)
overdoseLethality: Overdose data limited; QT prolongation and CNS effects are the concerns — monitor ECG; no specific antidote.
discontinuationSyndrome: No defined withdrawal syndrome; psychosis may recur.
baselineWorkup: Consider baseline ECG/electrolytes if QT risk factors or on other QT-prolonging drugs
ongoingMonitoring: Monitor for QT prolongation (ECG if risk factors), mental status/confusion, and effect on psychosis; watch Parkinson motor status (should not worsen).
majorDDI: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g., ketoconazole, clarithromycin) — reduce pimavanserin to 10 mg/day
Strong CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampin, carbamazepine) — reduce efficacy; avoid
Other QT-prolonging drugs (Class 1A/III antiarrhythmics, some antibiotics/antipsychotics) — additive QT risk
Additive CNS effects with other sedatives
contraindicatedCombos: Combination with other QT-prolonging agents where risk is significant (per label caution)
pregnancy: Limited human data; use only if benefit justifies risk. Note the population (older PD patients) makes pregnancy uncommon.
lactation: Limited data; decide considering benefit/relevance.
geriatric: Primary population; boxed warning for increased mortality in dementia-related psychosis — reserve for PD psychosis; watch confusion, falls, QT.
renalHepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment.
cardiac: Dose-dependent QT prolongation — avoid/caution with other QT drugs and in congenital long-QT.
pearls: US-only — for awareness (Nuplazid): approved for hallucinations/delusions of Parkinson disease psychosis; not available in Canada.

The mechanism is notable: a SELECTIVE 5-HT2A inverse agonist with NO D2 activity — so it controls psychosis without blocking dopamine, uniquely avoiding worsening of Parkinsonian motor symptoms (the reason typical/atypical D2 antipsychotics are problematic in PD).
QT prolongation is the defining safety caution; carries the class boxed warning for increased mortality in elderly dementia-related psychosis (it is NOT approved for dementia psychosis).
mnemonics: "Pimavanserin = Parkinson's psychosis, Pure 5-HT2A, no dopamine" — three P's; spares motor function.
confusables: Pimavanserin vs quetiapine/clozapine for PD psychosis — all avoid strong D2 blockade, but pimavanserin has NO D2 activity at all (pure 5-HT2A inverse agonist); quetiapine/clozapine are low-D2 atypicals still with off-target (sedation, metabolic, agranulocytosis for clozapine) effects.
classDifferentiators: The only antipsychotic-class agent that is a selective 5-HT2A inverse agonist with zero dopaminergic activity, purpose-built for Parkinson disease psychosis; profile dominated by QT and the dementia-mortality boxed warning rather than EPS/metabolic effects.
examHooks: Which agent treats Parkinson disease psychosis via selective 5-HT2A inverse agonism without any D2 blockade (so it does not worsen motor symptoms)? (pimavanserin)
counsellingPoints: Take once daily; it can take a few weeks to work.
It should not worsen your Parkinson movement symptoms.
Report fainting or palpitations (heart-rhythm effect).
Tell your prescriber about all medications, especially those affecting the liver enzyme CYP3A4 or the heart rhythm.

vignettes.stem: A 74-year-old with Parkinson disease develops distressing visual hallucinations. Standard antipsychotics previously worsened his rigidity and bradykinesia. Which agent controls the psychosis while sparing motor function because it has no dopamine-receptor activity?
vignettes.explanation: Pimavanserin is a selective 5-HT2A inverse agonist with no D2 activity, so it treats Parkinson disease psychosis without the dopamine blockade that worsens parkinsonism; the other options are D2 antagonists that worsen motor symptoms.
vignettes.testedFact: Pimavanserin selective 5-HT2A inverse agonism / no D2 → treats PD psychosis without motor worsening

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Pimozide is contraindicated in the treatment of simple tics or tics other than those associated with Tourette’s Disorder. Pimozide should not be used in patients taking drugs that may, themselves, cause motor and phonic tics (e.g., pemoline, methylphenidate and amphetamines) until such patients have been withdrawn from these drugs to determine whether or not the drugs, rather than Tourette’s Disorder, are responsible for the tics. Because pimozide prolongs the QT interval of the electrocardiogram it is contraindicated in patients with congenital long QT syndrome, patients with a history of cardiac arrhythmias, patients taking other drugs which prolong the QT interval of the electrocardiogram or patients with known hypokalemia or hypomagnesemia (see also PRECAUTIONS - Drug Interactions ). Pimozide is contraindicated in patients with severe toxic central nervous system depression or comatose states from any cause. Pimozide is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to it. As it is not known whether cross-sensitivity exists among the antipsychotics, pimozide should be used with appropriate caution in patients who have demonstrated hypersensitivity to other antipsychotic drugs. Ventricular arrhythmias have been rarely associated with the use of macrolide antibiotics in patients with prolonged QT intervals, as might be produced by pimozide. Specifically, …

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE & ADMINISTRATION General The suppression of tics by pimozide tablets requires a slow and gradual introduction of the drug. The patient’s dose should be carefully adjusted to a point where the suppression of tics and the relief afforded is balanced against the untoward side effects of the drug. An ECG should be done at baseline and periodically thereafter, especially during the period of dose adjustment (see WARNINGS and PRECAUTIONS - Laboratory Tests ). Periodic attempts should be made to reduce the dosage of pimozide tablets to see whether or not tics persist at the level and extent first identified. In attempts to reduce the dosage of pimozide tablets consideration should be given …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS The use of pimozide in the treatment of Tourette’s Disorder involves different risk/benefit considerations than when antipsychotic drugs are used to treat other conditions. Consequently, a decision to use pimozide should take into consideration the following (see also PRECAUTIONS - Information for Patients ). Tardive Dyskinesia A syndrome consisting of potentially irreversible, involuntary, dyskinetic movements may develop in patients treated with antipsychotic drugs. Although the prevalence of the syndrome appears to be highest among the elderly, especially elderly women, it is impossible to rely upon prevalence estimates to predict, at the inception of antipsychotic treatment, …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE In general, the signs and symptoms of overdosage with pimozide would be an exaggeration of known pharmacologic effects and adverse reactions, the most prominent of which would be: 1) electrocardiographic abnormalities, 2) severe extrapyramidal reactions, 3) hypotension, 4) a comatose state with respiratory depression. In the event of overdosage, gastric lavage, establishment of a patent airway and, if necessary, mechanically-assisted respiration are advised. Electrocardiographic monitoring should commence immediately and continue until the ECG parameters are within the normal range. Hypotension and circulatory collapse may be counteracted by use of intravenous fluids, plasma, or …

majorDDI.sourceText: DRUG INTERACTIONS Because pimozide prolongs the QT interval of the electrocardiogram, an additive effect on QT interval would be anticipated if administered with other drugs, such as phenothiazines, tricyclic antidepressants or antiarrhythmic agents, which prolong the QT interval. Accordingly, pimozide should not be given with dofetilide, sotalol, quinidine, other Class Ia and III anti-arrhythmics, mesoridazine, thioridazine, chlorpromazine, droperidol, sparfloxacin, gatifloxacin, moxifloxacin, halofantrine, mefloquine, pentamidine, arsenic trioxide, levomethadyl acetate, dolasetron mesylate, probucol, tacrolimus, ziprasidone, or other drugs that have demonstrated QT prolongation as one of …

pregnancy.sourceText: PREGNANCY Teratogenic Effects: Reproduction studies performed in rats and rabbits at oral doses up to 8 times the maximum human dose did not reveal evidence of teratogenicity. In the rat, however, this multiple of the human dose resulted in decreased pregnancies and in the retarded development of fetuses. These effects are thought to be due to an inhibition or delay in implantation which is also observed in rodents administered other antipsychotic drugs. In the rabbit, maternal toxicity, mortality, decreased weight gain, and embryotoxicity including increased resorptions were dose-related. Because animal reproduction studies are not always predictive of human response, pimozide should be …

moa: Diphenylbutylpiperidine; potent, selective D2 receptor antagonist (also blocks cardiac calcium channels → QT prolongation).
receptorProfile: High D2 affinity; low sedation/anticholinergic; the defining feature is marked, dose-dependent QT prolongation.
onsetOfEffect: Antipsychotic/anti-tic effect over days–weeks.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP3A4 (major) and CYP2D6. Strong inhibitors of either markedly raise levels and QT risk.
startingDose: Tourette: 1–2 mg/day (0.05 mg/kg in children), often at bedtime.
titration: Increase slowly every few days with ECG surveillance.
maxDose: Tourette: up to ~10 mg/day (or 0.2 mg/kg); use the lowest effective dose.
frequencyTiming: Once daily (long half-life); ECG before and during dose increases.
administrationNotes: Reserved largely for Tourette syndrome when other agents fail. Baseline and follow-up ECGs are mandatory; avoid with QT-prolonging or CYP3A4/2D6-inhibiting drugs and grapefruit juice.
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Pimozide is contraindicated in the treatment of simple tics or tics other than those associated with Tourette’s Disorder. Pimozide should not be used in patients taking drugs that may, themselves, cause motor and phonic tics (e.g., pemoline, methylphenidate and amphetamines) until such patients have been withdrawn from these drugs to determine whether or not the drugs, rather than Tourette’s Disorder, are responsible for the tics. Because pimozide prolongs the QT interval of the electrocardiogram it is contraindicated in patients with congenital long QT syndrome, patients with a history of cardiac arrhythmias, patients taking other drugs which prolong the QT interval of the electrocardiogram or patients with known hypokalemia or hypomagnesemia (see also PRECAUTIONS - Drug Interactions ). Pimozide is contraindicated in patients with severe toxic central nervous system …
seriousAE: QT prolongation / torsades de pointes (sudden death reported)
Increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis
overdoseLethality: Higher arrhythmia risk than most FGAs — QT prolongation/torsades; continuous ECG monitoring in overdose.
baselineWorkup: Review concomitant QT drugs and CYP3A4/2D6 inhibitors
ongoingMonitoring: Serial ECGs with dose changes; electrolytes; AIMS for TD.
majorDDI: CYP3A4 inhibitors — macrolides (clarithromycin/erythromycin), azole antifungals, protease inhibitors, grapefruit juice (CONTRAINDICATED)
pregnancy: Limited data; weigh maternal illness; neonatal EPS with late exposure.
lactation: Excreted in milk; monitor infant.
geriatric: Increased mortality in dementia; QT and EPS risk; caution.
cardiac: The high-QT FGA — dose-dependent QT prolongation with torsades/sudden death; ECG-driven dosing.
pearls: The QT champion among FGAs — dose-dependent QT prolongation with torsades; ECG monitoring is mandatory.
CYP3A4-dependent metabolism makes macrolides, azole antifungals, protease inhibitors and grapefruit juice CONTRAINDICATED (levels + QT rise).
Niche indication: Tourette syndrome refractory to other agents (a diphenylbutylpiperidine).
mnemonics: "PIMozide = Prolongs the QT, Interacts via 3A4, Manages tics."
confusables: Pimozide vs primidone (anticonvulsant) — similar names, unrelated drugs.
classDifferentiators: Diphenylbutylpiperidine with the strongest QT signal and the most restrictive CYP3A4/2D6 interaction list; Tourette niche.
examHooks: Which antipsychotic is contraindicated with clarithromycin because of QT prolongation via CYP3A4 inhibition? (pimozide).
counsellingPoints: Avoid grapefruit juice.
Tell every prescriber you take pimozide (antibiotic/antifungal interactions).
Report fainting or palpitations; keep scheduled ECGs.

vignettes.stem: A patient with Tourette syndrome on pimozide is prescribed clarithromycin for a chest infection. Why is this combination contraindicated?
options: Clarithromycin induces CYP3A4, lowering pimozide efficacy
Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4, raising pimozide levels and QT/torsades risk
vignettes.answer: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4, raising pimozide levels and QT/torsades risk
vignettes.explanation: Pimozide is metabolized by CYP3A4 and prolongs QT dose-dependently. CYP3A4 inhibitors (macrolides, azoles, protease inhibitors, grapefruit) raise pimozide levels, increasing the risk of torsades — hence the contraindication.

moa: Piperidine phenothiazine (Canada-specific; a.k.a. pipotiazine palmitate); D2 receptor antagonist available as a long-acting injectable.
receptorProfile: Moderate–high D2 affinity with some H1/α1 blockade; used essentially only as a maintenance depot.
onsetOfEffect: Maintenance antipsychotic effect over weeks; depot pharmacokinetics.
halfLife: Palmitate depot: sustained release over ~3–4 weeks.
metabolism: Hepatic.
startingDose: Palmitate LAI: individualized (e.g., ~25–50 mg IM test dose), titrated per response.
titration: Adjust dose/interval to response and tolerability.
maxDose: Per monograph (commonly up to ~200 mg per injection interval).
frequencyTiming: Deep IM palmitate depot approximately every 4 weeks.
administrationNotes: Canada-specific palmitate long-acting injectable (Piportil L4) for maintenance of schizophrenia; the palmitate ester gives roughly monthly dosing. Establish tolerability (ideally with an oral phenothiazine) before committing to depot.
seriousAE: Increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis
overdoseLethality: EPS, sedation, hypotension, QT prolongation; prolonged depot action complicates management.
ongoingMonitoring: AIMS for TD; EPS; adherence to injection schedule; BP.
pregnancy: Neonatal EPS/withdrawal with late exposure; weigh maternal illness.
lactation: Excreted in milk; monitor infant.
geriatric: Increased mortality in dementia; EPS/orthostasis; the fixed depot is hard to reverse — caution.
pearls: Canada-specific PALMITATE long-acting injectable (Piportil L4) — roughly MONTHLY (q4-week) IM maintenance dosing.
Distinguish the depot esters: decanoates (haloperidol, fluphenazine, zuclopenthixol, flupenthixol) vs pipotiazine PALMITATE.
Used essentially only as a maintenance depot — establish tolerability first, since depot effects can't be quickly withdrawn.
mnemonics: "PIPOtiazine PALMitate = a PALMful once a month" — the ~q4-week Canadian phenothiazine depot.
confusables: Pipotiazine PALMITATE vs the DECANOATE depots (haloperidol/fluphenazine/thioxanthenes) — different ester, similar monthly-ish depot concept.
classDifferentiators: Canadian phenothiazine notable specifically as a palmitate-ester LAI dosed about every 4 weeks.
examHooks: Which Canadian FGA depot uses a PALMITATE ester (vs the more common decanoate)? (pipotiazine).
counsellingPoints: Attend scheduled monthly injections.
Report stiffness, restlessness, or abnormal movements.
Depot levels persist for weeks after each dose.

vignettes.stem: A stable patient with schizophrenia and poor oral adherence is switched to a Canadian long-acting injectable dosed about every four weeks that uses a palmitate ester. Which agent is this?
vignettes.explanation: Pipotiazine palmitate (Piportil L4) is a Canada-specific phenothiazine LAI given roughly every 4 weeks; uniquely among the FGA depots here it uses a palmitate ester rather than a decanoate. Zuclopenthixol acetate is short-acting (acute phase), not maintenance.

boxedWarning.sourceText: BOXED WARNING Increased Mortality in Elderly Patients with Dementia-Related Psychosis Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Analyses of seventeen placebo-controlled trials (modal duration of 10 weeks), largely in patients taking atypical antipsychotic drugs, revealed a risk of death in drug-treated patients of between 1.6 to 1.7 times the risk of death in placebo-treated patients. Over the course of a typical 10-week controlled trial, the rate of death in drug-treated patients was about 4.5%, compared to a rate of about 2.6% in the placebo group. Although the causes of death were varied, most of the deaths appeared to be either cardiovascular (e.g., heart failure, sudden death) or infectious (e.g., pneumonia) in nature. Observational studies suggest that, similar to atypical antipsychotic drugs, treatment with conventional antipsychotic drugs may increase mortality. The extent to which the findings of increased mortality in observational studies may be attributed to the antipsychotic drug as opposed to some characteristic(s) of the patients is not clear. Prochlorperazine Edisylate Injection, USP is not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis (see WARNINGS ).

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Do not use in patients with known hypersensitivity to phenothiazines. Do not use in comatose states or in the presence of large amounts of central nervous system depressants (alcohol, barbiturates, narcotics, etc.). Do not use in pediatric surgery. Do not use in pediatric patients under 2 years of age or under 20 lbs. Do not use in children for conditions for which dosage has not been established.

If markedly discolored, solution should be discarded. Compatibility It is recommended that Prochlorperazine Edisylate Injection not be mixed with other agents in the syringe. Adults (For children's dosage and administration, see below.) Dosage should be increased more gradually in debilitated or emaciated patients. overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE (See also ADVERSE REACTIONS .) Symptoms Primarily involvement of the extrapyramidal mechanism producing some of the dystonic reactions described above. Symptoms of central nervous system depression to the point of somnolence or coma. Agitation and restlessness may also occur. Other possible manifestations include convulsions, EKG changes and cardiac arrhythmias, fever, and autonomic reactions such as hypotension, dry mouth and ileus. Treatment It is important to determine other medications taken by the patient since multiple drug therapy is common in overdosage situations. Treatment is essentially symptomatic and supportive. Keep patient under observation and maintain an open …
