moa: Piperazine phenothiazine used mainly as an ANTIEMETIC/anti-vertigo agent; D2 antagonism (chemoreceptor trigger zone) more than antipsychotic use.
receptorProfile: D2 antagonist (CTZ + vestibular pathways); some H1/M1 — but the clinical role is antiemesis, not psychosis.
onsetOfEffect: Antiemetic effect within ~30–60 min (oral/IM); faster IV.
metabolism: Hepatic (CYP2D6 and others).
startingDose: Nausea/vomiting: 5–10 mg PO TID–QID or IM; vertigo similar.
titration: Use short-term at the lowest effective antiemetic dose.
maxDose: Antiemetic use typically ≤40 mg/day; higher antipsychotic dosing is largely historical.
frequencyTiming: PO/IM/PR (suppository); IV in some settings (dystonia/hypotension risk).
administrationNotes: Primarily an antiemetic/anti-vertigo drug (Stemetil). Acute dystonic reactions — even after a single antiemetic dose — are a classic hazard, especially in young women.
boxedWarning: BOXED WARNING Increased Mortality in Elderly Patients with Dementia-Related Psychosis Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Analyses of seventeen placebo-controlled trials (modal duration of 10 weeks), largely in patients taking atypical antipsychotic drugs, revealed a risk of death in drug-treated patients of between 1.6 to 1.7 times the risk of death in placebo-treated patients. Over the course of a typical 10-week controlled trial, the rate of death in drug-treated patients was about 4.5%, compared to a rate of about 2.6% in the placebo group. Although the causes of death were varied, most of the deaths appeared to be either cardiovascular (e.g., heart failure, sudden death) or infectious (e.g., pneumonia) in nature. Observational studies suggest that, similar to atypical antipsychotic drugs, treatment …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Do not use in patients with known hypersensitivity to phenothiazines. Do not use in comatose states or in the presence of large amounts of central nervous system depressants (alcohol, barbiturates, narcotics, etc.). Do not use in pediatric surgery. Do not use in pediatric patients under 2 years of age or under 20 lbs. Do not use in children for conditions for which dosage has not been established.
seriousAE: Increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis
overdoseLethality: EPS/dystonia, sedation, hypotension, QT prolongation; supportive care with anticholinergic for dystonia.
ongoingMonitoring: Watch for acute dystonia after dosing; AIMS if prolonged use.
pregnancy: Sometimes used for hyperemesis; weigh benefit/risk; late exposure → neonatal EPS.
lactation: Excreted in milk; monitor infant.
geriatric: EPS and orthostasis; increased mortality in dementia; short-term antiemetic use only.
cardiac: QT prolongation possible.
pearls: Used mainly as an ANTIEMETIC / anti-vertigo agent (not primarily for psychosis) — but it is still a dopamine-blocking phenothiazine.
Acute dystonia can occur even after a single antiemetic dose, classically in young women — treat with an anticholinergic (benztropine) or diphenhydramine.
A good reminder that "antiemetic" dopamine blockers (also metoclopramide) carry EPS/TD risk.
mnemonics: "proCHLORPERazine → puke + spinning" (nausea/vertigo), but it can lock the neck (dystonia).
confusables: Prochlorperazine (antiemetic phenothiazine) vs chlorpromazine (antipsychotic) vs perphenazine — easily confused "-perazine/-promazine" names.
classDifferentiators: Phenothiazine whose main clinical use is antiemesis/vertigo; the exam angle is acute dystonia from a "just an antiemetic" dose.
examHooks: A young woman gets neck spasm and eye rolling after IV prochlorperazine for nausea — cause and treatment? (acute dystonia → benztropine/diphenhydramine).
counsellingPoints: Seek care for sudden muscle spasms of the neck/eyes/tongue.
Use short-term for nausea/vertigo.
May cause drowsiness and dizziness.

vignettes.stem: A 19-year-old woman given IV prochlorperazine for migraine-associated nausea develops involuntary neck twisting and an upward eye deviation 30 minutes later. What is the best immediate treatment?
vignettes.explanation: This is an acute dystonic reaction — a well-known EPS from dopamine-blocking antiemetics like prochlorperazine, most common in young patients (especially women). Treatment is an anticholinergic (benztropine) or diphenhydramine; dantrolene is for NMS.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS; and SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Increased Mortality in Elderly Patients with Dementia-Related Psychosis Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death [ see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ]. Quetiapine is not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis [ see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ]. Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies. These studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior with antidepressant use in patients over age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressant use in patients aged 65 and older [ see Warnings and Precautions (5.2) ]. In patients of all ages who are started on antidepressant therapy, monitor closely for worsening, and for emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Advise families and caregivers of the need for close observation and communication with the prescriber [ see Warnings and Precautions (5.2) ]. Quetiapine is not approved for use in pediatric patients under ten years of age [ see Use in Specific Populations (8.4) ]. WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS; …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE 10.1 Human Experience In clinical trials, survival has been reported in acute overdoses of up to 30 grams of quetiapine. Most patients who overdosed experienced no adverse reactions or recovered fully from the reported reactions. Death has been reported in a clinical trial following an overdose of 13.6 grams of quetiapine alone. In general, reported signs and symptoms were those resulting from an exaggeration of the drug’s known pharmacological effects, i.e., drowsiness, sedation, tachycardia, hypotension, and anticholinergic toxicity including coma and delirium. Patients with pre-existing severe cardiovascular disease may be at an increased risk of the effects of overdose …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (100 MG, 200 MG, 25 MG, 150 MG, 50 MG, 400 MG, 300 MG)
Tablet (Extended-Release) (100 MG, 200 MG, 25 MG, 150 MG, 50 MG, 400 MG, 300 MG)
Tablet (Immediate-Release) (100 MG, 200 MG, 25 MG, 150 MG, 50 MG, 400 MG, 300 MG)
moa: Combined 5-HT2A/D2 antagonist with low, transient (loose) D2 occupancy; active metabolite norquetiapine adds norepinephrine reuptake inhibition and 5-HT2C antagonism (antidepressant properties).
receptorProfile: Very high H1 (sedation), α1; loose/low D2 (low EPS and low prolactin); norquetiapine adds NET inhibition and 5-HT1A partial agonism.
onsetOfEffect: Sedation immediate; antipsychotic/antidepressant effect over weeks.
halfLife: Quetiapine ~6–7 h; norquetiapine ~9–12 h. XR permits once-daily dosing.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 (major). Strong 3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, ketoconazole) raise levels; inducers (carbamazepine, phenytoin) lower them substantially.
foodEffect: XR: take without food or with a light meal — a high-fat meal significantly raises Cmax. IR: with or without food.
approvedCanada: Adjunctive treatment of major depressive disorder (XR)
offLabel: Low-dose (25–50 mg) for insomnia (off-label; generally discouraged given side-effect burden)
canmatLineOfTherapy: Bipolar I — mania (acute): first-line (CANMAT/ISBD 2018 Bipolar Guidelines)
startingDose: Schizophrenia: 25 mg BID (IR) titrating up; bipolar depression: XR 50 mg → target 300 mg at bedtime.
titration: Titrate to reduce orthostasis; usual 300–600 mg/day (schizophrenia up to 800 mg).
maxDose: 800 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: IR divided BID; XR once daily at bedtime (sedating).
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce and titrate slowly in hepatic impairment (extensively hepatically metabolized).
administrationNotes: XR swallowed whole. Dose–effect ladder: low dose = sedative/anxiolytic, mid dose = antidepressant (norquetiapine NET), high dose = antipsychotic.
boxedWarning: WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS; and SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS Increased Mortality in Elderly Patients with Dementia-Related Psychosis Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death [ see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ]. Quetiapine is not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis [ see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ]. Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults in short-term studies. These studies did not show an increase in the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior with antidepressant use in patients over age 24; there was a reduction in risk with antidepressant use in patients aged 65 and older [ see Warnings and Precautions (5.2) ]. …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Hypersensitivity to quetiapine or to any excipients in the quetiapine formulation. Anaphylactic reactions have been reported in patients treated with quetiapine. Known hypersensitivity to quetiapine or any components in the formulation. ( 4 )
overdoseLethality: Sedation, tachycardia, hypotension, QT prolongation; more sedating in overdose than most SGAs but deaths uncommon alone.
baselineWorkup: Consider baseline lens (slit-lamp) examination for cataracts per label
ongoingMonitoring: Metabolic panel at 12 weeks then annually; periodic ophthalmologic (lens) examination per label; BP for orthostasis; AIMS for tardive dyskinesia; TSH if symptomatic.
majorDDI: CYP3A4 inducers (carbamazepine, phenytoin, rifampin) markedly lower levels
pregnancy: No clear major teratogen signal; often used in bipolar — weigh untreated illness. Tie to CANMAT perinatal guidance.
lactation: Low relative infant dose; monitor infant sedation.
geriatric: Increased mortality/stroke in dementia; orthostasis/sedation and falls — start low.
cardiac: Dose-related QT prolongation; orthostatic hypotension.
pearls: Very sedating (potent H1) with the LOWEST EPS/prolactin of these agents (loose D2 binding) → a go-to in Parkinson disease psychosis and in patients prone to EPS.
Dose-dependent identity: low = sedative, mid = antidepressant (norquetiapine NET inhibition), high = antipsychotic.
The label recommends periodic lens/cataract examination — a classic monitoring pearl.
mnemonics: "QUETiapine = QUIET" — H1 sedation, low EPS; watch the eyes (cataracts).
confusables: Quetiapine vs olanzapine — both sedating and metabolic, but quetiapine has lower EPS/prolactin and less weight gain, and carries the cataract/lens-exam caution.
classDifferentiators: Lowest EPS/prolactin (with clozapine); very sedating; broad bipolar approvals including bipolar depression; cataract monitoring.
examHooks: Which SGA is first-line for bipolar depression and preferred (with clozapine) in Parkinson psychosis for its very low EPS? (quetiapine)
counsellingPoints: Very sedating — usually taken at bedtime; rise slowly (orthostasis).
Take the XR form without a heavy/fatty meal.
Report vision changes; periodic eye checks are advised.

vignettes.stem: A 68-year-old with Parkinson disease develops medication-related psychosis. Which antipsychotic is preferred because of its very low likelihood of worsening parkinsonism?
vignettes.explanation: Quetiapine (and clozapine) have the lowest EPS liability owing to loose/low D2 occupancy, making them preferred in Parkinson disease psychosis; risperidone and haloperidol worsen motor symptoms.
vignettes.testedFact: Quetiapine low EPS in Parkinson psychosis

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Risperidone tablets are contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to either risperidone or paliperidone, or to any of the excipients in the risperidone tablets formulation. Hypersensitivity reactions, including anaphylactic reactions and angioedema, have been reported in patients treated with risperidone and in patients treated with paliperidone. Paliperidone is a metabolite of risperidone. Known hypersensitivity to risperidone, paliperidone, or to any excipients in risperidone tablets. ( 4 )

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE 10.1 Human Experience Premarketing experience included eight reports of acute risperidone tablets overdosage with estimated doses ranging from 20 to 300 mg and no fatalities. In general, reported signs and symptoms were those resulting from an exaggeration of the drug's known pharmacological effects, i.e., drowsiness and sedation, tachycardia and hypotension, and extrapyramidal symptoms. One case, involving an estimated overdose of 240 mg, was associated with hyponatremia, hypokalemia, prolonged QT, and widened QRS. Another case, involving an estimated overdose of 36 mg, was associated with a seizure. Postmarketing experience includes reports of acute risperidone tablets …

formulationsCanada: Solution (1 MG, 3 MG, 4 MG, 2 MG, 5 MG, 0.25 MG, 2.0 MG, 3.0 MG, 0.5 MG, 25 MG, 37.5 MG, 1.0 MG, 0.50 MG, 4.0 MG, 12.5 MG, 50 MG, 75 MG, 120 MG, 100 MG, 90 MG, 125 MG)
moa: Combined 5-HT2A and D2 receptor antagonist; the most D2-potent of the SGAs.
receptorProfile: High D2 and 5-HT2A affinity; α1/α2 and H1 antagonism; minimal muscarinic. High D2 potency drives dose-dependent EPS and hyperprolactinemia.
onsetOfEffect: Antipsychotic effect over days–weeks; full effect ~2–4 wk.
halfLife: Risperidone ~3 h; active metabolite 9-hydroxyrisperidone (paliperidone) ~21–24 h → "active moiety" ~20 h.
timeToSteadyState: ~1 day orally; LAI (Consta) requires 3 weeks of oral overlap and reaches steady state ~4–6 wk.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP2D6 → 9-hydroxyrisperidone (active). CYP2D6 poor metabolizers / inhibitors raise the parent level.
elimination: Renal (the active 9-OH moiety is renally cleared) — reduce dose in renal impairment.
foodEffect: May be taken with or without food.
canmatLineOfTherapy: Bipolar I — mania (acute): first-line (CANMAT/ISBD 2018 Bipolar Guidelines)
startingDose: 1–2 mg/day PO (0.5 mg BID in elderly/renal/hepatic).
titration: Increase by 1–2 mg/day at ≥24 h intervals; usual target 4–6 mg/day.
maxDose: 8 mg/day (little added benefit and more EPS above ~6 mg).
frequencyTiming: Once daily or divided BID; may dose at bedtime for sedation.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Start 0.5 mg BID and titrate slowly in significant renal or hepatic impairment (active moiety renally cleared).
startStopCrossTaper: Taper to switch; when converting to Consta LAI, continue oral for 3 weeks after the first injection.
administrationNotes: LAI Consta given IM every 2 weeks (requires 3-week oral overlap after first dose).
boxedWarning: WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Risperidone tablets are not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis. [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ] WARNING: INCREASED MORTALITY IN ELDERLY PATIENTS WITH DEMENTIA-RELATED PSYCHOSIS See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Risperidone tablets are not approved for use in patients with dementia-related psychosis. ( 5.1 )
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Risperidone tablets are contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to either risperidone or paliperidone, or to any of the excipients in the risperidone tablets formulation. Hypersensitivity reactions, including anaphylactic reactions and angioedema, have been reported in patients treated with risperidone and in patients treated with paliperidone. Paliperidone is a metabolite of risperidone. Known hypersensitivity to risperidone, paliperidone, or to any excipients in risperidone tablets. ( 4 )
seriousAE: Increased mortality and cerebrovascular events in elderly dementia patients
overdoseLethality: Relatively low lethality alone; sedation, tachycardia, hypotension, EPS and modest QT — supportive care.
baselineWorkup: Prolactin if symptomatic; ECG if cardiac risk
ongoingMonitoring: Weight at each visit early on; fasting glucose and lipids at ~12 weeks then annually; AIMS for tardive dyskinesia; prolactin if symptoms of hyperprolactinemia; BP for orthostasis.
majorDDI: CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, bupropion) raise levels
Additive CNS depression / QT with other centrally acting or QT drugs
pregnancy: Third-trimester exposure risks neonatal EPS/withdrawal; no clear major teratogenic signal — weigh untreated psychosis. Tie to CANMAT perinatal guidance.
lactation: Excreted in milk; monitor infant for sedation/EPS.
pediatric: Approved for irritability associated with autism.
geriatric: Increased mortality/stroke in dementia-related psychosis; start low, watch orthostasis and EPS.
cardiac: Modest QT effect.
pearls: The most D2-potent SGA → highest EPS and highest hyperprolactinemia of the atypicals (behaves like a high-potency FGA at higher doses).
Its active metabolite IS paliperidone (9-hydroxyrisperidone).
Consta LAI is the exception among long-acting injectables in requiring a 3-week oral overlap.
mnemonics: "RISperidone RAISES prolactin the most" — dopamine block at the tuberoinfundibular tract.
confusables: Risperidone vs paliperidone — paliperidone IS risperidone's active 9-OH metabolite (renally cleared, fewer CYP interactions).
classDifferentiators: Most EPS and most hyperprolactinemia of the SGAs; metabolic risk intermediate; renally-cleared active moiety.
examHooks: Which SGA most commonly causes hyperprolactinemia and dose-dependent EPS? (risperidone/paliperidone)
counsellingPoints: Report breast milk production, missed periods, or sexual dysfunction (prolactin).
Rise slowly from sitting/lying (orthostasis).
If on Consta, keep taking the oral form for the first 3 weeks.

vignettes.stem: A 26-year-old woman on an antipsychotic for schizophrenia develops amenorrhea, galactorrhea, and low libido; prolactin is elevated. Which agent is most likely responsible?
vignettes.explanation: Risperidone is the most D2-potent SGA and causes the most hyperprolactinemia; quetiapine, aripiprazole (a partial agonist that can lower prolactin), and clozapine are prolactin-sparing.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING Increased Mortality in Elderly Patients with Dementia-Related Psychosis Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Analyses of seventeen placebo-controlled trials (modal duration of 10 weeks), largely in patients taking atypical antipsychotic drugs, revealed a risk of death in drug-treated patients of between 1.6 to 1.7 times the risk of death in placebo-treated patients. Over the course of a typical 10-week controlled trial, the rate of death in drug-treated patients was about 4.5%, compared to a rate of about 2.6% in the placebo group. Although the causes of death were varied, most of the deaths appeared to be either cardiovascular (e.g., heart failure, sudden death) or infectious (e.g., pneumonia) in nature. Observational studies suggest that, similar to atypical antipsychotic drugs, treatment with conventional antipsychotic drugs may increase mortality. The extent to which the findings of increased mortality in observational studies may be attributed to the antipsychotic drug as opposed to some characteristic(s) of the patients is not clear. Trifluoperazine hydrochloride is not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis (see WARNINGS ).

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS A known hypersensitivity to phenothiazines, comatose or greatly depressed states due to central nervous system depressants and, in cases of existing blood dyscrasias, bone marrow depression and pre-existing liver damage.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Adults Dosage should be adjusted to the needs of the individual. The lowest effective dosage should always be used. Dosage should be increased more gradually in debilitated or emaciated patients. When maximum response is achieved, dosage may be reduced gradually to a maintenance level. Because of the inherent long action of the drug, patients may be controlled on convenient b.i.d. administration; some patients may be maintained on once-a-day administration. When trifluoperazine HCl is administered by intramuscular injection, equivalent oral dosage may be substituted once symptoms have been controlled. Note: Although there is little likelihood of contact dermatitis …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE (See also under ADVERSE REACTIONS ) Symptoms Primarily involvement of the extrapyramidal mechanism producing some of the dystonic reactions described above. Symptoms of central nervous system depression to the point of somnolence or coma. Agitation and restlessness may also occur. Other possible manifestations include convulsions, EKG changes and cardiac arrhythmias, fever, and autonomic reactions such as hypotension, dry mouth and ileus. Treatment It is important to determine other medications taken by the patient since multiple dose therapy is common in overdosage situations. Treatment is essentially symptomatic and supportive. Early gastric lavage is helpful. Keep patient …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (1 MG, 10 MG, 5 MG, 2 MG, 23.6 MG, 5.9 MG, 20 MG, 1.18 MG, 11.8 MG, 2.36 MG)
Syrup (1 MG, 10 MG, 5 MG, 2 MG, 23.6 MG, 5.9 MG, 20 MG, 1.18 MG, 11.8 MG, 2.36 MG)
moa: High-potency piperazine phenothiazine; potent D2 receptor antagonist.
receptorProfile: High D2 affinity; low H1/M1 (little sedation/anticholinergic) — EPS-predominant like other high-potency agents.
onsetOfEffect: Antipsychotic effect over days–weeks.
metabolism: Hepatic; phenothiazine oxidation/glucuronidation.
startingDose: 2–5 mg PO BID.
titration: Increase to response; usual 15–20 mg/day.
maxDose: Up to ~40 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: BID dosing.
administrationNotes: A high-potency piperazine phenothiazine; historically also used short-term for severe anxiety at low dose (now uncommon).
boxedWarning: WARNING Increased Mortality in Elderly Patients with Dementia-Related Psychosis Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Analyses of seventeen placebo-controlled trials (modal duration of 10 weeks), largely in patients taking atypical antipsychotic drugs, revealed a risk of death in drug-treated patients of between 1.6 to 1.7 times the risk of death in placebo-treated patients. Over the course of a typical 10-week controlled trial, the rate of death in drug-treated patients was about 4.5%, compared to a rate of about 2.6% in the placebo group. Although the causes of death were varied, most of the deaths appeared to be either cardiovascular (e.g., heart failure, sudden death) or infectious (e.g., pneumonia) in nature. Observational studies suggest that, similar to atypical antipsychotic drugs, treatment with …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS A known hypersensitivity to phenothiazines, comatose or greatly depressed states due to central nervous system depressants and, in cases of existing blood dyscrasias, bone marrow depression and pre-existing liver damage.
seriousAE: Increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis
overdoseLethality: EPS, sedation, hypotension, QT prolongation; supportive care.
ongoingMonitoring: AIMS for TD; EPS/akathisia; prolactin if symptomatic.
pregnancy: Neonatal EPS/withdrawal with late exposure; weigh maternal illness.
lactation: Excreted in milk; monitor infant.
geriatric: Increased mortality in dementia; EPS-prone; caution.
cardiac: QT prolongation possible.
pearls: High-potency piperazine phenothiazine: EPS-heavy, low sedation/anticholinergic — profile resembles haloperidol/fluphenazine.
Contrast with chlorpromazine (same phenothiazine family, but low-potency and sedating): potency, not chemistry, predicts the side-effect pattern.
Older low-dose use for severe anxiety is now uncommon.
mnemonics: "triFLUOPERazine = high-potency phenothiazine" — the piperazine phenothiazines (tri-, fluphen-, perphen-) trend higher potency/more EPS.
confusables: Trifluoperazine (high-potency, EPS) vs chlorpromazine (low-potency, sedating) — same phenothiazine class, opposite side-effect profiles.
classDifferentiators: High-potency phenothiazine; EPS-predominant, minimal sedation — the "phenothiazine that behaves like haloperidol."
examHooks: Two phenothiazines: one sedating/hypotensive, one EPS-heavy. Which is high-potency? (trifluoperazine vs chlorpromazine → trifluoperazine).
counsellingPoints: Report tremor, stiffness, restlessness, or abnormal movements.
Effect builds over weeks.
Do not stop abruptly.

vignettes.stem: Two phenothiazines are compared: chlorpromazine and trifluoperazine. Which statement about trifluoperazine is correct?
options: It is low-potency and highly sedating
It is high-potency with more EPS and less sedation than chlorpromazine
It causes more orthostatic hypotension than chlorpromazine
vignettes.answer: It is high-potency with more EPS and less sedation than chlorpromazine
vignettes.explanation: Trifluoperazine is a high-potency piperazine phenothiazine: more EPS/hyperprolactinemia but less sedation, orthostasis and anticholinergic effect than the low-potency chlorpromazine. Potency (D2 affinity), not the shared phenothiazine chemistry, drives the side-effect pattern.
vignettes.testedFact: High- vs low-potency phenothiazine side-effect trade-off

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Overdose of COBENFY may produce cholinergic, anticholinergic or a combination of cholinergic and anticholinergic signs and symptoms: • Cholinergic Signs and Symptoms: seizures, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, hyperhidrosis, salivary hypersecretion, and hypotension possibly preceded by hypertension. • Anticholinergic Signs and Symptoms (geriatric patients may be more susceptible): delirium, agitation, garbled speech, dizziness, hypertension, tachycardia, dry mouth and eyes, ileus, blurred vision, and urinary retention. Consider calling the Poison Help Line 1-800-222-1222 or a medical toxicologist for specific treatment recommendations.

sources.name: openFDA drug/label (US SPL)
sources.note: US label — cross-reference; Canadian monograph is authoritative for Canada.

moa: First-in-class antipsychotic that works WITHOUT any direct D2 receptor blockade. Xanomeline is a muscarinic M1/M4 receptor agonist; M4 activation in the striatum indirectly dampens dopamine signalling and M1 activation modulates cortical/limbic circuits, producing antipsychotic effect upstream of the dopamine synapse. Trospium is a peripherally-restricted (does not meaningfully cross the blood–brain barrier) muscarinic antagonist added solely to blunt xanomeline's peripheral cholinergic side effects.
receptorProfile: Xanomeline: M1/M4 muscarinic agonist (some M2/M3/M5 activity). Trospium: non-selective peripheral muscarinic antagonist (quaternary amine → CNS-sparing). No D2, 5-HT2A, H1, or α1 blockade — the whole point is a non-dopaminergic mechanism.
onsetOfEffect: Antipsychotic separation from placebo within weeks in trials.
halfLife: Xanomeline ~1–2 hours; trospium ~8.5 hours — hence twice-daily dosing.
timeToSteadyState: Within about a week of stable BID dosing.
metabolism: Xanomeline is hepatically metabolised (oxidative). Trospium is minimally metabolised and largely renally excreted (active tubular secretion) — a source of interactions with other renally secreted drugs.
foodEffect: Take on an empty stomach (≥1 h before or ≥2 h after food) — food raises trospium exposure and can worsen anticholinergic effects.
startingDose: Xanomeline 50 mg / trospium 20 mg PO twice daily, then titrate over days.
titration: Increase stepwise (e.g., to 100/20 then 125/30 BID) as tolerated, based on the label schedule.
maxDose: Xanomeline 125 mg / trospium 30 mg PO twice daily.
frequencyTiming: Twice daily on an empty stomach.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Not recommended in moderate/severe hepatic impairment or moderate/severe renal impairment (both components accumulate).
administrationNotes: Empty-stomach BID dosing and stepwise titration are key to tolerability; the trospium component is what makes the peripheral cholinergic load manageable.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS COBENFY is contraindicated in patients with: • urinary retention [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1) ] . • moderate (Child-Pugh Class B) or severe (Child-Pugh Class C) hepatic impairment [see Warnings and Precautions (5.2) ] . • gastric retention [see Warnings and Precautions (5.4) ] . • history of hypersensitivity to COBENFY or trospium chloride. Angioedema has been reported with COBENFY and trospium chloride [see Warnings and Precautions (5.5) ] . • untreated narrow-angle glaucoma [see Warnings and Precautions (5.6) ] . COBENFY is contraindicated in: • urinary retention ( 4 ) • moderate or severe hepatic impairment ( 4 ) • gastric retention ( 4 ) • history of hypersensitivity to COBENFY or trospium chloride ( 4 ) • untreated narrow-angle glaucoma ( 4 )
seriousAE: Anticholinergic effects incl. worsening narrow-angle glaucoma, ileus
syndromes: Anticholinergic toxicity (peripheral — dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention)
overdoseLethality: Overdose could produce mixed cholinergic (GI, bradycardia) and anticholinergic (retention, tachycardia, mydriasis) effects; supportive care, no specific antidote established.
discontinuationSyndrome: Cholinergic rebound possible on abrupt stop (as with other cholinergic/anticholinergic agents); watch for relapse.
baselineWorkup: Assess for urinary retention risk, narrow-angle glaucoma, and GI motility disorders before starting
ongoingMonitoring: Monitor heart rate/BP, GI tolerability, urinary symptoms, and LFTs. Notably, routine metabolic/prolactin/EPS monitoring concerns are lower because there is no D2 blockade — but do NOT ignore cholinergic/anticholinergic effects.
majorDDI: Other anticholinergics — additive peripheral antimuscarinic toxicity (retention, constipation, ileus)
Other cholinergic agents — additive GI/bradycardic effects
Drugs cleared by renal tubular secretion (e.g., metformin, digoxin, procainamide) — compete with trospium
Additive effects with agents that slow GI motility
contraindicatedCombos: Clinically significant urinary retention, gastric retention, or uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma (per label — trospium component)
pregnancy: Limited human data; weigh maternal illness. Anticholinergic/cholinergic effects on the neonate are theoretical — enrol in a pregnancy registry where available.
lactation: Limited data; monitor infant.
geriatric: Anticholinergic burden and orthostatic/cardiac effects warrant caution; note it lacks the dementia-mortality boxed warning framing of D2 antipsychotics, but safety in elderly psychosis is not established.
renalHepatic: Avoid in moderate/severe renal or hepatic impairment.
cardiac: Can raise heart rate and blood pressure and, via the cholinergic component, cause bradycardia/syncope — monitor.
pearls: US-only — for awareness (Cobenfy, formerly KarXT): first antipsychotic approved for schizophrenia that does NOT block dopamine receptors; not available in Canada.

The mechanism is the headline: an M1/M4 muscarinic agonist (xanomeline) acts upstream of dopamine, so there is NO D2 blockade → essentially no EPS, no tardive dyskinesia risk from D2 blockade, and no prolactin elevation. The side-effect profile is cholinergic/anticholinergic, not dopaminergic/metabolic.

Trospium is added purely as a peripheral muscarinic ANTAGONIST (CNS-sparing quaternary amine) to counter xanomeline's peripheral cholinergic effects (nausea, sweating, GI) without blunting the central antipsychotic action.
mnemonics: "KarXT = Karb (cholinergic) + Trospium (anti-cholinergic outside the brain)": central M1/M4 agonism does the work; peripheral antimuscarinic keeps the gut/bladder quiet.
confusables: Xanomeline–trospium vs every other antipsychotic — it is the ONLY one with no dopamine-receptor activity; classic and atypical agents all block (or partially agonise) D2.
classDifferentiators: Purely muscarinic (M1/M4 agonist) mechanism with no D2/5-HT2A blockade → no EPS, no TD-from-D2, no hyperprolactinemia, minimal metabolic/weight signal; side effects are cholinergic (nausea, GI, bradycardia) and peripheral anticholinergic (dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention).
examHooks: Which antipsychotic treats schizophrenia through M1/M4 muscarinic agonism with NO D2 blockade, paired with a peripheral anticholinergic to limit cholinergic side effects? (xanomeline–trospium)
counsellingPoints: Take twice daily on an empty stomach (well before or after meals).
Nausea, constipation, dry mouth and stomach upset are common early and often improve — report severe vomiting, inability to urinate, or fainting.
This medicine does not usually cause the movement or prolactin side effects of older antipsychotics.
Do not stop suddenly; keep monitoring visits.

vignettes.stem: A 29-year-old with schizophrenia cannot tolerate EPS or hyperprolactinemia on multiple dopamine-blocking antipsychotics. He is switched to xanomeline–trospium and improves without any movement side effects. Which mechanism explains the absence of EPS and prolactin elevation?
options: M1/M4 muscarinic agonism with no direct D2 receptor blockade
Potent 5-HT2A inverse agonism at D2 receptors
Irreversible D2 antagonism with rapid dissociation
vignettes.answer: M1/M4 muscarinic agonism with no direct D2 receptor blockade
vignettes.explanation: Xanomeline acts as an M1/M4 agonist upstream of dopamine transmission; because it does not block D2 receptors, it does not cause the EPS or prolactin rise seen with dopamine antagonists. Trospium only blocks peripheral muscarinic receptors.

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Do not use in patients with a known history of QT prolongation (4.1) Do not use in patients with recent acute myocardial infarction (4.1) Do not use in patients with uncompensated heart failure (4.1) Do not use in combination with other drugs that have demonstrated QT prolongation (4.1) Do not use in patients with known hypersensitivity to ziprasidone (4.2) Concomitant use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), or use within 14 days of stopping MAOIs. ( 4.3 ) 4.1 QT Prolongation Because of ziprasidone’s dose-related prolongation of the QT interval and the known association of fatal arrhythmias with QT prolongation by some other drugs, ziprasidone is contraindicated: in patients with a known history of QT prolongation (including congenital long QT syndrome) in patients with recent acute myocardial infarction in patients with uncompensated heart failure Pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic studies between ziprasidone and other drugs that prolong the QT interval have not been performed. An additive effect of ziprasidone and other drugs that prolong the QT interval cannot be excluded. Therefore, ziprasidone should not be given with: dofetilide, sotalol, quinidine, other Class Ia and III anti-arrhythmics, mesoridazine, thioridazine, chlorpromazine, droperidol, pimozide, sparfloxacin, gatifloxacin, moxifloxacin, halofantrine, mefloquine, pentamidine, arsenic …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE 10.1 Human Experience In premarketing trials involving more than 5400 patients and/or normal subjects, accidental or intentional overdosage of oral ziprasidone was documented in 10 patients. All of these patients survived without sequelae. In the patient taking the largest confirmed amount, 3,240 mg, the only symptoms reported were minimal sedation, slurring of speech, and transitory hypertension (200/95). Adverse reactions reported with ziprasidone overdose included extrapyramidal symptoms, somnolence, tremor, and anxiety. [see Adverse Reactions (6.2) ] 10.2 Management of Overdosage In case of acute overdosage, establish and maintain an airway and ensure adequate oxygenation …

moa: Combined 5-HT2A/D2 antagonist that is also a 5-HT1A partial agonist, 5-HT2C/1D antagonist, and a modest serotonin/norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor.
receptorProfile: 5-HT2A/D2 antagonism with 5-HT1A agonism; LOW H1/muscarinic → weight- and lipid-neutral, less sedation. Notable for QT prolongation.
onsetOfEffect: Days–weeks.
metabolism: Primarily aldehyde oxidase reduction (~2/3) with minor CYP3A4 — relatively few CYP interactions.
foodEffect: CRITICAL: absorption roughly DOUBLES with food — must be taken WITH A MEAL of ≥500 kcal or bioavailability falls ~50% (a common cause of apparent non-response).
approvedPopulations: Adults; short-acting IM available for acute agitation.
startingDose: 20 mg PO BID WITH food.
titration: Increase at ≥2-day intervals; usual 40–80 mg BID.
maxDose: 160 mg/day (80 mg BID).
frequencyTiming: Twice daily, each dose with food (≥500 kcal).
administrationNotes: Food requirement is the defining practical issue; short-acting IM for agitation.

Elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis treated with antipsychotic drugs are at an increased risk of death. Ziprasidone is not approved for the treatment of patients with dementia-related psychosis ( 5.1 )
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Do not use in patients with a known history of QT prolongation (4.1) Do not use in patients with recent acute myocardial infarction (4.1) Do not use in patients with uncompensated heart failure (4.1) Do not use in combination with other drugs that have demonstrated QT prolongation (4.1) Do not use in patients with known hypersensitivity to ziprasidone (4.2) Concomitant use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), or use within 14 days of stopping MAOIs. ( 4.3 ) 4.1 QT Prolongation Because of ziprasidone’s dose-related prolongation of the QT interval and the known association of fatal arrhythmias with QT prolongation by some other drugs, ziprasidone is contraindicated: in patients with a known history of QT prolongation (including congenital long QT syndrome) in patients with recent acute myocardial infarction in patients with uncompensated heart failure …
seriousAE: QT prolongation (greatest among SGAs) with torsades risk
overdoseLethality: Main concern is QT prolongation/arrhythmia; otherwise sedation and EPS — obtain ECG in significant ingestion.
baselineWorkup: ECG (baseline QTc) if cardiac risk or other QT drugs
ongoingMonitoring: ECG/electrolytes if QT risk factors or co-prescribed QT drugs; metabolic panel (though weight-neutral) per class practice; AIMS for tardive dyskinesia.
contraindicatedCombos: Use in the setting of recent MI, uncompensated heart failure, or known QT prolongation (per label cardiac cautions)
pregnancy: Limited data; third-trimester neonatal EPS/withdrawal risk — weigh untreated illness. Tie to CANMAT perinatal guidance.
lactation: Limited data; monitor infant.
geriatric: Increased mortality/stroke in dementia; check QT and electrolytes.
cardiac: Greatest QT prolongation of the SGAs — the defining caution; avoid with other QT drugs and correct electrolytes.
pearls: Must be taken WITH FOOD (≥500 kcal) — on an empty stomach absorption halves, a classic cause of "treatment failure".
Greatest QT prolongation of the SGAs → check ECG/electrolytes and avoid other QT-prolonging drugs.
Weight- and lipid-neutral (metabolically favourable) — a reason to choose it in metabolically vulnerable patients.
mnemonics: "Ziprasidone: Zzz needs a meal, and Zaps the QT" — food ≥500 kcal; watch the QT interval.
confusables: Ziprasidone vs lurasidone — both need food, but ziprasidone requires ≥500 kcal and prolongs QT, whereas lurasidone needs ≥350 kcal, has low QT risk, and is used for bipolar depression.
classDifferentiators: Weight/lipid-neutral but the QT-prolonging SGA with an absolute food requirement (≥500 kcal); twice-daily dosing.
examHooks: Which SGA must be taken with a ≥500 kcal meal and carries the greatest QT risk? (ziprasidone)
counsellingPoints: Always take with a full meal (≥500 kcal) — food is required for the drug to work.
Report palpitations or fainting; tell us about other heart medications.
It is weight-friendly compared with other options.

vignettes.stem: A patient with schizophrenia on ziprasidone reports little benefit despite adherence; on review, they take it with only black coffee in the morning and nothing at the second dose. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Reduced absorption from being taken without adequate food
vignettes.answer: Reduced absorption from being taken without adequate food
vignettes.explanation: Ziprasidone bioavailability roughly doubles with a ≥500 kcal meal; taken on an empty stomach, absorption falls ~50%, causing apparent non-response. It must be taken with food.

faersSource.note: Spontaneous reports — reflects reporting frequency, not incidence.

formulationsCanada: Liquid (50 MG, 500 MG, 25 MG, 10 MG, 40 MG, 200 MG)
Tablet (50 MG, 500 MG, 25 MG, 10 MG, 40 MG, 200 MG)
Solution (50 MG, 500 MG, 25 MG, 10 MG, 40 MG, 200 MG)
moa: Thioxanthene (Canada/Europe; not marketed in the US); D1 and D2 receptor antagonist.
receptorProfile: High D2 affinity with additional α1 and H1 blockade — moderately sedating for a higher-potency agent.
onsetOfEffect: Acetate (Acuphase) sedating within hours, effect over ~2–3 days; oral/depot over days–weeks.
metabolism: Hepatic — CYP2D6 and CYP3A4.
startingDose: Oral ~10–50 mg/day; acetate (Acuphase) 50–150 mg IM per episode.
titration: Adjust to response; acetate repeated no more often than every 2–3 days.
maxDose: Oral up to ~150 mg/day; limit cumulative acetate per treatment course per monograph.
frequencyTiming: Three distinct forms: oral (dihydrochloride), acetate/"Acuphase" (short-acting IM, effect ~72 h, for acute disturbance), and decanoate depot (deep IM every 2–4 weeks).
administrationNotes: Acuphase (acetate) is a Canadian tool for the acutely agitated/psychotic patient — one injection covers ~2–3 days, bridging to oral or depot. Do not use acetate for maintenance.
seriousAE: Increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis
overdoseLethality: Sedation, EPS, hypotension, QT prolongation; supportive care. Acetate's prolonged action complicates overdose.
ongoingMonitoring: AIMS for TD; EPS; sedation; BP.
pregnancy: Neonatal EPS/withdrawal with late exposure; weigh maternal illness.
lactation: Excreted in milk; monitor infant.
geriatric: Increased mortality in dementia; sedation/EPS/orthostasis; caution.
pearls: Canada-specific thioxanthene with THREE esters: oral, acetate (Acuphase, short-acting IM ~72 h for acute disturbance), and decanoate depot (q2–4 wk).
Acuphase is for acute management only — a single injection bridges ~2–3 days; it is NOT a maintenance depot.
Somewhat more sedating than pure high-potency agents due to α1/H1 blockade.
mnemonics: "AcuPHASE = acute phase" — zuclopenthixol acetate covers the acute 72-hour phase, then switch to oral/depot.
confusables: Zuclopenthixol ACETATE (Acuphase, short-acting) vs DECANOATE (maintenance depot) — same molecule, opposite purposes.
classDifferentiators: Canadian thioxanthene notable for the short-acting acetate (Acuphase) niche in acute psychosis, plus a maintenance decanoate depot.
examHooks: A single IM antipsychotic that covers ~2–3 days of acute psychotic agitation in Canada? (zuclopenthixol acetate / Acuphase).
counsellingPoints: The Acuphase injection works for a few days as a bridge.
Report stiffness, restlessness, or abnormal movements.
Attend scheduled depot injections for maintenance.

vignettes.stem: An acutely psychotic, agitated inpatient needs parenteral coverage lasting a couple of days while an oral regimen is established. Which Canadian formulation is designed for this "acute phase" bridge?
vignettes.explanation: Zuclopenthixol acetate (Acuphase) is a short-acting IM ester whose effect lasts about 2–3 days — used to bridge the acute phase, not for maintenance. The decanoate esters are the long-acting maintenance depots.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNINGS SERIOUS DERMATOLOGIC REACTIONS AND HLA-B*1502 ALLELE SERIOUS AND SOMETIMES FATAL DERMATOLOGIC REACTIONS, INCLUDING TOXIC EPIDERMAL NECROLYSIS (TEN) AND STEVENS-JOHNSON SYNDROME (SJS), HAVE BEEN REPORTED DURING TREATMENT WITH CARBAMAZEPINE. THESE REACTIONS ARE ESTIMATED TO OCCUR IN 1 TO 6 PER 10,000 NEW USERS IN COUNTRIES WITH MAINLY CAUCASIAN POPULATIONS, BUT THE RISK IN SOME ASIAN COUNTRIES IS ESTIMATED TO BE ABOUT 10 TIMES HIGHER. STUDIES IN PATIENTS OF CHINESE ANCESTRY HAVE FOUND A STRONG ASSOCIATION BETWEEN THE RISK OF DEVELOPING SJS/TEN AND THE PRESENCE OF HLA-B*1502, AN INHERITED ALLELIC VARIANT OF THE HLA-B GENE. HLA-B*1502 IS FOUND ALMOST EXCLUSIVELY IN PATIENTS WITH ANCESTRY ACROSS BROAD AREAS OF ASIA. PATIENTS WITH ANCESTRY IN GENETICALLY AT-RISK POPULATIONS SHOULD BE SCREENED FOR THE PRESENCE OF HLA-B*1502 PRIOR TO INITIATING TREATMENT WITH CARBAMAZEPINE. PATIENTS TESTING POSITIVE FOR THE ALLELE SHOULD NOT BE TREATED WITH CARBAMAZEPINE UNLESS THE BENEFIT CLEARLY OUTWEIGHS THE RISK (SEE WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS , LABORATORY TESTS). APLASTIC ANEMIA AND AGRANULOCYTOSIS APLASTIC ANEMIA AND AGRANULOCYTOSIS HAVE BEEN REPORTED IN ASSOCIATION WITH THE USE OF CARBAMAZEPINE. DATA FROM A POPULATION-BASED CASE CONTROL STUDY DEMONSTRATE THAT THE RISK OF DEVELOPING THESE REACTIONS IS 5 TO 8 TIMES GREATER THAN IN THE GENERAL POPULATION. HOWEVER, THE OVERALL RISK OF THESE …

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Carbamazepine should not be used in patients with a history of previous bone marrow depression, hypersensitivity to the drug, or known sensitivity to any of the tricyclic compounds, such as amitriptyline, desipramine, imipramine, protriptyline, nortriptyline, etc. Likewise, on theoretical grounds its use with monoamine oxidase (MAO) inhibitors is not recommended. Before administration of carbamazepine, MAO inhibitors should be discontinued for a minimum of 14 days, or longer if the clinical situation permits. Coadministration of carbamazepine and nefazodone may result in insufficient plasma concentrations of nefazodone and its active metabolite to achieve a therapeutic effect. Coadministration of carbamazepine with nefazodone is contraindicated.

therapeuticLevel.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION (SEE TABLE BELOW) Carbamazepine suspension in combination with liquid chlorpromazine or thioridazine results in precipitate formation, and, in the case of chlorpromazine, there has been a report of patient passing an orange rubbery precipitate in the stool following coadministration of the two drugs (see PRECAUTIONS, Drug Interactions). Because the extent to which this occurs with other liquid medications is not known, Carbamazepine suspension should not be administered simultaneously with other liquid medications or diluents. Monitoring of blood levels has increased the efficacy and safety of anticonvulsants (see PRECAUTIONS, Laboratory Tests). Dosage should be …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Serious Dermatologic Reactions Serious and sometimes fatal dermatologic reactions, including toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) and Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS), have been reported with carbamazepine treatment. The risk of these events is estimated to be about 1 to 6 per 10,000 new users in countries with mainly Caucasian populations. However, the risk in some Asian countries is estimated to be about 10 times higher. Carbamazepine should be discontinued at the first sign of a rash, unless the rash is clearly not drug-related. If signs or symptoms suggest SJS/TEN, use of this drug should not be resumed and alternative therapy should be considered. SJS/TEN and HLA-B*1502 Allele …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Acute Toxicity Lowest known lethal dose: adults, 3.2 g (a 24-year-old woman died of a cardiac arrest and a 24-year-old man died of pneumonia and hypoxic encephalopathy); children, 4 g (a 14-year-old girl died of a cardiac arrest), 1.6 g (a 3-year-old girl died of aspiration pneumonia). Oral LD 50 in animals (mg/kg): mice, 1100 to 3750; rats, 3850 to 4025; rabbits, 1500 to 2680; guinea pigs, 920. Signs and Symptoms The first signs and symptoms appear after 1 to 3 hours. Neuromuscular disturbances are the most prominent. Cardiovascular disorders are generally milder, and severe cardiac complications occur only when very high doses (greater than 60 g) have been ingested. Respiration …

faersSource.name: openFDA drug/event (FAERS)
faersSource.note: Spontaneous reports — reflects reporting frequency, not incidence.

moa: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in a use-dependent manner, stabilizing hyperexcitable neuronal membranes. Structurally tricyclic (related to TCAs).
onsetOfEffect: Antimanic effect over ~1–2 weeks (second-line vs lithium/valproate).
halfLife: Initially ~25–65 h; falls to ~12–17 h after autoinduction (over the first few weeks).
timeToSteadyState: ~3–5 days initially, but shifts as autoinduction develops over ~2–4 weeks.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 to the active carbamazepine-10,11-epoxide. POTENT AUTOINDUCER — induces CYP3A4 (and 1A2, 2C9, UGT, P-gp), so it accelerates its OWN metabolism and lowers its own level over 2–4 weeks; also a broad inducer of other drugs.
therapeuticLevel: 17–51 µmol/L (SI) / 4–12 µg/mL (conventional). Recheck after ~2–4 weeks once autoinduction has lowered the level.
approvedPopulations: Adults; screen at-risk ancestries before starting (see baseline workup).
startingDose: 200 mg PO BID.
titration: Increase by ~200 mg/day at weekly intervals to response/level; anticipate re-titration as autoinduction drops the level.
maxDose: ~1600 mg/day (occasionally higher under monitoring).
frequencyTiming: Divided BID–QID; extended-release allows BID dosing.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Caution/avoid in hepatic impairment; adjust for hyponatremia.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper to avoid rebound seizures; remember that stopping it REMOVES enzyme induction — co-medication levels (e.g., warfarin, OCPs) will rise.
boxedWarning: WARNINGS SERIOUS DERMATOLOGIC REACTIONS AND HLA-B*1502 ALLELE SERIOUS AND SOMETIMES FATAL DERMATOLOGIC REACTIONS, INCLUDING TOXIC EPIDERMAL NECROLYSIS (TEN) AND STEVENS-JOHNSON SYNDROME (SJS), HAVE BEEN REPORTED DURING TREATMENT WITH CARBAMAZEPINE. THESE REACTIONS ARE ESTIMATED TO OCCUR IN 1 TO 6 PER 10,000 NEW USERS IN COUNTRIES WITH MAINLY CAUCASIAN POPULATIONS, BUT THE RISK IN SOME ASIAN COUNTRIES IS ESTIMATED TO BE ABOUT 10 TIMES HIGHER. STUDIES IN PATIENTS OF CHINESE ANCESTRY HAVE FOUND A STRONG ASSOCIATION BETWEEN THE RISK OF DEVELOPING SJS/TEN AND THE PRESENCE OF HLA-B*1502, AN INHERITED ALLELIC VARIANT OF THE HLA-B GENE. HLA-B*1502 IS FOUND ALMOST EXCLUSIVELY IN PATIENTS WITH ANCESTRY ACROSS BROAD AREAS OF ASIA. PATIENTS WITH ANCESTRY IN GENETICALLY AT-RISK POPULATIONS SHOULD BE SCREENED FOR THE PRESENCE OF HLA-B*1502 PRIOR TO INITIATING TREATMENT WITH CARBAMAZEPINE. PATIENTS …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Carbamazepine should not be used in patients with a history of previous bone marrow depression, hypersensitivity to the drug, or known sensitivity to any of the tricyclic compounds, such as amitriptyline, desipramine, imipramine, protriptyline, nortriptyline, etc. Likewise, on theoretical grounds its use with monoamine oxidase (MAO) inhibitors is not recommended. Before administration of carbamazepine, MAO inhibitors should be discontinued for a minimum of 14 days, or longer if the clinical situation permits. Coadministration of carbamazepine and nefazodone may result in insufficient plasma concentrations of nefazodone and its active metabolite to achieve a therapeutic effect. Coadministration of carbamazepine with nefazodone is contraindicated.
overdoseLethality: Moderate–high — CNS depression, seizures, and sodium-channel-mediated cardiotoxicity (QRS widening, arrhythmia, similar to TCA overdose); coma. Supportive care; multiple-dose activated charcoal.
discontinuationSyndrome: Taper to avoid rebound seizures; expect rising levels of previously induced co-medications.
baselineWorkup: HLA-B*1502 genotyping in patients of Asian (esp. Han Chinese/Southeast Asian) ancestry
ongoingMonitoring: Serum level with a recheck at ~2–4 weeks (autoinduction); periodic CBC, LFTs, and sodium — sooner if symptomatic (fever/sore throat, rash, lethargy).
majorDDI: Oral contraceptives — induction lowers levels → contraceptive FAILURE (use a non-hormonal method)
Warfarin, many psychotropics, DOACs — levels fall via 3A4 induction
Grapefruit juice / 3A4 inhibitors — raise carbamazepine to toxic levels
pregnancy: Teratogen — neural-tube defects (spina bifida) and craniofacial/cardiac malformations (less severe overall than valproate). Use high-dose folate; consider alternatives.
lactation: Generally considered compatible with monitoring of the infant.
geriatric: Higher hyponatremia and interaction burden; start low.
cardiac: Can impair AV conduction; caution with pre-existing block.
pearls: AUTOINDUCER — levels drop over the first 2–4 weeks as it speeds its own metabolism, so re-titrate and recheck the level at ~1 month.
Screen HLA-B*1502 (Han Chinese / Southeast Asian ancestry) before starting — strong predictor of SJS/TEN; HLA-A*3101 predicts DRESS.
Potent 3A4 inducer — a classic cause of oral-contraceptive failure and subtherapeutic warfarin.
Tricyclic structure → 14-day MAOI separation, and TCA-like sodium-channel cardiotoxicity in overdose.
mnemonics: "Carba = Cs": CYP inducer, Contraceptive failure, Cytopenias (agranulocytosis/aplastic anemia), Cutaneous SJS (HLA-B*1502), and Cardiac/Na (autoinduction Cuts its own level).
confusables: Carbamazepine (potent autoinducer, marrow suppression, HLA-B*1502) vs oxcarbazepine (weaker inducer, no active epoxide, MORE hyponatremia, less marrow monitoring).
SJS/TEN skin risk (HLA-B*1502) vs DRESS multi-organ risk (HLA-A*3101).
classDifferentiators: The enzyme-INDUCING sodium-channel blocker whose level self-lowers (autoinduction); defined by HLA-linked SJS, marrow suppression, hyponatremia, and defeat of OCPs.
examHooks: Which mood stabilizer autoinduces and needs a repeat level at ~1 month? (carbamazepine)
Which HLA allele mandates screening before carbamazepine? (HLA-B*1502)
Why does an OCP fail on carbamazepine? (3A4 induction)
counsellingPoints: Report fever, sore throat, mouth ulcers, easy bruising (blood counts), or any rash immediately.
Hormonal birth control may not work — use an additional/non-hormonal method.
Regular blood tests for counts, liver, and sodium are required.

vignettes.stem: A 22-year-old man of Han Chinese ancestry is being considered for carbamazepine. Which pre-treatment test most reduces the risk of a life-threatening reaction?
vignettes.explanation: HLA-B*1502 (prevalent in Han Chinese and Southeast Asian populations) strongly predisposes to carbamazepine-induced Stevens–Johnson syndrome / TEN; carriers should not receive the drug. Autoinduction separately mandates a repeat level at ~1 month.
vignettes.testedFact: HLA-B*1502 screening before carbamazepine / SJS risk

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Signs of acute toxicity in animals included ataxia, labored breathing, ptosis, sedation, hypoactivity, or excitation. Acute oral overdoses of gabapentin have been reported. Symptoms have included double vision, tremor, slurred speech, drowsiness, altered mental status, dizziness, lethargy, and diarrhea. Fatal respiratory depression has been reported with gabapentin overdose, alone and in combination with other CNS depressants. Gabapentin can be removed by hemodialysis. If overexposure occurs, call your poison control center at 1-800-222-1222.

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Pregnancy Exposure Registry There is a pregnancy exposure registry that monitors pregnancy outcomes in women exposed to antiepileptic drugs (AEDs), such as Gabapentin, during pregnancy. Encourage women who are taking Gabapentin during pregnancy to enroll in the North American Antiepileptic Drug (NAAED) Pregnancy Registry by calling the toll free number 1-888-233-2334 or visiting http://www.aedpregnancyregistry.org/. Risk Summary There are no adequate data on the developmental risks associated with the use of Gabapentin in pregnant women. In nonclinical studies in mice, rats, and rabbits, gabapentin was developmentally toxic (increased fetal skeletal and visceral abnormalities, …

formulationsCanada: Capsule (100 MG, 400 MG, 300 MG, 600 MG, 800 MG, 250 MG)
Tablet (100 MG, 400 MG, 300 MG, 600 MG, 800 MG, 250 MG)
moa: Binds the α2δ subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release. Despite the name, it does NOT act at GABA receptors.
onsetOfEffect: Days for anxiolytic/adjunctive effects.
metabolism: NOT metabolized — excreted unchanged by the kidney; no CYP involvement. Absorption is saturable/nonlinear (bioavailability falls at higher doses).
elimination: Renal, as unchanged drug.
startingDose: 300 mg PO (often 300 mg at bedtime on day 1), titrating upward.
titration: Increase to 300 mg TID over days, then further as tolerated/needed.
maxDose: ~3600 mg/day in divided doses (TID).
frequencyTiming: TID (short half-life).
renalHepaticAdjustment: Renal dose reduction required (renally cleared); no hepatic adjustment.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper — abrupt discontinuation can cause anxiety/agitation and (rarely) withdrawal seizures.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Gabapentin is contraindicated in patients who have demonstrated hypersensitivity to the drug or its ingredients. Known hypersensitivity to gabapentin or its ingredients (4)
seriousAE: Respiratory depression when combined with opioids/CNS depressants
overdoseLethality: Low when taken alone; the danger is additive respiratory depression with opioids or other CNS depressants.
discontinuationSyndrome: Taper to avoid rebound anxiety/insomnia and rare withdrawal seizures.
ongoingMonitoring: Renal function; sedation and, given misuse potential, signs of escalation/diversion.
majorDDI: Opioids / CNS depressants — additive sedation and respiratory depression
Aluminum/magnesium antacids — reduce absorption (separate by ~2 hours)
contraindicatedCombos: Caution (not absolute) with opioids and other CNS depressants — respiratory depression
pregnancy: Limited data; use only if clearly needed.
lactation: Excreted in milk in small amounts; often considered compatible with monitoring.
geriatric: Reduce dose for renal decline; watch sedation, falls, and edema.
renalHepatic: Renally cleared — dose to renal function; a clean choice when CYP interactions must be avoided.
pearls: NOT a primary mood stabilizer — negative bipolar RCTs; used adjunctively for anxiety, sleep, or pain.
Renally excreted with NO CYP metabolism → a clean interaction profile (useful when polypharmacy/CYP issues matter).
Recognized misuse potential (increasingly monitored/scheduled in some jurisdictions), and respiratory depression when combined with opioids.
The α2δ calcium-channel mechanism is shared with pregabalin — the name is misleading (no GABA-receptor activity).
mnemonics: "GabapentIN → kidney IN, liver OUT" — renally cleared, no CYP.
confusables: Gabapentin/pregabalin (α2δ calcium-channel ligands) vs benzodiazepines/GABA drugs — different mechanism despite "GABA" in the name.
Gabapentin (nonlinear, saturable absorption, TID) vs pregabalin (linear kinetics, more potent).
classDifferentiators: A renally cleared α2δ ligand with no CYP interactions — an anxiety/sleep/pain adjunct, explicitly NOT a bipolar mood stabilizer.
examHooks: Is gabapentin an effective mood stabilizer? (no — negative bipolar trials)
How is gabapentin eliminated? (renally, unchanged — no CYP)
What raises overdose risk with gabapentin? (co-administered opioids/CNS depressants)
counsellingPoints: Do not combine with opioids, sleeping pills, or heavy alcohol without medical advice (breathing risk).
Separate from antacids by a couple of hours.
Do not stop abruptly; taper with your prescriber.

vignettes.stem: A patient with bipolar disorder asks whether gabapentin can serve as their primary mood stabilizer. What is the best evidence-based response?
options: Yes — it is first-line for mania
No — controlled trials do not support gabapentin as a primary mood stabilizer; it is used adjunctively for anxiety/sleep/pain
Yes — it is superior to lithium for maintenance
Only if the lithium level is subtherapeutic
vignettes.answer: No — controlled trials do not support gabapentin as a primary mood stabilizer; it is used adjunctively for anxiety/sleep/pain
vignettes.explanation: Randomized trials failed to show efficacy for gabapentin in bipolar disorder; it has a role only as an adjunct (anxiety, sleep, pain). It is renally cleared with no CYP metabolism.
vignettes.testedFact: Gabapentin is not a primary mood stabilizer

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: SERIOUS SKIN RASHES Lamotrigine can cause serious rashes requiring hospitalization and discontinuation of treatment. The incidence of these rashes, which have included Stevens-Johnson syndrome, is approximately 0.3% to 0.8% in pediatric patients (aged 2 to 17 years) and 0.08% to 0.3% in adults receiving lamotrigine. One rash-related death was reported in a prospectively followed cohort of 1,983 pediatric patients (aged 2 to 16 years) with epilepsy taking lamotrigine as adjunctive therapy. In worldwide postmarketing experience, rare cases of toxic epidermal necrolysis and/or rash-related death have been reported in adult and pediatric patients, but their numbers are too few to permit a precise estimate of the rate. In addition to age, factors that may increase the risk of occurrence or the severity of rash caused by lamotrigine include (1) coadministration of lamotrigine with valproate (includes valproic acid and divalproex sodium), (2) exceeding the recommended initial dose of lamotrigine, (3) exceeding the recommended dose escalation for lamotrigine, or (4) the presence of the HLA-B*1502 allele However, cases have occurred in the absence of these factors. Nearly all cases of life-threatening rashes caused by lamotrigine have occurred within 2 to 8 weeks of treatment initiation. However, isolated cases have occurred after prolonged treatment (e.g., 6 months). …

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Lamotrigine is contraindicated in patients who have demonstrated hypersensitivity (e.g., rash, angioedema, acute urticaria, extensive pruritus, mucosal ulceration) to the drug or its ingredients [see Boxed Warning , Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 , 5.3 )]. Hypersensitivity to the drug or its ingredients. ( Boxed Warning , 4 )

( Boxed Warning , 5.1 ) Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis: Consider this diagnosis and evaluate patients immediately if they develop signs or symptoms of systemic inflammation. Discontinue lamotrigine if an alternative etiology is not established. ( 5.2 ) Fatal or life-threatening hypersensitivity reaction: Multiorgan hypersensitivity reactions, also known as drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms, may be fatal or life threatening. overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE 10.1 Human Overdose Experience Overdoses involving quantities up to 15 g have been reported for lamotrigine, some of which have been fatal. Overdose has resulted in ataxia, nystagmus, seizures (including tonic-clonic seizures), decreased level of consciousness, coma, and intraventricular conduction delay. 10.2 Management of Overdose There are no specific antidotes for lamotrigine. Following a suspected overdose, hospitalization of the patient is advised. General supportive care is indicated, including frequent monitoring of vital signs and close observation of the patient. If indicated, emesis should be induced; usual precautions should be taken to protect the airway. It …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Significant drug interactions with lamotrigine are summarized in this section. Uridine 5´-diphospho-glucuronyl transferases (UGT) have been identified as the enzymes responsible for metabolism of lamotrigine. Drugs that induce or inhibit glucuronidation may, therefore, affect the apparent clearance of lamotrigine. Strong or moderate inducers of the cytochrome P450 3A4 (CYP3A4) enzyme, which are also known to induce UGT, may also enhance the metabolism of lamotrigine. Those drugs that have been demonstrated to have a clinically significant impact on lamotrigine metabolism are outlined in Table 13. Specific dosing guidance for these drugs is provided in the Dosage and …

Encourage women who are taking lamotrigine during pregnancy to enroll in the North American Antiepileptic Drug (NAAED) Pregnancy Registry by calling 1-888-233-2334 or visiting http://www.aedpregnancyregistry.org/ . formulationsCanada: Tablet (200 MG, 25 MG, 100 MG, 150 MG, 5 MG, 2 MG)
moa: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels and inhibits presynaptic glutamate release, stabilizing neuronal membranes.
onsetOfEffect: Slow — because of the mandatory slow titration; used for maintenance and bipolar depression rather than acute mania.
halfLife: ~25–33 h as monotherapy; ~14 h with enzyme inducers; prolonged to ~60+ h when combined with valproate.
timeToSteadyState: ~5–7 days at a given dose (but titration spans weeks).
metabolism: Hepatic glucuronidation (UGT), NOT CYP. Valproate inhibits this (doubles the level); enzyme inducers, estrogen-containing OCPs, and pregnancy lower it.
canmatLineOfTherapy: Bipolar — maintenance (depression prevention): first-line (CANMAT/ISBD 2018 Bipolar Guidelines)
approvedPopulations: Adults for bipolar maintenance; anticonvulsant use across ages.
startingDose: Monotherapy: 25 mg PO daily × 2 weeks. WITH valproate: 25 mg every other day (halved). Slow titration is critical to prevent SJS.
titration: SLOW: 25 mg/day ×2 wk → 50 mg/day ×2 wk → then increase by ~50 mg weekly toward 200 mg/day. Halve every step if on valproate; go faster/higher with inducers. Restart the full titration if doses are missed for >5 days.
maxDose: ~200 mg/day for bipolar monotherapy (≈100 mg/day with valproate; up to ~400 mg/day with inducers).
frequencyTiming: Once or twice daily.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce in significant hepatic impairment.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper to reduce seizure risk; if restarting after a gap >5 days, re-titrate from the beginning.
boxedWarning: WARNING: SERIOUS SKIN RASHES Lamotrigine can cause serious rashes requiring hospitalization and discontinuation of treatment. The incidence of these rashes, which have included Stevens-Johnson syndrome, is approximately 0.3% to 0.8% in pediatric patients (aged 2 to 17 years) and 0.08% to 0.3% in adults receiving lamotrigine. One rash-related death was reported in a prospectively followed cohort of 1,983 pediatric patients (aged 2 to 16 years) with epilepsy taking lamotrigine as adjunctive therapy. In worldwide postmarketing experience, rare cases of toxic epidermal necrolysis and/or rash-related death have been reported in adult and pediatric patients, but their numbers are too few to permit a precise estimate of the rate. In addition to age, factors that may increase the risk of occurrence or the severity of rash caused by lamotrigine include (1) coadministration of lamotrigine with …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Lamotrigine is contraindicated in patients who have demonstrated hypersensitivity (e.g., rash, angioedema, acute urticaria, extensive pruritus, mucosal ulceration) to the drug or its ingredients [see Boxed Warning , Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 , 5.3 )]. Hypersensitivity to the drug or its ingredients. ( Boxed Warning , 4 )
seriousAE: Stevens–Johnson syndrome / TEN (esp. with fast titration or valproate co-therapy)
overdoseLethality: Relatively low; large ingestions can cause seizures, QRS widening, and coma.
discontinuationSyndrome: Taper to avoid rebound seizure/mood relapse.
baselineWorkup: No routine bloodwork required — counsel thoroughly on rash and the slow titration schedule
ongoingMonitoring: Clinical monitoring for rash/hypersensitivity; no routine serum level. In pregnancy, levels fall substantially — monitor and increase as needed, then reduce postpartum to avoid toxicity.
majorDDI: Valproate — DOUBLES the lamotrigine level (halve the dose; SJS risk)
Carbamazepine / phenytoin / rifampin — induce glucuronidation and LOWER the level
Estrogen-containing oral contraceptives — lower the level ~50% (and it rises during the pill-free week)
contraindicatedCombos: No absolute drug contraindication; exercise heightened caution and dose-halving with valproate
pregnancy: Comparatively pregnancy-SAFER — no consistent major teratogenic signal; often a preferred maintenance stabilizer in pregnancy. Note that levels fall markedly during pregnancy (may need dose increases) and must be reduced postpartum.
lactation: Passes into milk in measurable amounts; can be used with infant monitoring.
geriatric: Well tolerated; standard slow titration still applies.
pearls: Agent for bipolar DEPRESSION and MAINTENANCE — not effective for acute mania.
The SLOW titration exists to prevent SJS/TEN — never front-load, and restart from scratch after a >5-day gap.
Valproate doubles the level (halve the dose); carbamazepine and estrogen-containing OCPs lower it.
No routine serum level; pregnancy dramatically lowers levels (adjust up antenatally, down postpartum).
mnemonics: "Lamotrigine — LOW and SLOW" to avoid the rash; and "VALproate → VALue doubles" (halve the lamotrigine dose).
confusables: Lamotrigine = bipolar DEPRESSION/maintenance; lithium/valproate = better for MANIA.
Direction of the valproate interaction: valproate RAISES lamotrigine (glucuronidation inhibition), whereas carbamazepine LOWERS it (induction).
classDifferentiators: The maintenance/anti-depressive stabilizer with the best pregnancy profile and no routine levels — defined by slow titration to avoid SJS and by opposite interactions with valproate (up) vs carbamazepine (down).
examHooks: Which stabilizer is best for bipolar depression and pregnancy? (lamotrigine)
Why halve lamotrigine when adding valproate? (glucuronidation inhibition doubles the level → SJS)
What must you do if lamotrigine is missed for >5 days? (re-titrate from the start)
counsellingPoints: Follow the slow dose schedule exactly — do not speed it up.
Report ANY rash, especially with fever, mouth/eye involvement, or swelling — seek care urgently.
Tell your prescriber before starting or stopping birth control pills or valproate (doses may need to change).

vignettes.stem: A patient stabilized on valproate for bipolar I is started on lamotrigine for depressive episodes. Compared with lamotrigine monotherapy, how should the lamotrigine be dosed?
options: Doubled, because valproate lowers lamotrigine levels
Halved and titrated more slowly, because valproate roughly doubles lamotrigine levels
Started at the full target dose to speed response
vignettes.answer: Halved and titrated more slowly, because valproate roughly doubles lamotrigine levels
vignettes.explanation: Valproate inhibits lamotrigine glucuronidation, roughly doubling its concentration and markedly increasing SJS/TEN risk. Lamotrigine must be halved and titrated even more slowly when co-prescribed with valproate.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: LITHIUM TOXICITY Lithium toxicity is closely related to serum lithium concentrations, and can occur at doses close to therapeutic concentrations. Facilities for prompt and accurate serum lithium determinations should be available before initiating treatment [see Dosage and Administration ( 2.3 ), Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 )]. WARNING: LITHIUM TOXICITY See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. Lithium toxicity is closely related to serum lithium concentrations, and can occur at doses close to therapeutic concentrations. Facilities for prompt and accurate serum lithium determinations should be available before initiating therapy ( 2.3 , 5.1 ).

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Lithium is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to any inactive ingredient in the lithium carbonate capsule or lithium citrate products [see Adverse Reactions ( 6 )]. Known hypersensitivity to any inactive ingredient in the drug product ( 4 )

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE The toxic concentrations for lithium (≥ 1.5 mEq/L) are close to the therapeutic concentrations [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 )]. At lithium concentrations greater than 3 mEq/L, patients may progress to seizures, coma, and irreversible brain damage. Treatment: For current information on the management of poisoning or overdosage, contact the National Poison Control Center at 1-800-222-1222 or www.poison.org . No specific antidote for lithium poisoning is known. Mild symptoms of lithium toxicity can usually be treated by reduction in dose or cessation of the drug. In severe cases of lithium poisoning, the goal of treatment is elimination of this ion from the patient. …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary: Lithium may cause harm when administered to a pregnant woman. Early voluntary reports to international birth registries suggested an increase in cardiovascular malformations, especially for Ebstein’s anomaly, with first trimester use of lithium. Subsequent case-control and cohort studies indicate that the increased risk for cardiac malformations is likely to be small; however, the data are insufficient to establish a drug-associated risk. There are concerns for maternal and/or neonatal lithium toxicity during late pregnancy and the postpartum period [see Clinical Considerations]. Published animal developmental and toxicity studies in mice and rats report an …
