startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION For the Symptomatic Relief of Anxiety Clorazepate dipotassium tablets are administered orally in divided doses. The usual daily dose is 30 mg. The dose should be adjusted gradually within the range of 15 to 60 mg daily in accordance with the response of the patient. In elderly or debilitated patients it is advisable to initiate treatment at a daily dose of 7.5 to 15 mg. Clorazepate dipotassium tablets may also be administered in a single dose daily at bedtime; the recommended initial dose is 15 mg. After the initial dose, the response of the patient may require adjustment of subsequent dosage. Lower doses may be indicated in the elderly patient. Drowsiness may …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Overdosage of benzodiazepines is characterized by central nervous system depression ranging from drowsiness to coma. In mild to moderate cases, symptoms can include drowsiness, confusion, dysarthria, lethargy, hypnotic state, diminished reflexes, ataxia, and hypotonia. Rarely, paradoxical or disinhibitory reactions (including agitation, irritability, impulsivity, violent behavior, confusion, restlessness, excitement, and talkativeness) may occur. In severe overdosage cases, patients may develop respiratory depression and coma. Overdosage of benzodiazepines in combination with other CNS depressants (including alcohol and opioids) may be fatal (see WARNINGS: Dependence and …

majorDDI.sourceText: DRUG INTERACTIONS The concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids increases the risk of respiratory depression because of actions at different receptor sites in the CNS that control respiration. Benzodiazepines interact at GABAA sites and opioids interact primarily at mu receptors. When benzodiazepines and opioids are combined, the potential for benzodiazepines to significantly worsen opioid-related respiratory depression exists. Limit dosage and duration of concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids, and monitor patients closely for respiratory depression and sedation. If clorazepate dipotassium is to be combined with other drugs acting on the central nervous system, careful …

moa: Benzodiazepine PRODRUG: decarboxylated in acidic stomach to desmethyldiazepam (nordiazepam), a long-acting GABA-A positive allosteric modulator (↑ Cl- channel opening frequency).
receptorProfile: Acts via its active metabolite at the GABA-A benzodiazepine site.
onsetOfEffect: PO onset ~30–60 min (after gastric conversion to nordiazepam); long duration.
halfLife: Effective half-life is that of desmethyldiazepam (nordiazepam) — up to ~100–200 h (LONG class; accumulates).
metabolism: Prodrug → desmethyldiazepam (formed pre-systemically in the stomach), then hepatic oxidation to oxazepam (glucuronidated).
activeMetabolites: Desmethyldiazepam (nordiazepam, long-acting) — the active moiety; then oxazepam.
startingDose: 7.5–15 mg PO 2–3×/day for anxiety (lower in elderly).
titration: Titrate gradually; long metabolite means effects build over days.
maxDose: Up to ~60 mg/day in divided doses.
frequencyTiming: BID–TID or once daily at steady state (long active metabolite).
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce/avoid in elderly/hepatic impairment — nordiazepam accumulates; a LOT agent is safer.
startStopCrossTaper: Long half-life supports a smooth self-taper; still taper after regular use.
administrationNotes: Requires gastric acid for conversion — antacids/achlorhydria may alter absorption.
boxedWarning: WARNING: RISKS FROM CONCOMITANT USE WITH OPIOIDS; ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION; and DEPENDENCE AND WITHDRAWAL REACTIONS Concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Limit dosages and durations to the minimum required. Follow patients for signs and symptoms of respiratory depression and sedation (see WARNINGS and PRECAUTIONS ). The use of benzodiazepines, including clorazepate dipotassium, exposes users to risks of abuse, misuse, and addiction, which can lead to overdose or death. Abuse and misuse of benzodiazepines commonly involve concomitant use of other medications, alcohol, and/or illicit substances, which is associated with an increased frequency of serious adverse outcomes. Before prescribing …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Clorazepate dipotassium tablets are contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to the drug and in those with acute narrow angle glaucoma.
overdoseLethality: LOW alone; HIGH with opioids/alcohol/other CNS depressants.
discontinuationSyndrome: Withdrawal on stopping; long half-life gives a gentler self-tapering course — still taper after high-dose/long use.
ongoingMonitoring: Sedation/accumulation (esp. elderly/hepatic), dependence, respiratory status with opioids.
contraindicatedCombos: Opioids (avoid; lowest dose/shortest duration if unavoidable)
Severe respiratory insufficiency / untreated sleep apnea
pregnancy: Avoid; late/regular use → neonatal sedation/hypotonia/withdrawal.
lactation: Long-acting metabolite — infant accumulation possible; prefer a shorter agent.
geriatric: Beers — avoid; nordiazepam accumulation → sedation, confusion, falls.
renalHepatic: Active metabolite accumulates in hepatic/renal impairment — avoid there.
pearls: A prodrug converted in the acidic stomach to desmethyldiazepam (nordiazepam) — the same long-acting active metabolite shared with diazepam and chlordiazepoxide.
Long half-life → self-tapering; but accumulates in the elderly/hepatic disease (avoid there, like diazepam).
Gastric acid is needed for activation — antacids can reduce conversion.
mnemonics: "Clorazepate = the pro-drug that becomes Nordiazepam" — long-acting, like diazepam.
confusables: Clorazepate vs clonazepam — sound-alike, but clorazepate is a long-acting prodrug of nordiazepam whereas clonazepam is a distinct high-potency long-acting agent.
Clorazepate vs chlordiazepoxide — both yield desmethyldiazepam.
classDifferentiators: Long-acting benzodiazepine PRODRUG (→ nordiazepam); gastric-acid-activated; behaves like diazepam/chlordiazepoxide and accumulates in elderly/hepatic disease.
examHooks: Which benzodiazepine is a prodrug converted to desmethyldiazepam (nordiazepam) in the stomach? (clorazepate).
counsellingPoints: Effects and drowsiness build over days.
Avoid alcohol/opioids.
Do not stop abruptly after regular use.

vignettes.stem: Clorazepate is described as a "prodrug." Which active moiety is chiefly responsible for its long duration of action?
vignettes.explanation: Clorazepate is decarboxylated in the stomach to desmethyldiazepam (nordiazepam), a long-acting active metabolite it shares with diazepam and chlordiazepoxide, accounting for its long half-life and accumulation in the elderly/hepatic disease.

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE There is limited clinical experience with QUVIVIQ overdose. In clinical pharmacology studies, healthy subjects were administered single doses of up to 200 mg (4 times the maximum recommended dose) of QUVIVIQ. The following adverse reactions were observed: somnolence, muscle weakness, cataplexy-like symptoms, sleep paralysis, disturbance in attention, fatigue, headache, and constipation. There is no specific antidote to an overdosage of QUVIVIQ. In the event of an overdose, general symptomatic and supportive medical care, along with immediate gastric lavage where appropriate, should be provided and patients should be carefully monitored. Dialysis is unlikely to be effective as …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors: Avoid concomitant use. ( 2.2 , 7.1 ) Moderate CYP3A4 inhibitors: Maximum recommended dose is 25 mg. ( 2.2 , 7.1 ) Moderate or Strong CYP3A4 inducers: Avoid concomitant use. ( 7.1 ) 7.1 Effects of Other Drugs on QUVIVIQ Table 2 describes clinically significant drug interactions where the concomitant use of other drugs affects QUVIVIQ. Table 2 Effects of Other Drugs on QUVIVIQ Strong or Moderate CYP3A4 Inhibitors Clinical Implications: Concomitant use with a strong or moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor increases exposure to daridorexant [see Clinical Pharmacology (12.3) ] , which may increase the risk of QUVIVIQ adverse reactions. Prevention or Management: …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Pregnancy Exposure Registry There will be a pregnancy exposure registry that monitors pregnancy outcomes in women exposed to QUVIVIQ during pregnancy. Pregnant women exposed to QUVIVIQ and healthcare providers are encouraged to call Idorsia Pharmaceuticals Ltd at 1-833-400-9611. Risk Summary There are no available data on QUVIVIQ use in pregnant women to evaluate for drug-associated risks of major birth defects, miscarriage, or other adverse maternal or fetal outcomes. In animal reproduction studies, oral administration of daridorexant to pregnant rats and rabbits during the period of organogenesis did not cause fetal toxicity or malformation at doses up to 8 and 10 times the …

moa: Dual Orexin Receptor Antagonist (DORA) — blocks OX1R and OX2R, reducing orexin wake drive to promote sleep onset and maintenance. No GABA/benzodiazepine activity.
receptorProfile: OX1R/OX2R antagonist.
onsetOfEffect: Rapid — reduces sleep latency the night of dosing.
halfLife: ~8 hours (shorter than lemborexant → designed to minimize next-day residual effects).
metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 (major).
startingDose: 25 mg PO ~30 minutes before bedtime.
titration: May increase to 50 mg based on response/tolerability.
maxDose: 50 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once nightly with ≥7 h remaining in bed; onset delayed if taken with food.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Max 25 mg in moderate hepatic impairment; avoid in severe hepatic impairment.
startStopCrossTaper: No taper required — minimal rebound/withdrawal.
administrationNotes: Its shorter half-life is the selling point versus lemborexant for daytime function.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS QUVIVIQ is contraindicated: in patients with narcolepsy. in patients with a history of hypersensitivity to daridorexant or any components of QUVIVIQ. Angioedema with pharyngeal involvement has been reported [see Adverse Reactions (6.2) ] . Narcolepsy. ( 4 ) Known hypersensitivity to daridorexant or other components of QUVIVIQ. ( 4 )
overdoseLethality: Low; excessive somnolence expected; no specific antidote.
discontinuationSyndrome: Minimal — no significant physiologic dependence or rebound.
ongoingMonitoring: Assess residual daytime somnolence (less than other hypnotics) and mood.
pregnancy: Limited data; use only if clearly needed.
lactation: Limited data; caution.
geriatric: Shorter half-life is advantageous; still start 25 mg and watch falls.
pearls: A DORA like lemborexant, but with a SHORTER half-life (~8 h) → engineered for less next-day impairment.
Non-dependence-forming orexin antagonist — a benzo-free option for both sleep onset and maintenance.
CYP3A4-dependent — avoid strong CYP3A4 inhibitors.
mnemonics: "DARIdorexant = DAYtime-friendlier DORA (shorter half-life)."
confusables: Daridorexant vs lemborexant — same DORA class; daridorexant is the SHORTER-acting one (less next-day carryover).
classDifferentiators: DORA with the shortest half-life of the marketed orexin antagonists → least next-day impairment; non-dependence-forming.
examHooks: Which orexin antagonist has the shortest half-life / least next-day impairment? (daridorexant)
counsellingPoints: Take ~30 min before bed with ≥7 h to sleep.
Take on an empty stomach for faster onset.
Less next-day grogginess than older hypnotics, but still use care driving.
Report dream-enactment or brief muscle weakness.

vignettes.stem: Two orexin antagonists are compared for a patient who reports next-morning grogginess. Which agent has the shorter half-life and is preferred to minimize next-day residual sedation?
vignettes.explanation: Daridorexant (~8 h half-life) is shorter-acting than lemborexant (~17–19 h) and was developed specifically to reduce next-day impairment while still treating sleep onset and maintenance.
vignettes.testedFact: Daridorexant shorter half-life / less next-day impairment

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: RISKS FROM CONCOMITANT USE WITH OPIOIDS; ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION; and DEPENDENCE AND WITHDRAWAL REACTIONS • Concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Limit dosages and durations to the minimum required. Follow patients for signs and symptoms of respiratory depression and sedation (see WARNINGS and PRECAUTIONS ). • The use of benzodiazepines, including diazepam tablets, exposes users to risks of abuse, misuse, and addiction, which can lead to overdose or death. Abuse and misuse of benzodiazepines commonly involve concomitant use of other medications, alcohol, and/or illicit substances, which is associated with an increased frequency of serious adverse outcomes. Before prescribing diazepam tablets and throughout treatment, assess each patient’s risk for abuse, misuse, and addiction (see WARNINGS ) . • The continued use of benzodiazepines, including diazepam tablets, may lead to clinically significant physical dependence. The risks of dependence and withdrawal increase with longer treatment duration and higher daily dose. Abrupt discontinuation or rapid dosage reduction of diazepam tablets after continued use may precipitate acute withdrawal reactions, which can be …

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Diazepam tablets are contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to diazepam and, because of lack of sufficient clinical experience, in pediatric patients under 6 months of age. Diazepam tablets are also contraindicated in patients with myasthenia gravis, severe respiratory insufficiency, severe hepatic insufficiency, and sleep apnea syndrome. They may be used in patients with open-angle glaucoma who are receiving appropriate therapy, but is contraindicated in acute narrow-angle glaucoma.

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Risks from Concomitant Use with Opioids Concomitant use of benzodiazepiones, including diazepam tablets, and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Because of these risks, reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Observational studies have demonstrated that concomitant use of opioid analgesics and benzodiazepines increases the risk of drug-related mortality compared to use of opioids alone. If a decision is made to prescribe diazepam tablets concomitantly with opioids, prescribe the lowest effective dosages and minimum durations of concomitant use, and follow patients …

pregnancy.sourceText: Neonatal Sedation and Withdrawal Syndrome Use of diazepam tablets late in pregnancy can result in sedation (respiratory depression, lethargy, hypotonia) and/or withdrawal symptoms (hyperreflexia, irritability, restlessness, tremors, inconsolable crying, and feeding difficulties) in the neonate (see PRECAUTIONS: Pregnancy ). Monitor neonates exposed to diazepam during pregnancy or labor for signs of sedation and monitor neonates exposed to diazepam during pregnancy for signs of withdrawal; manage these neonates accordingly. formulationsCanada: Tablet (10 MG, 5 MG, 2 MG, 1 MG, 15 MG)
Solution (10 MG, 5 MG, 2 MG, 1 MG, 15 MG)
moa: Benzodiazepine: GABA-A positive allosteric modulator (↑ Cl- channel opening frequency); highly lipophilic → very rapid CNS onset.
receptorProfile: Non-selective GABA-A benzodiazepine-site agonist.
onsetOfEffect: Very rapid (PO ~15 min; IV within 1–3 min) due to high lipophilicity, but redistributes quickly; long terminal accumulation via active metabolites.
halfLife: Parent ~20–50 h; active metabolite desmethyldiazepam (nordiazepam) up to ~200 h (LONG class — accumulates).
timeToSteadyState: Up to ~2 weeks because of long-lived active metabolites.
metabolism: Hepatic oxidation via CYP2C19 and CYP3A4 to ACTIVE metabolites (desmethyldiazepam → oxazepam, temazepam).
activeMetabolites: Desmethyldiazepam (nordiazepam, very long half-life), plus temazepam and oxazepam — accumulate, especially in the elderly.
startingDose: 2–10 mg PO 2–4×/day depending on indication; 5–10 mg IV for seizures/withdrawal per protocol.
titration: Titrate to effect; long metabolites mean effects build over days.
maxDose: Commonly up to ~40 mg/day PO in divided doses (higher in supervised withdrawal/seizure settings).
frequencyTiming: BID–QID; rectal and IV forms exist for seizures.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Avoid/reduce in hepatic impairment and elderly — active metabolites accumulate. A glucuronidated (LOT) benzo is safer there.
startStopCrossTaper: Its long half-life makes it a preferred agent for tapering/cross-tapering off shorter-acting benzos and for alcohol-withdrawal "self-tapering" regimens.
administrationNotes: Rapid onset + long tail: fast symptom relief but prolonged accumulation.
boxedWarning: WARNING: RISKS FROM CONCOMITANT USE WITH OPIOIDS; ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION; and DEPENDENCE AND WITHDRAWAL REACTIONS • Concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Limit dosages and durations to the minimum required. Follow patients for signs and symptoms of respiratory depression and sedation (see WARNINGS and PRECAUTIONS ). • The use of benzodiazepines, including diazepam tablets, exposes users to risks of abuse, misuse, and addiction, which can lead to overdose or death. Abuse and misuse of benzodiazepines commonly involve concomitant use of other medications, alcohol, and/or illicit substances, which is associated with an increased frequency of serious adverse outcomes. Before prescribing …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Diazepam tablets are contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to diazepam and, because of lack of sufficient clinical experience, in pediatric patients under 6 months of age. Diazepam tablets are also contraindicated in patients with myasthenia gravis, severe respiratory insufficiency, severe hepatic insufficiency, and sleep apnea syndrome. They may be used in patients with open-angle glaucoma who are receiving appropriate therapy, but is contraindicated in acute narrow-angle glaucoma.
overdoseLethality: LOW alone; HIGH with opioids/alcohol/other CNS depressants (respiratory depression).
discontinuationSyndrome: Withdrawal on stopping; long half-life gives a slower, milder, self-tapering course — but seizures still possible after high-dose/long-term use.
ongoingMonitoring: Sedation, accumulation (esp. elderly/hepatic), respiratory status with opioids, dependence.
majorDDI: CYP2C19/3A4 inhibitors (e.g., omeprazole, fluvoxamine → raise levels)
contraindicatedCombos: Opioids (avoid; lowest dose/shortest duration if unavoidable)
Severe respiratory insufficiency / untreated sleep apnea
Myasthenia gravis / severe hepatic impairment (caution per label)
pregnancy: Avoid where possible; late/regular use → neonatal hypotonia, sedation, withdrawal ("floppy infant").
lactation: Long-acting with active metabolites — accumulation in the infant possible; a shorter-acting benzo is preferred if one is needed.
geriatric: Beers — avoid; active metabolites (desmethyldiazepam) accumulate → prolonged sedation, falls, confusion.
renalHepatic: Metabolites accumulate in hepatic impairment and renal failure; avoid in favour of a LOT agent.
pearls: Long-acting via active metabolites (desmethyldiazepam ~200 h) → smooth, self-tapering course; classic for alcohol withdrawal and for tapering off shorter benzos.
Very lipophilic → fast onset for muscle spasm/acute seizures, but a long accumulation tail.
Accumulation in the elderly/hepatic patients is the key pitfall — opposite of the "LOT" agents.
mnemonics: "diazepam = Deep and Durable" — Desmethyldiazepam Drags on for Days (accumulates).
confusables: Diazepam vs lorazepam — diazepam is long-acting with active metabolites (accumulates); lorazepam is glucuronidated and liver-safe.
Desmethyldiazepam is the shared active metabolite of diazepam, clorazepate, and chlordiazepoxide.
classDifferentiators: Long half-life via long-lived active metabolites, rapid lipophilic onset, muscle-relaxant/anticonvulsant/withdrawal uses; accumulates in elderly/hepatic disease.
examHooks: Which benzodiazepine self-tapers due to its long-half-life active metabolite (desmethyldiazepam)? (diazepam).
Why avoid diazepam in the elderly? (active-metabolite accumulation → falls/sedation).
counsellingPoints: Effects and drowsiness can build up over days.
Avoid alcohol/opioids.
Do not stop abruptly after regular use.

vignettes.stem: A 30-year-old in alcohol withdrawal with normal liver function is treated with a symptom-triggered benzodiazepine regimen. Which agent’s long-acting active metabolite provides a smooth, "self-tapering" course?
vignettes.explanation: Diazepam is oxidized to desmethyldiazepam (nordiazepam), an active metabolite with a very long half-life, giving a self-tapering effect useful in alcohol withdrawal when hepatic function is preserved; in significant liver disease, glucuronidated lorazepam/oxazepam are preferred.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: RISKS FROM CONCOMITANT USE WITH OPIOIDS; ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION; and DEPENDENCE AND WITHDRAWAL REACTIONS Concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Limit dosages and durations to the minimum required. Follow patients for signs and symptoms of respiratory depression and sedation [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 ) and Drug Interactions ( 7.1 ) ]. The use of benzodiazepines, including flurazepam hydrochloride capsules, exposes users to risks of abuse, misuse, and addiction, which can lead to overdose or death. Abuse and misuse of benzodiazepines commonly involve concomitant use of other medications, alcohol, and/or illicit substances, which is associated with an increased frequency of serious adverse outcomes. Before prescribing flurazepam hydrochloride capsules, and throughout treatment, assess each patient’s risk for abuse, misuse, and addiction [ see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.2 ) ]. The continued use of benzodiazepines, including flurazepam hydrochloride capsules, may lead to clinically significant physical dependence. The risks of dependence and withdrawal increase with longer treatment duration and higher daily dose. Abrupt discontinuation or rapid dosage reduction …

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Flurazepam hydrochloride capsules are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to flurazepam or other benzodiazepines. Rare cases of angioedema involving the tongue, glottis or larynx have been reported in patients after taking the first or subsequent doses of flurazepam. Some patients have had additional symptoms such as dyspnea, throat closing, or nausea and vomiting that suggest anaphylaxis. Patients who develop such reactions should not be rechallenged with flurazepam. Hypersensitivity to flurazepam or other benzodiazepines. ( 4 )

startingDose.sourceText: 2 DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Use the lowest dose effective for the patient. Recommended initial dose is 15 mg for women and 15 mg or 30 mg for men. ( 2.1 ) Elderly or debilitated patients: recommended dose is 15 mg. ( 2.2 ) 2.1 Dosage in Adults Use the lowest dose effective for the patient, as important adverse effects of flurazepam hydrochloride capsules are dose related. The recommended initial dose is 15 mg for women and either 15 mg or 30 mg for men. The 15 mg dose can be increased to 30 mg if necessary for efficacy. The recommended initial doses for women and men are different because flurazepam clearance is lower in women [see Pharmacokinetics ( 12.3 ) ]. 2.2 Dosage in Elderly or …

seriousAE.sourceText: 5 WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS CNS depressant effects: Impaired alertness and motor coordination, including risk of falling. Daytime impairment. Caution patients against driving and other activities requiring complete mental alertness. ( 5.4 ) The failure of insomnia to remit after 7 to 10 days of treatment may indicate the presence of a primary psychiatric and/or medical illness that should be evaluated. ( 5.5 ) Severe anaphylactic/anaphylactoid reactions: Angioedema and anaphylaxis have been reported. Do not rechallenge if such reactions occur. ( 5.6 ) Sleep driving and other complex behaviors while not fully awake: Risk increases with dose and concomitant CNS depressants and alcohol. …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Overdosage of benzodiazepines is characterized by central nervous system depression ranging from drowsiness to coma. In mild to moderate cases, symptoms can include drowsiness, confusion, dysarthria, lethargy, hypnotic state, diminished reflexes, ataxia, and hypotonia. Rarely, paradoxical or disinhibitory reactions (including agitation, irritability, impulsivity, violent behavior, confusion, restlessness, excitement, and talkativeness) may occur. In severe overdosage cases, patients may develop respiratory depression and coma. Overdosage of benzodiazepines in combination with other CNS depressants (including alcohol and opioids) may be fatal [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.2 …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, and death. ( 7.1 ) CNS Depressants: Downward dose adjustment may be necessary due to additive effects. ( 7.2 ) 7.1 Opioids The concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids increases the risk of respiratory depression because of actions at different receptor sites in the CNS that control respiration. Benzodiazepines interact at GABA A sites, and opioids interact primarily at mu receptors. When benzodiazepines and opioids are combined, the potential for benzodiazepines to significantly worsen opioid-related respiratory depression exists. Limit dosage and duration of …

( 8.2 ) 8.1 Pregnancy Pregnancy Exposure Registry: There is a pregnancy exposure registry that monitors pregnancy outcomes in women exposed to psychiatric medications, including flurazepam, during pregnancy. Healthcare providers are encouraged to register patients by calling the National Pregnancy Registry for Psychiatric Medications at 1-866-961-2388 or visiting online at https://womensmentalhealth.org/pregnancyregistry/ . moa: Benzodiazepine: GABA-A positive allosteric modulator (↑ Cl- channel opening frequency); long-acting hypnotic via active metabolites.
receptorProfile: Non-selective GABA-A benzodiazepine-site agonist.
onsetOfEffect: PO onset ~30–60 min; effect prolonged by long-lived metabolites.
halfLife: Parent short, but active metabolite N-desalkylflurazepam ~40–250 h (LONG class — accumulates).
metabolism: Hepatic oxidation (CYP) to N-desalkylflurazepam (very long half-life).
activeMetabolites: N-desalkylflurazepam (active, very long-acting) — accumulates → next-day/2nd-day sedation.
startingDose: 15 mg PO at bedtime (elderly).
titration: Lowest effective dose; short-term.
maxDose: 30 mg at bedtime.
frequencyTiming: At bedtime; sedation accumulates over successive nights.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce/avoid in elderly/hepatic impairment — active metabolite accumulates.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper after regular use; long metabolite gives a gentler, self-tapering withdrawal.
administrationNotes: Classic example of hypnotic accumulation → next-day and cumulative sedation.
boxedWarning: WARNING: RISKS FROM CONCOMITANT USE WITH OPIOIDS; ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION; and DEPENDENCE AND WITHDRAWAL REACTIONS Concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Limit dosages and durations to the minimum required. Follow patients for signs and symptoms of respiratory depression and sedation [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 ) and Drug Interactions ( 7.1 ) ]. The use of benzodiazepines, including flurazepam hydrochloride capsules, exposes users to risks of abuse, misuse, and addiction, which can lead to overdose or death. Abuse and misuse of benzodiazepines commonly involve concomitant use of other medications, alcohol, and/or illicit substances, which is associated with an increased frequency …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Flurazepam hydrochloride capsules are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to flurazepam or other benzodiazepines. Rare cases of angioedema involving the tongue, glottis or larynx have been reported in patients after taking the first or subsequent doses of flurazepam. Some patients have had additional symptoms such as dyspnea, throat closing, or nausea and vomiting that suggest anaphylaxis. Patients who develop such reactions should not be rechallenged with flurazepam. Hypersensitivity to flurazepam or other benzodiazepines. ( 4 )
overdoseLethality: LOW alone; HIGH with opioids/alcohol/other CNS depressants.
discontinuationSyndrome: Rebound insomnia; long metabolite softens withdrawal but accumulation is the main issue.
ongoingMonitoring: Cumulative/next-day sedation, falls in elderly, dependence, respiratory status with opioids.
contraindicatedCombos: Opioids (avoid; lowest dose/shortest duration if unavoidable)
Severe respiratory insufficiency / untreated sleep apnea
pregnancy: Avoid; late use → neonatal sedation/hypotonia/withdrawal.
lactation: Long-acting metabolite — infant accumulation possible; avoid/prefer a shorter hypnotic.
geriatric: Beers — avoid; the accumulating active metabolite makes next-day sedation and falls especially likely.
pearls: Long-acting hypnotic via N-desalkylflurazepam (up to ~250 h) → cumulative next-day sedation over successive nights — the classic "accumulating hypnotic".
Contrast with temazepam (intermediate, glucuronidated, no active metabolite) for a cleaner hypnotic in the elderly/hepatic patients.
mnemonics: "Flurazepam = Forever" — the active metabolite lingers, so sedation accumulates.
confusables: Flurazepam vs temazepam — flurazepam accumulates (long active metabolite), temazepam does not (intermediate, glucuronidated).
classDifferentiators: Long-acting hypnotic driven by a very long active metabolite → cumulative next-day sedation; the opposite of the LOT hypnotic temazepam.
examHooks: Which hypnotic benzodiazepine accumulates over successive nights because of a long-lived active metabolite? (flurazepam — N-desalkylflurazepam).
counsellingPoints: Grogginess can build up night after night — report it.
Avoid alcohol/opioids.
Short-term use; caution driving.

vignettes.stem: After several nights of flurazepam, an elderly patient is progressively more sedated during the day. Which pharmacologic feature explains the cumulative effect?
vignettes.explanation: Flurazepam is converted to N-desalkylflurazepam, whose extremely long half-life leads to accumulation with repeated nightly dosing, causing progressive daytime sedation — pronounced in the elderly.

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Hydroxyzine, when administered to the pregnant mouse, rat, and rabbit, induced fetal abnormalities in the rat and mouse at doses substantially above the human therapeutic range. Clinical data in human beings are inadequate to establish safety in early pregnancy. Until such data are available, hydroxyzine is contraindicated in early pregnancy. Hydroxyzine is contraindicated in patients with a prolonged QT interval. Hydroxyzine pamoate is contraindicated for patients who have shown a previous hypersensitivity to any component of this medication. Hydroxyzine is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to hydroxyzine products, and in patients with known hypersensitivity to cetirizine hydrochloride or levocetirizine hydrochloride.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE For symptomatic relief of anxiety and tension associated with psychoneurosis and as an adjunct in organic disease states in which anxiety is manifested: in adults, 50 mg to 100 mg q.i.d.; children under 6 years, 50 mg daily in divided doses; and over 6 years, 50 mg to 100 mg daily in divided doses. For use in the management of pruritus due to allergic conditions such as chronic urticaria and atopic and contact dermatoses, and in histamine-mediated pruritus: in adults, 25 mg t.i.d. or q.i.d.; children under 6 years, 50 mg daily in divided doses; and over 6 years, 50 mg to 100 mg daily in divided doses. As a sedative when used as a premedication and following general anesthesia: 50 mg …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Nursing Mothers It is not known whether this drug is excreted in human milk. Since many drugs are so excreted, hydroxyzine should not be given to nursing mothers.

syndromes.sourceText: WARNINGS Nursing Mothers It is not known whether this drug is excreted in human milk. Since many drugs are so excreted, hydroxyzine should not be given to nursing mothers.

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE The most common manifestation of overdosage of hydroxyzine pamoate is hypersedation. Other reported signs and symptoms were convulsions, stupor, nausea and vomiting. As in the management of overdosage with any drug, it should be borne in mind that multiple agents may have been taken. If vomiting has not occurred spontaneously, it should be induced. Immediate gastric lavage is also recommended. General supportive care, including frequent monitoring of the vital signs and close observation of the patient, is indicated. Hypotension, though unlikely, may be controlled with intravenous fluids and vasopressors ( do not use epinephrine as hydroxyzine counteracts its pressor action ). …

moa: First-generation (piperazine) H1-antihistamine. Anxiolytic/sedative via central H1 antagonism; also antimuscarinic and some 5-HT2 antagonism. No GABA/benzodiazepine activity.
receptorProfile: H1 antagonist (central + peripheral), antimuscarinic, mild 5-HT2 antagonism.
onsetOfEffect: Rapid — anxiolysis/sedation within ~30–60 minutes, so it CAN be used PRN.
halfLife: ~20 hours in adults (longer, ~29 h, in the elderly).
metabolism: Hepatic; the major metabolite cetirizine is itself an active (marketed) antihistamine.
startingDose: Anxiety 25–50 mg PO TID–QID; pruritus 25 mg PO TID–QID.
titration: Titrate to effect within the daily maximum; often used PRN.
maxDose: Up to ~100 mg/day for anxiety (use the lowest effective dose; higher risk of anticholinergic/QT effects above this).
frequencyTiming: TID–QID or PRN; a bedtime dose exploits its sedation.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce dose/frequency in hepatic or renal impairment.
startStopCrossTaper: No taper needed — no dependence or withdrawal.
administrationNotes: A non-dependence-forming PRN option; useful when abuse potential is a concern.
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Hydroxyzine, when administered to the pregnant mouse, rat, and rabbit, induced fetal abnormalities in the rat and mouse at doses substantially above the human therapeutic range. Clinical data in human beings are inadequate to establish safety in early pregnancy. Until such data are available, hydroxyzine is contraindicated in early pregnancy. Hydroxyzine is contraindicated in patients with a prolonged QT interval. Hydroxyzine pamoate is contraindicated for patients who have shown a previous hypersensitivity to any component of this medication. Hydroxyzine is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to hydroxyzine products, and in patients with known hypersensitivity to cetirizine hydrochloride or levocetirizine hydrochloride.
commonSE: Anticholinergic effects (constipation, blurred vision, urinary retention)
overdoseLethality: Low–moderate; anticholinergic toxidrome and QT prolongation in large ingestions.
discontinuationSyndrome: None — no physiologic dependence.
baselineWorkup: Consider ECG if cardiac risk factors, electrolyte abnormalities, or other QT-prolonging drugs, especially at higher doses
ongoingMonitoring: Watch anticholinergic load and sedation in the elderly; QT if combined with other QT-prolonging agents.
majorDDI: CNS depressants (alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines) — additive sedation
contraindicatedCombos: Other QT-prolonging drugs at high dose (additive torsades risk)
pregnancy: Avoid in early pregnancy and near term (neonatal effects); use only if clearly needed.
lactation: Antihistamines pass into milk and may sedate the infant / reduce supply — use with caution.
geriatric: Anticholinergic and sedating — on Beers-type caution lists; risk of falls, confusion, urinary retention.
cardiac: Dose-related QT prolongation — caution with QT risk factors or other QT drugs.
pearls: An antihistamine used as a non-dependence-forming anxiolytic — good PRN option when abuse potential is a concern (e.g., substance-use history).
Watch two things at higher doses: anticholinergic burden (esp. elderly) and dose-related QT prolongation.
Its active metabolite, cetirizine, is itself a marketed second-generation antihistamine.
mnemonics: "Hydroxyzine — the antiHISTAMINE that calms and itches less; no addiction, but dry + QT."
confusables: Hydroxyzine vs hydralazine (antihypertensive) vs hydroxychloroquine — sound-alike, unrelated drugs.
classDifferentiators: H1-antihistamine anxiolytic; rapid/PRN and non-dependence-forming (unlike Z-drugs/benzos), but anticholinergic and QT-prolonging (unlike buspirone/melatonergics).
examHooks: Non-addictive anxiolytic usable PRN with anticholinergic + QT cautions? (hydroxyzine)
counsellingPoints: May cause drowsiness — avoid driving until you know your response.
Do not combine with alcohol.
Dry mouth/constipation are common; report palpitations or fainting.

vignettes.stem: A 45-year-old with GAD and active opioid use disorder needs short-term anxiolysis without dependence potential, usable as needed. Which agent best fits?
vignettes.explanation: Hydroxyzine is a rapidly acting H1-antihistamine anxiolytic with no abuse/dependence potential, appropriate PRN in patients where controlled sedative-hypnotics are undesirable; watch anticholinergic effects and QT at higher doses.

startingDose.sourceText: 2 DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Recommended dose is 5 mg taken no more than once per night, immediately before going to bed, with at least 7 hours remaining before the planned time of awakening. Dosage may be increased to 10 mg based on clinical response and tolerability. ( 2.1 ) The maximum recommended dose is 10 mg once daily. ( 2.1 ) Time to sleep onset may be delayed if taken with or soon after a meal. ( 2.1 ) Hepatic Impairment: ( 2.3 ) ○ Moderate hepatic impairment: Initial and maximum recommended dosage is 5 mg no more than once per night. ○ Severe hepatic impairment: Not recommended. 2.1 Dosing Information The recommended dosage of DAYVIGO is 5 mg taken no more than once per night, …

seriousAE.sourceText: 5 WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS CNS Depressant Effects and Daytime Impairment: Impairs alertness and motor coordination including morning impairment. Risk increases with dose and use with other central nervous system (CNS) depressants. For patients taking DAYVIGO 10 mg, caution against next-day driving and other activities requiring complete mental alertness. ( 5.1 ) Sleep Paralysis, Hypnagogic/Hypnopompic Hallucinations, and Cataplexy-like Symptoms: May occur with use of DAYVIGO. ( 5.2 ) Complex Sleep Behaviors: Behaviors including sleep-walking, sleep-driving, and engaging in other activities while not fully awake may occur. Discontinue immediately if a complex sleep behavior occurs. ( 5.3 ) …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE There is limited clinical experience with DAYVIGO overdose. In clinical pharmacology studies, healthy patients who were administered multiple doses of up to 75 mg (7.5 times the maximum recommended dose) of DAYVIGO showed dose-dependent increases in the frequency of somnolence. There is no available specific antidote to an overdose of DAYVIGO. In the event of overdose, standard medical practice for the management of any overdose should be used. In managing overdose, provide supportive care, including close medical supervision and monitoring and consider the possibility of multiple drug involvement. Consult a Certified Poison Control Center for the most up to date information …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Strong or moderate CYP3A inhibitors: Avoid concomitant use. ( 7.1 ) Weak CYP3A inhibitors: The maximum recommended dose is 5 mg. ( 2.2 , 7.1 ) Strong or moderate CYP3A inducers: Avoid concomitant use. ( 7.1 ) 7.1 Drugs Having Clinically Important Interactions with DAYVIGO Table 2: Clinically Important Drug Interactions with DAYVIGO Effect of Other Drugs on DAYVIGO Strong, Moderate, and Weak CYP3A Inhibitors Clinical Impact: Concomitant use with a strong, moderate, or weak CYP3A inhibitor increases lemborexant AUC and C max which may increase the risk of DAYVIGO adverse reactions [see Clinical Pharmacology ( 12.3 )]. Intervention: Avoid concomitant use of DAYVIGO with …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Pregnancy Exposure Registry There is a pregnancy exposure registry that monitors pregnancy outcomes in women who are exposed to psychiatric medications, including DAYVIGO, during pregnancy. Healthcare providers are encouraged to advise patients to register by calling the National Pregnancy Registry for Psychiatric Medications, at1-866-961-2388 or visiting online at https://womensmentalhealth.org/research/pregnancyregistry . In animal reproduction studies, oral administration of lemborexant to …

moa: Dual Orexin Receptor Antagonist (DORA) — competitively blocks OX1R and OX2R, reducing orexin-A/B wake-promoting signalling → promotes sleep onset AND maintenance. No GABA/benzodiazepine activity.
receptorProfile: OX1R/OX2R antagonist; no meaningful GABAergic, histaminergic, or monoaminergic action.
onsetOfEffect: Rapid — reduces sleep latency the same night.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 (major, some 3A5).
startingDose: 5 mg PO immediately before bedtime.
titration: May increase to 10 mg based on response/tolerability.
maxDose: 10 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once nightly, with ≥7 h remaining in bed; onset delayed if taken with/soon after food.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Max 5 mg in moderate hepatic impairment; avoid in severe hepatic impairment.
startStopCrossTaper: No taper required — minimal rebound/withdrawal.
administrationNotes: Take on an empty stomach for fastest onset.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS DAYVIGO is contraindicated in patients with narcolepsy. DAYVIGO is contraindicated in patients with narcolepsy. ( 4 )
seriousAE: Next-day driving/psychomotor impairment (dose-related, greater at 10 mg)
overdoseLethality: Low; excessive somnolence expected. Much less dangerous than barbiturates; no specific antidote.
discontinuationSyndrome: Minimal — no significant physiologic dependence or rebound.
ongoingMonitoring: Assess next-day somnolence (especially at 10 mg) and driving safety; screen for depression.
majorDDI: Strong/moderate CYP3A4 inhibitors — avoid or reduce dose
pregnancy: Limited data; use only if clearly needed.
lactation: Limited data; caution.
geriatric: Next-day somnolence and falls are the main concern; use 5 mg.
pearls: Orexin antagonists (DORAs) are non-dependence-forming and promote both sleep onset and maintenance — a mechanistically distinct alternative to benzo-site Z-drugs.
Next-day somnolence is dose-related and greater at 10 mg — counsel about driving.
CYP3A4-dependent — avoid with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors.
mnemonics: "OREXIN keeps you awake → block it (DORA) to sleep."
confusables: Lemborexant vs daridorexant — both DORAs; lemborexant has the LONGER half-life (~17–19 h) → more next-day somnolence than daridorexant.
classDifferentiators: DORA (orexin antagonist) — longer half-life than daridorexant; non-dependence-forming; sleep onset + maintenance; next-day somnolence at higher dose.
examHooks: Which sleep drug class blocks orexin receptors? (DORAs — lemborexant, daridorexant)
counsellingPoints: Take right before bed with ≥7 h to sleep.
Take on an empty stomach for fastest effect.
You may feel next-day drowsiness — be cautious driving.
Report dream-enactment, sleep paralysis, or brief muscle weakness.

vignettes.stem: A 58-year-old with sleep-onset AND maintenance insomnia, and a history of sedative misuse, needs a non-dependence-forming hypnotic. Which mechanism is most appropriate?
vignettes.explanation: DORAs such as lemborexant block wake-promoting orexin signalling, improving both sleep onset and maintenance without the dependence liability of benzo-site agents — favourable when abuse potential is a concern.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: RISKS FROM CONCOMITANT USE WITH OPIOIDS; ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION; and DEPENDENCE AND WITHDRAWAL REACTIONS • Concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs for patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Limit dosages and durations to the minimum required. Follow patients for signs and symptoms of respiratory depression and sedation (see WARNINGS and PRECAUTIONS ). • The use of benzodiazepines, including lorazepam, exposes users to risks of abuse, misuse, and addiction, which can lead to overdose or death. Abuse and misuse of benzodiazepines commonly involve concomitant use of other medications, alcohol, and/or illicit substances, which is associated with an increased frequency of serious adverse outcomes. Before prescribing lorazepam and throughout treatment, assess each patient’s risk for abuse, misuse, and addiction (see WARNINGS ). • The continued use of benzodiazepines, including lorazepam, may lead to clinically significant physical dependence. The risks of dependence and withdrawal increase with longer treatment duration and higher daily dose. Abrupt discontinuation or rapid dosage reduction of lorazepam after continued use may precipitate acute withdrawal reactions, which can be life-threatening. To reduce the risk of …

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Lorazepam is contraindicated in patients with: • hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines or to any components of the formulation • acute narrow-angle glaucoma.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Lorazepam tablets are administered orally. For optimal results, dose, frequency of administration, and duration of therapy should be individualized according to patient response. To facilitate this, 0.5 mg, 1 mg, and 2 mg tablets are available. The usual range is 2 to 6 mg/day given in divided doses, the largest dose being taken before bedtime, but the daily dosage may vary from 1 to 10 mg/day. For anxiety, most patients require an initial dose of 2 to 3 mg/day given two times a day or three times a day. For insomnia due to anxiety or transient situational stress, a single daily dose of 2 to 4 mg may be given, usually at bedtime. For elderly or debilitated …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Risks from Concomitant Use with Opioids Concomitant use of benzodiazepines, including lorazepam, and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Because of these risks, reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Observational studies have demonstrated that concomitant use of opioid analgesics and benzodiazepines increases the risk of drug-related mortality compared to use of opioids alone. If a decision is made to prescribe lorazepam concomitantly with opioids, prescribe the lowest effective dosages and minimum durations of concomitant use, and follow patients closely for …

syndromes.sourceText: WARNINGS Risks from Concomitant Use with Opioids Concomitant use of benzodiazepines, including lorazepam, and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Because of these risks, reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Observational studies have demonstrated that concomitant use of opioid analgesics and benzodiazepines increases the risk of drug-related mortality compared to use of opioids alone. If a decision is made to prescribe lorazepam concomitantly with opioids, prescribe the lowest effective dosages and minimum durations of concomitant use, and follow patients closely for …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Overdosage of benzodiazepines is characterized by central nervous system depression ranging from drowsiness to coma. In mild to moderate cases, symptoms can include drowsiness, confusion, dysarthria, lethargy, hypnotic state, diminished reflexes, ataxia, and hypotonia. Rarely, paradoxical or disinhibitory reactions (including agitation, irritability, impulsivity, violent behavior, confusion, restlessness, excitement, and talkativeness) may occur. In severe overdosage cases, patients may develop respiratory depression and coma. Overdosage of benzodiazepines in combination with other CNS depressants (including alcohol and opioids) may be fatal (see ). Markedly abnormal (lowered or …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions The concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids increases the risk of respiratory depression because of actions at different receptor sites in the CNS that control respiration. Benzodiazepines interact at GABA A sites and opioids interact primarily at mu receptors. When benzodiazepines and opioids are combined, the potential for benzodiazepines to significantly worsen opioid-related respiratory depression exists. Limit dosage and duration of concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids, and monitor patients closely for respiratory depression and sedation. The benzodiazepines, including lorazepam, produce increased CNS-depressant effects when administered with other …

Limit dosage and duration of concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids, and monitor patients closely for respiratory depression and sedation. The benzodiazepines, including lorazepam, produce increased CNS-depressant effects when administered with other …

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy Pregnancy Exposure Registry There is a pregnancy registry that monitors pregnancy outcomes in women exposed to psychiatric medications, including lorazepam, during pregnancy. Healthcare providers are encouraged to register patients by calling the National Pregnancy Registry for Psychiatric Medications at 1-866-961-2388 or visiting online at https://womensmentalhealth.org/pregnancyregistry/. Risk Summary Neonates born to mothers using benzodiazepines late in pregnancy have been reported to experience symptoms of sedation and/or neonatal withdrawal (see and Clinical Considerations ). Available data from published observational studies of pregnant women exposed to benzodiazepines do …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (1 MG, 2 MG, 0.5 MG, .5 MG, 4 MG, 2.0 MG, 1.0 MG)
moa: Benzodiazepine: positive allosteric modulator at GABA-A, increasing the frequency of chloride-channel opening → sedation, anxiolysis, anticonvulsant effect.
receptorProfile: Non-selective GABA-A benzodiazepine-site agonist.
onsetOfEffect: PO ~20–30 min; IV within 1–3 min; intermediate lipophilicity gives a slightly slower CNS onset than diazepam but a longer duration of clinical effect.
metabolism: GLUCURONIDATION (UGT) to an inactive glucuronide — NO oxidative CYP step. Unaffected by CYP inhibitors/inducers and largely preserved in liver disease.
elimination: Renal (inactive conjugate).
approvedPopulations: Adults; used across settings given IM/IV availability.
startingDose: 0.5–1 mg PO 2–3×/day for anxiety; 1–2 mg IV/IM for acute agitation or status epilepticus (repeat per protocol).
titration: Lowest effective dose for the shortest duration; increase gradually.
maxDose: Up to ~6–10 mg/day in divided doses for anxiety (lower in elderly).
frequencyTiming: BID–TID (intermediate half-life); can be given IM/IV, unlike most oral-only benzos.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Preferred benzodiazepine in hepatic impairment (glucuronidation preserved); IV formulation contains propylene glycol — watch for toxicity/lactic acidosis with high-dose infusions.
startStopCrossTaper: Taper gradually after regular use to avoid withdrawal (rebound anxiety, insomnia, seizures).
administrationNotes: One of the "LOT" liver-safe agents; reliable IM absorption makes it a workhorse for agitation and status epilepticus.
boxedWarning: WARNING: RISKS FROM CONCOMITANT USE WITH OPIOIDS; ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION; and DEPENDENCE AND WITHDRAWAL REACTIONS • Concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs for patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Limit dosages and durations to the minimum required. Follow patients for signs and symptoms of respiratory depression and sedation (see WARNINGS and PRECAUTIONS ). • The use of benzodiazepines, including lorazepam, exposes users to risks of abuse, misuse, and addiction, which can lead to overdose or death. Abuse and misuse of benzodiazepines commonly involve concomitant use of other medications, alcohol, and/or illicit substances, which is associated with an increased frequency of serious adverse outcomes. Before prescribing lorazepam …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Lorazepam is contraindicated in patients with: • hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines or to any components of the formulation • acute narrow-angle glaucoma.
seriousAE: Propylene-glycol toxicity with prolonged high-dose IV
overdoseLethality: LOW when taken alone; HIGH risk of fatal respiratory depression when combined with opioids, alcohol, or other CNS depressants. Flumazenil reverses but may precipitate seizures.
discontinuationSyndrome: Withdrawal after regular use — rebound anxiety/insomnia, tremor, autonomic arousal, and (abrupt cessation) seizures; taper slowly.
ongoingMonitoring: Sedation, respiratory status (especially with opioids), signs of misuse/dependence, falls risk in elderly.
majorDDI: Clozapine (rare respiratory/cardiac arrest with parenteral benzodiazepines)
contraindicatedCombos: Opioids (combine only when no alternative; lowest dose/shortest duration)
Use in severe respiratory insufficiency / untreated sleep apnea
pregnancy: Avoid where possible; late-pregnancy/regular use risks neonatal sedation, hypotonia ("floppy infant") and withdrawal. Weigh against untreated maternal illness.
lactation: Small amounts in milk; monitor infant for sedation/poor feeding. Preferred benzos in lactation are shorter-acting agents without active metabolites.
geriatric: Beers-listed — avoid in older adults (falls, fractures, cognitive impairment, delirium). If unavoidable, this glucuronidated agent is often preferred over long-acting/active-metabolite benzos; use the lowest dose.
renalHepatic: Hepatically safer than CYP-metabolized benzos; no active metabolite to accumulate in renal impairment.
pearls: A "LOT" agent (Lorazepam/Oxazepam/Temazepam): glucuronidation only, no active metabolite — the benzo of choice in hepatic impairment and the elderly.
Reliable IM/IV absorption → first-line for status epilepticus and acute agitation; IV drug of choice for benzodiazepine-treated status.
Used as the "lorazepam challenge" in suspected catatonia.
mnemonics: "LOT" = Lorazepam, Oxazepam, Temazepam → glucuronidated, Liver-safe, no active metabolite.
confusables: Lorazepam vs alprazolam — both short/intermediate anxiolytics, but lorazepam is glucuronidated (liver-safe, IM/IV) while alprazolam is CYP3A4 and higher-potency with worse interdose rebound.
Lorazepam vs clonazepam — clonazepam is long-acting.
classDifferentiators: Short–intermediate half-life, glucuronidation (no CYP, no active metabolite), parenteral (IM/IV) availability — the versatile liver-safe workhorse.
examHooks: Which benzodiazepine is preferred in hepatic impairment/elderly and why? (lorazepam — glucuronidation, no active metabolite).
First-line benzodiazepine for status epilepticus? (IV lorazepam).
counsellingPoints: Do not combine with alcohol or opioids — risk of fatal breathing suppression.
Do not stop suddenly after regular use — taper with your prescriber.
May impair driving and cause memory gaps.

vignettes.stem: A 74-year-old with cirrhosis needs a benzodiazepine for severe alcohol-withdrawal agitation. Which agent is preferred because its clearance does not depend on hepatic oxidation?
vignettes.explanation: Lorazepam (with oxazepam and temazepam — "LOT") is cleared by glucuronidation without a CYP oxidation step and has no active metabolite, so it is preferred in significant liver disease and the elderly, where oxidatively metabolized/long-acting benzos accumulate.

startingDose.sourceText: DIRECTIONS Take 15 minutes before meals Adults : 20 drops in a little water, 2 times per day

maxDose.sourceText: DIRECTIONS Take 15 minutes before meals Adults : 20 drops in a little water, 2 times per day

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Stop use and ask doctor if symptoms persist more than 5 days. If pregnant or breast-feeding ask a health professional before use. Keep out of reach of children. In case of overdose, get medical help or contact a Poison Control Center right away. Contains ethyl alcohol 30%

moa: Endogenous pineal hormone; agonist at MT1 (attenuates the wake-promoting/alerting signal → sleep onset) and MT2 (circadian phase-shifting) receptors.
receptorProfile: MT1/MT2 agonist; no GABA/benzodiazepine activity.
onsetOfEffect: Immediate-release ~30–60 min for a hypnotic effect; circadian phase-shifting depends on TIMING relative to the body clock, not dose.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP1A2 (major) → 6-hydroxymelatonin.
approvedCanada: Sold OTC as a Natural Health Product (NPN, not a DIN) in Canada
startingDose: Circadian/phase-shifting 0.5–1 mg timed to the desired shift; hypnotic 2–5 mg at bedtime.
titration: Use the lowest effective dose; correct timing matters more than a higher dose.
maxDose: Commonly up to ~5–10 mg; higher doses rarely add benefit.
frequencyTiming: For circadian disorders, timing (e.g., early evening for delayed sleep phase) is the key variable.
startStopCrossTaper: No taper — no dependence.
administrationNotes: OTC status means variable product quality; counsel patients to choose a reputable NPN-labelled product.
overdoseLethality: Very low — one of the safest sleep aids; excess causes drowsiness.
discontinuationSyndrome: None — no dependence or withdrawal.
ongoingMonitoring: Minimal; reassess whether timing/sleep hygiene is addressing the circadian problem.
majorDDI: Fluvoxamine (potent CYP1A2 inhibitor) markedly RAISES melatonin levels
Other CYP1A2 inhibitors (ciprofloxacin) raise levels; smoking (CYP1A2 induction) lowers them
pregnancy: Data limited; generally avoided/not recommended routinely in pregnancy given limited evidence.
lactation: Limited data; generally cautious.
geriatric: Endogenous melatonin declines with age; low doses can help sleep onset with minimal risk.
pearls: In Canada melatonin is sold OTC as a Natural Health Product with an NPN (Natural Product Number), NOT a DIN — a distinguishing Canadian regulatory nuance.
For circadian rhythm disorders, the TIMING of the dose (relative to the body clock) matters more than the amount — low doses (0.5–1 mg) are used for phase-shifting.
Fluvoxamine (potent CYP1A2 inhibitor) dramatically increases melatonin exposure — the same CYP1A2 pathway that makes fluvoxamine dangerous with ramelteon.
mnemonics: "MelatoNiN = NPN, Not a DIN" — OTC natural health product in Canada.
confusables: Melatonin (endogenous hormone, OTC/NPN) vs ramelteon (synthetic MT1/MT2 agonist, prescription, US-only).
classDifferentiators: Endogenous melatonergic agonist; OTC/NPN in Canada; best for circadian/timing problems; extremely safe; no dependence.
examHooks: What regulatory number does OTC melatonin carry in Canada? (NPN, not DIN)
Which drug interaction sharply raises melatonin levels? (fluvoxamine — CYP1A2)
counsellingPoints: For jet lag / delayed sleep phase, timing matters more than dose.
Choose a reputable NPN-labelled product.
It is not a sedative "knockout" pill — it nudges your body clock.
Tell your doctor if you take fluvoxamine.

vignettes.stem: A patient asks why the melatonin they buy in a Canadian pharmacy has an "NPN" on the bottle rather than a DIN like their other prescriptions. What does this reflect?
options: It is regulated as a Natural Health Product, sold over-the-counter
It requires a prescription in Canada
vignettes.answer: It is regulated as a Natural Health Product, sold over-the-counter
vignettes.explanation: In Canada melatonin is marketed OTC as a Natural Health Product and carries a Natural Product Number (NPN), not a Drug Identification Number (DIN) — a Canadian regulatory distinction.

source.name: Health Canada Natural Health Products Regulations

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNINGS Personnel and Equipment for Monitoring and Resuscitation Adults and Pediatrics: Intravenous midazolam hydrochloride has been associated with respiratory depression and respiratory arrest, especially when used for sedation in noncritical care settings. In some cases, where this was not recognized promptly and treated effectively, death or hypoxic encephalopathy has resulted. Intravenous midazolam hydrochloride should be used only in hospital or ambulatory care settings, including physicians' and dental offices, that provide for continuous monitoring of respiratory and cardiac function, e.g., pulse oximetry. Immediate availability of resuscitative drugs and age- and size-appropriate equipment for bag/valve/mask ventilation and intubation, and personnel trained in their use and skilled in airway management should be assured (see WARNINGS ). For deeply sedated pediatric patients, a dedicated individual, other than the practitioner performing the procedure, should monitor the patient throughout the procedure. Risks From Concomitant Use With Opioids Concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Monitor patients for respiratory depression and sedation (see WARNINGS and PRECAUTIONS ; Drug Interactions ). Individualization of Dosage Midazolam hydrochloride must never be used without individualization of …

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Midazolam injection is contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to the drug. Benzodiazepines are contraindicated in patients with acute narrow-angle glaucoma. Benzodiazepines may be used in patients with open-angle glaucoma only if they are receiving appropriate therapy. Measurements of intraocular pressure in patients without eye disease show a moderate lowering following induction with midazolam hydrochloride; patients with glaucoma have not been studied. Midazolam is not intended for intrathecal or epidural administration due to the presence of the preservative benzyl alcohol in the dosage form. Midazolam injection is contraindicated for use in premature infants because the formulation contains benzyl alcohol (see WARNINGS and PRECAUTIONS , Pediatric Use ).

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Note: CONTAINS BENZYL ALCOHOL (see WARNINGS and PRECAUTIONS , Pediatric Use ) Midazolam hydrochloride injection is a potent sedative agent that requires slow administration and individualization of dosage. Clinical experience has shown midazolam hydrochloride to be 3 to 4 times as potent per mg as diazepam. BECAUSE SERIOUS AND LIFE-THREATENING CARDIORESPIRATORY ADVERSE EVENTS HAVE BEEN REPORTED, PROVISION FOR MONITORING, DETECTION AND CORRECTION OF THESE REACTIONS MUST BE MADE FOR EVERY PATIENT T0 WHOM MIDAZOLAM HYDROCHLORIDE INJECTION IS ADMINISTERED, REGARDLESS OF AGE OR HEALTH STATUS. Excessive single doses or rapid intravenous administration may result in …

Clinical experience has shown midazolam hydrochloride to be 3 to 4 times as potent per mg as diazepam. seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Personnel and Equipment for Monitoring and Resuscitation Prior to the intravenous administration of midazolam in any dose, the immediate availability of oxygen, resuscitative drugs, age- and size-appropriate equipment for bag/valve/mask ventilation and intubation, and skilled personnel for the maintenance of a patent airway and support of ventilation should be ensured. Patients should be continuously monitored for early signs of hypoventilation, airway obstruction, or apnea with means readily available (e.g., pulse oximetry). Hypoventilation, airway obstruction, and apnea can lead to hypoxia and/or cardiac arrest unless effective countermeasures are taken immediately. The immediate …

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy: Risk Summary Neonates born to mothers using benzodiazepines late in pregnancy have been reported to experience symptoms of sedation and/or neonatal withdrawal (see WARNINGS: Neonatal Sedation and Withdrawal Syndrome, and Clinical Considerations ). Available data from published observational studies of pregnant women exposed to benzodiazepines do not report a clear association with benzodiazepines and major birth defects (see Data). The background risk of major birth defects and miscarriage for the indicated population is unknown. All pregnancies have a background risk of birth defect, loss, or other adverse outcomes. formulationsCanada: Liquid (1 MG, 5 MG, 2.5 MG, 7.5 MG, 10 MG)
Solution (1 MG, 5 MG, 2.5 MG, 7.5 MG, 10 MG)
moa: Benzodiazepine: GABA-A positive allosteric modulator (↑ Cl- channel opening frequency); water-soluble at low pH, highly lipophilic at physiologic pH → rapid CNS entry.
receptorProfile: Non-selective GABA-A benzodiazepine-site agonist.
onsetOfEffect: Very fast: IV ~1–3 min, IM ~5–15 min, intranasal/buccal minutes — brief duration.
timeToSteadyState: Rapid; usually given as single/procedural doses or infusion.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 to 1-hydroxymidazolam (then glucuronidated) → strong CYP3A4-interaction potential; clearance falls in critical illness/hepatic dysfunction.
activeMetabolites: 1-hydroxymidazolam (active; can accumulate with prolonged infusions or renal failure).
startingDose: Procedural sedation: titrate small IV increments (e.g., 0.5–2 mg) to effect; seizures: 10 mg IM (or intranasal/buccal) per protocol.
titration: Titrate slowly to effect with continuous monitoring; effects potentiated by opioids.
maxDose: Setting-dependent; titrate to clinical endpoint under monitoring rather than a fixed ceiling.
frequencyTiming: Single doses or infusion; IV/IM/intranasal/buccal routes.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Active metabolite (1-OH-midazolam) accumulates in renal failure and prolonged infusions → delayed awakening; reduce in hepatic dysfunction/critical illness.
administrationNotes: Requires resuscitation-capable monitoring; intranasal/buccal/IM routes make it useful for seizures without IV access.
boxedWarning: WARNINGS Personnel and Equipment for Monitoring and Resuscitation Adults and Pediatrics: Intravenous midazolam hydrochloride has been associated with respiratory depression and respiratory arrest, especially when used for sedation in noncritical care settings. In some cases, where this was not recognized promptly and treated effectively, death or hypoxic encephalopathy has resulted. Intravenous midazolam hydrochloride should be used only in hospital or ambulatory care settings, including physicians' and dental offices, that provide for continuous monitoring of respiratory and cardiac function, e.g., pulse oximetry. Immediate availability of resuscitative drugs and age- and size-appropriate equipment for bag/valve/mask ventilation and intubation, and personnel trained in their use and skilled in airway management should be assured (see WARNINGS ). For deeply sedated pediatric patients, a …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Midazolam injection is contraindicated in patients with a known hypersensitivity to the drug. Benzodiazepines are contraindicated in patients with acute narrow-angle glaucoma. Benzodiazepines may be used in patients with open-angle glaucoma only if they are receiving appropriate therapy. Measurements of intraocular pressure in patients without eye disease show a moderate lowering following induction with midazolam hydrochloride; patients with glaucoma have not been studied. Midazolam is not intended for intrathecal or epidural administration due to the presence of the preservative benzyl alcohol in the dosage form. Midazolam injection is contraindicated for use in premature infants because the formulation contains benzyl alcohol (see WARNINGS and PRECAUTIONS , Pediatric Use ).
overdoseLethality: LOW alone but respiratory depression/apnea is dose-related and rapid; HIGH with opioids. Requires airway/monitoring capability; flumazenil available.
discontinuationSyndrome: Withdrawal after prolonged ICU infusions — taper the infusion.
ongoingMonitoring: Continuous respiratory rate/SpO2, blood pressure, level of sedation; capnography for deeper sedation.
majorDDI: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (azoles, protease inhibitors, clarithromycin → raise levels/prolong sedation)
contraindicatedCombos: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g., oral midazolam with protease inhibitors/azoles — contraindicated per label)
pregnancy: Reserve for procedural/emergency indications; neonatal exposure risks sedation/respiratory depression.
lactation: Single procedural doses generally compatible; monitor infant after larger/repeated exposure.
geriatric: Highly sensitive to respiratory depression/hypotension — use markedly reduced, slowly titrated doses.
renalHepatic: Active metabolite accumulates in renal failure and with prolonged infusion; reduce/monitor.
pearls: Ultra-short acting, water-soluble → procedural sedation workhorse; IM/intranasal/buccal routes treat seizures without IV access.
CYP3A4 substrate — strong inhibitors prolong sedation; 1-hydroxymidazolam accumulates in renal failure/long infusions → delayed awakening.
Only give where airway management and monitoring are available; flumazenil reverses.
mnemonics: "Midazolam = Minutes" — ultra-short onset/offset for procedures.
confusables: Midazolam vs lorazepam for status epilepticus — IM/intranasal midazolam is preferred when there is no IV access; IV lorazepam when access exists.
classDifferentiators: Ultra-short half-life, CYP3A4 metabolism with an active renally-cleared metabolite, multiple parenteral routes — the procedural-sedation/anesthesia benzo.
examHooks: Benzodiazepine of choice for seizures when there is NO IV access? (IM or intranasal midazolam).
Why can ICU midazolam infusions cause delayed awakening in renal failure? (accumulation of active 1-hydroxymidazolam).
counsellingPoints: You may not remember the procedure (expected amnesia).
Do not drive or make important decisions for 24 h.
Arrange an escort home.

vignettes.stem: A child in the community has ongoing tonic-clonic seizures and no IV access. Which benzodiazepine and route is most appropriate as first-line?
vignettes.explanation: Without IV access, IM or intranasal/buccal midazolam has rapid absorption and reliably aborts seizures (RAMPART trial); IV lorazepam is first-line only when IV access exists.
vignettes.testedFact: Midazolam (IM/intranasal) for status without IV access

moa: Benzodiazepine: GABA-A positive allosteric modulator (↑ Cl- channel opening frequency); long-acting hypnotic.
receptorProfile: Non-selective GABA-A benzodiazepine-site agonist.
onsetOfEffect: PO onset ~30–60 min; long duration → next-day residual sedation.
metabolism: Hepatic nitro-reduction/acetylation to inactive metabolites.
activeMetabolites: None clinically significant (but long parent half-life).
startingDose: 5 mg PO at bedtime (2.5 mg in elderly).
titration: Lowest effective dose; short-term use.
maxDose: ~10 mg at bedtime.
frequencyTiming: At bedtime; long half-life causes next-day carryover.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce in elderly/hepatic impairment (long half-life, carryover).
startStopCrossTaper: Taper after regular nightly use.
administrationNotes: A Canada/Europe-available long-acting hypnotic (not US-marketed).
overdoseLethality: LOW alone; HIGH with opioids/alcohol/other CNS depressants.
discontinuationSyndrome: Rebound insomnia/anxiety after regular use; taper.
ongoingMonitoring: Next-day sedation/falls, dependence, respiratory status with opioids.
contraindicatedCombos: Opioids (avoid; lowest dose/shortest duration if unavoidable)
Severe respiratory insufficiency / untreated sleep apnea
pregnancy: Avoid; late use → neonatal sedation/hypotonia/withdrawal.
lactation: Long-acting — infant accumulation possible; a shorter hypnotic preferred.
geriatric: Beers — avoid; long half-life → next-day impairment and falls in older adults.
pearls: Long-acting hypnotic (Canada/Europe, not US) → next-day hangover and falls, especially in the elderly.
Contrast with the intermediate glucuronidated hypnotic temazepam, which accumulates less.
mnemonics: "Nitrazepam = Night-and-day" — long half-life leaves a next-day hangover.
confusables: Nitrazepam vs temazepam — both benzo hypnotics; nitrazepam is long-acting (carryover) while temazepam is intermediate/glucuronidated (liver-safe, less carryover).
classDifferentiators: Long half-life hypnotic; Canada/Europe-available (US-unavailable); notable next-day residual sedation.
examHooks: Which benzodiazepine hypnotic is long-acting with notable next-day sedation and is not US-marketed? (nitrazepam).
counsellingPoints: Expect possible next-morning grogginess; caution driving.
Avoid alcohol/opioids.
Short-term use; taper when stopping.

vignettes.stem: An 80-year-old on nightly nitrazepam presents with morning falls and daytime drowsiness. What property of the drug best explains this?
options: Long half-life causing next-day carryover in the elderly
vignettes.answer: Long half-life causing next-day carryover in the elderly
vignettes.explanation: Nitrazepam is long-acting; in older adults its prolonged half-life produces next-day residual sedation, unsteadiness, and falls — a reason it is avoided (Beers) and shorter agents are preferred.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: RISKS FROM CONCOMITANT USE WITH OPIOIDS; ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION; and DEPENDENCE AND WITHDRAWAL REACTIONS Concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs for use in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Limit dosages and durations to the minimum required. Follow patients for signs and symptoms of respiratory depression and sedation [see WARNINGS and PRECAUTIONS ] . The use of benzodiazepines, including oxazepam, exposes users to risks of abuse, misuse, and addiction, which can lead to overdose or death. Abuse and misuse of benzodiazepines commonly involve concomitant use of other medications, alcohol, and/or illicit substances, which is associated with an increased frequency of serious adverse outcomes. Before prescribing oxazepam and throughout treatment, assess each patient’s risk for abuse, misuse, and addiction [see WARNINGS ] . The continued use of benzodiazepines, including oxazepam, may lead to clinically significant physical dependence. The risks of dependence and withdrawal increase with longer treatment duration and higher daily dose. Abrupt discontinuation or rapid dosage reduction of oxazepam after continued use may precipitate acute withdrawal reactions, which can be life-threatening. To reduce the risk of …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Risks from Concomitant Use with Opioids: Concomitant use of benzodiazepines, including oxazepam, and opioids may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Because of these risks, reserve concomitant prescribing of these drugs in patients for whom alternative treatment options are inadequate. Observational studies have demonstrated that concomitant use of opioid analgesics and benzodiazepines increases the risk of drug-related mortality compared to use of opioids alone. If a decision is made to prescribe oxazepam concomitantly with opioids, prescribe the lowest effective dosages and minimum durations of concomitant use, and follow patients closely for …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Overdosage of benzodiazepines is characterized by central nervous system depression ranging from drowsiness to coma. In mild to moderate cases, symptoms can include drowsiness, confusion, dysarthria, lethargy, hypnotic state, diminished reflexes, ataxia, and hypotonia. Rarely, paradoxical or disinhibitory reactions (including agitation, irritability, impulsivity, violent behavior, confusion, restlessness, excitement, and talkativeness) may occur. In severe overdosage cases, patients may develop respiratory depression and coma. Overdosage of benzodiazepines in combination with other CNS depressants (including alcohol and opioids) may be fatal (see WARNINGS, Abuse, Misuse, and …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions The concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids increases the risk of respiratory depression because of actions at different receptor sites in the CNS that control respiration. Benzodiazepines interact at GABAA sites and opioids interact primarily at mu receptors. When benzodiazepines and opioids are combined, the potential for benzodiazepines to significantly worsen opioid-related respiratory depression exists. Limit dosage and duration of concomitant use of benzodiazepines and opioids and monitor patients closely for respiratory depression and sedation.

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy Risk Summary Neonates born to mothers using benzodiazepines late in pregnancy have been reported to experience symptoms of sedation and/or neonatal withdrawal (see WARNINGS, Neonatal Sedation and Withdrawal Syndrome and PRECAUTIONS: Clinical Considerations ) . Available data from published observational studies of pregnant women exposed to benzodiazepines do not report a clear association with benzodiazepines and major birth defects (see Data). The estimated background risk of major birth defects and miscarriage for the indicated population is unknown. All pregnancies have a background risk of birth defect, loss, or other adverse outcomes.
