overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Symptoms Postmarketing reports of guanfacine overdosage indicate that hypotension, drowsiness, lethargy, and bradycardia have been observed following overdose. Initial hypertension may develop early and may be followed by hypotension. Similar symptoms have been described in voluntary reports to the American Association of Poison Control Center’s National Poison Data System. Miosis of the pupils may be noted on examination . No fatal overdoses of guanfacine have been reported in published literature. Treatment Consult a Certified Poison Control Center by calling 1-800-222-1222 for up-to-date guidance and advice. Management of guanfacine extended-release tablets overdose should …

baselineWorkup.sourceText: 5 WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS Hypotension, bradycardia, syncope: Titrate slowly and monitor vital signs frequently in patients at risk for hypotension, heart block, bradycardia, syncope, cardiovascular disease, vascular disease, cerebrovascular disease or chronic renal failure. Measure heart rate and blood pressure prior to initiation of therapy, following dose increases, and periodically while on therapy. Avoid concomitant use of drugs with additive effects unless clinically indicated. Advise patients to avoid becoming dehydrated or overheated ( 5.1 ). Sedation and somnolence: Occur commonly with guanfacine extended-release tablets. Consider the potential for additive sedative effects with …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Pregnancy Exposure Registry There is a pregnancy exposure registry that monitors pregnancy outcomes in women exposed to ADHD medications, including guanfacine extended-release tablets, during pregnancy. Healthcare providers are encouraged to register patients by calling the National Pregnancy Registry for ADHD Medications at 1-866-961-2388. Risk Summary Available data with guanfacine over decades of use in pregnant women have not identified a drug-associated risk of major birth defects, miscarriage or adverse maternal or fetal outcomes. However, use of guanfacine in pregnant women over this time has been infrequent. In animal reproduction studies, rabbits and rats exposed to 3 …

moa: Selective α2A-adrenergic receptor AGONIST. NON-stimulant. Postsynaptic α2A stimulation on prefrontal pyramidal neurons strengthens working-memory/attention network connectivity. More α2A-selective than clonidine → relatively less sedation/hypotension for its effect (though both still occur).
receptorProfile: α2A-selective agonist (postsynaptic PFC action); weaker at α2B/α2C than clonidine.
onsetOfEffect: Gradual over ~1–2 weeks (non-stimulant).
metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 — strong 3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole) raise levels; inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine) lower them.
approvedCanada: ADHD (extended-release, as monotherapy or adjunct to a stimulant)
startingDose: XR 1 mg PO once daily.
titration: Increase by ≤1 mg/week to response and tolerability; weight-based dosing (~0.05–0.12 mg/kg/day).
maxDose: 4 mg/day (children 6–12 y); up to ~7 mg/day in adolescents by weight.
frequencyTiming: Once daily; may dose at bedtime if daytime sedation is limiting. Do NOT crush the XR tablet and avoid taking with a high-fat meal (raises exposure).
startStopCrossTaper: TAPER on discontinuation (e.g., decrease by 1 mg every 3–7 days) to avoid rebound hypertension; do not stop abruptly.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Guanfacine extended-release tablets are contraindicated in patients with a history of a hypersensitivity reaction to guanfacine extended-release tablets or its inactive ingredients, or other products containing guanfacine. Rash and pruritus have been reported. History of hypersensitivity to guanfacine extended-release tablets, its inactive ingredients, or other products containing guanfacine ( 4 ).
overdoseLethality: Bradycardia, hypotension, sedation/lethargy and respiratory depression; supportive care with cardiac monitoring. Pediatric ingestions can be significant.
discontinuationSyndrome: Abrupt cessation → rebound hypertension, tachycardia, nervousness — taper.
baselineWorkup: Cardiac history / syncope; screen for conduction abnormalities
Review concomitant antihypertensives and CNS depressants
ongoingMonitoring: BP and HR at baseline, after titration, and periodically (watch for hypotension/bradycardia/syncope); sedation; growth in children; do not stop abruptly.
majorDDI: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole) — reduce guanfacine dose
CYP3A4 inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine) — may need higher dose
contraindicatedCombos: Additive with other centrally-acting antihypertensives and sedatives — use caution (not an absolute contraindication)
pregnancy: Limited data; individualized risk/benefit.
pediatric: Useful non-stimulant, particularly with tics or when a stimulant is insufficient/poorly tolerated; monitor BP/HR, sedation and growth. Abrupt stop → rebound hypertension.
cardiac: Causes bradycardia, hypotension and syncope; caution with heart block, bradycardia, syncope history, or other AV-nodal/hypotensive drugs.
pearls: α2A-SELECTIVE agonist — less sedating/hypotensive than clonidine for a given effect, and the only α2 agonist with a specific ADHD (XR) indication + stimulant-adjunct role.
Metabolized by CYP3A4 — dose down with strong inhibitors, up with inducers; avoid crushing the XR tablet.
Never stop abruptly — rebound hypertension (though generally milder than with clonidine).
mnemonics: "GuanfAcine = α2A" — the A-selective, less-sedating α2 agonist; taper to avoid rebound.
confusables: Guanfacine (α2A-selective, longer-acting, less sedating) vs clonidine (less selective, more sedating, more rebound HTN).
classDifferentiators: Non-stimulant α2A-selective agonist; ADHD/stimulant-adjunct indication; CYP3A4; sedation + hypotension + rebound HTN.
examHooks: Which non-stimulant α2A-selective agonist is approved as ADHD monotherapy or stimulant adjunct? (guanfacine XR)
Why taper guanfacine? (rebound hypertension)
counsellingPoints: May cause drowsiness and low blood pressure/dizziness — rise slowly; use caution driving.
Do not stop suddenly — blood pressure can rebound; taper with your prescriber.
Swallow the XR tablet whole; do not take with a fatty meal.
Tell your prescriber about other blood-pressure or sedating medications.

vignettes.stem: A 10-year-old on extended-release guanfacine for ADHD runs out of medication and stops it abruptly. Three days later he presents with headache, agitation, HR 120 and BP 150/95. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Rebound hypertension from abrupt α2-agonist withdrawal
Hypertensive crisis from a tyramine meal
vignettes.answer: Rebound hypertension from abrupt α2-agonist withdrawal
vignettes.explanation: Sudden withdrawal of a centrally acting α2 agonist causes a surge in sympathetic outflow → rebound hypertension and tachycardia. Guanfacine (and clonidine) should be tapered rather than stopped abruptly.
vignettes.testedFact: Guanfacine rebound hypertension on abrupt discontinuation

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION Lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules have a high potential for abuse and misuse, which can lead to the development of a substance use disorder, including addiction. Misuse and abuse of CNS stimulants, including lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules, can result in overdose and death [see Overdosage (10) ] , and this risk is increased with higher doses or unapproved methods of administration, such as snorting or injection. Before prescribing lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules, assess each patient’s risk for abuse, misuse, and addiction. Educate patients and their families about these risks, proper storage of the drug, and proper disposal of any unused drug. Throughout lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules treatment, reassess each patient’s risk of abuse, misuse, and addiction and frequently monitor for signs and symptoms of abuse, misuse, and addiction [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1 ), and Drug Abuse and Dependence (9.2) ]. WARNING: ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. Lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules have a high potential for abuse and misuse, which can lead to the development of a substance use disorder, including addiction. Misuse and abuse of CNS stimulants, including lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules, can result in overdose and death ( 5.1 , 9.2 , 10 ): Before prescribing …

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules are contraindicated in patients with: Known hypersensitivity to amphetamine products or other ingredients of lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules. Anaphylactic reactions, Stevens-Johnson Syndrome, angioedema, and urticaria have been observed in postmarketing reports [see Adverse Reactions (6.2) ] . Patients taking monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), or within 14 days of stopping MAOIs (including MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue), because of an increased risk of hypertensive crisis [see Warnings and Precautions (5.7) and Drug Interactions (7.1) ] . Known hypersensitivity to amphetamine products or other ingredients in lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules ( 4 ) Use with monoamine oxidase (MAO) inhibitor, or within 14 days of the last MAO inhibitor dose ( 4 , 7.1 )

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Clinical Effects of Overdose Overdose of CNS stimulants is characterized by the following sympathomimetic effects: Cardiovascular effects including tachyarrhythmias, and hypertension or hypotension. Vasospasm, myocardial infarction, or aortic dissection may precipitate sudden cardiac death. Takotsubo cardiomyopathy may develop. CNS effects including psychomotor agitation, confusion, and hallucinations. Serotonin syndrome, seizures, cerebral vascular accidents, and coma may occur. Life-threatening hyperthermia (temperatures greater than 104°F) and rhabdomyolysis may develop. Overdose Management Consider the possibility of multiple drug ingestion. The pharmacokinetic profile of …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary The limited available data from published literature and postmarketing reports on use of lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules in pregnant women are not sufficient to inform a drug-associated risk for major birth defects and miscarriage. Adverse pregnancy outcomes, including premature delivery and low birth weight, have been seen in infants born to mothers dependent on amphetamines [see Clinical Considerations ] . In animal reproduction studies, lisdexamfetamine dimesylate (a prodrug of d-amphetamine) had no effects on embryo-fetal morphological development or survival when administered orally to pregnant rats and rabbits throughout the period of organogenesis. …

formulationsCanada: Capsule (60 MG, 20 MG, 50 MG, 70 MG, 30 MG, 10 MG, 40 MG)
Tablet (Chewable) (60 MG, 20 MG, 50 MG, 70 MG, 30 MG, 10 MG, 40 MG)
moa: Inactive PRODRUG: d-amphetamine covalently bound to L-lysine. Enzymatically hydrolyzed in the blood (by red-blood-cell peptidases) to active dextroamphetamine + L-lysine, which then releases and blocks reuptake of DA/NE. Rate-limited conversion gives a smooth, long duration and LOWER misuse liability (cannot be "activated" by snorting/injecting).
receptorProfile: Acts through liberated d-amphetamine — presynaptic DA/NE release + reuptake inhibition.
onsetOfEffect: Same-day; smooth onset over ~1–2 h with long duration (~13–14 h).
halfLife: Prodrug itself <1 h; the liberated dextroamphetamine ~11 h (drives the clinical duration).
metabolism: Activated by hydrolysis via RBC peptidases (not CYP-dependent for activation) → dextroamphetamine, then metabolized as amphetamine.
offLabel: Binge eating disorder (approved in the US)
startingDose: 30 mg PO once daily in the morning.
titration: Adjust by ~10–20 mg/day at weekly intervals to response.
maxDose: 70 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once daily in the morning; the prodrug design gives a consistent all-day profile without a midday dose.
administrationNotes: Capsule may be opened and dissolved in water/yogurt; take the whole dose at once.

Before prescribing lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules, assess each patient’s risk for abuse, misuse, and addiction. Educate patients and their families about these risks, proper storage of the drug, and proper disposal of any unused drug. Throughout lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules treatment, reassess each patient’s risk of abuse, misuse, and addiction and frequently monitor for signs and symptoms of abuse, misuse, and …

Anaphylactic reactions, Stevens-Johnson Syndrome, angioedema, and urticaria have been observed in postmarketing reports [see Adverse Reactions (6.2) ] . Patients taking monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), or within 14 days of stopping MAOIs (including MAOIs such as linezolid or intravenous methylene blue), because of an increased risk of hypertensive crisis [see Warnings and Precautions (5.7) and Drug Interactions (7.1) ] . Known hypersensitivity to amphetamine products or other ingredients in lisdexamfetamine dimesylate capsules ( 4 ) Use with monoamine oxidase (MAO) inhibitor, or within 14 days of the last MAO inhibitor dose ( 4 , 7.1 )
seriousAE: Sudden cardiac death with structural heart disease
overdoseLethality: Sympathomimetic crisis as with amphetamine; onset may be smoothed by prodrug kinetics but large ingestions are still dangerous — supportive care, benzodiazepines.
baselineWorkup: Cardiac + family history of sudden death / structural disease
Screen for psychosis/bipolar and misuse risk
ongoingMonitoring: BP/HR each visit; growth in children; appetite, sleep, mood, tics; misuse/diversion (lower but not zero).
contraindicatedCombos: MAOIs (during use or within 14 days of stopping)
maoiWashout: Do not use with an MAOI or within 14 days of stopping one.
pregnancy: Limited data; amphetamine class — individualized risk/benefit.
pediatric: Convenient once-daily option with lower abuse liability; monitor growth, appetite, sleep.
cardiac: Same cautions as amphetamine — avoid in serious structural cardiac disease/arrhythmia.
pearls: PRODRUG activated in the bloodstream by RBC peptidases — the classic reason its abuse potential and IV/intranasal "high" are blunted.
Once-daily, long smooth profile from rate-limited conversion; also FDA-approved for binge eating disorder.
mnemonics: "LISdexamfetamine is a LIStless prodrug until the blood LYSes off the lysine."
confusables: Lisdexamfetamine (Vyvanse, prodrug) vs dextroamphetamine (its active drug) vs mixed amphetamine salts (Adderall XR).
classDifferentiators: The amphetamine PRODRUG — inactive until hydrolyzed in blood; lower misuse liability; once-daily.
examHooks: Which ADHD stimulant is an inactive prodrug converted to dextroamphetamine in the blood? (lisdexamfetamine)
counsellingPoints: Take once each morning.
It is inactive until your body converts it — do not expect a faster effect by altering the capsule.
Store securely; still a controlled substance.
Never combine with an MAOI.

vignettes.stem: A clinician chooses lisdexamfetamine over immediate-release dextroamphetamine partly for its lower misuse liability. What pharmacologic feature explains this?
options: It blocks dopamine reuptake without any release
It is an inactive prodrug requiring enzymatic conversion in the blood to active drug
It has no central nervous system penetration
It is metabolized entirely by CYP3A4
vignettes.answer: It is an inactive prodrug requiring enzymatic conversion in the blood to active drug
vignettes.explanation: Lisdexamfetamine must be hydrolyzed (by RBC peptidases) to liberate dextroamphetamine. This rate-limited activation blunts the rapid "high" from snorting/injecting, lowering abuse liability.

WARNING: ABUSE AND DEPENDENCE See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. CNS stimulants, including methylphenidate hydrochloride extended-release capsules , other methylphenidate-containing products, and amphetamines, have a high potential for abuse and dependence ( 5.1 , 9.2 , 9.3 ). contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Hypersensitivity to methylphenidate or other components of methylphenidate hydrochloride extended-release capsules. Hypersensitivity reactions such as angioedema and anaphylactic reactions have been reported in patients treated with methylphenidate [ s ee Adverse Reactions ( 6.1 )]. Concomitant treatment with monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), or within 14 days following discontinuation of treatment with an MAOI, because of the risk of hypertensive crises [ s ee Drug Interactions ( 7.1 )]. Known hypersensitivity to methylphenidate or product components. ( 4 ) Concurrent treatment with a monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAOI), or use of an MAOI within the preceding 14 days. ( 4 )

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary Published studies and postmarketing reports on methylphenidate use during pregnancy have not identified a drug-associated risk of major birth defects, miscarriage or adverse maternal or fetal outcomes. There may be risks to the fetus associated with the use of CNS stimulants use during pregnancy (see Clinical Considerations) . No effects on morphological development were observed in embryo-fetal development studies with oral administration of methylphenidate to pregnant rats and rabbits during organogenesis at doses up to 10 and 15 times, respectively, the maximum recommended human dose (MRHD) of 60 mg/day given to adolescents on a mg/m 2 basis. However, spina …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (Extended-Release) (20 MG, 10 MG, 5 MG, 27 MG, 54 MG, 18 MG, 36 MG, 80 MG, 40 MG, 15 MG, 2.5 MG, 10.00 MG, 60 MG, 50 MG, 30 MG, 20.00 MG, 25 MG, 55 MG, 85 MG, 5.00 MG, 100 MG, 35 MG, 45 MG, 70 MG)
moa: Blocks presynaptic dopamine and norepinephrine transporters (DAT/NET) → increases synaptic DA/NE. A pure REUPTAKE BLOCKER — it does NOT trigger vesicular release (the key contrast with amphetamines).
receptorProfile: DAT/NET reuptake inhibition; d-threo enantiomer is the active moiety. No direct receptor agonism.
onsetOfEffect: Behavioural effect within ~30–60 min of a dose; benefit is same-day (not delayed like non-stimulants).
halfLife: IR ~2–3 h (short); extended-release/OROS formulations give a longer functional duration (~8–12 h) despite a similar terminal half-life.
metabolism: De-esterified by carboxylesterase CES1A1 to ritalinic acid (inactive). Minimal CYP involvement → comparatively FEW pharmacokinetic drug interactions.
activeMetabolites: None clinically significant (ritalinic acid is inactive).
startingDose: IR 5 mg PO BID (breakfast + lunch); ER products dosed once each morning (e.g., OROS 18 mg AM).
titration: Increase weekly; individualize to response and tolerability; pediatric dosing often ~0.3–1 mg/kg/dose.
maxDose: ~60 mg/day for most IR/ER products (formulation-dependent; OROS up to 72 mg/day in adults).
frequencyTiming: Morning ± early afternoon; give the last IR dose by mid-afternoon to avoid insomnia. ER once each morning.
startStopCrossTaper: No physiologic taper needed pharmacologically; a rebound of ADHD symptoms as levels fall is common in the late afternoon.
administrationNotes: Do not crush/chew ER products. OROS shell is non-absorbable and passes intact in stool ("ghost tablet") — reassure patients this is expected.
boxedWarning: WARNING: ABUSE AND DEPENDENCE C N S stimulants, including methylphenidate hydrochloride extended-release capsules , other methylphenidate-containing products, and amphetamines, have a high potential for abuse and dependence. Assess the risk of abuse prior to prescribing, and monitor for signs of abuse and dependence while on therapy [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 ), Drug Abuse and Dependence ( 9.2 , 9.3 )] . WARNING: ABUSE AND DEPENDENCE See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. CNS stimulants, including methylphenidate hydrochloride extended-release capsules , other methylphenidate-containing products, and amphetamines, have a high potential for abuse and dependence ( 5.1 , 9.2 , 9.3 ). Assess the risk of abuse prior to prescribing, and monitor for signs of abuse and dependence while on therapy ( 5.1 , 9.2 ).
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Hypersensitivity to methylphenidate or other components of methylphenidate hydrochloride extended-release capsules. Hypersensitivity reactions such as angioedema and anaphylactic reactions have been reported in patients treated with methylphenidate [ s ee Adverse Reactions ( 6.1 )]. Concomitant treatment with monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs), or within 14 days following discontinuation of treatment with an MAOI, because of the risk of hypertensive crises [ s ee Drug Interactions ( 7.1 )]. Known hypersensitivity to methylphenidate or product components. ( 4 ) Concurrent treatment with a monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAOI), or use of an MAOI within the preceding 14 days. ( 4 )
seriousAE: Sudden cardiac death with pre-existing structural heart disease
Precipitation or worsening of psychosis or mania
overdoseLethality: Sympathomimetic overdose — agitation, hypertension, tachyarrhythmia, hyperthermia, seizures. Rarely fatal alone with supportive care, but dangerous; benzodiazepines are first-line for agitation/seizures.
baselineWorkup: Cardiac history + personal/family history of sudden death or structural heart disease
Screen for psychosis/bipolar disorder and tics
ongoingMonitoring: BP and HR at each visit; height/weight plotted on growth charts in children; appetite, sleep, mood, tics; adherence and diversion/misuse.
majorDDI: May raise levels of some CES1 substrates (e.g., warfarin, some anticonvulsants, TCAs)
contraindicatedCombos: MAOIs (during use or within 14 days of stopping an MAOI)
maoiWashout: Do not use with an MAOI or within 14 days of stopping one (hypertensive crisis).
pregnancy: Limited data; weigh maternal functional impairment against fetal exposure. Individualized decision.
pediatric: First-line pharmacotherapy for childhood ADHD. Monitor growth (height/weight percentiles); appetite suppression and insomnia are common; drug holidays are sometimes used.
cardiac: Avoid in serious structural cardiac abnormality, cardiomyopathy, or serious arrhythmia; use caution with pre-existing hypertension. ↑HR/BP expected.
pearls: Pure reuptake blocker (DAT/NET) — remember it does NOT release dopamine, unlike amphetamines.
Metabolized by carboxylesterase (CES1), not CYP → far fewer PK interactions than most psychotropics.
The OROS "ghost tablet" seen in stool is the empty non-absorbable shell — not treatment failure.
mnemonics: "methylpheniDATE just DATES the transporter" — it blocks reuptake (occupies DAT/NET) rather than forcing release.
confusables: Methylphenidate (Ritalin/Concerta/Biphentin) vs dexmethylphenidate (the active d-enantiomer).
Concerta = OROS methylphenidate vs Biphentin = multilayer beads — different release kinetics.
classDifferentiators: Stimulant that BLOCKS reuptake only (no release); CES1 (not CYP) metabolism; first-line, controlled.
examHooks: Which ADHD stimulant works by pure reuptake blockade rather than transmitter release? (methylphenidate)
A child on stimulant + MAOI risks what? (hypertensive crisis)
counsellingPoints: Take in the morning; avoid late-afternoon doses to protect sleep.
Appetite may drop — offer calorie-dense breakfast/evening snacks and monitor weight.
An empty tablet shell in the stool with OROS is normal.
Never combine with an MAOI.

vignettes.stem: A 9-year-old with ADHD on OROS methylphenidate is brought in by a worried parent who found "whole pills" in the toilet. Vitals and behaviour are unchanged and symptom control is good. What is the best explanation?
options: The medication is not being absorbed and the dose should be doubled
The child is not swallowing the tablets
The non-absorbable osmotic shell passes intact in stool
vignettes.answer: The non-absorbable osmotic shell passes intact in stool
vignettes.explanation: OROS methylphenidate uses an osmotic pump; the drug is released while the insoluble shell passes intact ("ghost tablet"). It does not indicate treatment failure and requires no dose change.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION Amphetamine sulfate tablets have a high potential for abuse and misuse, which can lead to the development of a substance use disorder, including addiction. Misuse and abuse of CNS stimulants, including amphetamine sulfate, can result in overdose and death ( see OVERDOSAGE ), and this risk is increased with higher doses or unapproved methods of administration, such as snorting or injection. Before prescribing amphetamine sulfate tablets, assess each patient’s risk for abuse, misuse, and addiction. Educate patients and their families about these risks, proper storage of the drug, and proper disposal of any unused drug. Throughout amphetamine sulfate tablets treatment, reassess each patient’s risk of abuse, misuse, and addiction and frequently monitor for signs and symptoms of abuse, misuse, and addiction ( see WARNINGS and DRUG ABUSE AND DEPENDENCE ).

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Known hypersensitivity to amphetamine products. During or within 14 days following the administration of monoamine oxidase inhibitors (hypertensive crises may result) ( see WARNINGS ).

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Regardless of indication, amphetamine should be administered at the lowest effective dosage and dosage should be individually adjusted. Late evening doses should be avoided because of resulting insomnia. Narcolepsy Usual dose is 5 to 60 milligrams per day in divided doses depending on the individual patient response. Narcolepsy seldom occurs in children under 12 years of age; however, when it does, amphetamine sulfate tablets may be used. The suggested initial dose for patients aged 6 to 12 is 5 mg daily; daily dose may be raised in increments of 5 mg at weekly intervals until optimal response obtained. In patients 12 years of age and older, start with 10 mg daily; …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Clinical Effects of Overdose Overdose of CNS stimulants is characterized by the following sympathomimetic effects: Cardiovascular effects including tachyarrhythmias, and hypertension or hypotension. Vasospasm, myocardial infarction, or aortic dissection may precipitate sudden cardiac death. Takotsubo cardiomyopathy may develop. CNS effects including psychomotor agitation, confusion, and hallucinations. Serotonin syndrome, seizures, cerebral vascular accidents, and coma may occur. Life-threatening hyperthermia (temperatures greater than 104°F) and rhabdomyolysis may develop. Overdose Management Consider the possibility of multiple drug ingestion. D-amphetamine is not dialyzable. …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions MAO inhibitors - MAOI antidepressants, as well as a metabolic of furazolidone, slow amphetamine metabolism. This slowing potentiates amphetamines, increasing their effect on the release of norepinephrine and other monoamines from adrenergic nerve endings; this can cause headaches and other signs of hypertensive crisis. A variety of neurological toxic effects and malignant hyperpyrexia can occur, sometimes with fatal results. Serotonergic drugs - The concomitant use of amphetamine sulfate tablets and serotonergic drugs increases the risk of serotonin syndrome. Initiate with lower doses and monitor patients for signs and symptoms of serotonin syndrome, particularly during …

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy Teratogenic Effects Dextroamphetamine sulfate has been shown to have embryotoxic and teratogenic effects when administered to A/Jax mice and C57BL mice in doses approximately 41 times the maximum human dose. Embryotoxic effects were not seen in New Zealand white rabbits given the drug in doses 7 times the human dose nor in rats given 12.5 times the maximum human dose. There are no adequate and well-controlled studies in pregnant women. Amphetamine sulfate tablets should be used during pregnancy only if the potential benefit justifies the potential risk to the fetus. Nonteratogenic Effects Infants born to mothers dependent on amphetamines have an increased risk of premature …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (5 MG, 10 MG, 15 MG, 20 MG, 25 MG, 30 MG, 2.5 MG)
Capsule (Sustained-Release) (5 MG, 10 MG, 15 MG, 20 MG, 25 MG, 30 MG, 2.5 MG)
Capsule (Extended-Release) (5 MG, 10 MG, 15 MG, 20 MG, 25 MG, 30 MG, 2.5 MG)
moa: Mixed amphetamine salts (≈3:1 dextro- to levo-amphetamine). Like all amphetamines, drives presynaptic RELEASE of dopamine and norepinephrine and blocks their reuptake. The l-isomer contributes relatively more peripheral noradrenergic (cardiovascular) effect.
receptorProfile: Presynaptic DA/NE release + reuptake inhibition; d- and l-enantiomer blend.
onsetOfEffect: Same-day; XR beads give an extended once-daily profile.
halfLife: d-amphetamine ~10 h, l-amphetamine ~13 h (l-isomer slightly longer).
metabolism: Hepatic CYP2D6 and oxidative deamination; renal excretion is urine-pH dependent.
startingDose: XR 10 mg PO each morning (adults may start 20 mg).
titration: Increase by ~10 mg/day at weekly intervals to response.
maxDose: ~30 mg/day in children; adults individualized (higher in some indications).
frequencyTiming: Once daily in the morning (XR); the beaded design avoids a midday dose.
administrationNotes: XR capsule may be opened and sprinkled on applesauce; do not chew the beads.
boxedWarning: WARNING: ABUSE, MISUSE, AND ADDICTION Amphetamine sulfate tablets have a high potential for abuse and misuse, which can lead to the development of a substance use disorder, including addiction. Misuse and abuse of CNS stimulants, including amphetamine sulfate, can result in overdose and death ( see OVERDOSAGE ), and this risk is increased with higher doses or unapproved methods of administration, such as snorting or injection. Before prescribing amphetamine sulfate tablets, assess each patient’s risk for abuse, misuse, and addiction. Educate patients and their families about these risks, proper storage of the drug, and proper disposal of any unused drug. Throughout amphetamine sulfate tablets treatment, reassess each patient’s risk of abuse, misuse, and addiction and frequently monitor for signs and symptoms of abuse, misuse, and addiction ( see WARNINGS and DRUG ABUSE AND DEPENDENCE ).
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Known hypersensitivity to amphetamine products. During or within 14 days following the administration of monoamine oxidase inhibitors (hypertensive crises may result) ( see WARNINGS ).
seriousAE: Sudden cardiac death with structural heart disease
overdoseLethality: Sympathomimetic crisis — hypertension, tachyarrhythmia, hyperthermia, agitation, seizures; supportive care, benzodiazepines, cooling.
baselineWorkup: Cardiac + family history of sudden death / structural disease
Screen for psychosis/bipolar and misuse risk
ongoingMonitoring: BP/HR each visit; growth in children; appetite, sleep, mood, tics; misuse/diversion.
contraindicatedCombos: MAOIs (during use or within 14 days of stopping)
maoiWashout: Do not use with an MAOI or within 14 days of stopping one.
pregnancy: Limited data; amphetamine class — individualized risk/benefit.
pediatric: Effective once-daily XR option; monitor growth, appetite, sleep; controlled substance.
cardiac: Avoid in serious structural cardiac disease/arrhythmia; l-isomer adds noradrenergic cardiovascular load.
pearls: A 3:1 d:l-amphetamine blend — the l-component adds relatively more peripheral noradrenergic (cardiovascular) effect than pure dextroamphetamine.
Mechanistically a releaser (plus reuptake block), so it shares amphetamine's MAOI contraindication and misuse potential.
mnemonics: "Mixed salts = mixed enantiomers" — d for the brain, l for the body (more peripheral NE).
confusables: Mixed amphetamine salts (Adderall XR) vs lisdexamfetamine (prodrug) vs dextroamphetamine (pure d-isomer).
classDifferentiators: d+l amphetamine blend; releaser + reuptake blocker; once-daily XR; controlled.
examHooks: Which ADHD product is a d:l-amphetamine salt mixture? (mixed amphetamine salts / Adderall XR)
counsellingPoints: Take once each morning.
Store securely; controlled substance with misuse potential.
Report chest pain, palpitations, or new hallucinations.
Never combine with an MAOI.

vignettes.stem: A patient on mixed amphetamine salts is also taking high-dose sodium bicarbonate for a GI complaint and develops worsening tachycardia and agitation. What is the most likely pharmacokinetic mechanism?
options: Urinary alkalinization reduces amphetamine excretion, raising drug levels
Bicarbonate blocks CYP2D6, lowering amphetamine levels
Sodium loading increases renal amphetamine clearance
vignettes.answer: Urinary alkalinization reduces amphetamine excretion, raising drug levels
vignettes.explanation: Amphetamine is a weak base; alkaline urine increases reabsorption and reduces renal clearance, raising plasma levels and sympathomimetic toxicity. Acidifying the urine has the opposite effect.

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Acamprosate calcium is contraindicated in patients who previously have exhibited hypersensitivity to acamprosate calcium or any of its components ( 4.1 ). Acamprosate calcium is contraindicated in patients with severe renal impairment ( 4.2 ). 4.1 Hypersensitivity to Acamprosate Calcium Acamprosate calcium is contraindicated in patients who previously have exhibited hypersensitivity to acamprosate calcium or any of its components. 4.2 Severe Renal Impairment Acamprosate calcium is contraindicated in patients with severe renal impairment (creatinine clearance of ≤30 mL/min) [ see Dosage and Administration (2.1) , Warnings and Precautions (5.1) , Use in Specific Populations (8.6) , and Clinical Pharmacology (12.3) ].

startingDose.sourceText: 2 DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Recommended dose: 666 mg (two 333 mg tablets) taken three times daily ( 2 ). Dose reduction to one 333 mg tablet taken three times daily for patients with moderate renal impairment (creatinine clearance 30 to 50 mL/min) ( 2.1 ). Acamprosate calcium delayed-release tablets are contraindicated in patients with severe renal impairment (creatinine clearance of ≤30 mL/min) ( 2.1 , 4.2 , 5.1 , 8.6 , 12.3 ). The recommended dose of acamprosate calcium delayed-release tablets is two 333 mg tablets (each dose should total 666 mg) taken three times daily. A lower dose may be effective in some patients. Although dosing may be done without regard to meals, dosing with meals …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE In all reported cases of acute overdosage with acamprosate calcium (total reported doses of up to 56 grams of acamprosate calcium), the only symptom that could be reasonably associated with acamprosate calcium was diarrhea. Hypercalcemia has not been reported in cases of acute overdose. A risk of hypercalcemia should be considered in chronic overdosage only. Treatment of overdose should be symptomatic and supportive.

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Acamprosate does not affect the pharmacokinetics of alcohol. The pharmacokinetics of acamprosate are not affected by alcohol, diazepam, or disulfiram, and clinically important interactions between naltrexone and acamprosate were not observed [ see Clinical Pharmacology (12.3) ].

contraindicatedCombos.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Acamprosate does not affect the pharmacokinetics of alcohol. The pharmacokinetics of acamprosate are not affected by alcohol, diazepam, or disulfiram, and clinically important interactions between naltrexone and acamprosate were not observed [ see Clinical Pharmacology (12.3) ].

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Teratogenic effects: Acamprosate calcium has been shown to be teratogenic in rats when given in doses that are approximately equal to the human dose (on a mg/m 2 basis) and in rabbits when given in doses that are approximately 3 times the human dose (on a mg/m 2 basis). Acamprosate calcium produced a dose-related increase in the number of fetuses with malformations in rats at oral doses of 300 mg/kg/day or greater (approximately equal to the maximum recommended human daily (MRHD) oral dose on a mg/m 2 basis). The malformations included hydronephrosis, malformed iris, retinal dysplasia, and retroesophageal subclavian artery. No findings were observed at an oral dose of 50 …

moa: Modulates glutamatergic (NMDA) and GABAergic neurotransmission — helps restore the excitatory/inhibitory balance disrupted by chronic alcohol, easing protracted withdrawal/craving and supporting maintenance of abstinence.
receptorProfile: NMDA-receptor modulation and GABA-ergic effects; a structural analogue of taurine. No abuse potential.
onsetOfEffect: Craving/abstinence benefit builds over weeks; best started once abstinence is achieved.
metabolism: NOT metabolized — excreted unchanged by the kidneys (no hepatic metabolism, negligible CYP interactions).
elimination: Renal (unchanged) — the rate-limiting route.
foodEffect: May be taken with meals (aids the TID schedule); food modestly reduces absorption but not clinically significant.
approvedCanada: Maintenance of abstinence in alcohol use disorder (in abstinent patients)
startingDose: 666 mg PO three times daily (two 333 mg tablets TID) in patients ≥60 kg; start after abstinence is achieved.
titration: Usually fixed dosing; reduce to 333 mg TID in moderate renal impairment (CrCl 30–50).
maxDose: 1998 mg/day (666 mg TID).
frequencyTiming: Three times daily — adherence is the main practical limitation.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce dose if CrCl 30–50 mL/min; CONTRAINDICATED if CrCl <30. Safe in hepatic impairment (no hepatic metabolism).
administrationNotes: Enteric-coated — swallow whole; diarrhea is the common early effect.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Acamprosate calcium is contraindicated in patients who previously have exhibited hypersensitivity to acamprosate calcium or any of its components ( 4.1 ). Acamprosate calcium is contraindicated in patients with severe renal impairment ( 4.2 ). 4.1 Hypersensitivity to Acamprosate Calcium Acamprosate calcium is contraindicated in patients who previously have exhibited hypersensitivity to acamprosate calcium or any of its components. 4.2 Severe Renal Impairment Acamprosate calcium is contraindicated in patients with severe renal impairment (creatinine clearance of ≤30 mL/min) [ see Dosage and Administration (2.1) , Warnings and Precautions (5.1) , Use in Specific Populations (8.6) , and Clinical Pharmacology (12.3) ].
overdoseLethality: Very low toxicity in overdose; the main effect is diarrhea. No abuse potential.
ongoingMonitoring: Renal function (baseline and as clinically indicated); mood/suicidality; abstinence support.
majorDDI: Minimal — not protein-bound, no hepatic metabolism, no significant CYP interactions (an advantage over disulfiram/naltrexone). Can be combined with naltrexone.
pregnancy: Limited human data; use only if benefit outweighs risk.
lactation: Limited data; caution.
renalHepatic: Renally cleared → adjust/avoid in renal impairment; hepatically SAFE (good option in alcoholic liver disease).
pearls: Renally excreted unchanged, not hepatically metabolized → a good choice in alcoholic liver disease, but must adjust/avoid in renal impairment.
Maintains abstinence (best in already-abstinent patients) — it does NOT treat acute withdrawal and is not aversive.
TID dosing hurts adherence; diarrhea is the signature side effect.
Minimal drug interactions; can be paired with naltrexone.
mnemonics: "ACamprosate = Abstinence Continued; Avoid in CKD" — renal clearance, TID, keeps sober patients sober.
confusables: Acamprosate (glutamate/GABA modulator, renal, TID, anti-craving) vs naltrexone (opioid antagonist, hepatic, reduces reward) vs disulfiram (ALDH inhibitor, aversive).
classDifferentiators: Anti-craving AUD agent that is renally (not hepatically) cleared and non-aversive — the liver-friendly option; contrast with hepatically-handled naltrexone and aversive disulfiram.
examHooks: Which AUD drug is renally excreted and safe in significant liver disease? (acamprosate)
Which AUD agent requires TID dosing? (acamprosate)
counsellingPoints: Take three times a day, every day — even if you feel well.
Diarrhea is common early and usually settles.
It helps you stay off alcohol; it is not a punishment drug and will not make you sick if you drink.
Tell your doctor about any kidney problems.

vignettes.stem: A 55-year-old with alcohol use disorder and Child-Pugh B cirrhosis wants pharmacotherapy to maintain abstinence. Renal function is normal. Which agent is best suited to their hepatic impairment?
vignettes.explanation: Acamprosate is not hepatically metabolized — it is eliminated unchanged by the kidneys — so it is preferred when the liver is impaired. Naltrexone and disulfiram are both hepatotoxic/hepatically handled. Acamprosate would be avoided instead if renal function were poor (CrCl <30).

source.name: openFDA/DailyMed acamprosate label — use in hepatic/renal impairment

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Buprenorphine sublingual tablet is contraindicated in patients with a history of hypersensitivity to buprenorphine, as serious adverse reactions, including anaphylactic shock, have been reported [see Warnings and Precautions (5.9)]. Hypersensitivity to buprenorphine. ( 4 )

startingDose.sourceText: 2 DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Administer buprenorphine sublingual tablet sublingually as a single daily dose. (2.1) Strongly consider prescribing naloxone at the time buprenorphine sublingual tablet is initiated or renewed because patients being treated for opioid use disorder have the potential for relapse, putting them at risk for opioid overdose. (2.2) To avoid precipitating withdrawal, induction with buprenorphine sublingual tablet should be undertaken when objective and clear signs of withdrawal are evident. Treatment of Overdose In the event of overdose, the respiratory and cardiac status of the patient should be monitored carefully. When respiratory or cardiac functions are depressed, primary attention should be given to the re-establishment of adequate respiratory exchange through provision of a patent airway and institution of assisted or controlled ventilation. Oxygen, IV fluids, vasopressors, and other supportive measures should be employed as indicated. In the case of overdose, the primary management should be the …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (8 MG, 12 MG, 16 MG, 5 MCG, 0.3 MG, 20 MCG, 2 MG, 75 MCG, 10 MCG, 900 MCG, 450 MCG, 80 MG, 100 MG, 300 MG, 600 MCG, 750 MCG, 150 MCG, 300 MCG, 15 MCG, 4 MG)
Patch (8 MG, 12 MG, 16 MG, 5 MCG, 0.3 MG, 20 MCG, 2 MG, 75 MCG, 10 MCG, 900 MCG, 450 MCG, 80 MG, 100 MG, 300 MG, 600 MCG, 750 MCG, 150 MCG, 300 MCG, 15 MCG, 4 MG)
Solution (8 MG, 12 MG, 16 MG, 5 MCG, 0.3 MG, 20 MCG, 2 MG, 75 MCG, 10 MCG, 900 MCG, 450 MCG, 80 MG, 100 MG, 300 MG, 600 MCG, 750 MCG, 150 MCG, 300 MCG, 15 MCG, 4 MG)
Film (Soluble) (8 MG, 12 MG, 16 MG, 5 MCG, 0.3 MG, 20 MCG, 2 MG, 75 MCG, 10 MCG, 900 MCG, 450 MCG, 80 MG, 100 MG, 300 MG, 600 MCG, 750 MCG, 150 MCG, 300 MCG, 15 MCG, 4 MG)
moa: PARTIAL mu-opioid receptor agonist (high affinity, low intrinsic activity) with kappa antagonism. Partial activation gives a ceiling on respiratory depression; high affinity displaces full agonists.
receptorProfile: High mu affinity, partial agonist; slow receptor dissociation → long duration and a plateau in opioid effect at higher doses.
onsetOfEffect: Sublingual onset ~30–60 min; long duration allows once-daily (or less) dosing.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 → norbuprenorphine (weakly active) plus glucuronidation.
elimination: Mostly biliary/fecal; renal minor.
foodEffect: Sublingual — avoid eating/drinking until the film/tablet dissolves.
startingDose: Induction ONLY once in objective withdrawal (e.g., COWS ≥ ~8–12) to avoid precipitated withdrawal: 2–4 mg SL, reassess in 1–2 h; day-1 total often 8–12 mg.
titration: Titrate by 2–4 mg to suppress withdrawal/craving; usual maintenance 12–24 mg/day.
maxDose: Little added benefit above ~24 mg/day (max ~24–32 mg).
frequencyTiming: Once daily sublingually (some patients alternate-day at higher doses).
startStopCrossTaper: Because of high mu affinity, it can precipitate withdrawal if a full agonist is still on board — start only in withdrawal; micro-dosing ("Bernese") protocols allow overlap induction.
administrationNotes: Sublingual — let it dissolve fully; observed dosing initially, carries with stability.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Buprenorphine sublingual tablet is contraindicated in patients with a history of hypersensitivity to buprenorphine, as serious adverse reactions, including anaphylactic shock, have been reported [see Warnings and Precautions (5.9)]. Hypersensitivity to buprenorphine. ( 4 )
seriousAE: Respiratory depression (ceiling, but rises with other CNS depressants)
syndromes: Opioid overdose (less likely due to ceiling)
overdoseLethality: LOWER than methadone — the ceiling on respiratory depression makes buprenorphine alone relatively safe. Danger rises sharply with benzodiazepines/alcohol/other sedatives. High receptor affinity can make naloxone reversal harder (may need higher/repeated doses).
ongoingMonitoring: Assess withdrawal/craving and sedation; urine drug screens; LFTs if hepatic risk.
majorDDI: CYP3A4 inhibitors (raise levels) and inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine → lower levels)
CNS depressants — benzodiazepines, alcohol, gabapentinoids (respiratory depression)
contraindicatedCombos: Concurrent full mu agonists (precipitated withdrawal / competition)
Caution with benzodiazepines and other CNS depressants
pregnancy: Acceptable OAT in pregnancy; associated with less severe neonatal abstinence syndrome than methadone (MOTHER trial). Do not withdraw OAT in pregnancy. Tie to CRISM/CANMAT OAT guidance.
lactation: Compatible — low milk transfer; breastfeeding encouraged if stable.
pearls: Partial agonist with a CEILING on respiratory depression → safer in overdose than methadone.
High mu affinity displaces full agonists → precipitated withdrawal if started too soon; wait for objective withdrawal (COWS) or use micro-dosing induction.
Long half-life → once-daily; minimal QT effect vs methadone.
CRISM guidance favours buprenorphine/naloxone as first-line OAT over methadone for most patients (safety).
mnemonics: "Buprenorphine = the Brake" — partial agonist puts a ceiling on respiratory depression. "Start too soon and you Bring on Bad withdrawal."
confusables: Buprenorphine (partial agonist, ceiling, precipitated withdrawal) vs methadone (full agonist, no ceiling, QT/overdose).
Buprenorphine mono product vs buprenorphine/naloxone (added abuse deterrent).
classDifferentiators: The partial-agonist OAT: ceiling on respiratory depression (safer), precipitated-withdrawal risk on induction, minimal QT — opposite the methadone profile.
examHooks: Which OAT agent has a ceiling on respiratory depression? (buprenorphine)
Why must buprenorphine be started only once the patient is in withdrawal? (high mu affinity displaces residual full agonist → precipitated withdrawal)
counsellingPoints: Do not take your first dose until you feel clearly in withdrawal.
Let the film/tablet dissolve under the tongue — do not swallow, eat, or drink until gone.
Avoid alcohol and benzodiazepines.
Much safer than methadone in overdose, but not risk-free with sedatives.

vignettes.stem: A patient who used hydromorphone 4 hours ago is given sublingual buprenorphine in clinic. Within 30 minutes they develop worsening yawning, gooseflesh, abdominal cramps, myalgias and dysphoria — worse than before dosing. What is the mechanism?
options: Precipitated withdrawal — high-affinity partial agonist displacing the full agonist from mu receptors
Opioid overdose from additive full agonism
vignettes.answer: Precipitated withdrawal — high-affinity partial agonist displacing the full agonist from mu receptors
vignettes.explanation: Buprenorphine binds mu receptors with high affinity but only partially activates them. Given while a full agonist still occupies receptors, it displaces the full agonist and drops net opioid effect, precipitating acute withdrawal. Induction should wait until the patient is already in objective withdrawal (COWS).
vignettes.testedFact: Buprenorphine precipitated withdrawal on early induction

formulationsCanada: Tablet (8 MG, 12 MG, 16 MG, 5 MCG, 0.3 MG, 20 MCG, 2 MG, 75 MCG, 10 MCG, 900 MCG, 450 MCG, 80 MG, 100 MG, 300 MG, 600 MCG, 750 MCG, 150 MCG, 300 MCG, 15 MCG, 4 MG)

Film (Soluble) (8 MG, 12 MG, 16 MG, 5 MCG, 0.3 MG, 20 MCG, 2 MG, 75 MCG, 10 MCG, 900 MCG, 450 MCG, 80 MG, 100 MG, 300 MG, 600 MCG, 750 MCG, 150 MCG, 300 MCG, 15 MCG, 4 MG)
moa: Combination: buprenorphine (partial mu agonist, ceiling on respiratory depression) + naloxone (mu antagonist). Naloxone is poorly bioavailable sublingually → essentially inert when taken correctly, but if the product is dissolved and injected the naloxone blocks the high and precipitates withdrawal (abuse deterrent).
receptorProfile: Buprenorphine drives the therapeutic effect (high-affinity partial agonist); naloxone contributes only if misused parenterally.
onsetOfEffect: Sublingual ~30–60 min (buprenorphine-driven); long duration.
halfLife: Buprenorphine ~24–42 h; sublingual naloxone negligible (poorly absorbed, rapidly cleared).
metabolism: Buprenorphine via CYP3A4 → norbuprenorphine + glucuronidation; naloxone glucuronidated.
elimination: Biliary/fecal (buprenorphine); renal (naloxone conjugates).
foodEffect: Sublingual — do not eat/drink until dissolved.
approvedCanada: Opioid use disorder (opioid agonist therapy) — first-line
startingDose: Dosed by buprenorphine content; induction ONLY once in objective withdrawal (COWS) to avoid precipitated withdrawal: start ~2/0.5–4/1 mg SL, day-1 total ~8/2–12/3 mg.
titration: Titrate buprenorphine component to suppress craving; maintenance ~12/3–24/6 mg/day.
maxDose: Little added benefit above ~24 mg buprenorphine/day.
frequencyTiming: Once daily sublingually.
startStopCrossTaper: Same precipitated-withdrawal caution as buprenorphine; the mono (naloxone-free) product is sometimes preferred in pregnancy.
administrationNotes: Sublingual film/tablet — let dissolve fully; abuse-deterrent formulation.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Buprenorphine and naloxone sublingual tablet is contraindicated in patients with a history of hypersensitivity to buprenorphine or naloxone as serious adverse reactions, including anaphylactic shock, have been reported [see Warnings and Precautions (5.9) ] . Hypersensitivity to buprenorphine or naloxone. ( 4 )
seriousAE: Precipitated withdrawal (early induction or if injected)
syndromes: Opioid overdose (less likely due to ceiling)
overdoseLethality: LOWER than methadone owing to the buprenorphine ceiling; naloxone adds diversion deterrence, not overdose protection when taken as directed. Risk rises with benzodiazepines/alcohol.
ongoingMonitoring: Withdrawal/craving and sedation; urine drug screens; LFTs if hepatic risk.
pregnancy: OAT is standard of care in pregnancy; some guidance prefers the buprenorphine mono product over the combination in pregnancy, though the combination is also used. Do not withdraw OAT. Tie to CRISM/CANMAT OAT guidance.
lactation: Compatible if stable.
pearls: Naloxone is inactive sublingually (poor bioavailability) — it only matters if the product is injected, where it precipitates withdrawal to deter IV misuse.
First-line OAT (CRISM) over methadone for most patients because of the buprenorphine safety ceiling.
Precipitated-withdrawal caution on induction is driven by buprenorphine, not naloxone — start in objective withdrawal.
mnemonics: "Suboxone: the naloxone is a Sub-lingual dud, a Shot spoiler" — inert if dissolved under the tongue, but wrecks the high if injected.
confusables: The naloxone here is a diversion deterrent — do not confuse with naloxone the overdose-rescue drug or with naltrexone the maintenance antagonist.
classDifferentiators: Same partial-agonist ceiling/precipitated-withdrawal profile as buprenorphine, plus an abuse-deterrent naloxone component; first-line OAT.
examHooks: Why is naloxone added to sublingual buprenorphine? (deters IV misuse — inactive sublingually, precipitates withdrawal if injected)
Which OAT is generally first-line in Canada? (buprenorphine/naloxone)
counsellingPoints: Take under the tongue and let it dissolve — never inject it (the naloxone will cause severe withdrawal).
Start only once you are clearly in withdrawal.
Avoid alcohol and benzodiazepines.

vignettes.stem: A patient asks why their sublingual buprenorphine product also contains naloxone, since naloxone reverses opioids. What is the correct explanation?
options: Naloxone provides the main therapeutic anti-craving effect
Naloxone protects against overdose when taken as directed
Naloxone is poorly absorbed sublingually and is inert if taken correctly, but precipitates withdrawal if the product is injected — an abuse deterrent
vignettes.answer: Naloxone is poorly absorbed sublingually and is inert if taken correctly, but precipitates withdrawal if the product is injected — an abuse deterrent
vignettes.explanation: Sublingual naloxone has very low bioavailability, so when the combination is used as directed it contributes nothing. If dissolved and injected, naloxone becomes bioavailable and precipitates withdrawal, discouraging parenteral misuse. Buprenorphine remains the active therapeutic component.
vignettes.testedFact: Naloxone as sublingual abuse deterrent in buprenorphine/naloxone

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: Disulfiram should never be administered to a patient when he is in a state of alcohol intoxication, or without his full knowledge. The physician should instruct relatives accordingly. Disulfiram should never be administered to a patient when he is in a state of alcohol intoxication, or without his full knowledge. The physician should instruct relatives accordingly.

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS: Patients who are receiving or have recently received metronidazole, paraldehyde, alcohol, or alcohol-containing preparations, e.g., cough syrups, tonics and the like, should not be given disulfiram. Disulfiram is contraindicated in the presence of severe myocardial disease or coronary occlusion, psychoses, and hypersensitivity to disulfiram or to other thiuram derivatives used in pesticides and rubber vulcanization.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION: Disulfiram should never be administered until the patient has abstained from alcohol for at least 12 hours. Initial Dosage Schedule: In the first phase of treatment, a maximum of 500 mg daily is given in a single dose for one to two weeks. Although usually taken in the morning, disulfiram may be taken on retiring by patients who experience a sedative effect. Alternatively, to minimize, or eliminate, the sedative effect, dosage may be adjusted downward. Maintenance Regimen: The average maintenance dose is 250 mg daily (range, 125 to 500 mg), it should not exceed 500 mg daily. Note: Occasionally patients, while seemingly on adequate maintenance doses of disulfiram, …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS: Disulfiram should never be administered to a patient when he is in a state of alcohol intoxication, or without his full knowledge. The physician should instruct relatives accordingly. The patient must be fully informed of the disulfiram-alcohol reaction. He must be strongly cautioned against surreptitious drinking while taking the drug, and he must be fully aware of the possible consequences. He should be warned to avoid alcohol in disguised forms, i.e., in sauces, vinegars, cough mixtures, and even in aftershave lotions and back rubs. He should also be warned that reactions may occur with alcohol up to 14 days after ingesting disulfiram. The Disulfiram-Alcohol Reaction: …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE: No specific information is available on the treatment of overdosage with disulfiram. It is recommended that the physician contact the local Poison Control Center.

PRIOR TO ADMINISTERING DISULFIRAM TO A PATIENT ON PHENYTOIN THERAPY, A BASELINE PHENYTOIN SERUM LEVEL SHOULD BE OBTAINED. SUBSEQUENT TO INITIATION OF DISULFIRAM THERAPY, SERUM LEVELS OF PHENYTOIN SHOULD BE DETERMINED ON DIFFERENT DAYS FOR EVIDENCE OF AN INCREASE OR FOR A CONTINUING RISE IN LEVELS. …

pregnancy.sourceText: Usage in Pregnancy: The safe use of this drug in pregnancy has not been established. Therefore, disulfiram should be used during pregnancy only when, in the judgment of the physician, the probable benefits outweigh the possible risks.

moa: Irreversibly inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH). If alcohol is consumed, acetaldehyde accumulates and produces the aversive "disulfiram–alcohol reaction" (flushing, throbbing headache, nausea/vomiting, tachycardia, hypotension, dyspnea) — a deterrent to drinking.
receptorProfile: Enzyme inhibitor (ALDH; also inhibits dopamine-β-hydroxylase and several CYPs, e.g., 2E1, 2C9, 3A4).
onsetOfEffect: Aversive sensitivity develops over ~12 h; because ALDH inhibition is irreversible, sensitivity persists ~1–2 weeks after the last dose.
halfLife: Parent ~7 h, but the clinical effect outlasts it (irreversible enzyme inhibition) — reactions possible up to ~14 days after stopping.
metabolism: Hepatic; potent inhibitor of several CYPs and of ALDH/dopamine-β-hydroxylase.
elimination: Hepatic metabolism; renal/pulmonary excretion of metabolites.
approvedCanada: Adjunct in the management of chronic alcohol use disorder (aversive therapy in motivated, abstinent patients)
approvedPopulations: Adults with good insight/motivation and no significant cardiac/hepatic contraindication.
startingDose: 250 mg PO once daily (range 125–500 mg); begin only after ≥12–24 h of abstinence from alcohol.
titration: Usually maintained at 250 mg/day; up to 500 mg if reactions are insufficient at usual exposure.
maxDose: 500 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once daily; supervised (e.g., partner-observed) dosing improves adherence.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Avoid in significant hepatic disease; monitor LFTs.
administrationNotes: Best under supervision; patient must understand the reaction and avoid all alcohol sources.
boxedWarning: WARNING: Disulfiram should never be administered to a patient when he is in a state of alcohol intoxication, or without his full knowledge. The physician should instruct relatives accordingly. Disulfiram should never be administered to a patient when he is in a state of alcohol intoxication, or without his full knowledge. The physician should instruct relatives accordingly.
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS: Patients who are receiving or have recently received metronidazole, paraldehyde, alcohol, or alcohol-containing preparations, e.g., cough syrups, tonics and the like, should not be given disulfiram. Disulfiram is contraindicated in the presence of severe myocardial disease or coronary occlusion, psychoses, and hypersensitivity to disulfiram or to other thiuram derivatives used in pesticides and rubber vulcanization.
seriousAE: Disulfiram–alcohol reaction (can be severe: arrhythmia, hypotension, MI, seizures, death with a large alcohol load)
overdoseLethality: The drug alone is moderately toxic (hepatotoxicity, neuropsychiatric effects). Combined with alcohol the acetaldehyde reaction can be life-threatening — cardiovascular collapse and death with a large alcohol load.
baselineWorkup: Baseline LFTs; assess cardiac status and motivation/insight before starting
ongoingMonitoring: Periodic LFTs (baseline, ~2 weeks, then periodically); watch for neuropathy/visual changes and psychiatric effects.
majorDDI: Alcohol in ANY form — including mouthwash, cough/cold syrups, sauces, hand sanitizer, aftershave, and alcohol-vehicle medications
contraindicatedCombos: Alcohol in any form (including hidden sources)
pregnancy: Avoid — teratogenicity concerns and the risk of an acetaldehyde reaction; generally not used in pregnancy.
lactation: Avoid.
renalHepatic: Hepatotoxic — avoid in significant liver disease; monitor LFTs.
pearls: Aversive (not anti-craving) therapy — needs a motivated, insightful, abstinent patient; NOT first-line (naltrexone/acamprosate preferred).
Avoid ALL alcohol, including hidden sources (mouthwash, cough syrup, sauces, hand sanitizer, aftershave); the reaction can occur up to ~2 weeks after the last dose (irreversible ALDH inhibition).
Hepatotoxic — baseline and periodic LFTs; can be fulminant.
Inhibits several CYPs → raises warfarin (bleeding) and phenytoin; avoid metronidazole (psychosis).
mnemonics: "diSULFIRam → you SUFFER if you drink" — acetaldehyde builds up: flushing, headache, vomiting, racing heart.
confusables: Disulfiram (aversive, ALDH inhibitor) vs naltrexone/acamprosate (anti-craving, not aversive). Beware disulfiram-LIKE reactions from metronidazole and some cephalosporins.
classDifferentiators: The only aversive AUD agent — it punishes drinking via acetaldehyde rather than reducing craving; hepatotoxic, interaction-prone, and second-line vs naltrexone/acamprosate.
examHooks: A patient on disulfiram flushes and vomits after cough syrup — why? (hidden alcohol → acetaldehyde reaction)
Which AUD drug inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase? (disulfiram)
counsellingPoints: Avoid every source of alcohol — read labels on mouthwash, cough syrups, sauces, hand sanitizer, and aftershave.
A reaction can happen even up to 2 weeks after your last dose.
Take it only when committed to abstinence; supervised dosing helps.
Report yellow skin, dark urine, numbness/tingling, or vision changes.

vignettes.stem: A patient on disulfiram for alcohol use disorder develops sudden facial flushing, a pounding headache, nausea/vomiting, palpitations and hypotension about 20 minutes after taking an over-the-counter cough syrup. What is the mechanism?
options: Accumulation of acetaldehyde because disulfiram inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase and the syrup contained alcohol
vignettes.answer: Accumulation of acetaldehyde because disulfiram inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase and the syrup contained alcohol
vignettes.explanation: Disulfiram irreversibly inhibits ALDH, so any ingested alcohol — including the ethanol in many cough syrups — is metabolized only to acetaldehyde, which accumulates and produces the flushing, headache, vomiting, tachycardia and hypotension of the disulfiram–alcohol reaction. Patients must avoid all hidden alcohol sources.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: SERIOUS AND LIFE-THREATENING RISKS FROM USE OF METHADONE HYDROCHLORIDE INJECTION Addiction, Abuse, and Misuse Because the use of Methadone Hydrochloride Injection exposes patients and other users to the risks of opioid addiction, abuse, and misuse, which can lead to overdose and death, assess each patient’s risk prior to prescribing and reassess all patients regularly for the development of these behaviors and conditions (see WARNINGS ). Life-Threatening Respiratory Depression Serious, life-threatening, or fatal respiratory depression may occur with use of Methadone Hydrochloride Injection, especially during initiation or following a dosage increase. To reduce the risk of respiratory depression, proper dosing and titration of Methadone Hydrochloride Injection are essential (see WARNINGS ). Neonatal Opioid Withdrawal Syndrome (NOWS) Neonatal opioid withdrawal syndrome (NOWS) is an expected and treatable outcome of use of Methadone Hydrochloride Injection during pregnancy. NOWS may be life-threatening if not recognized and treated in the neonate. The balance between the risks of NOWS and the benefits of maternal Methadone Hydrochloride Injection use may differ based on the risks associated with the mother’s underlying condition, pain, or addiction. Advise the patient of the risk of NOWS so that appropriate planning for management of the neonate can occur (see WARNINGS …

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Methadone Hydrochloride Injection is contraindicated in patients with: • Significant respiratory depression • Acute or severe bronchial asthma in an unmonitored setting or in the absence of resuscitative equipment • Known or suspected gastrointestinal obstruction, including paralytic ileus • Hypersensitivity to methadone hydrochloride (e.g. anaphylaxis) or any other ingredient in Methadone Hydrochloride Injection.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Important General Information Consider the following important factors that differentiate methadone from other opioids: • The peak respiratory depressant effect of methadone occurs later and persists longer than its peak pharmacologic effect. • A high degree of opioid tolerance does not eliminate the possibility of methadone overdose, iatrogenic or otherwise. Deaths have been reported during conversion to methadone from chronic, high-dose treatment with other opioid agonists and during initiation of methadone treatment of addiction in subjects previously abusing high doses of other opioid agonists. • There is high interpatient variability in absorption, metabolism, …

Deaths have been reported during conversion to methadone from chronic, high-dose treatment with other opioid agonists and during initiation of methadone treatment of addiction in subjects previously abusing high doses of other opioid agonists. • There is high interpatient variability in absorption, metabolism, …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Addiction, Abuse and Misuse Methadone Hydrochloride Injection contains methadone, a Schedule II controlled substance. As an opioid, Methadone Hydrochloride Injection exposes users to the risks of addiction, abuse, and misuse. Although the risk of addiction in any individual is unknown, it can occur in patients appropriately prescribed Methadone Hydrochloride Injection. Addiction can occur at recommended doses and if the drug is misused or abused. The risk of opioid-related overdose or overdose-related death is increased with higher opioid doses, and this risk persists over the course of therapy. In postmarketing studies, addiction, abuse, misuse, and fatal and non-fatal opioid …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Clinical Presentation Acute overdosage with methadone can be manifested by respiratory depression, somnolence progressing to stupor or coma, skeletal-muscle flaccidity, cold and clammy skin, constricted pupils, and, in some cases, pulmonary edema, bradycardia, hypotension, hypoglycemia, partial or complete airway obstruction, atypical snoring, and death. Marked mydriasis rather than miosis may be seen with hypoxia in overdose situations (see CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY ). In severe overdosage, particularly by the intravenous route, apnea, circulatory collapse, cardiac arrest, and death may occur. Methadone overdosage is associated with rhabdomyolysis. Seek medical attention, especially …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions Table 1: Clinically Significant Drug Interactions with Methadone Hydrochloride Injection Inhibitors of CYP3A4, CYP2B6, CYP2C19, CYP2C9, or CYP2D6 Clinical Impact: Methadone undergoes hepatic N-demethylation by several cytochrome P450 (CYP) isoforms, including CYP3A4, CYP2B6, CYP2C19, CYP2C9, and CYP2D6. The concomitant use of Methadone Hydrochloride Injection and CYP3A4, CYP2B6, CYP2C19, CYP2C9, or CYP2D6 inhibitors can increase the plasma concentration of methadone, resulting in increased or prolonged opioid effects, and may result in a fatal overdose, particularly when an inhibitor is added after a stable dose of Methadone Hydrochloride Injection is achieved. These …

The concomitant use of Methadone Hydrochloride Injection and CYP3A4, CYP2B6, CYP2C19, CYP2C9, or CYP2D6 inhibitors can increase the plasma concentration of methadone, resulting in increased or prolonged opioid effects, and may result in a fatal overdose, particularly when an inhibitor is added after a stable dose of Methadone Hydrochloride Injection is achieved. These …

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy There are no pharmacokinetic studies of parenteral methadone in pregnancy. The disposition of oral methadone has been studied in approximately 30 pregnant patients in 2 nd and 3 rd trimesters. Elimination of methadone was significantly changed in pregnancy. Total body clearance of methadone was increased in pregnant patients compared to the same patients postpartum or to non-pregnant opioid-dependent women. The terminal half-life of methadone is decreased during second and third trimesters. The decrease in plasma half-life and increased clearance of methadone resulting in lower methadone trough levels during pregnancy can lead to withdrawal symptoms in some pregnant patients. The …

moa: Full mu-opioid receptor agonist (also NMDA antagonist + monoamine reuptake inhibition). Long-acting; cross-tolerance suppresses withdrawal/craving and blunts the euphoria of other opioids.
receptorProfile: Full agonist at mu (some kappa/delta); NMDA antagonism; SNRI-like monoamine effects. No ceiling on respiratory depression.
onsetOfEffect: Analgesia 30–60 min; withdrawal suppression within hours, but OAT steady state takes ~5 days.
halfLife: Long and highly variable (~8–59 h, can exceed 100 h). Elimination half-life far outlasts the ~6–8 h analgesic duration → accumulation risk.
timeToSteadyState: ~5 days (4–5 half-lives) — most induction deaths occur here as levels climb.
metabolism: Hepatic, chiefly CYP3A4 (also 2B6, 2C19, 2D6) → EDDP (inactive). Substrate of many CYP inducers/inhibitors.
elimination: Hepatic metabolism with renal and fecal excretion.
foodEffect: Not clinically significant.
approvedPopulations: Adults; specially authorized prescribers for OAT.
startingDose: OAT induction typically 10–30 mg PO on day 1 (do not exceed ~30–40 mg first day); "start low, go slow".
titration: Increase by ~5–10 mg only every ≥5 days (steady state), watching for sedation; usual maintenance 60–120 mg/day.
maxDose: No fixed ceiling — individualized to tolerance; higher maintenance doses reduce illicit opioid use but raise QT risk.
frequencyTiming: Once daily for OAT (witnessed dosing initially); split (q6–12h) for analgesia.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Caution in hepatic impairment (accumulation).
administrationNotes: Witnessed daily dosing at induction; take-home "carries" earned with stability and clean urine screens.
boxedWarning: WARNING: SERIOUS AND LIFE-THREATENING RISKS FROM USE OF METHADONE HYDROCHLORIDE INJECTION Addiction, Abuse, and Misuse Because the use of Methadone Hydrochloride Injection exposes patients and other users to the risks of opioid addiction, abuse, and misuse, which can lead to overdose and death, assess each patient’s risk prior to prescribing and reassess all patients regularly for the development of these behaviors and conditions (see WARNINGS ). Life-Threatening Respiratory Depression Serious, life-threatening, or fatal respiratory depression may occur with use of Methadone Hydrochloride Injection, especially during initiation or following a dosage increase. To reduce the risk of respiratory depression, proper dosing and titration of Methadone Hydrochloride Injection are essential (see WARNINGS ). Neonatal Opioid Withdrawal Syndrome (NOWS) Neonatal opioid withdrawal syndrome (NOWS) is …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Methadone Hydrochloride Injection is contraindicated in patients with: • Significant respiratory depression • Acute or severe bronchial asthma in an unmonitored setting or in the absence of resuscitative equipment • Known or suspected gastrointestinal obstruction, including paralytic ileus • Hypersensitivity to methadone hydrochloride (e.g. anaphylaxis) or any other ingredient in Methadone Hydrochloride Injection.
overdoseLethality: HIGH — full agonist with no ceiling on respiratory depression; the long half-life prolongs toxicity and delays overdose during induction. Fatal with alcohol/benzodiazepines. Reverse with naloxone but expect re-narcotization (naloxone is far shorter-acting) → repeat doses or infusion.
baselineWorkup: Baseline ECG (QTc), especially with cardiac risk factors, other QT drugs, or doses >100 mg/day
ongoingMonitoring: ECG at baseline, ~30 days, then periodically (and with dose >100 mg or risk factors); close observation for sedation during induction; urine drug screens.
majorDDI: CYP3A4/2B6 inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine, phenytoin, efavirenz) → lower levels/withdrawal
CYP3A4 inhibitors (azoles, macrolides, protease inhibitors) → toxicity
CNS depressants — benzodiazepines, alcohol, gabapentinoids (respiratory depression)
contraindicatedCombos: Concurrent alcohol / benzodiazepines / other CNS depressants (respiratory-depression risk)
pregnancy: OAT (methadone or buprenorphine) is standard of care in pregnancy — do NOT withdraw. Neonatal opioid withdrawal (NAS) is expected and managed. Tie to CRISM/CANMAT OAT guidance.
lactation: Compatible — small amounts in milk; breastfeeding encouraged if stable.
cardiac: Dose-dependent QT prolongation → torsades; obtain baseline and follow-up ECG.
pearls: Full mu agonist → NO ceiling on respiratory depression (unlike buprenorphine).
Long, variable half-life → accumulation and delayed overdose during induction; most induction deaths fall in the first 1–2 weeks — "start low, go slow".
Dose-dependent QT prolongation / torsades — get an ECG (baseline, then follow-up, especially >100 mg).
CYP3A4 substrate — inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine) precipitate withdrawal; inhibitors cause toxicity.
mnemonics: "Methadone = the marathon opioid" — long half-life, slow induction, QT watch. And "methaDONE can leave your heart DONE" (torsades).
confusables: Methadone (full agonist, no ceiling, QT/overdose risk, long half-life) vs buprenorphine (partial agonist, ceiling, precipitated withdrawal).
classDifferentiators: The full-agonist OAT: no ceiling, QT/torsades, long-half-life accumulation, CYP3A4-driven interactions — opposite the buprenorphine safety profile.
examHooks: Which OAT agent needs baseline + follow-up ECG for QT? (methadone)
Why do methadone overdose deaths cluster in the first 2 weeks of induction? (accumulation from long, variable half-life)
counsellingPoints: Daily observed dosing at first — do not miss or double up.
Never combine with alcohol, benzodiazepines, or other sedatives.
Toxicity builds over days; do not "top up" between doses.
Store securely — a single dose can be lethal to a child.

vignettes.stem: A 34-year-old begins methadone for opioid use disorder at 30 mg/day. Feeling little effect on day 3, they are tempted to take extra. On day 5 they are found deeply sedated with a respiratory rate of 6. What pharmacologic property best explains this delayed deterioration?
options: Rapid CYP3A4 induction lowering the dose
Accumulation due to a long, variable elimination half-life reaching steady state over ~5 days
A ceiling effect on respiratory depression
vignettes.answer: Accumulation due to a long, variable elimination half-life reaching steady state over ~5 days
vignettes.explanation: Methadone is a full mu agonist with an elimination half-life (up to >100 h) far longer than its analgesic duration. Plasma levels accumulate over ~4–5 days to steady state, so a dose that felt sub-therapeutic early can become toxic — hence "start low, go slow" and no early dose escalation.

source.name: openFDA/DailyMed methadone label — warnings (accumulation, respiratory depression)

vignettes.stem: A patient stable on high-dose methadone develops syncope. ECG shows QTc 520 ms and a run of polymorphic VT. Which intervention is most directly indicated by methadone’s characteristic cardiac risk?
options: Start a beta-blocker for rate control
Review/withdraw QT-prolonging co-medications and correct electrolytes for torsades risk
Increase the methadone dose to stabilize levels
vignettes.answer: Review/withdraw QT-prolonging co-medications and correct electrolytes for torsades risk
vignettes.explanation: Methadone causes dose-dependent QT prolongation and torsades de pointes. Management targets the arrhythmogenic substrate: correct K+/Mg2+, stop other QT-prolonging agents, and reconsider the methadone dose or switch to buprenorphine (much lower QT risk).

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: SERIOUS AND LIFE-THREATENING RISKS FROM USE OF PENTAZOCINE AND NALOXONE TABLETS Addiction, Abuse, and Misuse Because the use of Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets exposes patients and other users to the risks of opioid addiction, abuse, and misuse, which can lead to overdose and death, assess each patient's risk prior to prescribing and reassess all patients regularly for the development of these behaviors and conditions [ see <WARNINGS ]. Life-Threatening Respiratory Depression Serious, life-threatening, or fatal respiratory depression may occur with use of Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets, especially during initiation or following a dosage increase. To reduce the risk of respiratory depression, proper dosing and titration of Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets are essential [ see <WARNINGS ]. Accidental Ingestion Accidental ingestion of even one dose of Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets, especially by children, can result in a fatal overdose of Pentazocine Hydrochloride [ see <WARNINGS ]. Risks From Concomitant Use With Benzodiazepines Or Other CNS Depressants Concomitant use of opioids with benzodiazepines or other central nervous system (CNS) depressants, including alcohol, may result in profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death. Reserve concomitant prescribing of Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets and benzodiazepines or other CNS depressants for use in …

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets are contraindicated in patients with: Significant respiratory depression [see WARNINGS ] Acute or severe bronchial asthma in an unmonitored setting or in the absence of resuscitative equipment [see WARNINGS ]. Patients with known or suspected gastrointestinal obstruction, including paralytic ileus [see WARNINGS ] Patients with hypersensitivity to either pentazocine, naloxone, or any of the formulation excipients (e.g., anaphylaxis) [see WARNINGS ].

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Important Dosage and Administration Instructions Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets should be prescribed only by healthcare professionals who are knowledgeable about the use of opioids and how to mitigate the associated risks. Use the lowest effective dosage for the shortest duration of time consistent with individual patient treatment goals [see Warnings and Precautions ] . Reserve titration to higher doses of Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets for patients in whom lower doses are insufficiently effective and in whom the expected benefits of using a higher dose opioid clearly outweigh the substantial risks. Many acute pain conditions (e.g., the pain that occurs with a …

Marked mydriasis rather than miosis may be seen with hypoxia in overdose situations [see CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY]. Toxic leukoencephalopathy has been reported after opioid overdose and can present hours, days, or weeks after apparent recovery from the initial intoxication. For pentazocine alone in single doses …

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy Risk Summary Use of opioid analgesics for an extended period of time during pregnancy may cause neonatal opioid withdrawal syndrome [see Warnings ]. There are no available data with Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets in pregnant women to inform a drug-associated risk for major birth defects and miscarriage. In animal reproduction studies, pentazocine administered subcutaneously to pregnant hamsters during the early gestational period produced neural tube defects (i.e., exencephaly and cranioschisis) at 2.6 times the maximum daily dose (MDD). In pregnant rats administered pentazocine:naloxone during organogenesis, there were increased incidences of resorptions and extra ribs at 0.2 …
