brandsCanada: Naloxone Hydrochloride Injection Sdz Preservative Free
formulationsCanada: Liquid (0.02 MG, 1 MG, 0.4 MG, 2 MG, 3 MG, 2.50 MG, 4 MG, 5 MG, 0.5 MG, 20 MG, 10 MG, 8 MG)
Solution (0.02 MG, 1 MG, 0.4 MG, 2 MG, 3 MG, 2.50 MG, 4 MG, 5 MG, 0.5 MG, 20 MG, 10 MG, 8 MG)
Tablet (0.02 MG, 1 MG, 0.4 MG, 2 MG, 3 MG, 2.50 MG, 4 MG, 5 MG, 0.5 MG, 20 MG, 10 MG, 8 MG)
Tablet (Extended-Release) (0.02 MG, 1 MG, 0.4 MG, 2 MG, 3 MG, 2.50 MG, 4 MG, 5 MG, 0.5 MG, 20 MG, 10 MG, 8 MG)
Spray (Metered-Dose) (0.02 MG, 1 MG, 0.4 MG, 2 MG, 3 MG, 2.50 MG, 4 MG, 5 MG, 0.5 MG, 20 MG, 10 MG, 8 MG)
moa: Pure opioid receptor ANTAGONIST — competitively displaces opioids at mu receptors, rapidly reversing respiratory depression and sedation. The opioid-overdose rescue agent.
receptorProfile: Competitive mu (and kappa/delta) antagonist; no agonist activity.
onsetOfEffect: IV ~1–2 min; IM/intranasal ~2–5 min.
halfLife: SHORT (~30–90 min) — often shorter than the opioid being reversed (e.g., methadone, sustained-release or fentanyl analogues), so re-narcotization can occur.
metabolism: Hepatic glucuronidation (rapid).
elimination: Renal (conjugates).
approvedCanada: Emergency treatment of known or suspected opioid overdose (respiratory/CNS depression)
approvedPopulations: All ages, including in pregnancy for maternal overdose (benefit outweighs risk).
startingDose: 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/SC, repeat every 2–3 min as needed, titrated to adequate respiration; intranasal 4 mg per spray, repeat in alternate nostril if no response.
titration: Titrate to restore breathing — not to full alertness — to limit precipitated withdrawal; repeat because it is short-acting.
maxDose: No fixed max — give what is needed to restore ventilation; consider an infusion for long-acting opioid overdoses.
frequencyTiming: Repeat every 2–3 min PRN; continuous infusion for long-acting opioids (methadone, extended-release).
administrationNotes: Take-home naloxone kits (IM or intranasal) for patients/families; always call emergency services — effect may wear off.
boxedWarning: WARNING: SERIOUS AND LIFE-THREATENING RISKS FROM USE OF PENTAZOCINE AND NALOXONE TABLETS Addiction, Abuse, and Misuse Because the use of Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets exposes patients and other users to the risks of opioid addiction, abuse, and misuse, which can lead to overdose and death, assess each patient's risk prior to prescribing and reassess all patients regularly for the development of these behaviors and conditions [ see <WARNINGS ]. Life-Threatening Respiratory Depression Serious, life-threatening, or fatal respiratory depression may occur with use of Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets, especially during initiation or following a dosage increase. To reduce the risk of respiratory depression, proper dosing and titration of Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets are essential [ see <WARNINGS ]. Accidental Ingestion Accidental ingestion of even one dose of Pentazocine and Naloxone …
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Pentazocine and Naloxone Tablets are contraindicated in patients with: Significant respiratory depression [see WARNINGS ] Acute or severe bronchial asthma in an unmonitored setting or in the absence of resuscitative equipment [see WARNINGS ]. Patients with known or suspected gastrointestinal obstruction, including paralytic ileus [see WARNINGS ] Patients with hypersensitivity to either pentazocine, naloxone, or any of the formulation excipients (e.g., anaphylaxis) [see WARNINGS ].
commonSE: Acute opioid withdrawal (agitation, nausea/vomiting, sweating, tachycardia)
seriousAE: Precipitated acute withdrawal in dependence (vomiting → aspiration; agitation)
overdoseLethality: Naloxone itself is essentially non-toxic (no opioid activity). The main harms are precipitated withdrawal in dependent patients and recurrence of overdose (re-narcotization) once it wears off — hence monitoring and repeat dosing.
ongoingMonitoring: Continuous observation for recurrent respiratory depression (re-narcotization), especially after long-acting opioids; monitor airway and vitals.
majorDDI: Reverses/blocks all opioid analgesics and agonist effects (including buprenorphine — may need higher doses given high buprenorphine affinity)
contraindicatedCombos: (None absolute in overdose) — use cautiously in known dependence to avoid severe precipitated withdrawal, but never withhold in life-threatening overdose
pregnancy: Use for maternal opioid overdose — maternal benefit outweighs risk; may precipitate withdrawal/fetal distress, so monitor.
lactation: Compatible in the emergency setting.
pearls: Overdose RESCUE agent — SHORT-acting, so it can wear off before long opioids (methadone, fentanyl, extended-release) → watch for re-narcotization and re-dose or start an infusion.
Precipitates withdrawal in dependence — titrate to restore breathing, not full consciousness.
Available as take-home IM and intranasal kits; always summon emergency help even after a response.
mnemonics: "NaloxONE = number ONE for overdose rescue" — fast and short, so keep watching (re-narcotization).
confusables: Naloxone (SHORT-acting acute overdose rescue) vs naltrexone (LONG-acting maintenance antagonist). Also distinct from the naloxone inside buprenorphine/naloxone (a sublingual abuse deterrent).
classDifferentiators: The acute rescue antagonist: rapid onset, short duration (re-narcotization risk), given emergently — contrast with naltrexone (chronic maintenance blockade) and with the agonists.
examHooks: Why can a patient who overdosed on methadone deteriorate again after an initial naloxone response? (naloxone is shorter-acting than methadone → re-narcotization)
Naloxone vs naltrexone — which is the acute overdose rescue? (naloxone)
counsellingPoints: Keep a naloxone kit accessible and make sure family/friends know how to use it.
Always call emergency services — the effect can wear off and breathing may stop again.
Stay with the person and be ready to give a second dose.

vignettes.stem: A patient who overdosed on methadone is revived with intranasal naloxone and begins breathing normally. Forty-five minutes later they again become unresponsive with a respiratory rate of 5. What best explains this?
options: Re-narcotization — naloxone’s short duration of action wore off before the long-acting methadone
vignettes.answer: Re-narcotization — naloxone’s short duration of action wore off before the long-acting methadone
vignettes.explanation: Naloxone has a short half-life (~30–90 min), typically shorter than methadone. As naloxone is cleared, unopposed methadone at the mu receptor again causes respiratory depression (re-narcotization). Such patients need repeat naloxone or a continuous infusion and prolonged observation.
vignettes.testedFact: Naloxone short duration → re-narcotization with long-acting opioids

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Naltrexone hydrochloride is contraindicated in Patients receiving opioid analgesics. Patients currently dependent on opioids, including those currently maintained on opiate agonists (e.g., methadone ) or partial agonists (e.g., buprenorphine) Patients in acute opioid withdrawal (see WARNINGS ). Any individual who has failed the naloxone challenge test or who has a positive urine screen for opioids. Any individual with a history of sensitivity to naltrexone hydrochloride or any other components of this product.It is not known if there is any cross-sensitivity with naloxone or the phenanthrene containing opioids.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION To reduce the risk of precipitated withdrawal in patients dependent on opioids, or exacerbation of a preexisting subclinical withdrawal syndrome, opioid-dependent patients, including those being treated for alcohol dependence, should be opioid-free (including tramadol) before starting naltrexone hydrochloride treatment. An opioid-free interval of a minimum of 7 to 10 days is recommended for patients previously dependent on short-acting opioids. Switching from Buprenorphine, Buprenorphine/Naloxone, or Methadone There are no systematically collected data that specifically address the switch from buprenorphine or methadone to naltrexone hydrochloride; however, review …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Vulnerability to Opioid Overdose After opioid detoxification, patients are likely to have reduced tolerance to opioids. As the blockade of exogenous opioids provided by naltrexone hydrochloride wanes and eventually dissipates completely, patients who have been treated with naltrexone hydrochloride may respond to lower doses of opioids than previously used, just as they would shortly after completing detoxification. This could result in potentially life-threatening opioid intoxication (respiratory compromise or arrest, circulatory collapse, etc.) if the patient uses previously tolerated doses of opioids. Cases of opioid overdose with fatal outcomes have been reported in patients …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE There is limited clinical experience with naltrexone hydrochloride overdosage in humans. In one study, subjects who received 800 mg daily naltrexone hydrochloride for up to one week showed no evidence of toxicity. In the mouse, rat and guinea pig, the oral LD 50s were 1,100 to 1,550 mg/kg; 1,450 mg/kg; and 1,490 mg/kg; respectively. High doses of naltrexone hydrochloride (generally ≥ 1,000 mg/kg) produced salivation, depression/reduced activity, tremors, and convulsions. Mortalities in animals due to high-dose naltrexone hydrochloride administration usually were due to clonic-tonic convulsions and/or respiratory failure. Treatment of Overdosage In view of the lack of actual …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions Studies to evaluate possible interactions between naltrexone hydrochloride and drugs other than opiates have not been performed. Consequently, caution is advised if the concomitant administration of naltrexone hydrochloride and other drugs is required. The safety and efficacy of concomitant use of naltrexone hydrochloride and disulfiram is unknown, and the concomitant use of two potentially hepatotoxic medications is not ordinarily recommended unless the probable benefits outweigh the known risks. Lethargy and somnolence have been reported following doses of naltrexone hydrochloride and thioridazine. Patients taking naltrexone hydrochloride may not benefit from opioid …

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy:Teratogenic Effects Pregnancy Category C:- Naltrexone has been shown to increase the incidence of early fetal loss when given to rats at doses ≥ 30 mg/kg/day (180 mg/m 2 /day; 5 times the recommended therapeutic dose, based on body surface area) and to rabbits at oral doses ≥ 60 mg/kg/day (720 mg/m 2 /day; 18 times the recommended therapeutic dose, based on body surface area). There was no evidence of teratogenicity when naltrexone was administered orally to rats and rabbits during the period of major organogenesis at doses up to 200 mg/kg/day (32 and 65 times the recommended therapeutic dose, respectively, based on body surface area). Rats do not form appreciable quantities of …

moa: Opioid receptor ANTAGONIST — competitively blocks mu (and kappa/delta) receptors. In alcohol use disorder it blunts the endogenous-opioid–mediated reward of drinking, reducing craving and heavy-drinking days.
receptorProfile: Pure competitive mu antagonist; no agonist activity.
onsetOfEffect: Opioid blockade within ~1 h of oral dosing; AUD craving benefit over days–weeks.
halfLife: Naltrexone ~4 h; active metabolite 6-β-naltrexol ~13 h. IM depot (Vivitrol) provides ~4 weeks of blockade.
metabolism: Hepatic (non-CYP; dihydrodiol dehydrogenase) → 6-β-naltrexol (active).
elimination: Renal (metabolites).
foodEffect: May be taken with food to reduce nausea.
approvedCanada: Opioid use disorder (relapse prevention in the detoxified/abstinent patient)
startingDose: Oral 50 mg PO daily. For OUD the patient MUST be opioid-free ~7–10 days first (optionally confirmed by naloxone challenge) or blockade precipitates withdrawal. IM: 380 mg gluteal every 4 weeks.
titration: Usually fixed dosing (50 mg/day oral; some use 100 mg on alternate schedules).
maxDose: Oral ~100 mg/day; IM 380 mg q4 weeks.
frequencyTiming: Once daily (oral) or once monthly (IM depot).
renalHepaticAdjustment: Avoid in acute hepatitis or hepatic failure; caution with elevated transaminases.
startStopCrossTaper: For OUD, confirm an opioid-free window before the first dose; there is no cross-taper — it is the opposite of an agonist.
administrationNotes: IM depot improves adherence; carry a card noting opioid blockade for emergencies.
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Naltrexone hydrochloride is contraindicated in Patients receiving opioid analgesics. Patients currently dependent on opioids, including those currently maintained on opiate agonists (e.g., methadone ) or partial agonists (e.g., buprenorphine) Patients in acute opioid withdrawal (see WARNINGS ). Any individual who has failed the naloxone challenge test or who has a positive urine screen for opioids. Any individual with a history of sensitivity to naltrexone hydrochloride or any other components of this product.It is not known if there is any cross-sensitivity with naloxone or the phenanthrene containing opioids.
seriousAE: Overdose on relapse due to lost tolerance
overdoseLethality: Naltrexone itself has low intrinsic toxicity. The lethal danger is indirect: blockade lowers opioid tolerance, so relapse (or attempts to override the block with high-dose opioids) can cause fatal overdose.
baselineWorkup: Baseline LFTs; confirm opioid-free status (history ± urine ± naloxone challenge) before starting for OUD
ongoingMonitoring: Periodic LFTs; monitor for relapse risk; ensure a plan for acute pain (opioids will be blocked).
majorDDI: Opioids of any kind — analgesia is BLOCKED (plan non-opioid/regional analgesia for pain or surgery)
Yohimbine/other agents may worsen withdrawal in dependent patients
contraindicatedCombos: Current opioid use or opioid dependence (precipitates withdrawal)
pregnancy: Data more limited than agonist OAT; agonist therapy is usually preferred in pregnancy. Individualize. Tie to CRISM/CANMAT guidance.
lactation: Limited data; use with caution.
renalHepatic: Hepatotoxic potential — avoid in acute hepatitis/liver failure; monitor LFTs.
pearls: ANTAGONIST — the patient must be opioid-free ~7–10 days before starting for OUD, or you precipitate withdrawal.
Dual use: reduces craving/heavy drinking in alcohol use disorder AND prevents relapse in detoxified OUD.
Blocks opioid analgesia — plan ahead for surgery/acute pain (non-opioid or regional).
Hepatotoxic — check baseline and periodic LFTs; IM depot (Vivitrol) aids adherence.
mnemonics: "NaltrexONE = the long ONE that blocks" — maintenance antagonist (daily pill / monthly shot), for the long haul.
confusables: Naltrexone (long-acting maintenance antagonist; AUD + OUD relapse prevention; hepatic) vs naloxone (short-acting acute overdose RESCUE). Same "opioid antagonist" family, opposite clinical use.
classDifferentiators: Maintenance opioid antagonist (not an agonist) — needs an opioid-free window, treats alcohol AND opioid use disorder, and is hepatotoxic; contrast with naloxone (acute rescue) and with the agonists (methadone/buprenorphine).
examHooks: Why must a patient be opioid-free before starting naltrexone for OUD? (antagonist precipitates withdrawal)
Which agent treats BOTH alcohol and opioid use disorder? (naltrexone)
counsellingPoints: Do not use any opioids — they will not work and could be dangerous.
Tell any surgeon/anaesthetist you are on naltrexone (opioid analgesia is blocked).
Your opioid tolerance drops — relapse carries a high overdose risk.
Report yellowing skin, dark urine, or right-upper-quadrant pain (liver).

vignettes.stem: A patient who used heroin two days ago requests oral naltrexone for opioid use disorder. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Give 50 mg naltrexone now to block further use
Do NOT start yet — confirm an opioid-free interval of ~7–10 days (± naloxone challenge) to avoid precipitated withdrawal
vignettes.answer: Do NOT start yet — confirm an opioid-free interval of ~7–10 days (± naloxone challenge) to avoid precipitated withdrawal
vignettes.explanation: Naltrexone is a full opioid antagonist. Given while opioids remain on board it abruptly displaces them and precipitates withdrawal. Guidelines require an opioid-free window (roughly 7–10 days, shorter for short-acting opioids) before induction, sometimes verified with a naloxone challenge.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: SERIOUS NEUROPSYCHIATRIC EVENTS Serious neuropsychiatric events including, but not limited to, depression, suicidal ideation, suicide attempt, and completed suicide have been reported in patients taking CHANTIX. Some reported cases may have been complicated by the symptoms of nicotine withdrawal in patients who stopped smoking. Depressed mood may be a symptom of nicotine withdrawal. Depression, rarely including suicidal ideation, has been reported in smokers undergoing a smoking cessation attempt without medication. However, some of these symptoms have occurred in patients taking CHANTIX who continued to smoke. All patients being treated with CHANTIX should be observed for neuropsychiatric symptoms including changes in behavior, hostility, agitation, depressed mood, and suicide-related events, including ideation, behavior, and attempted suicide. These symptoms, as well as worsening of pre-existing psychiatric illness and completed suicide, have been reported in some patients attempting to quit smoking while taking CHANTIX in the postmarketing experience. When symptoms were reported, most were during CHANTIX treatment, but some were following discontinuation of CHANTIX therapy. These events have occurred in patients with and without pre-existing psychiatric disease. Patients with serious psychiatric illness such as schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and major depressive …

CHANTIX should be used cautiously in patients with a history of seizures or other factors that can lower the seizure threshold. ( 5.2 ) • Interaction with alcohol: Increased effects of alcohol have been reported. Instruct patients to reduce the amount of alcohol they consume until they know whether CHANTIX affects them. ( 5.3 ) • Accidental injury: Accidental injuries (e.g., traffic accidents) have been reported. Instruct patients to use caution driving or operating machinery until they know how CHANTIX may affect them. ( 5.4 ) • Cardiovascular events: A meta-analysis of 15 …

Varenicline has been shown to be dialyzed in patients with end stage renal disease [see Clinical Pharmacology (12.3) ], however, there is no experience in dialysis following overdose.

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Based on varenicline characteristics and clinical experience to date, CHANTIX has no clinically meaningful pharmacokinetic drug interactions [see Clinical Pharmacology (12.3) ]. • Other smoking cessation therapies: Safety and efficacy in combination with other smoking cessation therapies has not been established. Coadministration of varenicline and transdermal nicotine resulted in a high rate of discontinuation due to adverse events. ( 7.1 ) • Effect of smoking cessation: Pharmacokinetics or pharmacodynamics of certain drugs may be altered due to smoking cessation with CHANTIX, necessitating dose adjustment. pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Pregnancy Category C. There are no adequate and well-controlled studies of CHANTIX use in pregnant women. In animal studies, CHANTIX caused decreased fetal weights, increased auditory startle response, and decreased fertility in offspring. CHANTIX should be used during pregnancy only if the potential benefit justifies the potential risk to the fetus. In reproductive and developmental toxicity studies, pregnant rats and rabbits received varenicline succinate during organogenesis at oral doses up to 15 and 30 mg/kg/day, respectively. These exposures were 36 (rats) and 50 (rabbits) times the human exposure (based on AUC) at the maximum recommended human dose (MRHD) of 1 mg twice …

moa: Partial agonist at the α4β2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor — partial stimulation relieves craving/withdrawal while blocking nicotine binding reduces the reward of smoking.
receptorProfile: High-affinity α4β2 nicotinic partial agonist (also some α7/5-HT3 activity, contributing to nausea).
onsetOfEffect: Start ~1 week before the quit date; craving/withdrawal relief over the first week or two.
metabolism: Minimal metabolism — excreted largely UNCHANGED by the kidneys.
elimination: Renal (unchanged) — the rate-limiting route.
foodEffect: Take with food and a full glass of water to reduce nausea.
startingDose: Begin 1 week before the target quit date. Days 1–3: 0.5 mg once daily; days 4–7: 0.5 mg twice daily; day 8 onward: 1 mg twice daily.
titration: Fixed titration to 1 mg BID over the first week; reduce in severe renal impairment.
maxDose: 1 mg twice daily (2 mg/day).
frequencyTiming: Twice daily after titration; take with food/water. Typical course 12 weeks (may extend 12 more).
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce dose in severe renal impairment (CrCl <30: max 0.5 mg BID); no hepatic adjustment needed.
administrationNotes: Some patients set the quit date up to ~5 weeks in (flexible-quit) or reduce-to-quit over up to 12 weeks.
boxedWarning: WARNING: SERIOUS NEUROPSYCHIATRIC EVENTS Serious neuropsychiatric events including, but not limited to, depression, suicidal ideation, suicide attempt, and completed suicide have been reported in patients taking CHANTIX. Some reported cases may have been complicated by the symptoms of nicotine withdrawal in patients who stopped smoking. Depressed mood may be a symptom of nicotine withdrawal. Depression, rarely including suicidal ideation, has been reported in smokers undergoing a smoking cessation attempt without medication. However, some of these symptoms have occurred in patients taking CHANTIX who continued to smoke. All patients being treated with CHANTIX should be observed for neuropsychiatric symptoms including changes in behavior, hostility, agitation, depressed mood, and suicide-related events, including ideation, behavior, and attempted suicide. These symptoms, as well as …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS CHANTIX is contraindicated in patients with a known history of serious hypersensitivity reactions or skin reactions to CHANTIX. History of serious hypersensitivity or skin reactions to CHANTIX. ( 4 )
seriousAE: Neuropsychiatric symptoms (mood/behaviour changes, agitation) — monitor, esp. with psychiatric history
overdoseLethality: Low intrinsic toxicity in overdose; supportive care.
ongoingMonitoring: Monitor mood/behaviour and any new neuropsychiatric symptoms; assess quit progress; renal function if impaired.
majorDDI: Smoking cessation itself raises levels of CYP1A2 substrates (e.g., clozapine, theophylline) once tobacco is stopped — an indirect effect
pregnancy: Limited data; behavioural support and, if needed, nicotine replacement are generally preferred in pregnancy — individualize.
lactation: Limited data; caution.
renalHepatic: Renally cleared → reduce dose in severe renal impairment; hepatically fine.
pearls: α4β2 nicotinic PARTIAL agonist — the most effective single pharmacotherapy for smoking cessation.
Nausea and vivid/abnormal dreams are the hallmark side effects; start ~1 week before the quit date.
Renally cleared with few drug interactions — adjust only in severe renal impairment.
EAGLES trial: neuropsychiatric risk was lower than the earlier boxed-warning concerns suggested, but still monitor mood/behaviour.
mnemonics: "Varenicline — Vivid dreams + Vomiting (nausea)" hallmark side effects; a partial agonist that keeps you off the nicotine.
confusables: Varenicline (α4β2 nicotinic partial agonist) vs bupropion (NDRI + nicotinic antagonist) — both aid smoking cessation but by different mechanisms.
classDifferentiators: Nicotinic (not opioid or alcohol) target — a partial agonist for smoking cessation; renally cleared, interaction-light, with neuropsychiatric monitoring rather than QT/hepatotoxicity concerns.
examHooks: Most effective monotherapy for smoking cessation? (varenicline)
At which receptor is varenicline a partial agonist? (α4β2 nicotinic)
counsellingPoints: Keep smoking for the first week — set your quit date about 8 days in.
Take with food and water to limit nausea.
Vivid dreams are common and usually harmless.
Report any new depression, agitation, or unusual thoughts/behaviour.

vignettes.stem: A motivated smoker wants the most effective single medication to quit. Which agent, and by what mechanism, best fits?
options: Varenicline — α4β2 nicotinic partial agonist that reduces craving while blocking nicotine reward
vignettes.answer: Varenicline — α4β2 nicotinic partial agonist that reduces craving while blocking nicotine reward
vignettes.explanation: Varenicline is a partial agonist at the α4β2 nicotinic receptor: partial stimulation relieves withdrawal/craving, while occupying the receptor blunts the reinforcing effect of smoked nicotine. It is the most effective single pharmacotherapy for cessation. (Bupropion is an NDRI/nicotinic antagonist, not an SSRI.)
vignettes.testedFact: Varenicline mechanism / efficacy in smoking cessation

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Because strategies for the management of overdose are continually evolving, it is advisable to contact a Poison Control Center to determine the latest recommendations for the management of an overdose of any drug. As in any case of overdose, general supportive measures should be utilized. Overdosage with cholinesterase inhibitors can result in cholinergic crisis characterized by severe nausea, vomiting, salivation, sweating, bradycardia, hypotension, respiratory depression, collapse, and convulsions. Increasing muscle weakness is a possibility and may result in death if respiratory muscles are involved. Tertiary anticholinergics such as atropine may be used as an antidote for …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary There are no adequate data on the developmental risks associated with the use of donepezil hydrochloride in pregnant women. In animal studies, developmental toxicity was not observed when donepezil was administered to pregnant rats and rabbits during organogenesis, but administration to rats during the latter part of pregnancy and throughout lactation resulted in increased stillbirths and decreased offspring survival at clinically relevant doses [ see Data ]. In the U.S. general population, the estimated background risks of major birth defects and miscarriage in clinically recognized pregnancies are 2% to 4% and 15% to 20%, respectively. The background risks of …

moa: Reversible, centrally selective acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitor → less breakdown of synaptic acetylcholine → enhanced cholinergic transmission in cortex/hippocampus. Symptomatic only; does not modify disease progression.
receptorProfile: Selective for AChE (piperidine); little butyrylcholinesterase or peripheral activity relative to central effect.
onsetOfEffect: Cognitive/functional benefit assessed over weeks; modest symptomatic effect, not disease-modifying.
halfLife: ~70 hours — the longest of the AChE inhibitors, allowing convenient once-daily dosing.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP2D6 and CYP3A4, plus glucuronidation. Not a significant CYP inhibitor itself; strong 2D6/3A4 inhibitors raise its levels.
elimination: Renal (metabolites and some unchanged) and hepatic.
foodEffect: May be taken with or without food; bedtime dosing traditional.
approvedPopulations: Adults with mild, moderate, and severe Alzheimer disease.
startingDose: 5 mg PO once daily (often at bedtime).
titration: May increase to 10 mg/day after 4–6 weeks; a 23 mg/day formulation exists for moderate–severe disease (US).
maxDose: 10 mg/day (mild–moderate); 23 mg/day for moderate–severe (US high-dose tablet).
frequencyTiming: Once daily. If vivid dreams/nightmares occur with bedtime dosing, switch to the morning.
startStopCrossTaper: When switching between cholinesterase inhibitors, allow a washout appropriate to half-life; abrupt discontinuation can cause noticeable cognitive/behavioural decline.
administrationNotes: Longest half-life of the class → simplest (once-daily) dosing and the most forgiving of missed doses.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Known hypersensitivity to donepezil hydrochloride or to piperidine derivatives ( 4 ) Donepezil hydrochloride is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to donepezil hydrochloride or to piperidine derivatives.
seriousAE: GI bleeding (increased gastric acid — peptic ulcer risk)
syndromes: Cholinergic excess (nausea/vomiting/diarrhea, bradycardia, salivation, urinary urgency)
overdoseLethality: Overdose produces a cholinergic crisis (severe nausea/vomiting, salivation, sweating, bradycardia, hypotension, seizures, respiratory depression); atropine is the antidote.
baselineWorkup: Assess pulse/history of syncope or conduction disease before starting
ongoingMonitoring: Heart rate and syncope symptoms; weight and GI tolerance; cognitive/functional response to justify continuation.
majorDDI: Anticholinergics (oxybutynin, tolterodine, TCAs, first-gen antihistamines, benztropine) — OPPOSE the drug and worsen cognition
Beta-blockers, digoxin, non-dihydropyridine CCBs — additive bradycardia/AV block
Other cholinergic/cholinomimetic agents — additive cholinergic toxicity
NSAIDs — additive GI-bleed risk (increased gastric acid)
CYP2D6/3A4 inhibitors (paroxetine, fluoxetine, ketoconazole) raise donepezil levels
contraindicatedCombos: Strong anticholinergics (pharmacodynamically self-defeating and worsen cognition)
pregnancy: Rarely relevant (dementia population); limited data — use only if clearly needed.
geriatric: The target population — watch for bradycardia, syncope and falls, anorexia/weight loss, and vivid dreams; review the full medication list for anticholinergic burden.
cardiac: Vagotonic effect → bradycardia and syncope; caution with sick sinus syndrome or supraventricular conduction defects and with other rate-slowing drugs. QT prolongation has been reported.
pearls: Longest half-life AChE inhibitor → once daily and forgiving of a missed dose.
Bedtime dosing can cause vivid dreams/nightmares — move to the morning if bothersome.
Symptomatic benefit only — it does not stop Alzheimer progression; set expectations with families.
Unexplained syncope in a treated patient warrants an ECG for bradycardia/heart block before attributing it to the dementia itself.
mnemonics: "doneP EZ-il = EASY once-daily" (longest half-life). Cholinergic excess = SLUDGE/DUMBELS — think the vagotonic Bradycardia when you dose it.
confusables: Donepezil (AChE inhibitor) vs memantine (NMDA antagonist) — donepezil for mild–severe and causes cholinergic/GI/brady effects; memantine for moderate–severe with no cholinergic effects.
Donepezil vs galantamine — galantamine adds nicotinic modulation and is mild–moderate only.
classDifferentiators: The default first-line AChE inhibitor across ALL stages of Alzheimer (mild through severe); longest half-life, purely AChE-selective, once daily.
examHooks: Which anti-dementia drug is approved across mild-to-severe Alzheimer and has the longest half-life? (donepezil)
A demented patient on donepezil develops syncope — next investigation? (ECG for bradycardia/AV block)
counsellingPoints: Nausea/diarrhea and reduced appetite are common early and usually settle — take with food.
If you have vivid dreams, we can move the dose to the morning.
It helps symptoms modestly but does not cure or stop the disease.
Report fainting, dizziness or a very slow pulse.

vignettes.stem: A 78-year-old woman with moderate Alzheimer disease on donepezil is prescribed oxybutynin by another clinic for urinary urgency. Over the next few weeks her family reports worse confusion. What is the BEST explanation?
options: Donepezil dose is too low and should be increased
The anticholinergic oxybutynin pharmacodynamically opposes donepezil and worsens cognition
This is expected progression of Alzheimer disease
Oxybutynin has raised donepezil levels via CYP inhibition
vignettes.answer: The anticholinergic oxybutynin pharmacodynamically opposes donepezil and worsens cognition
vignettes.explanation: Cholinesterase inhibitors raise acetylcholine; anticholinergics block muscarinic receptors — directly opposing actions. Co-prescribing them is a classic error that blunts benefit and can worsen cognition. Use a non-anticholinergic option (e.g., mirabegron) for the bladder.
vignettes.testedFact: AChE inhibitor + anticholinergic = opposing actions (prescribing error)

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Because strategies for the management of overdose are continually evolving, it is advisable to contact a poison control center to determine the latest recommendations for the management of an overdose of any drug. As in any case of overdose, general supportive measures should be utilized. Signs and symptoms of significant overdosing of galantamine are predicted to be similar to those of overdosing of other cholinomimetics. These effects generally involve the central nervous system, the parasympathetic nervous system, and the neuromuscular junction. In addition to muscle weakness or fasciculations, some or all of the following signs of cholinergic crisis may develop: severe …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary There are no adequate data on the developmental risk associated with the use of galantamine hydrobromide extended-release capsules in pregnant women. In studies conducted in animals, administration of galantamine during pregnancy resulted in developmental toxicity (increased incidence of morphological abnormalities and decreased growth in offspring) at doses similar to or greater than those used clinically (see Data). In the U.S. general population, the estimated background risk of major birth defects and miscarriage in clinically recognized pregnancies is 2-4% and 15-20%, respectively. The background risk of major birth defects and miscarriage for the indicated …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (12 MG, 8 MG, 16 MG, 4 MG, 24 MG)
Capsule (Extended-Release) (12 MG, 8 MG, 16 MG, 4 MG, 24 MG)
moa: Competitive, reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor PLUS allosteric potentiation of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors — a dual cholinergic mechanism that further enhances acetylcholine signalling. Symptomatic only.
receptorProfile: AChE inhibition combined with positive allosteric modulation of nicotinic receptors (its distinguishing pharmacology).
onsetOfEffect: Symptomatic benefit assessed over weeks; not disease-modifying.
metabolism: Hepatic CYP2D6 and CYP3A4, with renal excretion of drug/metabolites. Strong 2D6/3A4 inhibitors raise its levels.
elimination: Renal (partly unchanged) and hepatic.
foodEffect: Take WITH food (and maintain hydration) to reduce nausea and slow absorption.
startingDose: Immediate-release 4 mg PO BID; extended-release 8 mg PO once daily in the morning.
titration: Increase to 8 mg BID (ER 16 mg/day) after ≥4 weeks, then to 12 mg BID (ER 24 mg/day) after a further ≥4 weeks as tolerated.
maxDose: 24 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: IR twice daily with food; ER once daily in the morning with food.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Avoid in severe renal impairment (CrCl <9 mL/min) and severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C); reduce dose (≤16 mg/day) in moderate hepatic impairment.
administrationNotes: Maintain fluid intake during titration; slower titration reduces GI dropout.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Galantamine hydrobromide extended-release capsules are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to galantamine hydrobromide or to any excipients used in the formulation. Known hypersensitivity to galantamine hydrobromide or any excipients ( 4 )
seriousAE: Serious skin reactions (Stevens–Johnson syndrome, acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis — rare)
Increased mortality signal when used for mild cognitive impairment (not an approved use)
overdoseLethality: Cholinergic crisis in overdose (severe GI symptoms, bradycardia, hypotension, seizures, muscle weakness/respiratory compromise); atropine is the antidote.
baselineWorkup: Pulse/history of syncope or conduction disease
ongoingMonitoring: Heart rate and syncope; weight/GI tolerance; skin (discontinue at first sign of serious rash).
majorDDI: Anticholinergics — OPPOSE the drug and reduce efficacy
CYP2D6/3A4 inhibitors (paroxetine, fluoxetine, ketoconazole, erythromycin) raise galantamine levels
pregnancy: Rarely relevant (dementia population); limited data — use only if clearly needed.
geriatric: Titrate slowly with food; respect renal/hepatic dose ceilings; watch bradycardia, weight loss and falls.
renalHepatic: Contraindicated in severe renal or severe hepatic impairment; cap at 16 mg/day in moderate hepatic impairment.
cardiac: Vagotonic → bradycardia/syncope; caution with sick sinus syndrome, conduction defects and other rate-slowing drugs.
pearls: Dual mechanism: AChE inhibition PLUS nicotinic receptor allosteric potentiation.
Approved for mild–moderate Alzheimer only (not severe disease).
Respect renal/hepatic limits — avoid in severe impairment; take with food and fluids to limit nausea.
An excess-mortality signal was seen in mild-cognitive-impairment trials — not an approved indication.
mnemonics: "GalantaMINE mines the NICOTINIC receptor too" — the dual AChE + nicotinic-modulator mechanism.
confusables: Galantamine vs donepezil — galantamine adds nicotinic modulation, is twice-daily (or ER once-daily), and is mild–moderate only; donepezil is AChE-selective, once daily, all stages.
Galantamine vs guanfacine — similar-sounding but unrelated (guanfacine is an α2 agonist for ADHD).
classDifferentiators: The AChE inhibitor with an ADDED nicotinic-receptor allosteric mechanism; restricted to mild–moderate Alzheimer; hard renal/hepatic dosing limits.
examHooks: Which cholinesterase inhibitor also allosterically modulates nicotinic receptors? (galantamine)
Which AChE inhibitor is contraindicated in severe renal/hepatic impairment? (galantamine)
counsellingPoints: Take with meals and plenty of fluids; nausea is common when increasing the dose.
Report any spreading rash or blistering immediately.
Report fainting or a very slow pulse.
It eases symptoms modestly but does not stop the disease.

vignettes.stem: You are choosing a cholinesterase inhibitor for a patient with mild Alzheimer disease and severe chronic kidney disease (eGFR 8 mL/min). Which agent is specifically CONTRAINDICATED in this setting?
vignettes.explanation: Galantamine is contraindicated in severe renal (CrCl <9 mL/min) and severe hepatic impairment because clearance falls and toxicity rises. Memantine is not a cholinesterase inhibitor (and is dose-reduced, not contraindicated, in renal impairment).
vignettes.testedFact: Galantamine contraindicated in severe renal/hepatic impairment

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Memantine hydrochloride tablets are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to memantine hydrochloride or to any excipients used in the formulation. Memantine hydrochloride tablets are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to memantine hydrochloride or to any excipients used in the formulation. (4)

startingDose.sourceText: 2 DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION The recommended starting dose of memantine hydrochloride tablets is 5 mg once daily. The dose should be increased in 5 mg increments to 10 mg/day (5 mg twice daily), 15 mg/day (5 mg and 10 mg as separate doses), and 20 mg/day (10 mg twice daily). The minimum recommended interval between dose increases is one week. The dosage shown to be effective in controlled clinical trials is 20 mg/day. Memantine hydrochloride tablets can be taken with or without food. If a patient misses a single dose of memantine hydrochloride tablets, that patient should not double up on the next dose. The next dose should be taken as scheduled. If a patient fails to take memantine …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Signs and symptoms most often accompanying memantine overdosage in clinical trials and from worldwide marketing experience, alone or in combination with other drugs and/or alcohol, include agitation, asthenia, bradycardia, confusion, coma, dizziness, ECG changes, increased blood pressure, lethargy, loss of consciousness, psychosis, restlessness, slowed movement, somnolence, stupor, unsteady gait, visual hallucinations, vertigo, vomiting, and weakness. The largest known ingestion of memantine worldwide was 2 grams in a patient who took memantine in conjunction with unspecified antidiabetic medications. The patient experienced coma, diplopia, and agitation, but subsequently …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS 7.1 Drugs that Make the Urine Alkaline The clearance of memantine was reduced by about 80% under alkaline urine conditions at pH 8. Therefore, alterations of urine pH towards the alkaline condition may lead to an accumulation of the drug with a possible increase in adverse effects. Urine pH is altered by diet, drugs (e.g. carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, sodium bicarbonate) and clinical state of the patient (e.g. renal tubular acidosis or severe infections of the urinary tract). Hence, memantine should be used with caution under these conditions. 7.2 Use with Other N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) Antagonists The combined use of memantine hydrochloride with other NMDA …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary There are no adequate data on the developmental risk associated with the use of memantine hydrochloride in pregnant women. Adverse developmental effects (decreased body weight, and skeletal ossification) were observed in the offspring of rats administered memantine during pregnancy at doses associated with minimal maternal toxicity. These doses are higher than those used in humans at the maximum recommended daily dose of memantine hydrochloride [see Data] . In the U.S. general population, the estimated background risk of major birth defects and miscarriage in clinically recognized pregnancies is 2% to 4% and 15% to 20%, respectively. The background risk of major …

moa: Uncompetitive, low-to-moderate-affinity, open-channel NMDA-receptor antagonist. It blocks pathological tonic glutamate-driven Ca²⁺ influx (excitotoxicity) while its voltage-dependence and fast off-rate spare physiological synaptic transmission. A mechanism entirely DISTINCT from the cholinesterase inhibitors.
receptorProfile: NMDA-receptor channel blocker; also weak 5-HT3 and nicotinic antagonism. No cholinergic (AChE) activity — hence no cholinergic GI/bradycardic side-effect profile.
onsetOfEffect: Symptomatic benefit assessed over weeks; not disease-modifying.
metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism — largely excreted UNCHANGED by the kidney (partly via active tubular secretion). Renal (not CYP/hepatic) handling is the key PK point. Alkaline urine markedly reduces clearance.
elimination: Renal; ~48% excreted unchanged. Clearance falls in renal impairment and with urinary alkalinisation.
foodEffect: May be taken with or without food.
offLabel: Adjunct to a cholinesterase inhibitor in moderate–severe disease
startingDose: 5 mg PO once daily (immediate-release); extended-release starts at 7 mg once daily.
titration: Increase by 5 mg/week to a target of 10 mg PO BID (IR) or 28 mg once daily (ER).
maxDose: 20 mg/day (immediate-release, 10 mg BID); 28 mg/day (extended-release).
frequencyTiming: IR twice daily once above 5 mg; ER once daily.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce the target dose in severe renal impairment (CrCl ~5–29 mL/min) to 5 mg BID (10 mg/day, or ER 14 mg/day). No cholinergic constraints; hepatic adjustment not routinely required.
startStopCrossTaper: Can be combined with, or added on top of, an AChE inhibitor without a washout (different mechanism, non-overlapping toxicity).
administrationNotes: Titrate weekly to limit dizziness/confusion; the long half-life makes dosing forgiving.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Memantine hydrochloride tablets are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to memantine hydrochloride or to any excipients used in the formulation. Memantine hydrochloride tablets are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to memantine hydrochloride or to any excipients used in the formulation. (4)
syndromes: CNS effects (dizziness, confusion, somnolence) — NOT cholinergic
No bradycardia/GI cholinergic syndrome (contrast with AChE inhibitors)
overdoseLethality: Generally low toxicity; large ingestions have caused restlessness, psychosis, somnolence, and rarely seizures — largely supportive care.
ongoingMonitoring: Renal function (dose depends on it); cognitive/behavioural response; blood pressure. No routine cardiac or GI cholinergic monitoring needed.
majorDDI: Other NMDA antagonists (amantadine, ketamine, dextromethorphan) — avoid combination (additive CNS effects)
Urinary alkalinisers (carbonic anhydrase inhibitors e.g. acetazolamide, sodium bicarbonate) — reduce renal clearance → higher memantine levels
Drugs cleared by renal cationic transport (e.g., cimetidine, hydrochlorothiazide/triamterene) — potential competition
pregnancy: Rarely relevant (dementia population); limited data — use only if clearly needed.
geriatric: Well tolerated; adjust for renal function; a good choice when cholinergic GI/bradycardic effects are problematic. Add to an AChE inhibitor for moderate–severe disease.
renalHepatic: Renally cleared — reduce dose in severe renal impairment; urinary alkalinisation raises levels.
cardiac: No vagotonic bradycardia (not a cholinergic drug); can mildly raise blood pressure. A safer cardiac profile than the cholinesterase inhibitors in patients with conduction disease.
pearls: Different mechanism (NMDA antagonist) → can be COMBINED with an AChE inhibitor in moderate–severe Alzheimer.
Renally excreted, largely UNCHANGED — dose-reduce in severe renal impairment (contrast the hepatically-metabolised AChE inhibitors).
Well tolerated: no cholinergic GI/bradycardic effects; main issues are dizziness, constipation and confusion.
Alkaline urine (e.g., carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, sodium bicarbonate) reduces clearance and raises levels.
Reserved for MODERATE–SEVERE disease — not first-line for mild Alzheimer (unlike the AChE inhibitors).
mnemonics: "meMANtine = the odd MAN out" — NMDA (not cholinergic), moderate–severe, renally cleared, combines with an AChE-I.
confusables: Memantine (NMDA antagonist, moderate–severe, renal, well tolerated) vs the cholinesterase inhibitors (cholinergic, GI/brady effects, hepatic).
Memantine vs amantadine — both NMDA-active; amantadine is antiviral/anti-parkinsonian and should not be co-prescribed with memantine.
classDifferentiators: The ONLY NMDA-receptor antagonist here — distinct mechanism, distinct side effects (no cholinergic GI/brady), renally cleared, indicated for moderate–severe Alzheimer, and safely combined with a cholinesterase inhibitor.
examHooks: Which anti-dementia drug has a non-cholinergic (NMDA) mechanism and is added to an AChE inhibitor in moderate–severe disease? (memantine)
Which anti-dementia drug is renally cleared and dose-reduced in severe renal impairment? (memantine)
counsellingPoints: It works differently from the other dementia drugs and can be taken alongside them.
Common effects are dizziness, constipation or mild confusion — tell us if they persist.
It does not cause the nausea/slow-pulse problems of the other class.
Dose depends on kidney function — let us know of any kidney problems.

vignettes.stem: A patient with moderate-to-severe Alzheimer disease is already stable on donepezil but continues to decline. Which addition is mechanistically rational and safe to combine?
options: Add galantamine for a second cholinesterase inhibitor
Add oxybutynin for coexisting urinary urgency
Add rivastigmine patch on top of donepezil
vignettes.explanation: Memantine has a distinct (glutamatergic NMDA) mechanism and non-overlapping toxicity, so it is combined with a cholinesterase inhibitor in moderate–severe disease. Stacking two cholinesterase inhibitors is not done, and an anticholinergic (oxybutynin) would oppose donepezil.
vignettes.testedFact: Memantine + AChE inhibitor combination in moderate–severe Alzheimer

(5.1) Discontinue rivastigmine in case of disseminated allergic dermatitis, which may occur after oral or transdermal administration (4, 5.2). In patients with suspected allergic contact dermatitis after transdermal rivastigmine use, switch to oral rivastigmine only after negative allergy testing. 5.1 Gastrointestinal Adverse Reactions Rivastigmine tartrate can cause gastrointestinal …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Because strategies for the management of overdose are continually evolving, it is advisable to contact a Poison Control Center to determine the latest recommendations for the management of an overdose of any drug. As rivastigmine has a short plasma half-life of about 1 hour and a moderate duration of acetylcholinesterase inhibition of 8 to 10 hours, it is recommended that in cases of asymptomatic overdoses, no further dose of rivastigmine tartrate capsules should be administered for the next 24 hours. As in any case of overdose, general supportive measures should be utilized. Overdosage with cholinesterase inhibitors can result in cholinergic crisis characterized by severe …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Concomitant use with metoclopramide, beta-blockers, or cholinomimetic and anticholinergic drugs is not recommended (7.1, 7.2, 7.3) 7.1 Metoclopramide Due to the risk of additive extrapyramidal adverse reactions, the concomitant use of metoclopramide and rivastigmine tartrate is not recommended. 7.2 Cholinomimetic and Anticholinergic Medications Rivastigmine tartrate may increase the cholinergic effects of other cholinomimetic medications and may also interfere with the activity of anticholinergic medications (e.g., oxybutynin, tolterodine). Concomitant use of rivastigmine tartrate with medications having these pharmacologic effects is not recommended unless deemed …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary There are no adequate data on the developmental risks associated with the use of rivastigmine tartrate in pregnant women. In animals, no adverse effects on embryo-fetal development were observed at oral doses 2 to 4 times the maximum recommended human dose (MRHD) (see Data) . The background risk of major birth defects and miscarriage for the indicated population is unknown. In the U.S. general population, the estimated background risk of major birth defects and miscarriage in clinically recognized pregnancies is 2% to 4% and 15% to 20%, respectively. formulationsCanada: Capsule (1.5 MG, 6 MG, 3 MG, 6.0 MG, 4.6 MG, 4.5 MG, 9.5 MG, 2 MG, 3.0 MG, 13.3 MG, 9 MG, 18 MG)
Patch (1.5 MG, 6 MG, 3 MG, 6.0 MG, 4.6 MG, 4.5 MG, 9.5 MG, 2 MG, 3.0 MG, 13.3 MG, 9 MG, 18 MG)
moa: Pseudo-irreversible (carbamate) inhibitor of BOTH acetylcholinesterase AND butyrylcholinesterase → raises synaptic acetylcholine. Dual esterase inhibition is its distinguishing mechanism; symptomatic only.
receptorProfile: Inhibits AChE and butyrylcholinesterase; butyrylcholinesterase becomes more important as Alzheimer advances, a proposed rationale for the dual target.
onsetOfEffect: Symptomatic benefit assessed over weeks; not disease-modifying.
halfLife: Oral parent ~1.5 hours, but enzyme inhibition (pseudo-irreversible) lasts ~10 hours; the transdermal patch gives smooth, sustained levels over 24 h.
timeToSteadyState: Reached within days with steady dosing/patch.
metabolism: Minimal CYP involvement — hydrolysed by cholinesterases to a decarbamylated metabolite, then renally excreted. Few CYP-based drug interactions (a practical advantage).
elimination: Renal (metabolites); negligible unchanged drug.
foodEffect: Take oral doses WITH food to reduce GI upset; the patch bypasses first-pass GI exposure.
approvedPopulations: Adults with mild–moderate Alzheimer disease AND mild–moderate Parkinson disease dementia.
startingDose: Oral 1.5 mg PO BID; transdermal patch 4.6 mg/24 h once daily.
titration: Oral: increase by 1.5 mg BID at ≥2-week intervals as tolerated. Patch: step up to 9.5 mg/24 h after ≥4 weeks, then 13.3 mg/24 h.
maxDose: Oral 6 mg BID (12 mg/day); patch 13.3 mg/24 h.
frequencyTiming: Oral twice daily with food; patch once daily, rotating application sites.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Titrate cautiously in low body weight, renal or hepatic impairment (higher exposure, more GI/cholinergic effects).
startStopCrossTaper: If oral therapy is interrupted for more than ~3 days, RESTART at the lowest dose and re-titrate — resuming at the previous dose risks severe vomiting (with reports of esophageal rupture). The same re-titration caution applies to the patch.
administrationNotes: The transdermal patch is the signature feature: smoother plasma levels reduce nausea/vomiting and aid adherence.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Rivastigmine tartrate capsules are contraindicated in patients with: known hypersensitivity to rivastigmine, other carbamate derivatives or other components of the formulation [see Description (11)] a previous history of application site reaction with rivastigmine transdermal patch suggestive of allergic contact dermatitis, in the absence of negative allergy testing [see Warnings and Precautions (5.2)] Isolated cases of generalized skin reactions have been described in postmarketing experience [see Adverse Reactions (6.2)]. Known hypersensitivity to rivastigmine, other carbamate derivatives or other components of the formulation. (4) History of application site reaction with rivastigmine transdermal patch suggestive of allergic contact dermatitis, in the absence of negative allergy testing (4, 5.2)
commonSE: Vomiting (prominent with oral, especially during titration)
seriousAE: Severe vomiting/dehydration (esp. on restarting without re-titration — esophageal rupture reported)
overdoseLethality: Cholinergic crisis in overdose (severe GI symptoms, bradycardia, hypotension, seizures); atropine is the antidote. Accidental patch overuse (multiple patches) has caused serious toxicity.
baselineWorkup: Pulse and history of conduction disease/syncope
ongoingMonitoring: Weight and hydration/GI tolerance; heart rate; application-site skin checks (patch); confirm no patch stacking.
majorDDI: Anticholinergics — OPPOSE the drug and reduce efficacy
Beta-blockers/other bradycardic agents — additive bradycardia and syncope
pregnancy: Rarely relevant (dementia population); limited data — use only if clearly needed.
geriatric: Titrate slowly to limit nausea/vomiting and weight loss; the patch is often preferred in frail/low-weight patients for tolerability.
cardiac: Vagotonic → bradycardia and syncope; caution with sick sinus syndrome, conduction defects and other rate-slowing drugs.
pearls: The transdermal PATCH reduces GI side effects (smoother levels) and improves adherence versus oral dosing.
The only cholinesterase inhibitor also approved for Parkinson disease dementia.
Dual AChE + butyrylcholinesterase inhibition; metabolised by esterases (not CYP) → few CYP interactions.
After an interruption >3 days, restart low and re-titrate — resuming the old dose risks severe vomiting.
mnemonics: "Rivastigmine RIDES a patch and RIVals in Parkinson" — patch = fewer GI SEs; approved for Parkinson disease dementia.
confusables: Rivastigmine vs donepezil — rivastigmine is dual-esterase, twice-daily (or patch), and covers Parkinson dementia; donepezil is AChE-selective, once daily, all stages of Alzheimer.
Rivastigmine patch vs oral — same drug; patch has markedly fewer GI effects.
classDifferentiators: Dual AChE + butyrylcholinesterase inhibitor; esterase-metabolised (few CYP interactions); available as a patch; the class member indicated for Parkinson disease dementia.
examHooks: Which cholinesterase inhibitor is approved for Parkinson disease dementia and comes as a patch? (rivastigmine)
Why re-titrate rivastigmine after a >3-day gap? (risk of severe vomiting/esophageal rupture)
counsellingPoints: Take oral doses with food; nausea is worst during dose increases.
The patch causes less nausea — rotate sites and apply only ONE at a time.
If you miss several days, do not restart at the old dose — contact us to re-titrate.
Report severe/persistent vomiting, fainting, or a very slow pulse.

vignettes.stem: A patient with Alzheimer disease stopped oral rivastigmine for 5 days during a hospital admission. On discharge, a nurse restarts the previous 4.5 mg BID dose and the patient develops severe, protracted vomiting. What is the key management principle that was violated?
options: Rivastigmine should never be restarted after any interruption
After an interruption longer than 3 days, restart at the lowest dose and re-titrate
The patient should have been switched to memantine
Rivastigmine must always be given with an antiemetic
vignettes.answer: After an interruption longer than 3 days, restart at the lowest dose and re-titrate
vignettes.explanation: Cholinergic tolerance is lost after a few days off rivastigmine; resuming at the prior dose can cause severe vomiting (esophageal rupture has been reported). Restart at 1.5 mg BID and re-titrate.

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Amantadine hydrochloride capsules, USP are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to amantadine hydrochloride or to any of the other ingredients in amantadine hydrochloride capsules, USP.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION The dose of amantadine hydrochloride capsules may need reduction in patients with congestive heart failure, peripheral edema, orthostatic hypotension, or impaired renal function (see Dosage for Impaired Renal Function ).

maxDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION The dose of amantadine hydrochloride capsules may need reduction in patients with congestive heart failure, peripheral edema, orthostatic hypotension, or impaired renal function (see Dosage for Impaired Renal Function ).

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Deaths Deaths have been reported from overdose with amantadine. The lowest reported acute lethal dose was 1 gram. Acute toxicity may be attributable to the anticholinergic effects of amantadine. Drug overdose has resulted in cardiac, respiratory, renal or central nervous system toxicity. Cardiac dysfunction includes arrhythmia, tachycardia and hypertension (see OVERDOSAGE ). Deaths due to drug accumulation (overdosage) have been reported in patients with renal impairment, who were prescribed higher than recommended doses of amantadine hydrochloride for their level of renal function [see DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION: Dosage of Impaired Renal Function and OVERDOSAGE ]. Suicide Attempts …

Because some patients have attempted suicide by overdosing with amantadine, prescriptions should be written for the smallest quantity consistent with good patient management. Acute toxicity may be attributable to the anticholinergic effects of amantadine. Drug overdose has resulted in cardiac, respiratory, renal or central nervous system toxicity. Cardiac dysfunction includes arrhythmia, tachycardia and hypertension. Pulmonary edema and respiratory distress (including adult respiratory distress syndrome – ARDS) have been reported; renal dysfunction including increased BUN, …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions Careful observation is required when amantadine is administered concurrently with central nervous system stimulants. Agents with anticholinergic properties may potentiate the anticholinergic-like side effects of amantadine. Coadministration of thioridazine has been reported to worsen the tremor in elderly patients with Parkinson’s disease, however, it is not known if other phenothiazines produce a similar response. Coadministration of triamterene and hydrochlorothiazide capsules resulted in a higher plasma amantadine concentration in a 61-year-old man receiving amantadine (hydrochloride capsules) 100 mg t.i.d. for Parkinson’s disease 1 . It is not known which of the …

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy The effect of amantadine on embryofetal and peri-postnatal development has not been adequately tested, that is, in studies conducted under Good Laboratory Practice (GLP) and according to current recommended methodology. However, in two non-GLP studies in rats in which females were dosed from 5 days prior to mating to Day 6 of gestation or on Days 7 to 14 of gestation, amantadine produced increases in embryonic death at an oral dose of 100 mg/kg (or 3 times the maximum recommended human dose on a mg/m 2 basis). In the non-GLP rat study in which females were dosed on Days 7 to 14 of gestation, there was a marked increase in severe visceral and skeletal malformations at oral doses of …

moa: Dopaminergic + NMDA-receptor antagonist: enhances dopamine release, blocks dopamine reuptake, and antagonizes glutamate NMDA receptors (also has historical anti-influenza-A activity).
receptorProfile: NMDA antagonist + indirect dopaminergic; mild anticholinergic-like effects.
onsetOfEffect: Antiparkinsonian/dyskinesia benefit over days.
halfLife: ~10–17 hours (markedly prolonged in renal impairment and the elderly).
metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism — excreted largely UNCHANGED by the kidneys (renal dose adjustment required).
elimination: Renal (unchanged drug); accumulates in renal impairment.
startingDose: 100 mg PO once daily.
titration: Increase to 100 mg BID after ~1 week if needed.
maxDose: ~300–400 mg/day (immediate-release); reduce in renal impairment.
frequencyTiming: Once–twice daily; avoid late evening dosing (insomnia).
renalHepaticAdjustment: REDUCE dose (and/or frequency) in renal impairment — renally cleared; toxicity/accumulation otherwise.
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Amantadine hydrochloride capsules, USP are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to amantadine hydrochloride or to any of the other ingredients in amantadine hydrochloride capsules, USP.
seriousAE: Hallucinations/confusion/psychosis (esp. elderly, high dose, renal impairment)
overdoseLethality: Narrow margin in overdose — arrhythmia, seizures, hallucinations, anticholinergic-like toxicity; potentially fatal. Risk rises with renal impairment.
ongoingMonitoring: Renal function; mental status (hallucinations/confusion); skin (livedo) and edema; do not stop abruptly.
majorDDI: Triamterene/hydrochlorothiazide, trimethoprim — reduce renal clearance (raise amantadine levels)
contraindicatedCombos: Significant renal failure without dose adjustment (accumulation/toxicity)
pregnancy: Limited data; use only if clearly needed.
geriatric: High risk of confusion/hallucinations and reduced renal clearance — lower dose; often preferred over anticholinergics for parkinsonism because it lacks their full anticholinergic burden.
renalHepatic: Renally excreted — the key adjustment. Accumulates and becomes neurotoxic if not reduced in renal impairment.
cardiac: Can prolong QT and cause orthostasis.
pearls: Dopaminergic + NMDA antagonist — used for DRUG-INDUCED PARKINSONISM (and as an adjunct for tardive dyskinesia/levodopa dyskinesia); an alternative to anticholinergics with LESS anticholinergic burden.
Signature adverse effects: LIVEDO RETICULARIS and ANKLE EDEMA; also hallucinations/confusion (esp. elderly).
Renally excreted UNCHANGED → MUST reduce dose in renal impairment; do NOT stop abruptly (NMS-like withdrawal).
mnemonics: “aMANtadine leaves a MANtled (livedo reticularis) skin and swollen ankles, and its clue is the KIDNEY (renal excretion).”
confusables: Amantadine (dopaminergic/NMDA — parkinsonism, renally cleared) vs memantine (NMDA antagonist for Alzheimer dementia) vs anticholinergics.
classDifferentiators: Not an anticholinergic and not a VMAT2 inhibitor: a dopaminergic/NMDA agent for DRUG-INDUCED PARKINSONISM (± TD adjunct). Renally cleared (renal dosing), with signature livedo reticularis/edema and hallucination risk.
examHooks: Drug-induced parkinsonism in an elderly patient where you want to AVOID anticholinergic burden → amantadine.
Mottled skin (livedo reticularis) + ankle edema on a parkinsonism drug → amantadine.
counsellingPoints: Do not stop suddenly (risk of a serious withdrawal reaction).
Report new confusion, hallucinations, or mood changes.
A lace-like purple skin mottling (livedo) and ankle swelling can occur and are usually reversible.
Take earlier in the day to avoid insomnia.

vignettes.stem: An elderly patient on an antipsychotic develops parkinsonism, but you want to avoid worsening their constipation and cognition with an anticholinergic. You also note they have mild renal impairment. Which agent is most appropriate, with dose adjustment?
vignettes.explanation: Amantadine (dopaminergic + NMDA antagonist) treats drug-induced parkinsonism with less anticholinergic burden than benztropine/trihexyphenidyl — useful in the elderly. It is renally excreted, so the dose must be reduced in renal impairment to avoid confusion/hallucinations.
vignettes.testedFact: Amantadine for parkinsonism; renal dosing; less anticholinergic burden

In patients with OSA, doses up to 250 mg/day, given as a single …

( 5.1 ) DRESS/Multi-organ Hypersensitivity Reactions: if suspected, discontinue armodafinil. ( 5.2 ) Angioedema and Anaphylaxis Reactions: if suspected, discontinue armodafinil. ( 5.3 ) Persistent Sleepiness: assess patients frequently for degree of sleepiness and, if appropriate, advise patients to avoid driving or engaging in any other potentially dangerous activity. ( 5.4 ) Psychiatric Symptoms: use particular caution in treating patients with a history of psychosis, depression, or mania. Consider discontinuing …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Fatal overdoses involving modafinil alone or involving armodafinil or modafinil in combination with other drugs have been reported in the postmarketing setting. Symptoms most often accompanying armodafinil or modafinil overdose, alone or in combination with other drugs, have included anxiety, dyspnea, insomnia; central nervous system symptoms such as restlessness, disorientation, confusion, excitation and hallucination; digestive changes such as nausea and diarrhea; and cardiovascular changes such as tachycardia, bradycardia, hypertension, and chest pain. No specific antidote exists for the toxic effects of a armodafinil overdose. Such overdoses should be managed with …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Effects of Armodafinil on CYP3A4/5 Substrates The clearance of drugs that are substrates for CYP3A4/5 (e.g., steroidal contraceptives, cyclosporine, midazolam, and triazolam) may be increased by armodafinil via induction of metabolic enzymes, which results in lower systemic exposure. Dosage adjustment of these drugs should be considered when these drugs are used concomitantly with armodafinil [see Clinical Pharmacology (12.3) ] . The effectiveness of steroidal contraceptives may be reduced when used with armodafinil and for one month after discontinuation of therapy. Alternative or concomitant methods of contraception are recommended for patients taking steroidal …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary Limited available data on armodafinil use in pregnant women are insufficient to inform a drug associated risk of adverse pregnancy outcomes. Intrauterine growth restriction and spontaneous abortion have been reported in association with armodafinil and modafinil. Although the pharmacology of armodafinil is not identical to that of the sympathomimetic amines, armodafinil shares some pharmacologic properties with this class [see Clinical Pharmacology (12.1) ] . Some sympathomimetics have been associated with intrauterine growth restriction and spontaneous abortions. In animal reproduction studies of armodafinil (R-modafinil) and modafinil (a mixture of R-and …

moa: Wakefulness-promoting agent; the longer-acting R-(−)-enantiomer of modafinil, with the same incompletely understood mechanism (weak DAT inhibition + orexin/histamine/noradrenergic effects).
receptorProfile: Atypical (as modafinil); not a classic stimulant.
onsetOfEffect: Wakefulness within ~1–2 h; sustained plasma levels later in the day than modafinil.
halfLife: ~13–15 hours; being the single R-enantiomer, effective concentrations persist longer into the day than racemic modafinil.
metabolism: Hepatic; CYP3A4 substrate. Induces CYP3A4 (↓ contraceptive efficacy) and inhibits CYP2C19 — same interaction profile as modafinil.
offLabel: (Same indications as modafinil where available: narcolepsy, OSA adjunct, shift-work disorder)
startingDose: 150 mg PO once daily in the morning (shift-work: ~1 h before shift).
titration: Usually 150–250 mg/day.
maxDose: 250 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once daily, morning.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Armodafinil tablets are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to modafinil or armodafinil or its inactive ingredients [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1 , 5.2 , 5.3) ] . Armodafinil tablets are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to modafinil or armodafinil.
overdoseLethality: Overdose → agitation, insomnia, tachycardia, hypertension; lower toxicity than classic stimulants.
ongoingMonitoring: BP/HR; watch for rash and psychiatric symptoms.
majorDDI: Hormonal contraceptives — reduced efficacy (CYP3A4 induction; use backup)
pregnancy: Avoid; effective contraception advised.
geriatric: Reduce dose; slower clearance.
cardiac: Can raise HR/BP; avoid with recent MI/unstable angina.
pearls: US-only — for awareness (Nuvigil): the R-enantiomer of modafinil; NOT marketed in Canada (the pipeline flags Canadian availability itself).
Once-daily like modafinil but sustains wakefulness longer into the day (single enantiomer); same CYP3A4-induction/contraceptive caveat.
Controlled substance (Schedule IV in the US) with misuse potential.
mnemonics: “ARmodafinil = the R-enantiomer that Runs longer” — one enantiomer, longer daytime coverage.
confusables: Armodafinil (R-enantiomer, US-only) vs modafinil (racemic, available in Canada).
classDifferentiators: Enantiomer refinement of modafinil — longer effective daytime levels; US-only. Same wakefulness role and same contraceptive-efficacy interaction.
examHooks: How does armodafinil differ from modafinil? It is the purified longer-acting R-enantiomer (US-only).
counsellingPoints: Take in the morning.
Use backup contraception if on hormonal birth control.
Stop and seek care if a rash develops.

vignettes.stem: Armodafinil differs pharmacologically from modafinil primarily in which way?
options: It is a classic amphetamine-type stimulant
It is the single R-(−)-enantiomer with longer-sustained daytime levels
It has no effect on CYP3A4
vignettes.answer: It is the single R-(−)-enantiomer with longer-sustained daytime levels
vignettes.explanation: Armodafinil is the R-enantiomer of racemic modafinil; its longer effective half-life sustains wakefulness later in the day. It shares modafinil’s CYP3A4-inducing (contraceptive-lowering) interaction. It is US-only.

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Hypersensitivity to benztropine mesylate tablets or to any component of the tablets. Because of its atropine-like side effects, this drug is contraindicated in pediatric patients under three years of age, and should be used with caution in older pediatric patients.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Benztropine mesylate tablets should be used when patients are able to take oral medication. The injection is especially useful for psychotic patients with acute dystonic reactions or other reactions that make oral medication difficult or impossible. It is recommended also when a more rapid response is desired than can be obtained with the tablets. Because of cumulative action, therapy should be initiated with a low dose which is increased gradually at five or six-day intervals to the smallest amount necessary for optimal relief. Increases should be made in increments of 0.5 mg, to a maximum of 6 mg, or until optimal results are obtained without excessive adverse …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Safe use in pregnancy has not been established. Benztropine mesylate may impair mental and/or physical abilities required for performance of hazardous tasks, such as operating machinery or driving a motor vehicle. When benztropine mesylate is given concomitantly with phenothiazines, haloperidol, or other drugs with anticholinergic or antidopaminergic activity, patients should be advised to report gastrointestinal complaints, fever or heat intolerance promptly. Paralytic ileus, hyperthermia and heat stroke, all of which have sometimes been fatal, have occurred in patients taking anticholinergic-type antiparkinsonism drugs, including benztropine mesylate, in combination with …

moa: Central muscarinic (anticholinergic) antagonist with antihistaminic and mild dopamine-reuptake-inhibiting properties; restores the striatal dopamine:acetylcholine balance disrupted by D2 blockade.
receptorProfile: Antimuscarinic (M1) + H1 antagonism; some dopamine reuptake inhibition.
onsetOfEffect: IM/IV relieves acute dystonia within minutes; oral effect over hours–days for parkinsonism.
halfLife: Not well characterized; long duration of action (once- or twice-daily oral dosing suffices).
metabolism: Hepatic (N-oxidation and N-dealkylation); not a major CYP interaction source — anticholinergic pharmacodynamics dominate.
approvedCanada: Drug-induced extrapyramidal disorders (acute dystonia, drug-induced parkinsonism)
offLabel: Prophylaxis of dystonia in high-risk patients starting high-potency antipsychotics
approvedPopulations: Adults; avoid in young children (susceptible to anticholinergic toxicity).
startingDose: Acute dystonia: 1–2 mg IM or IV, may repeat. EPS/parkinsonism: 1–2 mg PO once or twice daily.
titration: Titrate to effect; many patients need only 1–4 mg/day.
maxDose: 6 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Once daily to BID (long-acting).
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Hypersensitivity to benztropine mesylate tablets or to any component of the tablets. Because of its atropine-like side effects, this drug is contraindicated in pediatric patients under three years of age, and should be used with caution in older pediatric patients.
syndromes: Anticholinergic toxidrome (hot, dry, red, blind, mad — “dry as a bone, red as a beet, mad as a hatter”)
overdoseLethality: Anticholinergic toxidrome in overdose (delirium, hyperthermia, tachyarrhythmia, ileus); usually manageable but additive with other anticholinergics.
ongoingMonitoring: Bowel and bladder function; cognition (esp. elderly); reassess need — do not continue indefinitely once EPS resolve.
majorDDI: Additive anticholinergic burden with low-potency antipsychotics, TCAs, antihistamines, oxybutynin
Reduced GI motility can alter absorption of other drugs
Additive heat-intolerance/hyperthermia risk with other anticholinergics
contraindicatedCombos: Other high anticholinergic-load agents in frail/elderly (additive delirium, ileus, retention)
pregnancy: Limited data; use only if clearly needed.
lactation: May suppress lactation and cause infant anticholinergic effects; caution.
geriatric: High-risk — precipitates confusion, falls, constipation, retention; on Beers-type avoid lists. Prefer amantadine or dose reduction of the antipsychotic when possible.
pearls: First-line for ACUTE DYSTONIA (give IM/IV) and drug-induced parkinsonism — rebalances striatal ACh vs dopamine.
Does NOT treat akathisia well and can WORSEN tardive dyskinesia (TD reflects dopamine hypersensitivity — blocking ACh unmasks it).
Trades EPS for anticholinergic burden: dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, cognitive impairment.
mnemonics: Anticholinergic toxidrome: “hot as a hare, dry as a bone, red as a beet, blind as a bat, mad as a hatter.”
confusables: Benztropine (anticholinergic for EPS) vs benzatropine (same drug, INN spelling) vs bromocriptine (dopamine agonist for NMS).
classDifferentiators: Anticholinergic that treats ACUTE DYSTONIA + drug-induced PARKINSONISM; ineffective for akathisia and CONTRAINDICATED-in-effect for tardive dyskinesia (worsens it). IM/IV route makes it the go-to rescue for acute dystonia.
examHooks: Oculogyric crisis / torticollis hours after starting haloperidol → give IM benztropine (or diphenhydramine).
Which EPS is NOT helped (and may be worsened) by benztropine? Tardive dyskinesia.
counsellingPoints: Expect dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation — increase fluids/fibre.
Avoid overheating/strenuous exercise in hot weather (impaired sweating).
Report difficulty urinating, eye pain, or confusion.

vignettes.stem: Six hours after his first dose of haloperidol, a 22-year-old develops a painful sustained upward deviation of the eyes and neck stiffness. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
vignettes.explanation: Acute dystonic reaction (oculogyric crisis) responds rapidly to a parenteral anticholinergic (benztropine or diphenhydramine). Propranolol is for akathisia and VMAT2 inhibitors (tetrabenazine) are for tardive dyskinesia — neither treats acute dystonia.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: DEPRESSION AND SUICIDALITY IN PATIENTS WITH HUNTINGTON’S DISEASE AUSTEDO XR and AUSTEDO can increase the risk of depression and suicidal thoughts and behavior (suicidality) in patients with Huntington’s disease. Anyone considering the use of AUSTEDO XR or AUSTEDO must balance the risks of depression and suicidality with the clinical need for treatment of chorea. Closely monitor patients for the emergence or worsening of depression, suicidality, or unusual changes in behavior. Patients, their caregivers, and families should be informed of the risk of depression and suicidality and should be instructed to report behaviors of concern promptly to the treating physician. Particular caution should be exercised in treating patients with a history of depression or prior suicide attempts or ideation, which are increased in frequency in Huntington’s disease. AUSTEDO XR and AUSTEDO are contraindicated in patients who are suicidal, and in patients with untreated or inadequately treated depression [see Contraindications ( 4 ) and Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 )]. WARNING: DEPRESSION AND SUICIDALITY IN PATIENTS WITH HUNTINGTON’S DISEASE See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. Increases the risk of depression and suicidal thoughts and behavior (suicidality) in patients with Huntington’s disease ( 5.1 ) Balance risks of depression and suicidality with the …

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS AUSTEDO XR and AUSTEDO are contraindicated in patients: With Huntington’s disease who are suicidal, or have untreated or inadequately treated depression [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 )] . With hepatic impairment [see Use in Specific Populations ( 8.6 ), Clinical Pharmacology ( 12.3 )] . Taking reserpine. At least 20 days should elapse after stopping reserpine before starting AUSTEDO XR or AUSTEDO [see Drug Interactions ( 7.2 )] . Taking monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs). AUSTEDO XR and AUSTEDO should not be used in combination with an MAOI, or within 14 days of discontinuing therapy with an MAOI [see Drug Interactions ( 7.3 )] . Taking tetrabenazine or valbenazine [see Drug Interactions ( 7.6 )] . Suicidal, or untreated/inadequately treated depression in patients with Huntington’s disease ( 4 , 5.1 ) Hepatic impairment ( 4 , 8.6 , 12.3 ) Taking reserpine, MAOIs, tetrabenazine, or valbenazine ( 4 , 7.2 , 7.3 , 7.6 )
