overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Overdoses ranging from 100 mg to 1 g have been reported in the literature with tetrabenazine, a closely related VMAT2 inhibitor. The following adverse reactions occurred with overdosing: acute dystonia, oculogyric crisis, nausea and vomiting, sweating, sedation, hypotension, confusion, diarrhea, hallucinations, rubor, and tremor. Treatment should consist of those general measures employed in the management of overdosage with any central nervous system-active drug. General supportive and symptomatic measures are recommended. Cardiac rhythm and vital signs should be monitored. In managing overdosage, the possibility of multiple drug involvement should always be considered. The …

Concomitant use of strong CYP2D6 inhibitors (e.g., paroxetine, fluoxetine, quinidine, bupropion) has been shown to increase the systemic exposure to the active dihydro-metabolites of deutetrabenazine by approximately 3-fold. The daily dose of AUSTEDO XR or AUSTEDO should not …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary There are no adequate data on the developmental risk associated with the use of AUSTEDO XR or AUSTEDO in pregnant women. Administration of deutetrabenazine to rats during organogenesis produced no clear adverse effect on embryofetal development. However, administration of tetrabenazine to rats throughout pregnancy and lactation resulted in an increase in stillbirths and postnatal offspring mortality [see Data]. In the U.S. general population, the estimated background risk of major birth defects and miscarriage in clinically recognized pregnancies is 2-4% and 15-20%, respectively. The background risk of major birth defects and miscarriage for the indicated …

moa: Deuterated form of tetrabenazine — a VMAT2 inhibitor; carbon-deuterium bonds slow metabolism of the active HTBZ metabolites, giving steadier levels and twice-daily dosing.
receptorProfile: VMAT2 inhibition (presynaptic dopamine depletion), via active deuterated HTBZ metabolites.
onsetOfEffect: Chorea/TD improve over weeks with titration.
halfLife: Deuteration prolongs the active-metabolite half-life (~9–10 h) versus tetrabenazine → BID dosing with fewer peak-related effects.
metabolism: Carbonyl reductase to deuterated α-/β-HTBZ; CYP2D6 downstream (reduce dose with strong 2D6 inhibitors / poor metabolizers).
offLabel: (US indications: tardive dyskinesia; chorea of Huntington disease)
startingDose: 6 mg PO once daily (Huntington chorea) / 12 mg/day divided (tardive dyskinesia) — titrate slowly.
titration: Increase weekly by 6 mg/day to effect.
maxDose: Up to ~48 mg/day (lower with strong CYP2D6 inhibitors / poor metabolizers).
frequencyTiming: Twice daily with food (deuteration allows BID vs tetrabenazine’s more frequent dosing).

AUSTEDO XR and AUSTEDO are …

With hepatic impairment [see Use in Specific Populations ( 8.6 ), Clinical Pharmacology ( 12.3 )] . Taking reserpine. Taking tetrabenazine or valbenazine [see Drug Interactions ( 7.6 )] . Suicidal, or untreated/inadequately treated depression in patients with Huntington’s disease ( 4 , 5.1 ) Hepatic impairment ( 4 , 8.6 , 12.3 ) Taking reserpine, …
overdoseLethality: Expect sedation, hypotension, QT effects — supportive care.
ongoingMonitoring: Mood/suicidality, parkinsonism/akathisia, QT if risk factors.
majorDDI: CYP2D6 inhibitors raise active-metabolite levels (reduce dose)
pregnancy: Limited data; avoid unless clearly needed.
cardiac: QT prolongation — caution with QT drugs / electrolyte abnormalities.
pearls: US-only — for awareness (Austedo): the DEUTERATED tetrabenazine — deuterium slows metabolism → smoother levels, BID dosing, and generally better tolerability than tetrabenazine.
Approved (US) for BOTH tardive dyskinesia and Huntington chorea.
Same class caveats: depression/suicidality, parkinsonism, QT (dopamine depletion).
mnemonics: “DEUTEtrabenazine = tetrabenazine with a DEUTERIUM Durability upgrade” — heavy hydrogen → slower metabolism, BID dosing.
confusables: Deutetrabenazine (deuterated, US-only) vs tetrabenazine (Canada: Nitoman) vs valbenazine (once-daily, US-only).
classDifferentiators: Deuterated VMAT2 inhibitor for TD/Huntington chorea; US-only. Smoother PK and better tolerability than tetrabenazine while sharing its dopamine-depletion/QT profile.
examHooks: How does deutetrabenazine improve on tetrabenazine? Deuteration slows metabolism → steadier levels, BID dosing, fewer peak effects (US-only).
counsellingPoints: Take twice daily with food.
Report low mood or self-harm thoughts.
Report drowsiness affecting driving; report palpitations/fainting.

vignettes.stem: Deutetrabenazine was developed to improve on tetrabenazine. What is the pharmacologic basis for its advantages?
options: It blocks dopamine receptors rather than depleting dopamine
Deuterium substitution slows metabolism of the active metabolites, giving steadier levels and less frequent dosing
It has no risk of depression
vignettes.answer: Deuterium substitution slows metabolism of the active metabolites, giving steadier levels and less frequent dosing
vignettes.explanation: Deutetrabenazine is deuterated tetrabenazine; the stronger carbon–deuterium bonds slow metabolism of the active HTBZ metabolites, smoothing plasma levels and permitting twice-daily dosing with better tolerability. It still depletes dopamine (VMAT2) and can cause depression. It is US-only.

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE The tolerability of dexmedetomidine was studied in one study in which healthy adult subjects were administered doses at and above the recommended dose of 0.2 to 0.7 mcg/kg/hr. The maximum blood concentration achieved in this study was approximately 13 times the upper boundary of the therapeutic range. The most notable effects observed in two subjects who achieved the highest doses were first degree atrioventricular block and second-degree heart block. No hemodynamic compromise was noted with the atrioventricular block and the heart block resolved spontaneously within one minute. Five adult patients received an overdose of dexmedetomidine in the intensive care unit sedation …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Anesthetics, Sedatives, Hypnotics, Opioids: Enhancement of pharmacodynamic effects. Reduction in dosage of dexmedetomidine or the concomitant medication may be required. (7.1) 7.1 Anesthetics, Sedatives, Hypnotics, Opioids Co-administration of dexmedetomidine with anesthetics, sedatives, hypnotics, and opioids is likely to lead to an enhancement of effects. Specific studies have confirmed these effects with sevoflurane, isoflurane, propofol, alfentanil, and midazolam. No pharmacokinetic interactions between dexmedetomidine and isoflurane, propofol, alfentanil and midazolam have been demonstrated. However, due to possible pharmacodynamic interactions, when co-administered …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary Available data from published randomized controlled trials and case reports over several decades of use with intravenously administered dexmedetomidine during pregnancy have not identified a drug-associated risk of major birth defects and miscarriage; however, the reported exposures occurred after the first trimester. Most of the available data are based on studies with exposures that occurred at the time of caesarean section delivery, and these studies have not identified an adverse effect on maternal outcomes or infant Apgar scores. Available data indicate that dexmedetomidine crosses the placenta. In animal reproduction studies, fetal toxicity that lower fetal …

formulationsCanada: Solution (100 MCG, 0.1 MG, 0.5 MG, 4 MCG, 200 MCG, 400 MCG)
Gel (100 MCG, 0.1 MG, 0.5 MG, 4 MCG, 200 MCG, 400 MCG)
moa: Selective α2-adrenergic receptor agonist; reduces central sympathetic (noradrenergic) outflow to produce calming/sedation without significant respiratory depression.
receptorProfile: α2-adrenergic agonist (high α2:α1 selectivity).
onsetOfEffect: Sublingual/buccal film calms acute agitation within ~20–60 minutes.
metabolism: Hepatic — glucuronidation and CYP-mediated (CYP2A6) oxidation.
offLabel: (IV form used for procedural/ICU sedation; sublingual film is the psychiatric agitation product)
startingDose: Sublingual/buccal film 120 micrograms for acute agitation (lower in mild–moderate hepatic impairment / elderly); may re-dose per label.
titration: Repeat/step-down dosing per label based on agitation and vitals.
maxDose: Per label daily maximum for the sublingual film (limited repeat doses in 24 h).
frequencyTiming: PRN for acute agitation episodes (not maintenance therapy).
renalHepaticAdjustment: Lower dose in hepatic impairment.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS None. None. ( 4 )
overdoseLethality: Overdose → profound hypotension, bradycardia, sedation; supportive care/monitoring.
baselineWorkup: Blood pressure and heart rate before dosing
ongoingMonitoring: BP and HR after each dose (monitor for hypotension/bradycardia); orthostatic vitals; sedation level.
majorDDI: CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, opioids, alcohol) — additive sedation
Other bradycardic agents (β-blockers, digoxin) — additive bradycardia
pregnancy: Limited data; use only if clearly needed.
geriatric: More sensitive to hypotension/bradycardia — reduce dose, monitor vitals.
cardiac: Hypotension and bradycardia are the defining hazards; can prolong QT.
pearls: US-only — for awareness (Igalmi): a sublingual/buccal α2-agonist FILM for ACUTE AGITATION in schizophrenia or bipolar I/II; NOT marketed in Canada (pipeline flags availability).
Distinct from IM antipsychotics/benzodiazepines for agitation — non-dopaminergic, minimal respiratory depression, but requires BP/HR monitoring (hypotension/bradycardia/orthostasis).
It calms without the EPS risk of antipsychotics — but sedation and cardiovascular depression are the trade-offs.
mnemonics: “DEXmedetomidine DECKS agitation via α2 (like clonidine’s cousin) — but watch the BP and pulse DROP.”
confusables: Dexmedetomidine (α2 agonist for acute agitation, US-only) vs clonidine/guanfacine (α2 agonists) vs IM antipsychotics/benzodiazepines for agitation.
classDifferentiators: Not an EPS or movement-disorder drug: an α2-agonist for ACUTE AGITATION (schizophrenia/bipolar), US-only. Non-dopaminergic calming (no EPS) but with hypotension/bradycardia as the key hazard.
examHooks: Sublingual film α2-agonist for acute agitation in schizophrenia/bipolar (US-only)? Dexmedetomidine.
What must you monitor after dosing dexmedetomidine for agitation? BP and heart rate (hypotension/bradycardia).
counsellingPoints: Used for short-term acute agitation, not daily maintenance.
Expect drowsiness; blood pressure and pulse will be checked.
Rise slowly afterward (dizziness/fainting risk).

vignettes.stem: A sublingual film formulation of an α2-adrenergic agonist is approved (in the US) for acute agitation in adults with schizophrenia or bipolar disorder. Which agent is this, and what is the main monitoring concern?
options: Dexmedetomidine — monitor blood pressure and heart rate
vignettes.answer: Dexmedetomidine — monitor blood pressure and heart rate
vignettes.explanation: Sublingual dexmedetomidine (US-only) is an α2-agonist for acute agitation; because α2 agonism lowers sympathetic tone, hypotension and bradycardia (and orthostasis) are the key monitoring concerns. It is non-dopaminergic, so it avoids EPS.
vignettes.testedFact: Dexmedetomidine α2-agonist for agitation; monitor BP/HR

Modafinil is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to modafinil or armodafinil. ()

( 2.1 ) • SWD: 200 mg once a day, taken approximately one hour prior to start of the work shift. ( 2.2 ) • Severe Hepatic Impairment: reduce dose to half the recommended dose. ( 2.3 , 12.3 ) • Geriatric Patients: consider lower dose. ( 2.4 , 12.3 ) 2.1 Dosage in Narcolepsy and Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA) The recommended dosage of modafinil tablets for patients with narcolepsy or OSA is 200 mg taken orally once a day as a single dose in the morning. Doses up to 400 mg/day, given as a single dose, have been well tolerated, but …

seriousAE.sourceText: 5 WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS • Serious Rash, including Stevens-Johnson Syndrome: Discontinue modafinil at the first sign of rash, unless the rash is clearly not drug-related. ( 5.1 ) • Angioedema and Anaphylaxis Reactions: If suspected, discontinue modafinil. ( 5.2 ) • Multi-organ Hypersensitivity Reactions: If suspected, discontinue modafinil. ( 5.3 ) • Persistent Sleepiness: Assess patients frequently for degree of sleepiness and, if appropriate, advise patients to avoid driving or engaging in any other potentially dangerous activity. ( 5.4 ) • Psychiatric Symptoms: Use caution in patients with a history of psychosis, depression, or mania. Consider discontinuing modafinil if psychiatric …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE In clinical trials, a total of 151 protocol-specified doses ranging from 1000 to 1600 mg/day (5 to 8 times the recommended daily dose of modafinil) have been administered to 32 subjects, including 13 subjects who received doses of 1000 or 1200 mg/day for 7 to 21 consecutive days. In addition, several intentional acute overdoses occurred; the two largest being 4500 mg and 4000 mg taken by two subjects participating in foreign depression studies. None of these study subjects experienced any unexpected or life-threatening effects. Adverse reactions that were reported at these doses included excitation or agitation, insomnia, and slight or moderate elevations in hemodynamic …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Effects of Modafinil on CYP3A4/5 Substrates The clearance of drugs that are substrates for CYP3A4/5 (e.g., steroidal contraceptives, cyclosporine, midazolam, and triazolam) may be increased by modafinil via induction of metabolic enzymes, which results in lower systemic exposure. Dosage adjustment of these drugs should be considered when these drugs are used concomitantly with modafinil [see Clinical Pharmacology ( 12.3 )] . The effectiveness of steroidal contraceptives may be reduced when used with modafinil and for one month after discontinuation of therapy. Alternative or concomitant methods of contraception are recommended for patients taking steroidal contraceptives …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Teratogenic Effects Pregnancy Category C There are no adequate and well-controlled studies of modafinil in pregnant women. Intrauterine growth restriction and spontaneous abortion have been reported in association with modafinil (a mixture of R- and S-modafinil) and armodafinil (the R-enantiomer of modafinil). Although the pharmacology of modafinil is not identical to that of the sympathomimetic amines, it does share some pharmacologic properties with this class. Certain of these drugs have been associated with intrauterine growth restriction and spontaneous abortions. Whether the cases reported with modafinil are drug-related is unknown. In studies of modafinil and …

moa: Wakefulness-promoting agent; mechanism incompletely understood — weak dopamine-transporter inhibition (raises synaptic dopamine) plus downstream effects on orexin, histamine and noradrenergic systems.
receptorProfile: Atypical; low affinity for classic monoamine receptors — not a classic stimulant.
onsetOfEffect: Promotes wakefulness within ~1–2 hours; peak ~2–4 h.
metabolism: Hepatic; CYP3A4 substrate. INDUCES CYP3A4 (reduces hormonal contraceptive efficacy) and inhibits CYP2C19.
startingDose: 200 mg PO once daily in the morning (shift-work: ~1 h before the shift).
titration: Usually 200 mg/day; some use up to 400 mg/day (limited added benefit).
maxDose: 400 mg/day.
frequencyTiming: Morning (single dose); avoid late dosing (insomnia).
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment.
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Modafinil is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to modafinil or armodafinil or its inactive ingredients [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 , 5.2 , 5.3 )] . Modafinil is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to modafinil or armodafinil. ()
seriousAE: Serious rash — Stevens-Johnson syndrome / TEN / DRESS (rare)
Psychiatric effects (anxiety, mania, psychosis, suicidal ideation)
Cardiovascular events (avoid with recent MI / unstable angina / LVH)
overdoseLethality: Overdose → agitation, insomnia, tachycardia, hypertension; generally lower toxicity than classic stimulants.
ongoingMonitoring: Blood pressure/heart rate; watch for rash (stop immediately) and emergent psychiatric symptoms.
majorDDI: Hormonal contraceptives — REDUCED efficacy via CYP3A4 induction (use additional/backup contraception, during and ~1 month after)
CYP2C19 substrates (e.g., phenytoin, diazepam, propranolol) — levels rise
pregnancy: Avoid — signals of intrauterine growth restriction/congenital anomalies; effective contraception advised.
lactation: Limited data; caution.
geriatric: Reduce dose; slower clearance.
cardiac: Can raise HR/BP; avoid with recent MI, unstable angina, or significant LVH.
pearls: Wakefulness-promoter for narcolepsy / OSA (adjunct) / shift-work disorder — NOT a first-line ADHD or antidepressant drug.
CYP3A4 INDUCER → LOWERS ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE efficacy: counsel backup contraception during and for ~1 month after use (high-yield).
Controlled substance (Schedule IV in the US) with misuse potential; less activating than amphetamines.
mnemonics: “ModafiNIL leaves your birth control at NIL” — CYP3A4 induction cuts oral-contraceptive efficacy.
confusables: Modafinil vs armodafinil (its longer-acting R-enantiomer) vs classic stimulants (methylphenidate/amphetamine).
classDifferentiators: Wakefulness agent (not an EPS drug) — treats excessive sleepiness. Its defining interaction is CYP3A4 induction reducing contraceptive efficacy.
examHooks: Woman on the pill started on modafinil for narcolepsy → counsel backup contraception (CYP3A4 induction).
First-line pharmacotherapy for excessive daytime sleepiness in narcolepsy? Modafinil.
counsellingPoints: Take in the morning to avoid insomnia.
If on hormonal birth control, use an additional method during and for ~1 month after (reduced efficacy).
Stop and seek care if any rash develops.

vignettes.stem: A 28-year-old woman with narcolepsy is started on modafinil. She uses a combined oral contraceptive as her only method of contraception. What is the most important counselling point?
options: The contraceptive will increase modafinil levels dangerously
Modafinil may reduce the contraceptive’s efficacy — add a backup method
She must stop the contraceptive entirely
vignettes.answer: Modafinil may reduce the contraceptive’s efficacy — add a backup method
vignettes.explanation: Modafinil induces CYP3A4, lowering plasma levels of hormonal contraceptives and reducing their efficacy; patients should use an additional or alternative method during treatment and for about a month after stopping.

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Prazosin hydrochloride capsules are contraindicated in patients with known sensitivity to quinazolines, prazosin, or any of the inert ingredients.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION The dose of prazosin hydrochloride capsules should be adjusted according to the patient’s individual blood pressure response. The following is a guide to its administration: Initial Dose 1 mg two or three times a day (see WARNINGS. ) Maintenance Dose Dosage may be slowly increased to a total daily dose of 20 mg given in divided doses. The therapeutic dosages most commonly employed have ranged from 6 mg to 15 mg daily given in divided doses. Doses higher than 20 mg usually do not increase efficacy, however a few patients may benefit from further increases up to a daily dose of 40 mg given in divided doses. After initial titration some patients can be maintained …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS As with all alpha-blockers, prazosin hydrochloride may cause syncope with sudden loss of consciousness. In most cases, this is believed to be due to an excessive postural hypotensive effect, although occasionally the syncopal episode has been preceded by a bout of severe tachycardia with heart rates of 120 to 160 beats per minute. Syncopal episodes have usually occurred within 30 to 90 minutes of the initial dose of the drug; occasionally, they have been reported in association with rapid dosage increases or the introduction of another antihypertensive drug into the regimen of a patient taking high doses of prazosin hydrochloride. The incidence of syncopal episodes is approximately …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Accidental ingestion of at least 50 mg of prazosin hydrochloride in a two year old child resulted in profound drowsiness and depressed reflexes. No decrease in blood pressure was noted. Recovery was uneventful. Should overdosage lead to hypotension, support of the cardiovascular system is of first importance. Restoration of blood pressure and normalization of heart rate may be accomplished by keeping the patient in the supine position. If this measure is inadequate, shock should first be treated with volume expanders. If necessary, vasopressors should then be used. Renal function should be monitored and supported as needed. Laboratory data indicate prazosin hydrochloride is not …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions Prazosin hydrochloride has been administered without any adverse drug interaction in limited clinical experience to date with the following: (1) cardiac glycosides– digitalis and digoxin; (2) hypoglycemics–insulin, chlorpropamide, phenformin, tolazamide, and tolbutamide; (3) tranquilizers and sedatives–chlordiazepoxide, diazepam, and phenobarbital; (4) antigout– allopurinol, colchicine, and probenecid; (5) antiarrhythmics–procainamide, propranolol ( see WARNINGS however), and quinidine; and (6) analgesics, antipyretics and anti-inflammatories– propoxyphene, aspirin, indomethacin, and phenylbutazone. Addition of a diuretic or other antihypertensive agent to prazosin …

Addition of a diuretic or other antihypertensive agent to prazosin …

pregnancy.sourceText: Usage in Pregnancy: Pregnancy Category C. Prazosin hydrochloride has been shown to be associated with decreased litter size at birth, 1, 4, and 21 days of age in rats when given doses more than 225 times the usual maximum recommended human dose. No evidence of drug-related external, visceral, or skeletal fetal abnormalities were observed. No drug-related external, visceral, or skeletal abnormalities were observed in fetuses of pregnant rabbits and pregnant monkeys at doses more than 225 times and 12 times the usual maximum recommended human dose, respectively. The use of prazosin and a beta-blocker for the control of severe hypertension in 44 pregnant women revealed no drug-related fetal …

moa: Selective α1-adrenergic receptor antagonist; centrally blunts noradrenergic activation implicated in trauma nightmares, and peripherally causes vasodilation.
receptorProfile: α1 antagonist (crosses the blood–brain barrier).
onsetOfEffect: Nightmare/sleep benefit builds over days–weeks with titration.
halfLife: ~2–3 hours (short; dosed at bedtime for nightmares).
metabolism: Hepatic (demethylation and conjugation); highly protein bound.
startingDose: PTSD nightmares: 1 mg PO at bedtime (first-dose orthostasis risk).
titration: Increase gradually (e.g., to 2–6 mg qHS, higher in some, often higher effective doses in men) as BP tolerates.
maxDose: Individualized by response and orthostatic tolerance (commonly up to ~10–15 mg/day for nightmares).
frequencyTiming: At bedtime for nightmares (may add a daytime dose).
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Prazosin hydrochloride capsules are contraindicated in patients with known sensitivity to quinazolines, prazosin, or any of the inert ingredients.
overdoseLethality: Overdose → hypotension; generally supportive care.
ongoingMonitoring: Orthostatic blood pressure, especially with initiation and dose increases; nightmare/sleep response.
contraindicatedCombos: Combined with PDE5 inhibitors → risk of symptomatic hypotension
pregnancy: Limited data; use only if clearly needed.
geriatric: Orthostasis and fall risk — start low, titrate slowly.
cardiac: Orthostatic hypotension is the key hazard; warn about the first dose.
pearls: Off-label go-to for PTSD NIGHTMARES/trauma-related sleep disruption — dose at bedtime and titrate up.
FIRST-DOSE PHENOMENON: marked orthostatic hypotension/syncope — start 1 mg at bedtime and warn the patient.
Flag intraoperative floppy iris syndrome to ophthalmology before cataract surgery.
mnemonics: “PRAZosin for the PTSD dreams; the first dose can make you PLOP (orthostatic drop) — take it lying down at night.”
confusables: Prazosin (α1 blocker — PTSD nightmares) vs propranolol (β-blocker — akathisia/tremor) vs clonidine (α2 agonist).
classDifferentiators: The α1 antagonist of the group — a targeted PTSD-nightmare agent, not an EPS drug. Defining hazard is first-dose orthostatic hypotension (contrast propranolol’s bronchospasm/bradycardia).
examHooks: Combat veteran with recurrent trauma nightmares → prazosin at bedtime.
Which adjunct causes a “first-dose” orthostatic syncope? Prazosin.
counsellingPoints: Take the first dose at bedtime and get up slowly — dizziness/fainting can occur.
Rise slowly from sitting/lying, especially early in treatment.
Tell your eye surgeon you take prazosin before cataract surgery.

vignettes.stem: A 34-year-old veteran with PTSD reports recurrent vivid combat nightmares causing severe sleep disruption despite an SSRI. Which adjunctive medication most specifically targets the nightmares?
vignettes.explanation: Prazosin, an α1-adrenergic antagonist, reduces trauma-related nightmares and sleep disturbance in PTSD; start low at bedtime because of first-dose orthostatic hypotension.

source.name: CANMAT/ISTSS PTSD pharmacotherapy — prazosin for nightmares

moa: Central antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent; restores striatal dopamine:acetylcholine balance to relieve drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms.
receptorProfile: Antimuscarinic; weaker antihistaminic action than benztropine.
onsetOfEffect: Oral effect over 30–60 min; parenteral relief of dystonia within minutes.
metabolism: Hepatic; anticholinergic pharmacodynamics dominate interactions.
approvedCanada: Drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms (dystonia, parkinsonism, akathisia adjunct)
startingDose: 2.5 mg PO three times daily.
titration: Increase by 2.5 mg/day every few days as tolerated.
maxDose: Up to ~20–30 mg/day in divided doses.
frequencyTiming: TID (shorter-acting than benztropine).
overdoseLethality: Anticholinergic toxidrome in overdose; additive with other anticholinergics.
ongoingMonitoring: Cognition, bowel/bladder function; reassess ongoing need.
majorDDI: Additive anticholinergic effects with TCAs, antihistamines, low-potency antipsychotics
Altered GI absorption of co-administered drugs
contraindicatedCombos: Untreated narrow-angle glaucoma; significant GI/GU obstruction
pregnancy: Limited data; use only if needed.
geriatric: Same anticholinergic cautions as benztropine — confusion, falls, retention.
pearls: Anticholinergic essentially interchangeable with benztropine for EPS; more often dosed TID (shorter-acting).
Like all anticholinergics: helps acute dystonia and drug-induced parkinsonism, not akathisia, and can worsen tardive dyskinesia.
mnemonics: “ProCYCLIdine cycles the anticholinergic burden three times a day (TID).”
confusables: Procyclidine vs prochlorperazine (an antiEMETIC/antipsychotic that CAUSES dystonia) vs procyclidine’s look-alike benztropine.
classDifferentiators: Anticholinergic peer of benztropine/trihexyphenidyl; treats acute dystonia + parkinsonism, shorter-acting (TID). Not for akathisia; worsens TD.
examHooks: Anticholinergic option for drug-induced parkinsonism when benztropine is unavailable? Procyclidine or trihexyphenidyl.
counsellingPoints: Same anticholinergic effects as related agents — dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision.
Do not stop abruptly if on it long-term (rebound cholinergic EPS).
Report urinary difficulty or confusion.

vignettes.stem: A patient on a high-potency antipsychotic develops cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia and a resting tremor (drug-induced parkinsonism). An anticholinergic is chosen. Which statement is correct?
options: It will also resolve any co-existing tardive dyskinesia
Procyclidine can improve the parkinsonism but may unmask/worsen tardive dyskinesia
It has no anticholinergic side effects
vignettes.answer: Procyclidine can improve the parkinsonism but may unmask/worsen tardive dyskinesia
vignettes.explanation: Anticholinergics treat drug-induced parkinsonism and acute dystonia but can worsen tardive dyskinesia and are not first-line for akathisia (propranolol is). They carry anticholinergic burden.

boxedWarning.sourceText: Angina Pectoris There have been reports of exacerbation of angina and, in some cases, myocardial infarction, following abrupt discontinuance of propranolol therapy. Therefore, when discontinuance of propranolol is planned, the dosage should be gradually reduced over at least a few weeks, and the patient should be cautioned against interruption or cessation of therapy without the physician's advice. If propranolol therapy is interrupted and exacerbation of angina occurs, it usually is advisable to reinstitute propranolol therapy and take other measures appropriate for the management of unstable angina pectoris. Since coronary artery disease may be unrecognized, it may be prudent to follow the above advice in patients considered at risk of having occult atherosclerotic heart disease who are given propranolol for other indications.

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Propranolol is contraindicated in 1) cardiogenic shock; 2) sinus bradycardia and greater than first-degree block; 3) bronchial asthma; and 4) in patients with known hypersensitivity to propranolol hydrochloride.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION General Propranolol Hydrochloride Extended-Release Capsules, USP, provide propranolol hydrochloride in a sustained-release capsule for administration once daily. If patients are switched from propranolol hydrochloride tablets to Propranolol Hydrochloride Extended-Release Capsules, USP, care should be taken to assure that the desired therapeutic effect is maintained. Propranolol Hydrochloride Extended-Release Capsules, USP, should not be considered a simple mg-for-mg substitute for propranolol hydrochloride tablets. Propranolol Hydrochloride Extended-Release Capsules, USP, have different kinetics and produces lower blood levels. Retitration may be necessary, …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE Propranolol is not significantly dialyzable. In the event of overdosage or exaggerated response, the following measures should be employed: General If ingestion is or may have been recent, evacuate gastric contents, taking care to prevent pulmonary aspiration. Supportive Therapy Hypotension and bradycardia have been reported following propranolol overdose and should be treated appropriately. Glucagon can exert potent inotropic and chronotropic effects and may be particularly useful for the treatment of hypotension or depressed myocardial function after a propranolol overdose. Glucagon should be administered as 50-150 mcg/kg intravenously followed by continuous drip of 1-5 mg/hour …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions Caution should be exercised when Propranolol Hydrochloride Extended-Release Capsules, USP, are administered with drugs that have an affect on CYP2D6, 1A2, or 2C19 metabolic pathways. Co-administration of such drugs with propranolol may lead to clinically relevant drug interactions and changes on its efficacy and/or toxicity (see Drug Interactions in PHARMACOKINETICS AND DRUG METABOLISM ). Alcohol when used concomitantly with propranolol, may increase plasma levels of propranolol.

contraindicatedCombos.sourceText: Drug Interactions Caution should be exercised when Propranolol Hydrochloride Extended-Release Capsules, USP, are administered with drugs that have an affect on CYP2D6, 1A2, or 2C19 metabolic pathways. Co-administration of such drugs with propranolol may lead to clinically relevant drug interactions and changes on its efficacy and/or toxicity (see Drug Interactions in PHARMACOKINETICS AND DRUG METABOLISM ). Alcohol when used concomitantly with propranolol, may increase plasma levels of propranolol.

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy Pregnancy Category C In a series of reproductive and developmental toxicology studies, propranolol was given to rats by gavage or in the diet throughout pregnancy and lactation. At doses of 150 mg/kg/day, but not at doses of 80 mg/kg/day (equivalent to the MRHD on a body surface area basis), treatment was associated with embryotoxicity (reduced litter size and increased resorption rates) as well as neonatal toxicity (deaths). Propranolol hydrochloride also was administered (in the feed) to rabbits (throughout pregnancy and lactation) at doses as high as 150 mg/kg/day (about 5 times the maximum recommended human oral daily dose). No evidence of embryo or neonatal toxicity was …

formulationsCanada: Tablet (20 MG, 80 MG, 40 MG, 120 MG, 10 MG, 160 MG, 60 MG, 1 MG, 3.75 MG)
Capsule (Sustained-Release) (20 MG, 80 MG, 40 MG, 120 MG, 10 MG, 160 MG, 60 MG, 1 MG, 3.75 MG)
moa: Non-selective β-adrenergic antagonist (β1 and β2); in psychiatry blunts peripheral adrenergic symptoms and central noradrenergic tone.
receptorProfile: β1 + β2 antagonist; lipophilic (crosses the blood–brain barrier).
onsetOfEffect: Akathisia may improve within hours–days; single dose blunts performance-anxiety symptoms within ~30–60 min.
metabolism: Hepatic, extensive first-pass; substrate of CYP2D6, CYP1A2, CYP2C19. Highly protein bound.
approvedCanada: Hypertension, angina, arrhythmia, migraine prophylaxis, essential tremor (cardiology/neurology labels)
startingDose: Akathisia: 10–20 mg PO BID–TID. Performance anxiety: 10–40 mg once, ~30–60 min before the event.
titration: Increase gradually for akathisia to ~40–80 (up to ~120) mg/day as tolerated by HR/BP.
maxDose: For akathisia, typically up to ~80–120 mg/day; limited by bradycardia/hypotension.
frequencyTiming: BID–TID (IR); PRN before a stressor for performance anxiety.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Reduce in significant hepatic impairment (high first-pass metabolism).
startStopCrossTaper: Do NOT stop abruptly after chronic use — rebound tachycardia/hypertension/angina; taper.
boxedWarning: Angina Pectoris There have been reports of exacerbation of angina and, in some cases, myocardial infarction, following abrupt discontinuance of propranolol therapy. Therefore, when discontinuance of propranolol is planned, the dosage should be gradually reduced over at least a few weeks, and the patient should be cautioned against interruption or cessation of therapy without the physician's advice. If propranolol therapy is interrupted and exacerbation of angina occurs, it usually is advisable to reinstitute propranolol therapy and take other measures appropriate for the management of unstable angina pectoris. Since coronary artery disease may be unrecognized, it may be prudent to follow the above advice in patients considered at risk of having occult atherosclerotic heart disease who are given propranolol for other indications.
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Propranolol is contraindicated in 1) cardiogenic shock; 2) sinus bradycardia and greater than first-degree block; 3) bronchial asthma; and 4) in patients with known hypersensitivity to propranolol hydrochloride.
syndromes: Rebound sympathetic surge on abrupt withdrawal
overdoseLethality: Dangerous in overdose — bradycardia, hypotension, heart block, seizures and cardiogenic shock; membrane-stabilizing effects. (Antidotes: glucagon, high-dose insulin.)
baselineWorkup: Screen for asthma/COPD and bradyarrhythmia/AV block
ongoingMonitoring: HR and BP; ask about wheeze; in diabetics warn about masked hypoglycemia.
majorDDI: Other AV-nodal blockers (verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin) — additive bradycardia/block
CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, bupropion) raise propranolol levels
pregnancy: Used when needed; monitor for fetal bradycardia/growth and neonatal hypoglycemia near term.
lactation: Low levels in milk; generally compatible with monitoring.
geriatric: Watch bradycardia, hypotension, falls, fatigue.
cardiac: Bradycardia/hypotension are the dose-limiting effects; contraindicated with high-degree AV block.
pearls: FIRST-LINE for antipsychotic-induced AKATHISIA (± lower the antipsychotic dose or add a short-course benzodiazepine) — anticholinergics do NOT reliably help akathisia.
Also the classic agent for performance/“stage-fright” anxiety and for lithium/SSRI (and essential) tremor.
Caution or avoid in asthma/COPD, bradycardia, AV block, decompensated heart failure; never stop abruptly.
mnemonics: “A for Akathisia, A for Adrenergic block” — propranolol calms the restless legs of akathisia and the racing heart of stage fright.
confusables: Propranolol (akathisia, non-selective) vs metoprolol/atenolol (β1-selective — preferred if lung disease) vs prazosin (α1 blocker for PTSD nightmares).
classDifferentiators: The β-blocker of the group: treats AKATHISIA and adrenergic/tremor symptoms — the one EPS it targets is akathisia (dystonia → anticholinergic; TD → VMAT2 inhibitor). Non-selective → asthma caution.
examHooks: Inner restlessness / inability to sit still on an antipsychotic (akathisia) → propranolol (± dose reduction).
Fine tremor on lithium or an SSRI → propranolol.
counsellingPoints: For stage fright, take ~30–60 min before the event.
Do not stop suddenly — taper.
Report wheezing; rise slowly to avoid dizziness.
Diabetics: beta-blockers can hide low-blood-sugar warning signs.

vignettes.stem: Two weeks after starting risperidone, a patient reports an inner restlessness and a constant urge to move, pacing and shifting weight while seated. Which is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic treatment?
vignettes.explanation: This is akathisia. First-line management is dose reduction of the offending antipsychotic and/or a beta-blocker such as propranolol (± a benzodiazepine). Anticholinergics (benztropine) are for dystonia/parkinsonism, and VMAT2 inhibitors (tetrabenazine) are for tardive dyskinesia.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: DEPRESSION AND SUICIDALITY Tetrabenazine can increase the risk of depression and suicidal thoughts and behavior (suicidality) in patients with Huntington’s disease. Anyone considering the use of tetrabenazine must balance the risks of depression and suicidality with the clinical need for control of chorea. Close observation of patients for the emergence or worsening of depression, suicidality, or unusual changes in behavior should accompany therapy. Patients, their caregivers, and families should be informed of the risk of depression and suicidality and should be instructed to report behaviors of concern promptly to the treating physician. Particular caution should be exercised in treating patients with a history of depression or prior suicide attempts or ideation, which are increased in frequency in Huntington’s disease. Tetrabenazine is contraindicated in patients who are actively suicidal, and in patients with untreated or inadequately treated depression [see Contraindications ( 4 ), Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 )]. WARNING: DEPRESSION AND SUICIDALITY See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning . Increases the risk of depression and suicidal thoughts and behavior (suicidality) in patients with Huntington’s disease ( 5.1 ) Balance risks of depression and suicidality with the clinical need for control of chorea when considering the use of …

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS Tetrabenazine tablets are contraindicated in patients: Who are actively suicidal, or in patients with untreated or inadequately treated depression [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 )] . With hepatic impairment [see Use in Specific Populations ( 8.6 ), Clinical Pharmacology ( 12.3 )] . Taking monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs). Tetrabenazine tablets should not be used in combination with an MAOI, or within a minimum of 14 days of discontinuing therapy with an MAOI [see Drug Interactions ( 7.3 )] . Taking reserpine. At least 20 days should elapse after stopping reserpine before starting tetrabenazine tablets [see Drug Interactions ( 7.2 )] . Taking deutetrabenazine or valbenazine [see Drug Interactions ( 7.7 )] . Actively suicidal, or who have depression which is untreated or undertreated ( 4 , 5.1 ) Hepatic impairment ( 4 , 8.6 , 12.3 ) Taking monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) or reserpine ( 4 , 7.2 , 7.3 ) Taking deutetrabenazine or valbenazine ( 4 , 7.7 )

The 1 st week's starting dose is 12.5 mg daily; 2 nd week, 25 mg (12.5 mg twice daily); then slowly titrate at weekly intervals by 12.5 mg to a tolerated dose that reduces chorea. ( 2.1 , 2.2 ) Doses of 37.5 mg and up to 50 mg per day should be administered in three divided doses per day with a maximum recommended single dose not to exceed 25 mg. ( 2.2 ) Patients requiring doses above 50 mg per day should be genotyped for the drug metabolizing enzyme CYP2D6 to determine if the patient is a poor metabolizer (PM) or an extensive metabolizer (EM). ( 2.2 , 5.3 ) Maximum daily dose in PMs: 50 mg with …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Three episodes of overdose occurred in the open-label trials performed in support of registration. Eight cases of overdose with tetrabenazine have been reported in the literature. The dose of tetrabenazine in these patients ranged from 100 mg to 1 g. Adverse reactions associated with tetrabenazine overdose include acute dystonia, oculogyric crisis, nausea and vomiting, sweating, sedation, hypotension, confusion, diarrhea, hallucinations, rubor, and tremor. Treatment should consist of those general measures employed in the management of overdosage with any CNS-active drug. General supportive and symptomatic measures are recommended. Cardiac rhythm and vital signs should be …

majorDDI.sourceText: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS 7.1 Strong CYP2D6 Inhibitors In vitro studies indicate that α-HTBZ and β-HTBZ are substrates for CYP2D6. Strong CYP2D6 inhibitors (e.g., paroxetine, fluoxetine, quinidine) markedly increase exposure to these metabolites. A reduction in tetrabenazine dose may be necessary when adding a strong CYP2D6 inhibitor (e.g., fluoxetine, paroxetine, quinidine) in patients maintained on a stable dose of tetrabenazine. The daily dose of tetrabenazine should not exceed 50 mg per day and the maximum single dose of tetrabenazine should not exceed 25 mg in patients taking strong CYP2D6 inhibitors [see Dosage and Administration ( 2.3 ), Warnings and Precautions ( 5.3 ), Use in Specific …

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary There are no adequate data on the developmental risk associated with the use of tetrabenazine in pregnant women. Administration of tetrabenazine to rats throughout pregnancy and lactation resulted in an increase in stillbirths and postnatal offspring mortality. Administration of a major human metabolite of tetrabenazine to rats during pregnancy or during pregnancy and lactation produced adverse effects on the developing fetus and offspring (increased mortality, decreased growth, and neurobehavioral and reproductive impairment). The adverse developmental effects of tetrabenazine and a major human metabolite of tetrabenazine in rats occurred at clinically relevant …

moa: Reversible VMAT2 (vesicular monoamine transporter type 2) inhibitor — blocks packaging of monoamines into presynaptic vesicles, depleting dopamine (and serotonin/norepinephrine) to reduce hyperkinetic movements.
receptorProfile: VMAT2 inhibition (presynaptic dopamine depletion); weak D2 affinity via metabolites.
onsetOfEffect: Chorea/dyskinesia improve over days–weeks with titration.
halfLife: Parent short; active dihydrotetrabenazine (HTBZ) metabolites (~4–8 h) drive the effect.
metabolism: Carbonyl reductase → active α- and β-HTBZ; further metabolized by CYP2D6 → reduce dose with CYP2D6 inhibitors and in poor metabolizers.
approvedCanada: Hyperkinetic movement disorders — Huntington chorea; hemiballismus; tardive dyskinesia; Tourette/tics (Nitoman label)
offLabel: Tardive dyskinesia (where labelled elsewhere as a VMAT2 indication)
startingDose: 12.5 mg PO once–twice daily; titrate slowly.
titration: Increase by 12.5 mg every few days to effect/tolerability.
maxDose: Up to ~75–100 mg/day (lower with CYP2D6 inhibitors / poor metabolizers).
frequencyTiming: Divided doses (BID–TID); short-acting active metabolites.
renalHepaticAdjustment: Contraindicated in hepatic impairment (per US labelling).

Particular caution should be exercised in treating patients with a history of depression or prior suicide attempts or ideation, which are increased in frequency in Huntington’s disease. Tetrabenazine is contraindicated in patients who are …

With hepatic impairment [see Use in Specific Populations ( 8.6 ), Clinical Pharmacology ( 12.3 )] . Taking monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs). Tetrabenazine tablets should not be used in combination with an MAOI, or within a minimum of 14 days of discontinuing therapy with an MAOI [see Drug Interactions ( 7.3 )] . Taking reserpine. At least 20 days should elapse after stopping reserpine before starting tetrabenazine tablets [see Drug Interactions ( 7.2 )] . Taking deutetrabenazine or valbenazine [see Drug Interactions ( 7.7 )] . Actively suicidal, or who have depression which is untreated or undertreated ( 4 , 5.1 ) Hepatic impairment ( 4 , 8.6 , 12.3 ) Taking monoamine oxidase …
overdoseLethality: Overdose → sedation, hypotension, sweating, tremor; QT effects — supportive care.
ongoingMonitoring: Depression/suicidality at each visit; parkinsonism/akathisia; QT if risk factors.
majorDDI: CYP2D6 inhibitors (fluoxetine, paroxetine, bupropion) — raise HTBZ levels (reduce dose)
pregnancy: Limited data; use only if clearly needed.
geriatric: Sensitive to sedation, parkinsonism, orthostasis, depression.
cardiac: QT prolongation — caution with other QT drugs / electrolyte abnormalities.
pearls: VMAT2 inhibitor that DEPLETES dopamine to treat hyperkinetic movements (Huntington chorea, tardive dyskinesia) — the mechanistic OPPOSITE of what causes drug-induced parkinsonism.
Because it depletes dopamine it can CAUSE depression/suicidality and parkinsonism — screen mood and avoid in active depression.
Canadian brand is Nitoman; CYP2D6 metabolizes the active HTBZ metabolites, so 2D6 inhibitors/poor metabolizers need dose reduction.
mnemonics: “TETRAbenazine TAKES away dopamine (VMAT2) → can take away mood too (depression).”
confusables: Tetrabenazine (Canada: Nitoman) vs deutetrabenazine/valbenazine (US-only VMAT2 inhibitors) vs reserpine (older non-selective VMAT inhibitor).
classDifferentiators: VMAT2 inhibitor for TARDIVE DYSKINESIA / Huntington chorea (dopamine depletion) — the correct answer for TD where anticholinergics are WRONG. Canada-available (Nitoman); its dopamine depletion risks depression/parkinsonism/QT.
examHooks: Persistent orofacial/lingual choreoathetoid movements (tardive dyskinesia) → VMAT2 inhibitor (tetrabenazine), NOT an anticholinergic.
Which movement-disorder drug can precipitate depression/suicidality by depleting dopamine? Tetrabenazine.
counsellingPoints: Report low mood or any thoughts of self-harm immediately (dopamine depletion can worsen depression).
May cause drowsiness and slowed movements — avoid driving until you know your response.
Rise slowly (orthostatic dizziness).

vignettes.stem: A patient on long-term antipsychotic therapy develops persistent involuntary lip-smacking, tongue protrusion and choreiform finger movements that continue despite lowering the antipsychotic. Which medication specifically treats this condition?
vignettes.explanation: This is tardive dyskinesia. VMAT2 inhibitors (tetrabenazine, and US-only valbenazine/deutetrabenazine) deplete presynaptic dopamine and are the specific treatment. Anticholinergics (benztropine, trihexyphenidyl) can WORSEN TD; propranolol is for akathisia.

contraindications.sourceText: CONTRAINDICATIONS Trihexyphenidyl is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to trihexyphenidyl or to any of the other ingredients. Trihexyphenidyl is also contraindicated in patients with narrow angle glaucoma. Blindness after long-term use due to narrow angle glaucoma has been reported.

startingDose.sourceText: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION Dosage should be individualized. The initial dose should be low and then increased gradually, especially in patients over 60 years of age.Whether trihexyphenidyl may best be given before or after meals should be determined by the way the patient reacts. Postencephalitic patients, who are usually more prone to excessive salivation, may prefer to take it after meals and may, in addition, require small amounts of atropine which, under such circumstances, is sometimes an effective adjuvant. If trihexyphenidyl tends to dry the mouth excessively, it may be better to take it before meals, unless it causes nausea. If taken after meals, the thirst sometimes induced can be …

seriousAE.sourceText: WARNINGS Patients to be treated with trihexyphenidyl should have a gonioscope evaluation prior to initiation of therapy and close monitoring of intraocular pressures. The use of anticholinergic drugs may precipitate angle closure with an increase in intraocular pressure. If blurring of vision occurs during therapy, the possibility of narrow angle glaucoma should be considered. Blindness has been reported due to aggravation of narrow angle glaucoma. (See CONTRAINDICATIONS and ADVERSE REACTIONS ). Trihexyphenidyl should be administered with caution in hot weather, especially when given concomitantly with other atropine-like drugs to the chronically ill, alcoholics, those who have central …

overdoseLethality.sourceText: OVERDOSAGE The mean oral LD 50 of trihexyphenidyl has been reported to be 365 mg/kg (range, 325 to 410 mg/kg) in mice and 1660 mg/kg (1420 to 1940 mg/kg) in rats. At a dose of 40 mg/kg, dogs have exhibited emesis, restlessness followed by drowsiness, equilibrium disturbances, and mydriasis. In humans, doses up to 300 mg (5 mg/kg) have been ingested without fatalities or sequelae. However, rare cases of death associated with trihexyphenidyl overdosages taken in conjunction with other CNS-depressant agents have been reported or in patients with a compromised respiratory condition. Trihexyphenidyl blood concentrations associated with the fatalities ranged from 0.03 to 0.80 mg/l. Signs and …

majorDDI.sourceText: Drug Interactions Cannabinoids, barbiturates, opiates, and alcohol may have additive effects with trihexyphenidyl, and thus, an abuse potential exists. Concurrent use of alcohol or other CNS depressants with trihexyphenidyl may cause increased sedative effects. Monoamine oxidase inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants possessing significant anticholinergic activity may intensify the anticholinergic effects of antidyskinetic agents because of the secondary anticholinergic activities of these medications. Prophylactic administration of anticholinergic agents, such as trihexyphenidyl, as a prevention of drug-induced parkinsonism during neuroleptic therapy is not recommended. There may be an …

Concurrent use of alcohol or other CNS depressants with trihexyphenidyl may cause increased sedative effects. Monoamine oxidase inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants possessing significant anticholinergic activity may intensify the anticholinergic effects of antidyskinetic agents because of the secondary anticholinergic activities of these medications. Prophylactic administration of anticholinergic agents, such as trihexyphenidyl, as a prevention of drug-induced parkinsonism during neuroleptic therapy is not recommended. There may be an …

pregnancy.sourceText: Pregnancy Teratogenic Effects Pregnancy Category C Animal reproduction studies to evaluate teratogenic and embryotoxic potential have not been conducted with trihexyphenidyl. It is also not known whether trihexyphenidyl can cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman or can affect reproduction capacity. Trihexyphenidyl should be given to a pregnant woman only if clearly needed.

moa: Central antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent; corrects the relative cholinergic excess in the striatum caused by D2 blockade, relieving EPS and parkinsonism.
receptorProfile: Antimuscarinic (M1) antagonist; some direct antispasmodic action.
onsetOfEffect: Oral effect within ~1 hour.
halfLife: Relatively short — given in divided doses.
metabolism: Hepatic hydroxylation; anticholinergic pharmacodynamics dominate.
startingDose: 1 mg PO on day 1, increasing gradually.
titration: Increase by 2 mg every 3–5 days to usual 6–10 mg/day in divided doses.
maxDose: Up to ~15 mg/day in divided doses.
frequencyTiming: BID–QID (divided doses; shorter-acting).
contraindications: CONTRAINDICATIONS Trihexyphenidyl is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to trihexyphenidyl or to any of the other ingredients. Trihexyphenidyl is also contraindicated in patients with narrow angle glaucoma. Blindness after long-term use due to narrow angle glaucoma has been reported.
overdoseLethality: Anticholinergic toxidrome in overdose; additive with other anticholinergics.
ongoingMonitoring: Cognition, bowel/bladder; watch for misuse (euphoriant potential); reassess need.
majorDDI: Additive anticholinergic effects with TCAs, antihistamines, low-potency antipsychotics
Delays gastric emptying — alters absorption of other drugs
pregnancy: Limited data; use only if needed.
geriatric: High anticholinergic risk (confusion, falls, retention); avoid where possible.
pearls: Anticholinergic for EPS/parkinsonism; notable for a euphoriant/hallucinogenic MISUSE potential (unlike other adjuncts) — watch for diversion.
Same rule as its anticholinergic peers: helps dystonia + parkinsonism, not akathisia, worsens tardive dyskinesia.
mnemonics: “TriHEXYphenidyl — the anticholinergic that can HEX you into a high (euphoria/misuse).”
confusables: Trihexyphenidyl (Artane) vs benztropine vs procyclidine — interchangeable anticholinergics; trihexyphenidyl is the one flagged for abuse potential.
classDifferentiators: Anticholinergic peer of benztropine/procyclidine for dystonia + parkinsonism; distinctive for euphoriant misuse potential. Not for akathisia; worsens TD.
examHooks: Which EPS anticholinergic carries the most notable abuse/euphoriant potential? Trihexyphenidyl.
counsellingPoints: Expect dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation.
Do not exceed the prescribed dose (misuse risk).
Avoid overheating; report urinary difficulty or confusion.

vignettes.stem: A patient repeatedly requests dose increases of trihexyphenidyl beyond what their extrapyramidal symptoms warrant and describes feeling “high” on it. What property of this drug explains this behaviour?
vignettes.explanation: Trihexyphenidyl (and other anticholinergics) can produce euphoria and, at higher doses, anticholinergic delirium/hallucinations, giving it a misuse potential distinctive among EPS agents.

boxedWarning.sourceText: WARNING: DEPRESSION AND SUICIDAL IDEATION AND BEHAVIOR IN PATIENTS WITH HUNTINGTON’S DISEASE VMAT2 inhibitors, including INGREZZA and INGREZZA SPRINKLE, can increase the risk of depression and suicidal thoughts and behavior in patients with Huntington’s disease. Anyone considering the use of INGREZZA or INGREZZA SPRINKLE must balance the risks of depression and suicidal ideation and behavior with the clinical need for treatment of chorea. Closely monitor patients for the emergence or worsening of depression, suicidal ideation, or unusual changes in behavior. Inform patients, their caregivers, and families of the risk of depression and suicidal ideation and behavior and instruct them to report behaviors of concern promptly to the treating physician. Particular caution should be exercised in treating patients with a history of depression or prior suicide attempts or ideation, which are increased in frequency in patients with Huntington’s disease [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.1 )]. WARNING: DEPRESSION AND SUICIDAL IDEATION AND BEHAVIOR IN PATIENTS WITH HUNTINGTON’S DISEASE See full prescribing information for complete boxed warning. • Increases the risk of depression and suicidal thoughts and behavior in patients with Huntington’s disease ( 5.1 ) • Balance risks of depression, and suicidal ideation and behavior with the clinical need for treatment of chorea when considering …

contraindications.sourceText: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS INGREZZA and INGREZZA SPRINKLE are contraindicated in patients with a history of hypersensitivity to valbenazine or any components of INGREZZA or INGREZZA SPRINKLE. Rash, urticaria, and reactions consistent with angioedema (e.g., swelling of the face, lips, and mouth) have been reported with use of INGREZZA [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.2 ) and Adverse Reactions ( 6.2 )]. Known hypersensitivity to valbenazine or any components of INGREZZA or INGREZZA SPRINKLE. ( 4 )

overdoseLethality.sourceText: 10 OVERDOSAGE Human Experience The pre-marketing clinical trials involving INGREZZA in approximately 850 subjects do not provide information regarding symptoms with overdose. Management of Overdosage No specific antidotes for INGREZZA or INGREZZA SPRINKLE are known. In managing overdose, provide supportive care, including close medical supervision and monitoring, and consider the possibility of multiple drug involvement. Consider contacting the Poison Help line (1-800-222-1222) or a medical toxicologist for additional overdose management recommendations.

pregnancy.sourceText: 8.1 Pregnancy Risk Summary The limited available data on INGREZZA or INGREZZA SPRINKLE use in pregnant women are insufficient to inform a drug-associated risk. In animal reproductive studies, no malformations were observed when valbenazine was administered orally to rats and rabbits during the period of organogenesis at doses up to 1.8 or 24 times, respectively, the maximum recommended human dose (MRHD) of 80 mg/day based on mg/m 2 body surface area. However, administration of valbenazine to pregnant rats during organogenesis through lactation produced an increase in the number of stillborn pups and postnatal pup mortalities at doses <1 times the MRHD based on mg/m 2 [see Data ]. moa: Selective VMAT2 inhibitor (a purified/prodrug relative of tetrabenazine); depletes presynaptic dopamine to reduce tardive-dyskinesia movements.
receptorProfile: VMAT2 inhibition; hydrolyzes to the active metabolite (the α-HTBZ isomer).
onsetOfEffect: TD improvement over weeks.
metabolism: Hydrolysis to active metabolite; further via CYP3A4 (and CYP2D6). QT prolongation possible.
startingDose: 40 mg PO once daily.
titration: Increase to 80 mg once daily after ~1 week; 40 mg may be maintained if needed.
maxDose: 80 mg/day (lower with strong CYP3A4/2D6 issues).
frequencyTiming: Once daily (its key convenience advantage over tetrabenazine).
boxedWarning: WARNING: DEPRESSION AND SUICIDAL IDEATION AND BEHAVIOR IN PATIENTS WITH HUNTINGTON’S DISEASE VMAT2 inhibitors, including INGREZZA and INGREZZA SPRINKLE, can increase the risk of depression and suicidal thoughts and behavior in patients with Huntington’s disease. Anyone considering the use of INGREZZA or INGREZZA SPRINKLE must balance the risks of depression and suicidal ideation and behavior with the clinical need for treatment of chorea. Closely monitor patients for the emergence or worsening of depression, suicidal ideation, or unusual changes in behavior. Inform patients, their caregivers, and families of the risk of depression and suicidal ideation and behavior and instruct them to report behaviors of concern promptly to the treating physician. Particular caution should be exercised in treating patients with a history of depression or prior suicide attempts or ideation, which are …
contraindications: 4 CONTRAINDICATIONS INGREZZA and INGREZZA SPRINKLE are contraindicated in patients with a history of hypersensitivity to valbenazine or any components of INGREZZA or INGREZZA SPRINKLE. Rash, urticaria, and reactions consistent with angioedema (e.g., swelling of the face, lips, and mouth) have been reported with use of INGREZZA [see Warnings and Precautions ( 5.2 ) and Adverse Reactions ( 6.2 )]. Known hypersensitivity to valbenazine or any components of INGREZZA or INGREZZA SPRINKLE. ( 4 )
overdoseLethality: Limited data; expect sedation and QT effects — supportive care.
ongoingMonitoring: Sedation, parkinsonism, mood; QT if risk factors.
majorDDI: Strong CYP3A4 inducers reduce efficacy; strong CYP3A4/2D6 inhibitors raise levels
pregnancy: Limited data; avoid unless clearly needed.
cardiac: QT prolongation — caution with QT drugs.
pearls: US-only — for awareness (Ingrezza): VMAT2 inhibitor approved for tardive dyskinesia; NOT marketed in Canada (pipeline flags availability).
Key advantage over tetrabenazine: ONCE-DAILY dosing and generally better tolerability.
Same class caveats: parkinsonism, somnolence, QT, and dopamine-depletion mood effects.
mnemonics: “VALbenazine = VALue of once-daily” — the convenient once-daily VMAT2 inhibitor for TD.
confusables: Valbenazine vs deutetrabenazine (both US-only TD agents) vs tetrabenazine (Canada-available).
classDifferentiators: Once-daily VMAT2 inhibitor for tardive dyskinesia; US-only. Simpler dosing than tetrabenazine; shares dopamine-depletion/QT profile.
examHooks: Which VMAT2 inhibitor for TD is dosed once daily? Valbenazine (US-only).
counsellingPoints: Take once daily as prescribed.
Report drowsiness affecting driving.
Report palpitations/fainting (QT).

vignettes.stem: Among the VMAT2 inhibitors used for tardive dyskinesia, which is characterized by once-daily dosing (though it is not marketed in Canada)?
vignettes.explanation: Valbenazine (US-only) is a VMAT2 inhibitor dosed once daily for tardive dyskinesia, offering simpler dosing than tetrabenazine (BID–TID). Benztropine and amantadine are not VMAT2 inhibitors.
vignettes.testedFact: Valbenazine once-daily VMAT2 inhibitor for TD
